# System Design — Reference and Architectural Vocabulary

> **Primary purpose**: comprehensive reference of system design concepts that have surfaced as load-bearing for the orchestrator architecture. Each entry walks the standard taxonomy, options, and tradeoffs of a topic — accurate to the canon (DDIA, distributed systems literature, system design podcasts). Entries are organized MECE-style *within* each topic; the doc as a whole is not strict MECE at the top level (entries deliberately overlap where survey vs deep-dive entries cover the same dimension at different granularities). The system design content stands on its own merit as a fluency reference.
> **Secondary purpose**: architectural inspiration for the orchestrator's harness and the apps each org ships, added where a system design concept genuinely informs an architectural decision. The Layered architecture section below distinguishes which inspiration applies at the harness layer vs the app layer.
> **Both layers must be accurate. System design content takes precedence over orchestrator-specific framing when the two are in tension.**
> **Curation rubric for new entries**: (1) distinct system design concept with non-trivial taxonomy, (2) multiple options with real tradeoffs, (3) covers ground other entries don't, (4) substantive standard material. *(Secondary: does it produce orchestrator-relevant architectural inspiration?)* Tests 1–4 gate inclusion; test 5 is additive, not filtering.
> **Revisit when**: looking up a system design topic for fluency; making a system design decision and consulting the secondary layer; evaluating whether a proposed feature has a known pattern; harvesting new concepts.

## Layered architecture: harness vs apps

The orchestrator has **two distinct layers with different sovereignty disciplines**, and almost everything else in this doc is written from the harness perspective. Surfacing the layer distinction explicitly because it changes which architectural choices apply where — and because conflating the two layers leads to wrong calls (like reflexively rejecting tools at one layer when the rejection only applies at the other).

### The two layers

| Layer | What it is | Where it runs | Sovereignty discipline |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Orchestrator core (the harness)** | The navigator's workspace: harness, knowledge graph, skills, loops, dream state, event log, OODA cycle, shadow clones | Local machine, single process per human | Local-first, sovereign all the way down, single-machine, no external dependencies for core function |
| **App layer (per-org products)** | The products each org ships to users: customer-facing agents (servicegrid), games (pilgrimage), autonomous services (dreamtime), dashboards, anything end-users touch | Vercel / Railway / managed platforms, multi-instance | Production web app discipline: scale, availability, multi-user, multi-instance |

The **harness** is the place sovereignty matters because the navigator depends on it for the OODA cycle. If the harness fails, every loop fails; if it depends on a managed service, sovereignty is compromised at the foundation. **The harness is the one part of the stack that absolutely must run locally**, with no external dependencies for core function.

The **app layer** is where the orgs serve users. Apps have to scale, stay up, handle bursty load, and integrate with the messy outside world. Their fitness function is *can the app serve its users well at scale*, and that fitness function does not require local-first sovereignty. Apps can and should use managed services where managed services are the right tool.

### What changes between layers

| Concern | Harness layer | App layer |
|---|---|---|
| **Database** | SQLite / Postgres locally; event log on top; eventual migration to Iceberg+DuckDB possible | Postgres (Neon/Supabase), MySQL, MongoDB — whatever the app needs |
| **Cache** | In-process + OS page cache + LLM provider's prompt cache; no distributed cache | Redis (Upstash, Vercel KV, Railway Redis), CDN edge cache, browser cache |
| **Session storage** | N/A (single human, single session) | Redis, signed cookies, JWT, database-backed sessions |
| **Message queue / pub/sub** | Event log via consumer groups | BullMQ on Redis, Inngest, Trigger.dev, AWS SQS, NATS |
| **Real-time fan-out** | SSE for dashboard; in-process events for shadow clones | WebSockets via Pusher / Ably / Soketi / Redis pub/sub |
| **Rate limiting** | In-process token budget caps | Redis-backed rate limiters (Upstash Ratelimit), edge rate limiters |
| **Background jobs** | Sagas + event log + the harness as saga framework | BullMQ, Inngest, Trigger.dev, Cloudflare Queues |
| **Auth** | Single human; no auth model needed beyond OS permissions | Real auth: Clerk, Auth0, Supabase Auth, NextAuth |
| **Email / SMS** | Proactive messaging via Slack to the navigator | Resend / Postmark / SendGrid for transactional; Twilio for SMS |
| **Object storage** | Local filesystem; possibly backup to S3/R2 | S3, R2, Backblaze for user uploads, generated assets |
| **Search** | Inverted index in-process over the corpus | Algolia, Meilisearch, Typesense for user-facing search |
| **Observability** | Internal event log + projections + dashboard | External observability: Sentry, Datadog, Honeycomb, Vercel Analytics, PostHog |
| **Vector / embedding store** | Knowledge graph + grep + (eventually) DuckDB | Pinecone, Qdrant, Weaviate, pgvector for RAG features |
| **Deployment** | `npm start` on the local machine | GitHub Actions → Vercel / Railway via the distribute skill |
| **Multi-instance state coordination** | N/A (single instance) | The full distributed-systems toolkit applies |
| **CDN / edge caching** | N/A | Vercel edge, Cloudflare, Fastly |

### Why the two layers exist

The harness layer has **one human user (the navigator)** and exists to amplify their capacity through OODA cycles, shadow clones, and persistent state. Its fitness function is "does the navigator make better decisions faster," and the threat model is "the navigator's machine crashes, the navigator's external dependencies fail, or sovereignty is compromised by lock-in." Local-first, single-machine, no-external-dependencies is the architecture that matches that fitness function.

The app layer serves **other people** — customers, players, end users. Its fitness function is "does the app serve its users well at scale," and the threat model is the standard production web app threat model: traffic spikes, abuse, downtime, scaling bottlenecks, multi-instance state coordination. The architecture that matches *that* fitness function is the standard production web app stack: managed databases, Redis, queues, edge caching, observability, real auth.

**The two layers are not the same thing and shouldn't be reasoned about with the same constraints.** Trying to apply harness-layer sovereignty to the app layer would make the apps slow, unreliable, and impossible to scale. Trying to apply app-layer "managed services are fine" thinking to the harness would compromise the navigator's sovereignty over their own cognition.

### How this doc relates to the two layers

**Almost every entry in this doc is written from the harness perspective**, because that's where most of the architecturally interesting decisions live and where the navigator's-cosmos framing most directly applies. The "Architectural inspiration thread" sections in each entry should be read as *harness-layer* inspiration unless explicitly noted otherwise. The Quick reference and Cross-entry inspiration threads sections that follow are also harness-layer.

**App-layer architecture is mostly the standard production web app stack**, which is well-documented elsewhere and doesn't need a sovereign-aligned reframing. The walks in this doc are useful as fluency for app-layer decisions too — knowing how databases, caches, queues, and replication actually work is foundational regardless of layer — but the architectural-inspiration layer (the orchestrator-specific commitments) does not apply at the app layer in the same way.

**When in doubt about a tool, ask "is this for the harness or for an app?"** The same tool can be anti-pattern at one layer and the right answer at the other. Redis is the canonical example: anti-pattern at the harness layer (separate process, network surface, distributed cache that local-first doesn't need), right answer at the app layer (session storage, rate limiting, pub/sub fan-out across multi-instance deployments). The same is true for managed Postgres, queue services, vector databases, observability platforms, and most production web app infrastructure.

### Calibration note

This doc was harness-centric for its first 20 entries because the harness is where the architecturally novel work lives — most app-layer decisions have well-established answers from the broader web app community. But the app layer is real and load-bearing for the orgs the navigator serves: servicegrid ships customer-facing agents to small contractors; pilgrimage ships games; dreamtime ships autonomous services. Forgetting the app layer existed led to reflexively rejecting tools (like Redis) that are genuinely useful at that layer. **The two-layer framing is the corrective**, and entries from #22 onward (Concurrency primitives through Webhooks) explicitly carry layer notes that name harness vs app applicability.

### Layer-note coverage in older entries

Entries #1–#21 (Database selection through Service discovery) predate the explicit layer-note discipline. **By default, those entries are harness-layer**, with two narrow exceptions: **Replication** (the multi-leader / git-style sync sections explicitly address servicegrid's multi-human-org case at the app layer) and **API design** (the multi-surface and multi-transport sections apply at both layers). Reading any older entry's "Architectural inspiration thread" sections, treat the inspiration as harness-layer unless the entry explicitly names the app layer. **The cleanup-pass commitment was to backfill explicit layer notes into the older entries**; given the volume of entries, the lighter weight commitment was made instead — name the default here once, and let the older entries inherit it.

### Known overlaps and intentional non-MECE structure

The doc is **not strict MECE at the top level** — entries deliberately overlap where survey vs deep-dive entries cover the same dimension at different granularities, and where related concepts have legitimate cross-references. The known overlaps:

- **CAP theorem §3 (consistency models) vs Transactions §3 (isolation levels)** — these cover related but distinct ground (consistency models are about distributed reads/writes; isolation levels are about concurrent transactions in a single database). The two entries should be read together; the orchestrator's per-resource-consistency-by-stakes commitment lives in CAP §3, while the per-transaction-isolation discipline lives in Transactions §3.
- **Resilience patterns §6 (rate limiting) vs Authentication §7 (rate limiting in API context)** — both touch rate limiting from different angles (resilience as a defensive primitive; auth as part of an API surface protection story). They reference each other.
- **Search §6 (two-stage retrieval) vs A/B testing §4 (sequential testing) vs Recommender systems §3 (candidate generation + ranking) vs Cost engineering §8 (cheap-model-plus-frontier-verifier)** — independent convergence on the same two-stage cheap-then-expensive architectural shape, named in convergent pattern #12.
- **Stream processing §4 (stateful stream processing state) vs Event streaming §8 (CQRS projections) vs Data integration §6 (dbt transformations) vs Real-time §6 (live queries)** — all four are different vocabularies for "derived projection over the event log". Recognized in the "X IS Y" convergent pattern #2.
- **Database selection** is the survey entry for the storage subdomain; **Indexes**, **Transactions**, **Replication**, **Sharding**, and **Disk I/O** are deep-dive entries for individual dimensions. Each deep-dive references back to the survey, and each survey dimension references its deep-dive entry.
- **Event streaming and event sourcing** is the resolution entry; multiple later entries (Stream processing, Message queues, Data integration, Workflow orchestration, Webhooks, ML systems, SLO, A/B testing, Cost, Notifications, Search, Backup) confirm the resolution generalizes to their subdomain. The overlap is intentional and load-bearing.

The doc's organizing principle is **MECE within each entry, with deliberate cross-entry overlap for convergent patterns**. This is documented in the intro frontmatter.

## Quick reference: the architecture's positions (orchestrator / harness layer)

> This is the **secondary layer** — what the harness has provisionally adopted from the system design walks below. Each position is a one-line statement with a pointer back to the entry or convergent pattern that produced it. Use this view when making a harness-level decision in the moment, before reading the deep-dive entries.
>
> **Scope: harness layer only.** For app-layer decisions (production web apps each org ships to users), see the "Layered architecture: harness vs apps" section above and follow the standard production web app stack for the use case. The primary system design content lives in the per-concept entries; this section is the harness-inspiration distillation. Maintain as new entries surface new positions.

### When choosing how to persist state

- **Event log as the canonical state-mutation primitive — RESOLVED.** One log per org (cosmos as natural partition); idempotent producers; at-least-once + idempotent consumers; log compaction for state-snapshot streams + infinite retention for high-stakes events; event versioning baked in from day one. *(Convergent pattern #1; 12 of 20 entries; resolved in Event streaming entry; grounded in WAL literature via the Write-ahead log entry)*
- **The event log is a WAL applied at application-event granularity** — inherits all the known properties and disciplines of write-ahead logs (durability, replay-based recovery, replication via log shipping, time travel, audit, projection rebuilding). *(Write-ahead log entry)*
- **CQRS as the persistence model** — writes go to the event log; reads come from projections (knowledge graph, skill registry, calibration store, dashboard views are all projections). The current scattered persistence layer is *already* ad-hoc CQRS. *(Event streaming §8)*
- **State = fold(events)** — application state is computable by replaying events; snapshots speed up bootstrap. *(Event streaming §7)*
- **SQLite-vs-Postgres question DISSOLVED** — event log is canonical; projections use whatever local storage fits (SQLite for relational, files for markdown, graph store for graph). *(Event streaming §8)*
- **Single-leader topology within a single-human org instance**; followers (e.g., the dashboard) are read-only. **Multi-human orgs (servicegrid) use multi-leader with each human as their own leader.** *(Replication §1)*
- **Strict typed schemas at every persistent boundary, validated by code.** *(Database Selection §6, Transactions §2)*
- **Event versioning required from day one** — every event carries schema version; consumers branch on version when reading. *(Event streaming §6)*
- **Per-org partitioning; the cosmos boundary IS the shard boundary.** *(Sharding §1, §2)*
- **Graph-of-typed-nodes as the unifying data model** — graph is the addressing layer; each node has its own internal shape. *(Database Selection §1, §2)*
- **Content-addressable keys (`skill@version` style) wherever possible** — eliminates invalidation. *(Caching §5)*
- **Append-only by default; avoid patterns that create random-write hot paths.** *(Disk I/O §1)*
- **The event-log workload is structurally LSM-shaped, not B+tree-shaped** — append-only writes, time-bucketed immutable blocks, periodic compaction. SQLite/Postgres B+trees are fine today but the impedance mismatch becomes load-bearing at scale. **The sharper framing: amplification tradeoffs, not "reads vs writes" — LSM shifts cost from write amplification onto read amplification and transient space amplification.** *(Indexes §1, sharpened by LSM-vs-B-tree fold)*
- **CQRS is what lets the event log (LSM-shaped) and the projections (B+tree-shaped) pick different index structures for the same underlying data** — without the split, you'd be forced to pick one structure for both workloads and eat the impedance mismatch; with it, each side runs on whatever substrate matches its amplification profile. This is why CQRS is architecturally load-bearing and not just an enterprise-pattern label. *(Indexes §1 B+tree-vs-LSM synthesis; Event streaming §8 CQRS thread)*
- **Open table formats (Iceberg-formatted Parquet) are the candidate substrate when the event log outgrows SQLite** — ACID + snapshots + schema evolution + time travel + multi-engine compatibility on top of object storage; more sovereign-aligned than Postgres. *(Open table formats entry)*
- **DuckDB is the most aligned external query engine** for projections over the event log — single binary, embeddable, no server, vectorized, reads Parquet/Iceberg natively. *(Open table formats §7, Query engines §7)*
- **Time-bucket partitioning of the event log is a cheap TSDB primitive worth adopting now** — organize events into hourly/daily buckets so time-range queries can prune entire buckets. Same architectural move as partial indexes, applied to the temporal dimension. *(TSDBs §2, §3)*
- **TSDB-style compression (double-delta for timestamps, dictionary for tags, Gorilla for slowly-changing scores) makes "keep everything forever" affordable as the event log grows.** *(TSDBs §4)*

### When making any commit durable (sleep test consequences)

The sleep test extends beyond persistence to economics, liveness, and capability — every external boundary needs to respect that the user is absent during the most critical operations.

**Persistence:**
- **`fsync` required for every persistent commit** — non-negotiable. *(Disk I/O §3)*
- **`fsync` the *directory* after creating a new file** — subtle but mandatory; otherwise files can disappear on crash. *(Disk I/O §3)*
- **Write-through is the default; write-back caching is dangerous for persistent state.** *(Caching §4)*
- **No async commit; commit semantics are stricter than typical web apps because the user's absence is normal.** *(Transactions §4)*
- **RPO is a deliberate architectural choice, not a default of the backup tool.** *(Replication §4)*
- **Lazy backups fail the sleep test.** *(Replication §4)*
- **Stay above the filesystem abstraction; never put state on NFS/SMB.** *(Disk I/O §4)*
- **No fire-and-forget event producers** — events must be acknowledged before the producer continues. *(Event streaming §2)*

**Economics:**
- **Token budget is a first-class rate-limited resource with explicit caps and backpressure** — runaway spending under autonomous operation is a primary failure mode. *(Resilience patterns §6)*
- **Circuit breakers on external dependencies** — especially LLM provider; retry storms against dead dependencies are exactly the sleep-test failure mode. *(Resilience patterns §4)*

**Liveness:**
- **Heartbeat-based timeouts for long-running operations** — operations emit progress events; watcher monitors freshness, not elapsed time; distinguishes "still working" from "stuck." *(Resilience patterns §3)*

**Capability:**
- **Cheaper-model fallback as a named graceful degradation path** — when primary model is unavailable or budget is constrained, the orchestrator keeps operating at reduced quality rather than stopping. *(Resilience patterns §7)*

### When designing the harness's caching strategy

- **Token reuse, not byte reuse, is the metric.** *(Caching §1)*
- **Maximize prompt cache hit rate** — stable prefixes, deterministic skill ordering, volatile content at the end of the prompt. *(Caching §1, §3)*
- **Caching topology stays radically simple** — in-process + OS page cache + LLM provider's prompt cache; no distributed coordination. *(Caching §2)*
- **Capacity-based eviction is mostly unnecessary at the current parallelism density** — working set fits in memory. **Will become load-bearing as multi-agent parallelism scales up** (many concurrent shadow clones competing for cache space). *(Caching §6, qualified by multi-agent framing)*
- **In-memory state is a *view* of disk state, never the source of truth.** *(Caching §7)*
- **OS page cache is the free automatic cache; don't duplicate it.** *(Disk I/O §2)*

### When designing for multi-agent (shadow clones)

- **Shadow clones become consumer groups on the per-org event log** — each clone tracks its own offset, never writes to shared mutable state, coordination is essentially free. *(Event streaming §3 — resolves the multi-agent transfer pattern)*
- **Distributed-systems primitives transfer to in-process multi-agent** — expect to relearn them at the in-process scale, though many dissolve under event sourcing. *(Convergent pattern #3)*
- **Single-writer per clone, never per shared resource** — each clone writes events, no shared mutable state. *(Transactions §5, Event streaming §3)*
- **Bulkheads via consumer-group isolation, with explicit per-clone resource limits** — memory, CPU, token budget caps per clone. *(Resilience patterns §5)*
- **Capability-aware health checking, not just liveness** — clones are `(alive, capacity, capabilities, recent reliability)`. *(Load balancing §5)*
- **Sticky-by-task-id routing with the event log as externalized context store** — preserves task context, allows recovery if a clone dies. *(Load balancing §6, Event streaming §3)*
- **Consistent hashing for stable clone assignment under churn.** *(Load balancing §2)*
- **CRDTs for shared low-stakes state** (calibration, traces, metrics); **manual git-merge for high-stakes state** (dream state, design principles, skills). *(Replication §7)*
- **Idempotency at every operation boundary** — every operation should answer "what happens if called twice?"; at-least-once delivery + idempotent consumers gives exactly-once semantics. *(Transactions §8, Event streaming §4, Resilience patterns §1)*
- **Saga pattern for any multi-step operation with side effects** — compensation, not rollback; retry-until-compensation is the natural stopping rule. *(Transactions §6, §7, Resilience patterns §2)*
- **Failures are first-class events, not exceptional states** — dead letters become "events with failed flag" in the canonical log. *(Resilience patterns §8)*
- **Per-operation CAP choice (CP vs AP) when an agent isn't responding** — wait for it (CP) for high-stakes operations, proceed without it (AP) for high-throughput work; the policy is per-resource, not system-wide. *(CAP theorem §2, §8)*
- **Per-resource consistency model**, not system-wide — dream state and design principles want strong consistency; calibration data is fine with eventual; trace events are causally consistent by construction. *(CAP theorem §3)*
- **PACELC awareness** — even without partitions, the latency vs consistency tradeoff is real for high-stakes vs high-throughput agent operations. *(CAP theorem §4)*

### When designing service discovery (skills, loops, tools, clones, external services)

- **DNS-inspired hierarchical namespace rooted at the org boundary** — every harness resource has a fully-qualified name (`org.type.resource@version`); cosmos boundary is the namespace root. *(Service discovery §1)*
- **The harness IS the in-process service registry for its org** — same "X is Y" framing as "the harness IS a saga framework." Recognize existing infrastructure rather than build new. *(Service discovery §2)*
- **Cross-org discovery uses recursive resolution; no central registry across orgs** — each org owns its own namespace; the navigator plays the role of the DNS root. *(Service discovery §2)*
- **Registration is event-log-based; the registry is a projection** — skills, loops, tools, clones all register by writing events; the registry is a fold over registration events. Composes with log compaction. *(Service discovery §3)*
- **Health checking and discovery are the same primitive** — a clone that hasn't emitted a heartbeat in N seconds drops from the registry projection automatically. Unifies load balancing health, resilience-pattern heartbeats, and discovery into one mechanism. *(Service discovery §4)*
- **TTL-based caching with negative caching** — cache eagerly, expire by TTL, cache absences too. A "resource removed" event makes the registry projection drop the entry. *(Service discovery §5)*
- **Multiple record types per name** — same name answers multiple kinds of question: where, what, version, event log offset, dependencies. Maps to the knowledge graph's typed-edges model. *(Service discovery §6)*
- **Discovery failures split by stakes** — high-stakes lookups fail closed; low-stakes can fail open with stale data. *(Service discovery §7)*

### When optimizing performance (latency, throughput, cost)

- **Three-way tradeoff: latency vs throughput vs cost** — the orchestrator's metered economics make cost an explicit dimension; optimization decisions navigate all three. *(Latency and throughput §8)*
- **Optimize the binding constraint, not the visible component** — Theory of Constraints; currently the LLM provider (latency per call when serial, tokens per minute when parallel); other optimizations are wasted effort. *(Latency and throughput §3, §6)*
- **Latency is dominated by LLM round-trip; reduce call count, not call speed** — local I/O and graph traversal are microseconds; the only meaningful latency lever is fewer or more parallel LLM calls. *(Latency and throughput §2)*
- **Latency composes additively across sequential agent operations** — a 20-step loop at 5s/step is 100 seconds; critical path thinking is required at multi-agent scale. *(Latency and throughput §4)*
- **Track p50/p95/p99 for latency, not averages** — LLM latency has a wide distribution; averages mislead. *(Latency and throughput §5)*
- **Batching trades latency for throughput; per-operation choice based on stakes** — high-throughput latency-tolerant operations can batch; low-latency high-stakes operations cannot. *(Latency and throughput §7)*
- **Cheaper-model fallback as one navigation of the three-way tradeoff** — already named in Resilience patterns; latency and throughput frame clarifies why it's load-bearing. *(Resilience patterns §7, Latency and throughput §8)*

### When designing skills and loops (saga discipline)

- **Loops ARE sagas; model them as such explicitly** — each skill is a step, each step needs a compensating action defined. *(Saga orchestration §1)*
- **Every skill defines its compensating action as part of its contract** — even if the answer is "non-compensatable, escalate to human." *(Saga orchestration §3)*
- **Non-compensatable operations require explicit human approval** before running in a saga that might need to roll back. *(Saga orchestration §3)*
- **Forward recovery > pivot recovery > backward recovery** — try the same skill again, then try a different approach to the same goal, then compensate as the catastrophic fallback. *(Saga orchestration §4)*
- **Compensations themselves must be idempotent and retriable** — they inherit the same constraints as primary operations. *(Saga orchestration §6)*
- **The harness IS a saga framework** — name it explicitly; saga state lives in the event log; durable state, retries, compensation hooks, monitoring, timeouts already exist. *(Saga orchestration §5, §8)*
- **Skill isolation requires central orchestration within an org** — choreography would violate skill isolation; centralized within an org + sovereign across orgs is the coordination model. *(Saga orchestration §2)*

### When designing for multi-engineer mode (servicegrid is the current case — 3 humans + customer agents; other orgs may follow)

- **Each engineer is their own single-leader** — multi-leader topology overall. *(Replication §1)*
- **Git-style async sync, never centralized real-time** — preserves sovereignty. *(Replication §2, §5)*
- **Read-your-writes is the consistency floor.** *(Replication §5)*
- **No centralized routing or coordinators** — anti-pattern for sovereignty. *(Sharding §5)*

### When working with the knowledge graph

- **Graph traversal is the dominant access pattern**, not point lookup or range scan; the standard DB taxonomy misses this. *(Indexes §2)*
- **The graph is its own query planner today** — hand-coded traversals; open question whether to abstract. *(Indexes §5)*
- **Causal consistency at minimum** — A depends on B → can't see A without B. *(Database Selection §3)*
- **Orphan nodes are retrieval failures** — every new node must connect to ≥1 existing node. *(Already in dream state, sharpened by indexes)*

### When detecting drift and degradation

- **Drift detection is a first-class architectural concern** — performance drift, content drift, calibration drift, alignment drift all want the same defense. *(Load-bearing one-off thread, multiple sources)*
- **Verification beyond a single layer** — `fsync` lying defense; backup integrity checks; event log replay verification. *(Disk I/O §5)*
- **Time travel via event replay** — answer "what did the system know at time T?" by replaying the log up to that offset; available for free under event sourcing. *(Event streaming §7)*
- **Index drift via periodic slow-query audit.** *(Indexes §4)*
- **In-memory vs disk reconciliation triggers on suspicious state.** *(Caching §7)*

### When designing observability (logs, metrics, traces, dashboards, alerts)

- **Logs ARE events** — no separate logging system distinct from the event log. *(Observability §1)*
- **Metrics are projections from the event log** — counters, gauges, histograms, summaries all derivable by aggregation; no separate metrics store. *(Observability §2)*
- **Loop executions are traces; phases/skills/gates/tool calls are spans** — adopt the standard trace/span model. *(Observability §3)*
- **Dashboards are projections; alerts are pattern-matching consumers** — both natural under CQRS. *(Observability §7)*
- **OpenTelemetry-compatible event shapes from day one** — small upfront cost, optional payoff later, no lock-in. *(Observability §8)*
- **Keep every event at the current parallelism density** — sampling and cardinality concerns are mostly N/A while clone counts are modest; complete trace history is the default. **Will become load-bearing as multi-agent parallelism scales up** (high-volume shadow clone operations may need sampling; per-clone dimensions are real labels that could explode cardinality). *(Observability §4, §5, qualified by multi-agent framing)*
- **No separate observability infrastructure** — the event log IS the observability infrastructure. *(Observability §6)*

### When making system-model assumptions explicit

- **The orchestrator's timing model is partial synchrony** — bounds exist (LLM latency p99, network timeouts) but the architecture can't depend on them. Heartbeat-based timeouts are the partially-synchronous answer. *(System models §1)*
- **The failure model is crash-recovery + timing failures + soft-Byzantine** — agents can hallucinate or produce wrong output without "failing" technically. **Verification gates are the orchestrator's BFT mechanism**, with humans/agents as the consensus rather than cryptographic proof. *(System models §2)*
- **Reliable+ordered communication within an org via the event log; no assumptions across orgs.** *(System models §3)*
- **Compromised-dependency security model** — LLM provider, MCP servers, web fetch results are all potentially malicious or buggy. Verification at the boundary IS the security model. *(System models §4)*
- **Hybrid process model**: harness + Mech-1 sub-agents (in-session, share parent lifetime) + Mech-2 separate processes (outlive parent, OS-level isolation). Each has different failure semantics. *(System models §5)*
- **Local knowledge only per clone; common knowledge is impossible** — rules out coordination patterns requiring "all agents agree at the same instant." *(System models §6)*
- **Sleep test is a liveness commitment, not just a durability one** — distinguish safety mechanisms (gates, event log immutability) from liveness mechanisms (heartbeats, circuit breakers, proactive messaging). *(System models §7)*

### When working with the knowledge graph (query layer)

- **The current "query engine" is imperative TypeScript traversal** — has known scaling limits but is fine at current corpus size. *(Query engines §8)*
- **Filter pushdown is the highest-leverage optimization rule and can be adopted as a discipline without building an optimizer** — "don't load all nodes of type X and then filter by property Y; load only nodes where Y matches the predicate." *(Query engines §5)*
- **Traversal direction should be a deliberate choice with a comment, not a default** — the equivalent of physical planning without a cost model. *(Query engines §6)*
- **The four options for the graph query future, in order of effort**: (a) keep imperative traversal forever; (b) name explicit traversal disciplines like filter pushdown [recommended next step]; (c) build a real graph query layer with logical plans + optimization; (d) wrap the graph as a DuckDB/Polars data source. *(Query engines §8)*

### When designing partitioning and cross-org work

- **The cosmos boundary IS the persistence boundary** — at every layer. *(Sharding §1, §2)*
- **Orgs are naturally isolated; cross-org operations are rare and read-only.** *(Sharding §4, §7)*
- **Cross-org analysis is a separate, deliberately simple read-only layer** — never merged with per-org persistence. *(Sharding §4)*
- **Routing across orgs is a navigator-level concern, never system-level.** *(Sharding §5)*
- **Each org has full per-shard ACID; between orgs is eventually consistent or no consistency at all.** *(Sharding §8)*
- **Hotspots are the natural shape of the workload, not a problem to fix** — pay maintenance cost on the hot subset. *(Sharding §6, Indexes §2)*

### When designing concurrency and process management

- **Hybrid concurrency model**: single-threaded event loop for the harness (Node + async/await) + Mech-1 sub-agents (in-process, share parent lifetime, execute serially within parent's event loop) + Mech-2 separate processes (separate terminals, full OS isolation) + actor-style coordination via the event log. *(Concurrency primitives §1, System models §5)*
- **The event log IS structurally a lock-free queue**; the entire locking primitive layer (mutex, semaphore, monitor, condition variable) is unused at the harness layer because there's only one writer. *(Concurrency primitives §2)*
- **Classical concurrency bugs (race conditions, deadlocks, priority inversion, ABA) dissolve structurally rather than via correctness analysis.** *(Concurrency primitives §3)*
- **Monotonic event-log sequence numbers serve five roles**: WAL primitive (WAL §3), idempotency mechanism (Stream processing §5, Message queues §3, Webhooks §3), ABA-defense (Concurrency primitives §3), fencing token (Consensus §6), Lamport timestamp (Time and clocks §3).

### When designing search, retrieval, and the harness's six latent search surfaces

- **The harness has six latent search surfaces** all served by the same primitive: skill search, memory search, inbox search, knowledge graph query, past execution search, conversation history search. *(Search §8)*
- **A unified harness search service exposing one query API** with corpus selector + query, backed by FTS5 indices that are derived projections from the event log. *(Search §8)*
- **BM25 first, dense retrieval second, hybrid (BM25 + dense fused via reciprocal rank fusion) as the eventual destination.** *(Search §3)*
- **Code-aware tokenization is the right default** at the harness layer — most queries are identifiers and paths. *(Search §1)*
- **Two-stage retrieval (BM25 first stage + LLM rerank) for any harness search that returns to the LLM** — the LLM is structurally a high-quality but expensive reranker. *(Search §6)*
- **Filter pushdown is more load-bearing than query expansion** at the harness layer because the structured metadata is the reason. *(Search §4)*
- **The harness query log as a learning loop into skill creation** — queries returning nothing are skill-creation prompts. *(Search §7)*
- **Versioned embedding namespaces and query-pinned embedding versions** — same versioning discipline as ML feature stores. *(Search §5)*

### When recommending the next loop or move (the leverage protocol as N-of-1 recommender)

- **The leverage protocol IS structurally a content-based contextual recommender** for the navigator, with the loop catalog as the item set and the value equation as the ranking function. *(Recommender systems §6)*
- **With N=1, collaborative filtering dissolves entirely**; recommendation reduces to content-based + contextual + exploration-vs-exploitation. *(Recommender systems §2)*
- **Exploration via Thompson sampling at low rates** — the harness should occasionally recommend non-top loops to gather calibration signal rather than always picking the top-value option. *(Recommender systems §4)*
- **Adopt recsys discipline of guardrail metrics, exploration sampling, feedback-loop correction, cold-start handling for newly-created loops.** *(Recommender systems §6)*
- **Treat the value equation as a hypothesis to be calibrated rather than ground truth** — never updating it from realized outcomes is the harness equivalent of "shipping a recsys with no online learning". *(Recommender systems §5)*

### When designing the harness as a workflow orchestrator (the loop runner)

- **The harness loop runner IS structurally Temporal** — workflows = loops, activities = skills, sensors = gates, durable state = event log, replay/resume = from any offset. Future loop-runner enhancements should look at Temporal's primitives as prior art. *(Workflow orchestration §5, §6)*
- **The loop's phase structure IS a DAG; loop.json IS structurally a DAG specification.** *(Workflow orchestration §2)*
- **The gate primitive IS a workflow sensor** with the navigator as the external system. *(Workflow orchestration §3)*
- **Backfill is structurally event-log replay** — the harness gets it for free because every execution can be replayed from any offset. *(Workflow orchestration §7)*
- **The dashboard + SSE stream IS the workflow observability layer.** *(Workflow orchestration §8)*
- **Any new orchestration need should extend the loop runner rather than parallel it** — the loop runner already IS the task runner / job system / scheduler. *(Workflow orchestration §6)*

### When designing the harness as a stream processor and message broker

- **The harness IS a stream processor of its own event log** — source = event log, consumers = projections, exactly-once via at-least-once + sequence-number idempotency, replay from offset zero. *(Stream processing §8)*
- **The event log IS a message broker** structurally — log-as-broker pattern, projections as subscribers tracking their own offsets. *(Message queues §8)*
- **Session windows defined by punctuation events (loop START / loop COMPLETE) are the right default** for harness aggregations — semantic windows beat arbitrary time buckets. *(Stream processing §3)*
- **Event-time vs processing-time is already resolved in favor of event time** by event sourcing — every event carries its event-time timestamp. *(Stream processing §2)*
- **Stateful stream processing state IS derived projections from the event log** — the same architecture under different names. *(Stream processing §4)*
- **Backpressure dissolves at the harness layer** because human/LLM-speed producers cannot outpace in-process consumers. *(Stream processing §7, Message queues §7)*
- **Any feature proposal that wants "a queue" at the harness layer should be challenged with "use the event log first"** — the event log already has all the properties (persistence, ordering, replay, idempotency) without operational cost of a second substrate. *(Message queues §8)*

### When designing the harness as a CDC source and integration substrate

- **The event log IS log-based CDC** at the application-event layer — and the harness avoids the outbox pattern entirely because the event log IS the source of truth, not a derived stream. *(Data integration §2, §3)*
- **The harness is structurally a hub-and-spoke message bus** with the event log as the hub. *(Data integration §5)*
- **The harness's projection layer transfers dbt-style transformation discipline** (named SQL transformations, version-controlled, tested) but the substrate is the harness's projection runtime, not a separate dbt process. *(Data integration §6)*
- **The learning loop IS reverse ETL** — calibration insights computed from the event log flow back into operational decisions about how the harness behaves next. *(Data integration §7)*
- **Schema validation via Zod with backward-compatibility checks in CI for any event type with more than one consumer.** *(Data integration §4)*

### When designing notifications and proactive messaging

- **Slack as the harness's primary mobile notification channel** — the only channel that satisfies real-time + mobile-reachable + interactive + threadable. *(Notification systems §1)*
- **Notification priority levels (critical / attention / fyi) with per-level rate-limit budgets and quiet-hours respect** — extends the sleep test to attention discipline. *(Notification systems §4)*
- **Notifications persisted durably before delivery; per-notification status tracked as events in the event log.** *(Notification systems §5)*
- **Per-notification engagement tracking that feeds back into priority calibration** — same calibration discipline as the learning loop. *(Notification systems §7)*
- **Scheduled-with-cancel notification primitive as a saga** — schedule the notification, observe the trigger event, cancel the scheduled notification as the compensating action. *(Notification systems §8)*

### When designing the navigator-experience SLOs

- **The harness IS a navigator-experience SLO system** — adopt internal SLOs against navigator-experience SLIs even though there's no SLA, because measurement matters more than the contract. *(SLI/SLO §1, §7)*
- **The seven navigator-experience SLOs**: gate prompt arrival time, execution latency vs estimate, skill error rate, sub-agent completion success, loop completion rate, notification delivery success during waking hours, calibration coverage. *(SLI/SLO §7)*
- **Track all SLOs as derived projections from the event log, surfaced in a dedicated terminal view.** *(SLI/SLO §7)*
- **Multi-window multi-burn-rate alerting** from the Google SRE workbook for any monitorable SLO. *(SLI/SLO §5)*
- **Error budgets convert SLOs from targets into a budget for risk-taking** — the most important contribution of SRE methodology. *(SLI/SLO §4)*
- **The harness's calibration system IS the SLI tracker; the upgrade-proposal pipeline IS error-budget-based** — if a new skill version is regressing the calibrated quality SLO, don't promote. *(SLI/SLO §7)*

### When designing experimentation discipline (calibration as N-of-1 experimentation)

- **The harness IS an N-of-1 experimentation platform** — calibration system as substrate, executions as the unit, Bayesian sequential as the statistical method. *(A/B testing §8)*
- **Pre-registered primary metric + guardrail metrics; the upgrade-proposal pipeline must surface all three** before approval — currently only quality is surfaced; latency, token cost, and error rate must be added as guardrails. *(A/B testing §3)*
- **Deterministic per-execution variant assignment** via `hash(execution_id + experiment_id)` — same primitive as user-ID bucketing applied to executions. *(A/B testing §1)*
- **Exposure events as the cohort boundary, not assignment events** — every execution that actually runs a variant skill emits a `skill_variant_exposed` event. *(A/B testing §2)*
- **A/A test as a smoke test on the analysis pipeline; ramp-up as the experimentation equivalent of canary deploys.** *(A/B testing §6)*

### When measuring and controlling token cost (the dominant cost dimension)

- **The orchestrator is structurally a high-third-party-API-cost system; LLM tokens dominate over compute and storage** — cost engineering discipline is token accounting, not EC2-hour counting. *(Cost engineering §1)*
- **Token budget caps enforced at the model-call layer (not just dashboarded), with hierarchical cap structure** (per-skill / per-loop / per-org / per-month). *(Cost engineering §4)*
- **Three load-bearing token-cost defenses**: prompt caching for repeated long context, model-tier selection (cheap models for tasks that don't need frontier, escalate via calibration), batch API for non-interactive work (50% discount). *(Cost engineering §3)*
- **Per-loop and per-skill unit economics; quality-per-token as a calibration metric**; the upgrade-proposal pipeline ranks skill versions by quality-per-token, not just quality. *(Cost engineering §2)*
- **Token cost as an event field on every model call** — gives per-skill, per-loop, per-org cost attribution as derived projections, no separate accounting system. *(Cost engineering §5)*
- **Cheap-model-plus-frontier-verifier as the most generative cost-quality optimization** — use a cheap model to generate, use a frontier model to verify, accept the cheap output when the verifier passes. *(Cost engineering §8)*
- **The local-first commitment is economically aligned, not just architecturally** — local hardware has a flat cost while cloud compute scales with usage. *(Cost engineering §6)*

### When configuring the harness (config / secrets / flags)

- **Seven-category configuration taxonomy** (build-time / env / secret / runtime / flag / user-pref / tenant); each category gets its own storage and discipline. *(Configuration management §1)*
- **Harness config in version-controlled files** with environment variable overrides for ephemeral experiments and a managed secrets store for sensitive values. *(Configuration management §2)*
- **`sops` + `age` for sovereign-compatible encrypted secret storage** — secrets file lives in version control (encrypted) while the decryption key stays on the navigator's machine. *(Configuration management §3, Auth §6)*
- **Every config file has a Zod schema; loader fails fast on validation; schema is the single source of truth.** *(Configuration management §4)*
- **Hot-reload is opt-in for a deliberate subset of config** (token budget caps, model selection, slack prefs, logging verbosity); atomic swap with validation as the safety mechanism. *(Configuration management §5)*

### When designing for time correctness

- **Every duration measurement uses a monotonic clock; every timestamp for display/storage uses a wall clock; never confuse them.** *(Time and clocks §1)*
- **Past timestamps stored as UTC ISO 8601; future scheduled events stored as `(local_time, named_zone)` pairs**, never UTC because of potential DST changes between now and the event. *(Time and clocks §4)*
- **The harness's event log uses UTC timestamps**; future-scheduled work (notifications during waking hours) is stored as `(local_time, navigator's zone)` pairs.
- **Never assume wall clock is monotonic** — DST transitions break code that does. *(Time and clocks §5)*

### When designing for the soft real-time tier

- **The orchestrator never builds hard real-time systems; everything is at most soft real-time, and most harness features are near-real-time.** *(Real-time §1)*
- **Six near-real-time surfaces with explicit soft-real-time tiers**: gate prompt arrival (≤ 5s), execution progress updates (≤ 1s), skill completion feedback (already < 100ms), calibration recompute (background), sub-agent status (≤ 1s), question/response cycles (≤ 5s). *(Real-time §8)*
- **SSE as the default for one-way push** at the harness layer — already used for the dashboard's live execution updates. *(Real-time §4)*
- **Adaptive jitter buffers / debounced progress updates** so the navigator perceives steady progress rather than bursts of activity. *(Real-time §5)*
- **The harness's projection layer IS a live-query substrate** — projections continuously updated as new events arrive. *(Real-time §6)*

### When integrating with external systems via webhooks

- **Webhook receivers in the harness are thin shims**: verify HMAC signature with timestamp-based replay defense, persist to the event log with source event ID as dedup key, return 2xx, let downstream consumers do the business logic. *(Webhooks §6)*
- **HMAC signature verification non-negotiable** — every webhook receiver verifies before processing. *(Webhooks §2)*
- **Idempotency via per-source event ID dedup in a persistent store** — the event log is the natural dedup store. *(Webhooks §3)*
- **Cloudflare Tunnel as the sovereign-aligned local-development tunneling tool**; Hookdeck or Svix for capture-and-replay. *(Webhooks §5)*

### When designing the architecture's response to LLM API latency

- **Verify the LLM SDK reuses HTTP/2 connections per provider rather than opening fresh per call** — high-leverage optimization because LLM API latency is largely TLS handshake + connection establishment. *(Networking §4)*
- **HTTP/2 (or HTTP/3) for outbound API calls with one warm connection per origin.** *(Networking §2)*
- **Connection pooling discipline**: one shared HTTP client across all workers with a sensibly-sized pool, not per-worker pools. *(Networking §4)*

### Recognized architectural dissolutions (local-first eliminates entire categories)

The harness avoids the following entire categories of distributed-systems concern by structural choice — naming them explicitly so future feature proposals can be challenged with "can this live at the app layer instead?":

- Cross-machine concurrency (Concurrency primitives §1)
- Distributed system models / partial synchrony bets (System models §1)
- Service discovery across services (Service discovery §2)
- Load balancing (Networking §7, Load balancing entry)
- Geographic distribution (Geographic distribution §8)
- Consensus, leader election, distributed locks, group membership, split-brain prevention, CAP trade-off (Consensus §8)
- Geospatial concerns (Geospatial §6)
- Service mesh (Service mesh §6)
- Cross-machine clock concerns (Time and clocks §6)
- Backpressure (Stream processing §7, Message queues §7)
- Multi-tenancy isolation (Multi-tenancy §8)
- Presence and collaborative awareness (Real-time §7)
- Conflict resolution / CRDTs / sync engines (Mobile §6)
- Mobile-specific concerns (background sync, push triggers, network awareness, battery awareness) (Mobile §7)
- The outbox pattern (Data integration §3)
- Fan-out at the notification layer (Notification systems §3)

---

## Cross-entry inspiration threads (orchestrator / harness layer)

> This is the **secondary layer** — harness-specific architectural inspiration that emerged from the system design walks below. It synthesizes recurring patterns and load-bearing observations across entries. The primary system design content lives in the per-concept entries; this section captures what the harness took from those walks.
>
> **Scope: harness layer only.** App-layer architecture follows the standard production web app stack and is not the focus here — see the "Layered architecture: harness vs apps" section for the layer distinction. Maintain this section as new entries are added. Treat it as a working notebook for harness-level architectural commitments, not as the doc's primary purpose.

### Convergent patterns

Threads that appear in multiple entries, surfacing as recurring architectural questions or commitments. Strength is roughly the number of entries each pattern appears in; the strongest are at or above the bar where they would normally earn promotion to a Design Principle in the dream state.

1. **Event log as the universal load-bearing primitive — RESOLVED into a concrete commitment, grounded in WAL literature, generalizing across nearly the entire doc.** *(35+ of 50 entries reference the event log as the substrate that resolves their concern.)* Append-only event log surfaces as the answer to atomicity (each event is a single atomic append), isolation (snapshot reads, no write conflicts), durability (sequential WAL-friendly writes), concurrency control (lock-free by construction), replication (ship the log), recovery (replay the events), and the curation bottleneck (a canonical scannable substrate for trace triage). **The Event streaming and event sourcing entry resolved this thread by making it concrete**: one log per org (cosmos boundary as natural partition), idempotent producers, at-least-once + idempotent consumers for exactly-once semantics, log compaction for state-snapshot streams + infinite retention for high-stakes events, event versioning baked in from day one, CQRS naturally pairing with event sourcing to unify the existing scattered persistence layer. The Observability entry confirmed that the resolution generalizes (logs, metrics, traces, dashboards, alerts as projections/consumers). The Write-ahead log entry grounded it in 30+ years of database literature. The Open Table Formats and TSDBs entries described a coherent future substrate (Iceberg-formatted Parquet with TSDB-style time bucketing, queried by DuckDB). **The new entries reinforced this further**: Stream processing recognizes that the harness IS a stream processor of its own log; Message queues recognizes that the event log IS a log-as-broker; Data integration recognizes that the event log IS log-based CDC and dissolves the outbox pattern; Workflow orchestration recognizes that the event log is the durable state for a workflow runner; Webhooks treats received events as appends to the log; Backup and DR observes that application-consistent backup is trivial under event sourcing; Notifications and Search treat the event log as their persistence layer; SLOs treat navigator-experience metrics as derived projections. The architecture is sitting on a single dominant primitive that unifies persistence, audit, replication, recovery, observability, search indexing, message brokering, change data capture, workflow durable state, notification persistence, cost attribution, calibration tracking, and webhook ingestion.

2. **The "X IS structurally Y" recognition pattern.** *(11 explicit recognitions across 11 entries.)* The orchestrator's event-sourced, single-process, local-first architecture has been quietly absorbing the responsibilities of multiple distinct distributed-systems components, and naming the equivalences makes the borrowing of patterns from each subdomain legitimate. **Rather than building new infrastructure, the architectural value is recognition**: the harness IS each of these things already, and naming the equivalence unlocks decades of well-resolved patterns from each subdomain.
    - **The harness IS a saga framework** *(Saga orchestration §8)* — durable state, retries, compensation hooks, monitoring, timeout management already exist
    - **The harness IS the in-process service registry** for its org *(Service discovery §2)*
    - **The event log IS a WAL applied at application-event granularity** *(Write-ahead log entry)* — inheriting all of WAL's known properties
    - **The orchestrator IS an ML system** *(ML systems infrastructure §8)* — model lifecycle, feature stores, calibration, online/offline parity all in primitive form
    - **The event log IS the audit log** *(Data governance §5, Event streaming §7)* — compliance and audit trails for free
    - **Verification gates ARE the orchestrator's BFT mechanism** *(System models §2, Auth §8, Consensus §4)* — soft-Byzantine framing for agent output
    - **The harness IS a stream processor** of its own event log *(Stream processing §8)*
    - **The harness IS an N-of-1 experimentation platform** *(A/B testing §8)* — calibration system as the SLI tracker, upgrade pipeline as the experimentation lifecycle
    - **The harness IS a navigator-experience SLO system** *(SLI/SLO §7)*
    - **The event log IS a single-writer Raft log without the consensus part** *(Consensus §3)*
    - **The event log IS a message broker** *(Message queues §8)* — log-as-broker with projections as consumers
    - **The event log IS log-based CDC** at the application-event layer *(Data integration §2)* — and the harness avoids the outbox pattern entirely because the log is the source of truth
    - **The harness's projection layer IS a dbt-style transformation runtime** and **the learning loop IS a reverse-ETL flow** *(Data integration §6, §7)*
    - **The harness's projection layer IS a live-query substrate** *(Real-time §6)*
    - **The harness's loop runner IS structurally a workflow orchestration tool** equivalent to Temporal *(Batch processing / workflow orchestration §6)* — loops as workflows, skills as activities, gates as sensors
    - **The loop's phase structure IS a DAG; loop.json IS a DAG specification; the dashboard IS a workflow observability UI** *(Workflow orchestration §2, §8)*
    - **The harness IS the foundational local-first case** *(Mobile / offline-first sync §8)* — the offline-first apparatus exists to give other applications properties the harness has by default
    - **The harness's leverage protocol IS a content-based contextual recommender** for the navigator *(Recommender systems §6)*
    - **The loop runner IS structurally the control plane for the data plane of execution** *(Service mesh §3)*

   The pattern is itself the meta-observation: the orchestrator's event-sourced + local-first architecture naturally absorbs roles that distributed systems split across many separate components, and the architectural value is recognizing this rather than re-implementing.

3. **Local-first eliminates a class of distributed-systems concerns entirely.** *(15+ entries name specific dissolutions.)* The harness's commitment to "single local process" (no cluster, no network for state, no peers, no users beyond the navigator) doesn't just simplify those concerns — it eliminates them entirely as categories. The architectural value of each dissolution is that a category of bugs and operational complexity becomes structurally impossible rather than carefully avoided.
    - **Cross-machine concurrency** dissolved *(Concurrency primitives §1)*
    - **Distributed system models** dissolved (no partial synchrony bets across machines) *(System models §1)*
    - **Service discovery across services** dissolved (single-process service registry) *(Service discovery §2)*
    - **Load balancing** dissolved (no redundant peers) *(Networking §7, Load balancing entry)*
    - **Geographic distribution** dissolved (one navigator, one machine, one jurisdiction) *(Geographic distribution §8)*
    - **Consensus and distributed coordination** dissolved (consensus, leader election, distributed locks, group membership, split-brain prevention, CAP trade-off) *(Consensus and distributed coordination §8)*
    - **Geospatial concerns** dissolved (no concept of physical location) *(Geospatial systems §6)*
    - **Service mesh** dissolved (no services to mesh) *(Service mesh §6)*
    - **Cross-machine clock concerns** dissolved (no skew, no logical clocks needed, no HLC) *(Time and clocks §6)*
    - **Backpressure** dissolved at the harness layer (single-producer at human/LLM speed) *(Stream processing §7, Message queues §7)*
    - **Multi-tenancy isolation** dissolved (single-actor trust boundary) *(Multi-tenancy §8)*
    - **Presence and collaborative awareness** dissolved (one navigator) *(Real-time §7)*
    - **Conflict resolution / CRDTs / sync engines** dissolved (single writer) *(Mobile / offline-first §6)*
    - **Mobile-specific concerns** dissolved (background sync, push triggers, network awareness, battery awareness) *(Mobile §7)*
    - **The outbox pattern** dissolved (event log IS the source of truth, no dual-write race) *(Data integration §3)*
    - **Fan-out** dissolved at the notification layer (recipient set is always one) *(Notification systems §3)*

   This is the load-bearing simplification of the architecture: the harness avoids the entire distributed-systems apparatus by structural choice rather than careful implementation, and every dissolution reinforces the others. **Architectural commitment**: any future feature proposal at the harness layer that would require any of these dissolved concerns should be challenged with "can this live at the app layer instead?" — because adopting the apparatus at the harness means giving up the dissolution.

4. **The monotonic event-log sequence number serves multiple roles simultaneously.** *(5 distinct architectural roles for one primitive.)* The single primitive of "monotonically increasing sequence number on every event in the log" serves as: **WAL primitive** *(WAL §3)* — the log offset is the consistency boundary for recovery. **Idempotency mechanism** *(Stream processing §5, Message queues §3, Webhooks §3)* — consumers track the highest processed offset, retries are no-ops. **ABA-defense** *(Concurrency primitives §3)* — the sequence number prevents the classic ABA bug in lock-free patterns. **Fencing token** *(Consensus and distributed coordination §6)* — the same fencing-token discipline that prevents distributed-lock correctness bugs. **Lamport timestamp** for single-process *(Time and clocks §3)* — adapts the logical-clock primitive to the single-writer case. **One primitive, five roles**, all derived from the single-writer event log. This convergence is itself architecturally significant — when one primitive correctly serves five distinct concerns from different distributed-systems subdomains, the underlying architectural choice is doing more work than it appears to.

5. **Cross-machine concerns dissolve under local-first; in-process concerns shift from user-concurrency to agent-concurrency.** *(15 of 50 entries.)* This pattern was originally framed as "local-first eliminates entire dimensions of complexity," which conflated two distinct things: (1) running on one machine (no cluster, no network for state), and (2) single-user serial workload (no concurrency). The orchestrator commits to the first; it does NOT commit to the second. **The actual scale model is "many agents on one machine," not "single user single thread."** **Truly dissolved**: distributed topology, geographic distribution, quorum math, cross-region replication, network partitions across nodes. **Apparently dissolved but actually load-bearing as the agent layer matures**: eviction (many agents → cache competition), scale model (shifts from user count to agent count, not eliminated), working set / memory pressure, async I/O complexity, sequential-layout optimization, sampling, cardinality, throughput as a metric. **Many "doesn't apply at single-user scale" notes in earlier entries are overstated** and should be re-read as "doesn't apply yet but will when many agents are running."

6. **Distributed systems primitives transfer to in-process multi-agent — partially resolved via event sourcing, partially still load-bearing.** *(15+ of 50 entries.)* Event sourcing dissolves several distributed-systems primitives by construction (consensus, leader election, distributed locking, gossip), but CAP-style tradeoffs survive at the consumer-coordination level (CAP, throughput composition, failure isolation). The general lesson: **event sourcing shrinks the in-process equivalent of distributed systems concerns but doesn't eliminate all of them.** Concepts irrelevant at the network level become exactly the right vocabulary at the in-process multi-agent level once shadow clones become parallel and stateful. **The Event streaming and event sourcing entry resolved this**: shadow clones become consumer groups on the per-org event log, each tracking its own offset, never writing to shared mutable state.

7. **Sleep test as a hard architectural constraint with downstream forbidden patterns — extending beyond persistence to economics, liveness, capability, and attention.** *(11+ of 50 entries.)* Every persistent commitment AND every external resource interaction must respect that the user is absent during the most critical operations. The discipline forbids: async commit, write-back caching, lazy backups, lax RPO defaults, missing `fsync` calls, fire-and-forget event producers, retry storms against dead dependencies, runaway token spending, hangs without progress signals, alerts without enforcement of token caps, unacknowledged critical notifications during waking hours. **The Resilience patterns entry sharpened the sleep test by extending it from persistence to economics (token budget caps), liveness (heartbeat-based timeouts), and capability (cheaper-model fallback)**. **The System Models entry reframed the sleep test as primarily a liveness commitment** — distinguish safety mechanisms from liveness mechanisms and monitor each separately. **The Cost engineering entry sharpened the economics extension** — token budget caps must be enforced at the model-call layer, not just dashboarded; hierarchical cap structure (per-skill / per-loop / per-org / per-month). **The Notification systems entry extended the sleep test to attention discipline** — quiet hours, priority levels with rate-limit budgets, durable persistence so a 3am-queued notification reaches the navigator at 7am.

8. **Managed providers by default at the app layer; sovereign-aligned tools by default at the harness layer.** *(15+ entries.)* The two-layer architecture commitment cashes out into a consistent pattern: app-layer features delegate to managed providers wherever a battle-tested option exists, while harness-layer features prefer sovereign-aligned, embeddable, single-binary, MIT-licensed alternatives. **App-layer managed defaults**: Clerk/Auth0 for auth, Stripe for payment, SendGrid/Twilio for notifications, Cloudflare R2 for object storage, managed Postgres (Neon/Supabase) for database, GrowthBook/LaunchDarkly for feature flags, Vercel/Railway for deployment, Cloudflare for CDN/edge/DNS, managed secrets stores (Doppler/AWS), Airbyte+dbt for data integration, Linkerd if a mesh is needed, ElectricSQL for sync, OR-Tools/Vroom for routing. **Harness sovereign-aligned defaults**: SQLite for the event log, DuckDB for analytical projections, `sops`+`age` for encrypted secrets, vLLM for self-hosted serving (deferred), Cloudflare Tunnel for local dev tunneling, OpenTofu for IaC, Redpanda for Kafka-API replacement, MinIO for S3-API replacement, Nominatim for sovereign geocoding. **The architectural commitment**: don't apply harness sovereignty discipline to the app layer (it makes the apps slow and unscalable); don't apply app-layer "managed services are fine" thinking to the harness (it compromises the navigator's sovereignty over their own cognition). The same tool is anti-pattern at one layer and the right answer at the other (Redis is the canonical example).

9. **Existing dream-state principles sometimes turn system design concepts into "nothing to design."** *(5+ of 50 entries.)* When an existing dream-state principle (orgs as worlds, content addressability via skill versioning, the harness as differentiator) naturally provides the structure a system design concept assumes, the concept applies cleanly without transfer or workaround. The most generative meta-pattern because it inverts the usual question — instead of "how do we apply this concept to our architecture," it becomes "which of our existing commitments make this concept trivially solved?"

10. **DuckDB convergence — the same external tool answers multiple independent architectural needs.** *(2 of 50 entries: open table formats §7 and query engines §7 independently arrived at "DuckDB is the most aligned external tool for the orchestrator.")* Open Table Formats reached it via "we want a sovereign analytical engine for projections that reads Iceberg natively"; Query Engines reached it via "we want a mature query layer with optimization without building one." Same answer, different paths. **The convergence is itself a signal**: when two independent architectural questions arrive at the same external dependency, that dependency belongs in the architecture's vocabulary even if it's not adopted today. **DuckDB is the strongest candidate for "the one external tool the orchestrator might depend on"** because it's a single binary, embeddable, no server, MIT-licensed, vectorized, sovereign-aligned.

11. **The crossover-decision pattern — entries that surface deferred decisions that will become load-bearing at scale.** *(4+ of 50 entries: open table formats, query engines, time series databases, service discovery.)* Each of these entries names a substrate or technique the architecture is currently deferring. At today's scale, the deferral is correct. **At each crossover point, the deferred technique becomes load-bearing**, and the architecture has to decide: adopt the substrate, fold its primitives into a custom solution, or live with the impedance mismatch. **These entries together describe a coherent future architecture**: Iceberg-formatted Parquet event log with TSDB-style time bucketing, queried by DuckDB with filter pushdown, all local-first and sovereign. Walking these now matters because the option exists in the vocabulary so the crossover decisions can be made deliberately rather than reactively.

12. **Two-stage retrieval (cheap recall + expensive rerank) as a recurring architectural pattern.** *(3 entries with the same shape.)* The same cheap-then-expensive structure shows up independently in: **Search §6** (BM25 first stage + LLM rerank), **A/B testing §4** (sequential testing as cheap monitoring + expensive verification), **Recommender systems §3** (ANN candidate generation + cross-encoder ranking). **The connection to the harness's LLM economics**: the LLM is structurally a high-quality but expensive reranker, and any harness operation that touches the model can use the two-stage pattern to keep cost down while preserving quality. The cheap-model-plus-frontier-verifier pattern named in Cost engineering §8 is the same pattern at the model-tier level. This is one more case of independent convergence on the same architectural shape.

13. **Everything-is-a-node graph unification generalizes across scales — apps as typed nodes, org as app graph.** *(Extends the dream-state principle up one scale; one external worked example in servicegrid.)* The **everything-is-a-node graph unification** principle in DREAM-STATE-ooda.md was originally stated at the knowledge-graph level (typed nodes, typed edges, one query language, `ready/blocked/depends-on` semantics for work items and gates and artifacts alike). The same primitive generalizes **up a scale** to org-level app graphs: each app is a node, API consumption is the edge, and a small typed taxonomy covers the common cases. ServiceGrid's `servicegrid/.claude/REFERENCE-app-graph.md` names a reusable three-type taxonomy that applies to any org-level app graph:
    - **Capability node** — headless service that exposes APIs but has no user-facing surface (*sg-studio*: content-slicing APIs, consumed by other apps, no UI of its own)
    - **Product node** — hybrid: exposes APIs *and* has a primary user-facing surface (*servicegrid-app*: tenant UI + data/content APIs; *sg-phone-web*: voice surface + data outputs)
    - **Projection terminal** — read-only observer that produces no consumable API; renders human-visible state only (*dashboard*: CQRS read model / materialized view over the org's event stream)

   The triad has a useful direction-of-flow property: **capabilities → products → terminals**, with cross-consumption between capability nodes allowed. This is a degenerate case of graph flow with soft edge-type constraints rather than arbitrary connectivity. The same pattern has an equivalent runtime framing in **service mesh** vocabulary (identical topology; complementary operational toolkit — traffic routing, policy, observability, mTLS). At small scale (N ≈ 4 apps) the graph framing dominates; as the org scales past ~10 services the mesh vocabulary's operational toolkit becomes progressively more useful, which is itself a scale-driven crossover (compare pattern #11). **The architectural value of naming this explicitly**: when a new org is being structured, the taxonomy is a starter set for classifying each new app ("is this capability, product, or projection?") — and the answer constrains what the app should and shouldn't do: a capability node shouldn't grow a UI; a capability node shouldn't **store consumer output** (its API returns content, not storage side effects — consumers own their data); a projection terminal shouldn't start producing APIs. **The stateless-capability-node discipline** is the one that preserves wedge-C ("capability-as-a-service" / Stripe-for-X) posture by default — if a capability node starts persisting consumer output, it becomes a product node, and the wedge-C refactor stops being free. Servicegrid's sg-studio is the worked example: artifact storage lives in consumer apps (servicegrid-app's `studio_generations` table), not in sg-studio. See `servicegrid/.claude/REFERENCE-roadmap.md` "subsidiary commitment" for the full argument. **The meta-observation**: "everything-is-a-node" is not confined to knowledge-graph primitives — it's a fractal commitment that repeats at module / service / app / org scales, each scale borrowing the same query-and-dependency primitive. Worked examples of the pattern at other scales are worth harvesting as they appear.

### Structural classification of entries

A meta-pattern about how system design concepts relate to the orchestrator architecture. Seven structural classifications have emerged across the 50 entries:

1. **Choice-layer concepts** — the architecture actively picks among options. The entry's value is generating new architectural choices and patterns. *Examples: database selection, caching, indexes, transactions, open table formats, query engines, TSDBs, concurrency primitives, authentication and authorization, data governance, payment and billing, file storage, multi-tenancy, networking and protocols, configuration management, CI/CD, cost engineering, geospatial systems, real-time systems.*
2. **Doesn't-apply-but-transfers concepts** — the network-level / cross-machine concept is N/A under local-first, but the underlying primitives transfer to in-process multi-agent (shadow clones), to the app layer, or to a structurally-equivalent harness pattern. The entry's value is preparing the architecture for higher-parallelism multi-agent operation, or for app-layer scaling, or recognizing the dissolution itself. *Examples: load balancing, replication (multi-human-org transfer — live in servicegrid), service discovery (DNS-inspired hierarchy applied to in-process resource lookup), networking and protocols at depth, geographic distribution / edge / CDN, consensus and distributed coordination, service mesh, time and clocks.*
3. **Already-resolved-by-cosmos concepts** — an existing dream-state principle (orgs as worlds, content addressability via skill versioning) naturally provides the structure the concept assumes, so the concept applies cleanly without transfer or workaround. The entry's value is validating existing commitments and surfacing what they've already paid off. *Examples: sharding (entire entry), with echoes in transactions §2 and indexes §3.*
4. **Foundation-layer / inherited-constraint concepts** — mostly below the architecture's design space; the orchestrator inherits OS or hardware semantics rather than choosing them. The entry's value is identifying load-bearing constraints and defensive disciplines, not generating new architectural patterns. *Examples: disk I/O, system models (axioms layer rather than constraint layer, but same shape — naming what's inherited).*
5. **Convergent-pattern resolution concepts** — entries whose primary value is resolving a pattern that's been surfacing across multiple previous entries. After many entries point at the same underlying architectural primitive, one entry walks that primitive directly and makes it concrete enough to commit to. These are rare and high-leverage. *Examples: event streaming and event sourcing (resolves the event log convergent pattern).*
6. **Convergent-pattern resolution confirmation concepts** — entries whose primary value is showing that an existing convergent-pattern resolution generalizes to a new subdomain. After a resolution entry, subsequent entries on adjacent subdomains may find that ~80% of their dimensions collapse into "covered by the resolution." These confirm that the resolution is dominant rather than narrow. *Examples: observability (confirms event sourcing handles logs, metrics, traces, dashboards, alerts as projections/consumers); write-ahead log (grounds the event-log resolution in 30+ years of database literature); search and information retrieval (search index as derived projection from the event log); stream processing (the harness IS a stream processor); message queues and brokers (the event log IS a message broker); data integration / ETL / CDC (the event log IS log-based CDC and dissolves the outbox pattern); batch processing and workflow orchestration (the harness loop runner IS a workflow orchestration tool equivalent to Temporal); SLI/SLO (the harness IS a navigator-experience SLO system); A/B testing (the harness IS an N-of-1 experimentation platform); recommender systems (the leverage protocol IS a content-based contextual recommender); mobile and offline-first sync (the harness IS the foundational local-first case); ML systems infrastructure (the orchestrator IS an ML system); webhooks (the event log absorbs webhook reception).*
7. **Crossover-decision concepts** — entries that walk a deferred decision the architecture has been making implicitly (or by inheriting a default). At today's scale, the deferral is correct; the entry's value is naming the option space so the crossover decision can be made deliberately when the time comes. *Examples: open table formats (substrate for the event log when SQLite outgrows it), query engines (when imperative graph traversal stops being enough), time series databases (when general-purpose substrate stops fitting the time-series workload), service discovery (when ad-hoc per-resource discovery mechanisms stop being unifiable in your head), backup and disaster recovery (the upgrade path from "navigator's local backup discipline" to formal DR for paying customers), notification systems (the upgrade path from Slack-only to multi-channel managed providers), file storage and large object handling (when local-first artifacts need cloud sync for collaboration).*

When walking a new system design concept, asking "which structural classification does this fit?" early can help calibrate what kind of value the walk should be expected to produce — and whether the walk is generative or mostly confirmatory. **By the cleanup pass (after entry 29), classification #6 (convergent-pattern resolution confirmation) became the dominant classification** — most of the new entries from Tier A onward turned out to be cases where the harness already implements the concept structurally and the walk's job is to recognize the equivalence rather than to introduce new infrastructure. This is itself a meta-observation: the doc has converged on a small number of dominant primitives (event log, single-process locality, calibration loop) that absorb most distributed-systems concerns.

### Load-bearing one-off threads
