One log per org (matching the cosmos boundary as the natural partition), append-only, idempotent producers, at-least-once delivery + idempotent consumers, log compaction for state snapshots + infinite retention for high-stakes events, event versioning baked in from day one. **This is the resolution of the convergent pattern that's been surfacing across 6 of the previous 8 entries.** The dream state should commit to it.
2. **CQRS unifies the orchestrator's existing scattered persistence layer** (from §8). The current architecture is *already* an ad-hoc CQRS system — Postgres tables, JSON files, knowledge graph, log files are all projections of state that originates somewhere. Making the event log explicit unifies them rather than adding a new layer. **This dissolves the SQLite-vs-Postgres question** that's been hanging since the Database Selection entry: event log is canonical, projections use whatever storage fits.
3. **Shadow clone coordination becomes essentially free with event sourcing** (from §3). Multiple clones consume from the same org's log as a consumer group; each tracks its own offset; nobody writes to shared mutable state. **This is the structural resolution of the multi-agent coordination question** that's been hanging since the load balancing entry.
4. **Time travel and audit are free byproducts of event sourcing** (from §7). "What did the system know at time T?" becomes a trivial replay query. Every state change is recorded automatically. The orchestrator already wants both of these — they've shown up as inspiration threads in transactions, replication, and the drift detection thread.
5. **Log compaction is the right pattern for "current state" projections** (from §5). Skill registry, knowledge graph, calibration data are all naturally log-compacted streams: keep latest per key, drop earlier versions. This avoids unbounded log growth without losing the events that matter for high-stakes audit.
6. **Schema versioning needs to be baked in from day one** (from §6). Events live forever; retrofitting schema evolution after the log has accumulated content is much harder than committing to versioning up front. Every event type gets a Zod schema, every schema gets a version, consumers branch on version. Composes cleanly with the typed-schemas-at-every-persistent-boundary thread.

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This entry is structurally different from all the previous ones because it's the deep dive on the convergent pattern they all surfaced.** Most entries either generated new threads or confirmed existing ones; this entry **resolves** the dominant convergent pattern by making it concrete enough to commit to. After 8 entries pointing at "the event log primitive," this entry says "here's exactly what the event log primitive is, and here's how to build it." **This is a fifth structural classification for system design entries**: alongside choice-layer, doesn't-apply-but-transfers, already-resolved-by-cosmos, and foundation-layer-inherited, there's "**convergent-pattern resolution**" — entries whose primary value is resolving a pattern that's been surfacing across multiple previous entries. These are rare and high-leverage; you should expect at most one or two per major pattern.

**This entry is also the strongest candidate so far for triggering an actual dream state update**, because it doesn't just propose a Design Principle — it makes the event log primitive concrete enough to commit to in code. The next architectural move after this entry should probably be: (a) promote the event log to a Design Principle in the dream state, (b) add it to the harness's substrate decisions, (c) start migrating scattered logs/traces/decisions toward the canonical event-log substrate.

---

## Observability

Eight dimensions you have to make a decision on when designing or working with observability. For each one, what the options are and how it applies to the orchestrator's current architecture and dream state.

This entry is structurally interesting because most of its dimensions either confirm the event-log resolution from the previous entry or fall away as N/A at the current parallelism density (with the qualifier that some — sampling, cardinality — will become real as multi-clone parallelism scales up). The unique value is in identifying which observability concepts have specific orchestrator-relevant shapes (loop-executions-as-traces, OpenTelemetry alignment) and which collapse into "covered by event sourcing."

### 1. Logs — discrete event records

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Structured logs | JSON or key-value records, machine-parseable | Modern logging | Already done implicitly via execution logs |
| Log levels | DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR, FATAL | Standard severity tiers | Already in use |
| Contextual logging | Request/trace ID propagated through call chain | Distributed systems debugging | **The right pattern, currently ad-hoc** |
| Centralized vs local | One log stream vs per-process | Scale/aggregation tradeoff | Local for sovereignty, centralized for queryability |
| **Logs as events** | Every log entry IS an event in the event log | Event sourcing | **The right framing for the orchestrator** |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Logs and the event log are the same thing under the right framing.** Currently the orchestrator has separate "execution logs" (with phase/skill/gate categories) and the implicit state mutations that happen as a loop runs. Under the event log model from the Event streaming entry, these collapse: every log entry is an event in the canonical event log; every state mutation is also an event. **Logs aren't a separate concern from persistence; they're the same primitive.** This is one more place where event sourcing dissolves a category that traditional architectures keep separate. The architecture should not have a "logging system" distinct from the "state persistence system" — both are uses of the same event log.

### 2. Metrics — numerical measurements over time

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Counters | Monotonically increasing, count of events | Request counts, error counts | Skill executions, gate pass/fail counts |
| Gauges | Point-in-time values | CPU, memory, queue depth | Calibration scores, leverage scores |
| Histograms | Distribution of values across buckets | Latency distributions | Loop duration distributions |
| Summaries | Percentiles (p50, p95, p99) | Latency SLOs | Skill latency over time |
| Time-series databases | Specialized storage (Prometheus, InfluxDB) | Production monitoring | **N/A — metrics are projections from the event log** |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Metrics in the orchestrator are derivable projections from the event log, not a separate store.** Skill execution counts, calibration over time, gate pass rates, leverage scores — every metric the orchestrator currently maintains can be computed by aggregating events. **This means there's no need for a separate metrics store**: metrics are just one kind of projection in the CQRS framing from the previous entry. The Prometheus/InfluxDB layer that production systems use exists because their event logs aren't structured well enough to query directly for metrics; the orchestrator's event log can be designed to support this from day one.

### 3. Traces — spans showing flow across components

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Span** | A unit of work with start, end, attributes, parent reference | OpenTelemetry standard | **Each phase, skill, gate, tool call IS a span** |
| **Trace** | A tree/DAG of spans connected by parent-child relationships | Standard | **A loop execution IS a trace** |
| Context propagation | Trace ID + span ID propagated across boundaries | Distributed tracing | Trivially in-process today; distributes naturally to shadow clones |
| Sampling | Decide which traces to retain | Production scale | Mostly N/A — keep everything at this scale |
| Vendor backends | Jaeger, Honeycomb, Datadog, Tempo | Various | Optional integration, not load-bearing |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Loop executions are naturally traces and should be treated that way.** Each loop execution has a trace ID. Each phase, skill, gate, tool call is a span within that trace. Spans have parent-child relationships matching the call hierarchy. **The architecture should adopt the standard trace/span model for execution logging because it's the natural shape of the data and gives interoperability with standard tooling for free.** If shadow clones become real, the trace model extends naturally — each clone's spans share the same trace ID as the originating loop, with context propagation across clone boundaries.

### 4. Cardinality and dimensional explosion

| Concern | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| High-cardinality labels | Per-user IDs, request IDs blowing up storage | Production observability nightmare | **Bounded today — no per-user explosion. Per-clone labels will grow as multi-agent parallelism scales** |
| Tagging strategy | Which fields are labels vs attributes | Tradeoff between query power and cost | Bounded enough today; revisit as the per-clone dimension grows |
| Pre-aggregation | Reduce cardinality by aggregating before storing | Production tradeoff | Not needed at the current density |
| The "label explosion" problem | Combinatorial blow-up | Prometheus, Datadog | Possible at very high clone density; not load-bearing today |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Cardinality concerns are mostly N/A at the current parallelism density.** The orchestrator's natural label dimensions (org, skill, loop, clone, gate) are all bounded today and don't explode the way per-user labels do in production systems. **As multi-agent parallelism scales up, the per-clone dimension is the one most likely to grow** — many concurrent shadow clones each emitting their own labeled events. Cardinality is a candidate for re-evaluation when that happens, even though it's not architecturally generative right now.

### 5. Sampling strategies

| Strategy | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Head-based sampling | Decide at trace start whether to keep | Cheap, biases toward starting decisions | N/A — keep everything |
| Tail-based sampling | Decide after trace completes based on outcome | Better signal/noise, expensive | N/A — keep everything |
| Probabilistic | Keep N% randomly | Coarse but cheap | N/A |
| Adaptive | Sample more during anomalies | Smart but complex | N/A |
| Always-on for errors | Sample 100% of error traces | Standard practice | Implicit — every error trace stays |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Sampling is mostly not needed at the current parallelism density.** Keep every event. The volume is low enough and disk is large enough. **This eliminates one of the harder operational concerns of production observability today** — you don't have to make hard tradeoffs between completeness and cost. The discipline this enables: **assume every trace is available for any future debugging, analysis, or curation pass**, which is a powerful default for the data flywheel and learning loop. Connects to the curation thread from the scaling laws doc — the curation step assumes a complete trace history to select from. **Sampling becomes a real question as multi-clone parallelism scales the trace volume up**, but the default should remain "complete history" until the volume forces a hard tradeoff.

### 6. Storage and retention

| Concern | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time-based retention | Drop after N days/weeks | Production default | Possible for low-value telemetry |
| Hot/cold tiering | Recent in fast store, archive in cheap store | Long-term analytics | Recent in event log, archive compressed |
| Downsampling | Aggregate old data, drop raw | Long-term metrics | Possibly for histograms over time |
| Compression | Reduce storage cost | Standard | Free win for cold archives |
| **Already covered by event-log retention** | Same retention rules apply | Event sourcing | **No separate observability retention story needed** |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The retention story for observability data is the same as the retention story from the Event streaming entry.** No need for a separate observability storage layer. The event log handles retention via compaction + archival, and observability data is just events with specific types. **Yet another place where the unified event log dissolves a category** that traditional architectures keep separate. The orchestrator does not need a separate observability infrastructure — the event log is the observability infrastructure.

### 7. Querying, dashboards, and alerting

| Concern | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ad-hoc query languages | PromQL, LogQL, SQL | Production debugging | SQL over the event log + projections |
| Dashboards | Grafana, Datadog, custom | Real-time visualization | The orchestrator already has one |
| Alerts | Threshold-based, anomaly detection | Operational responsiveness | The proactive messaging system |
| Runbooks | Linked to alerts | On-call support | Emerges from skill library |
| **Dashboards as projections** | Read views computed from event log | Event sourcing | **The right framing** |
| **Alerts as event-log consumers** | Pattern-matching on event sequences | Event sourcing | **The right framing** |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Dashboards are projections; alerts are pattern-matching consumers; both are natural in the event sourcing model.** The orchestrator's dashboard is structurally a projection that can be rebuilt from the event log. The proactive messaging system is structurally an alert consumer. **Naming these as "projections" and "consumers" rather than separate subsystems would unify the architecture's observability story** with its persistence story. Same principle as the meta-pattern from the Event streaming entry: making the event log explicit unifies scattered subsystems.

### 8. OpenTelemetry as standardization layer

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| OpenTelemetry (OTel) | Vendor-neutral instrumentation standard | The modern default | Sovereignty-friendly choice |
| OTel data model | Standardized span, metric, log shapes | Interoperability | Useful schema even without a backend |
| OTel exporters | Send to any backend (Jaeger, Honeycomb, Datadog, Tempo) | Multi-vendor flexibility | Not needed today, useful later |
| Auto-instrumentation | Library-level OTel hooks | Reduce manual work | Minor benefit at this scale |
| Context propagation standard | W3C Trace Context | Distributed tracing across services | Trivial in-process; useful for shadow clones |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **OpenTelemetry alignment is a sovereignty-friendly choice for instrumentation format.** OTel is open-source, vendor-neutral, and gives interoperability with any standard backend if the orchestrator ever needs to integrate with one. The cost is some upfront instrumentation discipline; the benefit is not being locked into a custom event schema that only the orchestrator understands. **The architecture should consider emitting events in an OTel-compatible shape from day one**, even if it never connects to an OTel backend, because the schema is well-thought-out and gives compatibility for free. This is a "free standardization" move — small upfront cost, optional payoff later.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Six things from this walk that look like real architectural questions:

1. **Logs and the event log are the same thing** (from §1). The architecture should not have a separate "logging system" distinct from the event log. Logs are just events with specific types. Dissolves another category that traditional systems keep separate.
2. **Metrics are projections from the event log** (from §2). No separate metrics store needed; counters, gauges, histograms, summaries are all derivable by aggregating events. Confirms the CQRS framing from the Event streaming entry.
3. **Loop executions are naturally traces; phases/skills/gates/tool calls are spans** (from §3). The architecture should adopt the standard trace/span model because it's the natural shape of the data and gives interoperability with standard tooling for free. Composes with §8 for OTel alignment.
4. **Dashboards are projections; alerts are pattern-matching consumers** (from §7). The orchestrator's dashboard is structurally a projection from the event log; the proactive messaging system is structurally an alert consumer. Naming these as such unifies observability with persistence under CQRS.
5. **OpenTelemetry alignment as sovereignty-friendly standardization** (from §8). Emit OTel-compatible event shapes from day one even without connecting to a backend. Small upfront cost, optional payoff later, no lock-in.
6. **Sampling/cardinality concerns are mostly N/A at current parallelism density** (from §4, §5). Keep every event; bounded label space today. The discipline this enables is "assume every trace is available for future analysis" — a powerful default for the curation/learning loop. Both will become real as multi-clone parallelism scales up.

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This entry is mostly a confirmation that the event log + CQRS resolution from the previous entry generalizes to observability.** Almost every dimension either resolves to "see Event streaming entry" or "N/A at single-user scale." The genuinely new material is concentrated in three places: **loop-executions-as-traces** (natural shape, interoperability via OTel), **OpenTelemetry alignment** (sovereignty-friendly standardization), and **dashboards/alerts as projections+consumers** (unifying observability with persistence).

**This suggests a sixth structural classification for system design entries**: **convergent-pattern resolution confirmation** — entries whose primary value is showing that an existing convergent-pattern resolution generalizes to a new subdomain. Observability is the first example. The previous entry resolved the event log thread; this entry confirms the resolution by showing that observability becomes ~80% free under event sourcing. **Future entries may also fall into this category** — anything that's "could be its own subsystem in a traditional architecture but becomes a projection or consumer under event sourcing" will have this shape.

This is a less generative entry than the choice-layer entries earlier in the doc, but it's still load-bearing because it confirms the resolution generalizes. If the next 2-3 walks (resilience patterns, sagas, etc.) also fall into the resolution-confirmation pattern, that's even stronger evidence that event sourcing is the architecturally dominant move.

---

## Resilience patterns

Eight dimensions of resilience patterns. For each one, what the options are and how it applies to the orchestrator's current architecture and dream state.

This entry is interesting because it's a mix of **resolution-confirmation** (several patterns already covered or dissolved by the event sourcing resolution) **and** **genuinely new architectural material** (patterns that deal with the orchestrator's *external* surface — LLM provider, time budget, token budget — which event sourcing can't dissolve). The split is roughly 50/50, and the new material is concentrated where the orchestrator interacts with things it doesn't control.

### 1. Idempotency — operations safe to retry

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Idempotent operations | Same operation, called multiple times, has same effect | The foundation of all reliable retries | Already a load-bearing discipline (Transactions §8) |
| Idempotency keys | Stable token identifies a logical operation | Stripe-style API design | Event keys in the event log serve this role |
| Idempotent consumers | Consumer code de-duplicates by key | Required for at-least-once + exactly-once semantics | Already required by Event streaming §4 |
| Natural idempotency | Operations that are mathematically idempotent (set state, don't increment) | Preferred when possible | The right default — set state from events, don't accumulate |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Idempotency is already established as a load-bearing discipline** by the event sourcing entry; this entry confirms it and sharpens the discipline. Every operation in the orchestrator — tool calls, skill executions, gate transitions, external API calls — should be designed to be safely retried. The event log model enforces this naturally for state mutations (deterministic event keys mean retries become no-ops). **The remaining work is at the *operation* boundary**: every tool call, every skill execution, every external action needs to answer "what happens if this is called twice?" The architecture should make this a checklist item for new operations, similar to how the sleep test is a checklist for new persistence work. **"Idempotency check" deserves the same level of constraint discipline as "sleep test check."**

### 2. Retries with backoff — when and how to retry

| Strategy | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Immediate retry | Try again with no delay | Rarely useful | Almost never the right answer |
| Linear backoff | Wait N, 2N, 3N seconds | Simple but suboptimal | Suboptimal vs exponential |
| **Exponential backoff** | Wait N, 2N, 4N, 8N seconds | Standard for transient failures | The right default for external API retries |
| **Jittered exponential** | Exponential + random jitter to avoid thundering herd | Production best practice | Required when multiple clones might retry simultaneously |
| Maximum retry budget | Give up after N attempts | Bounded failure surface | The standard cap |
| **Retry-until-compensation** | Retry until saga compensation budget exhausted, then compensate | Saga pattern | **The right model for orchestrator loops** |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Jittered exponential backoff is the right default for retries**, but the more interesting question is *when to stop retrying* and surface the failure. The orchestrator's natural answer: **retry until the saga's compensation budget is exhausted, then trigger compensation** rather than retry until a fixed N. This couples retry with the saga pattern in a way that's natural for long-running multi-step operations. **The retry budget becomes a saga concern, not a per-call concern**: how much can the loop afford to spend retrying before it should give up and compensate? This is a more meaningful question than "how many retries before fail," and connects retry policy to the architecture's broader resource budget thinking.

### 3. Timeouts — bounded waiting

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Network timeouts | Bound how long a connection waits | TCP, HTTP timeouts | Inherited from HTTP libraries |
| Operation timeouts | Bound how long an operation runs | Standard service design | Skills have no explicit timeouts today |
| Total request budget | Bound the entire request, not just one operation | Cascading systems | Loop-level budget |
| Cascading timeouts | Parent timeout > sum of children | Defensive programming | Implicit but unenforced |
| **Heartbeat-based timeouts** | Watcher checks for progress events, not elapsed time | Long-running operations | **The right model for agents — distinguishes "still working" from "stuck"** |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Timeouts are harder for agents than for traditional services because "still working" looks like "stuck."** A skill can take many minutes legitimately if it's reasoning hard; the same skill taking many minutes can also mean it's hung. **The defensive move is heartbeats**: long-running operations periodically emit progress events to the event log, and the timeout watcher checks for heartbeat freshness rather than total elapsed time. **This converts the timeout question from "how long is too long?" into "how long without progress is too long?"** which is much easier to answer. The event log naturally supports this — heartbeats are just events with a `progress` type. **This is a small but real new architectural move**: every long-running operation should emit periodic heartbeat events, and a watcher should monitor heartbeat freshness as the timeout primitive.

### 4. Circuit breakers — stop sending to failing dependencies

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Closed (normal) | Requests flow through | Default state | Default state |
| Open (failing) | Requests fail fast without trying | After N consecutive failures | Stop pounding a dead dependency |
| Half-open (testing) | Try one request, if it succeeds, close | Recovery probe | Standard pattern |
| Per-dependency breakers | Separate breaker per external service | Production resilience | **One per external dependency: LLM provider, each MCP server, web fetch** |
| **LLM provider circuit breaker** | Stop hitting the API when it's down or rate-limited | Specific to LLM-driven systems | **Load-bearing for sleep test compliance** |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Circuit breakers are load-bearing for the orchestrator's external dependencies, especially the LLM provider.** When the LLM API is down or rate-limited, retrying every loop step against it creates a thundering herd problem and burns budget without making progress. **The architecture should have explicit circuit breakers for each external dependency** — LLM provider, MCP servers, web fetch. When a circuit opens, the orchestrator should fail fast and surface the issue to the human via proactive messaging, rather than retrying silently. **Connects directly to the sleep test**: a system that retries silently against a dead dependency while the human sleeps can rack up massive failure costs (and budget burn) before anyone notices. **Circuit breakers are part of the sleep-test discipline, not a separate concern.**

### 5. Bulkheads — resource isolation

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thread pool isolation | Separate pool per dependency | Production services | Limited within a single Node process; OS-level process isolation kicks in for Mechanism-2 shadow clones |
| Connection pool isolation | Separate pools per backend | Production services | Mostly handled by libraries |
| Process isolation | Separate process per concern | OS-level bulkhead | The shadow clone story |
| Resource quotas | Memory, CPU, token caps per request | Multi-tenant systems | **Per-clone budgets** |
| **Shadow clones as natural bulkheads** | Each clone is its own resource boundary | Multi-agent systems | **The right resolution** |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Bulkheads are already load-bearing because shadow clones already run in parallel** (Mechanism 2 spawns separate terminal processes which are OS-level bulkheads; Mechanism 1 spawns in-session sub-agents which are softer in-process bulkheads). Each shadow clone is a natural bulkhead — a failing clone should fail on its own without affecting other clones. **The architecture should preserve this property as parallelism density grows**: never share mutable state between clones (already covered by the event sourcing resolution), enforce per-clone resource limits (memory, CPU, token budget), allow clones to be killed independently. Most of this falls out of "shadow clones are consumer groups on the event log" if implemented correctly — each consumer can fail without affecting the log or the other consumers. **Bulkheads are essentially free under the event sourcing resolution**, *as long as the per-clone resource limits are explicit*.

### 6. Backpressure and rate limiting — flow control

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Producer-side rate limiting | Cap requests/second | API throttling | The orchestrator caps its own LLM call rate |
| Consumer-side backpressure | Signal upstream to slow down | Reactive Streams | Loop pauses when budget runs low |
| Token buckets / leaky buckets | Algorithmic rate limiting | Standard primitives | Standard library implementations |
| **LLM token budget as rate-limited resource** | Token budget per minute/hour/day | Specific to LLM systems | **The most important rate-limited resource for the orchestrator** |
| Reactive backpressure | Slow down when downstream signals overload | Streaming systems | Loop steps respond to clone signals |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Rate limiting against the LLM provider is the most important resilience pattern for the orchestrator's economics.** The token economy thread from caching already established that tokens are the marginal cost; rate limiting is how you avoid runaway spending. **The architecture should treat token budget as a first-class rate-limited resource** with explicit backpressure: when the budget is approaching exhaustion, slow down or pause loops, surface the situation to the human, and resume when the budget refreshes. **This is also a defense against the sleep test** — runaway spending while the human sleeps is exactly the kind of failure mode the architecture should prevent. The architecture should never let an unattended loop burn through a daily budget in minutes because of a retry storm or runaway agent reasoning. Token budget caps are not just an economic concern; they're a sleep-test compliance concern.

### 7. Graceful degradation — reduced functionality under failure

| Mode | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fail open vs fail closed | Whether to allow or deny on uncertainty | Security vs availability tradeoff | Mostly fail closed (safer) |
| Reduced functionality | Disable non-essential features | Production systems | Skip non-essential skills under time pressure |
| Static fallbacks | Use cached values or defaults | Caching tier | Use cached calibration when fresh data unavailable |
| **Cheaper model fallback** | Use Haiku when Opus is overloaded or budget is tight | LLM-specific | **The orchestrator-specific fallback pattern** |
| **Compressed context fallback** | Summarize history when context is constrained | LLM-specific | **The orchestrator-specific fallback pattern** |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Graceful degradation for the orchestrator means having explicit fallback paths for budget, time, and capability constraints.** When token budget is tight: fall back to a cheaper model. When time is tight: skip non-essential skills. When context is constrained: use compressed memory instead of full context. **The architecture should name these fallback modes explicitly** rather than letting them emerge as ad-hoc emergency responses. The dream state can specify which features are essential and which are degradable; the harness implements the fallback paths. **The most important fallback to name explicitly is the cheaper-model fallback** — when the primary model is unavailable or budget is constrained, the orchestrator should be able to keep operating (possibly with reduced quality) rather than stopping entirely. This requires that loops be able to function with multiple model tiers, which is itself an architectural commitment.

### 8. Compensation and recovery — sagas, dead letter handling, replay

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Saga compensation | Each step has a compensating action; failure triggers compensation | Distributed transactions | Already established (Transactions §6, §7) |
| Dead letter queues | Park failed messages for inspection | Message queue systems | **Reframed as "failed events" stored in the event log** |
| Replay from failure point | Restart processing from a known good state | Event sourcing | **Free under event sourcing — replay from any offset** |
| Manual intervention queues | Human-required cases get parked | Production support | The proactive messaging system |
| **Failures as first-class events** | Failed operations become events with `failed` flag | Event sourcing | **The right framing — no separate failure store** |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Compensation and recovery are mostly already covered by event sourcing + sagas + proactive messaging.** Sagas handle compensation (Transactions §6, §7). Event sourcing makes replay trivial (Event streaming §7). The proactive messaging system handles human escalation. **The one new framing worth naming**: dead letter queues become "events that failed to apply" stored as their own event type in the canonical event log. They're not a separate queue; they're just events with a `failed` flag and a `reason` payload. **The architecture should think of failures as first-class events**, not exceptional states that live in a separate system. This composes cleanly with the rest of the event sourcing model: failures are queryable, replayable, auditable, and visible to the same projections that handle successful events.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions:

1. **Idempotency check should be a first-class discipline at every operation boundary** (from §1). Same level of constraint as the sleep test. Every new operation should answer "what happens if called twice?" before being shipped.
2. **Retry-until-compensation, not retry-until-N** (from §2). Couple retry policy with the saga pattern; the retry budget becomes a saga concern, not a per-call concern. Gives a meaningful stopping condition.
3. **Heartbeat-based timeouts for long-running operations** (from §3). Convert "how long is too long?" into "how long without progress is too long?". Operations emit progress events; a watcher monitors freshness. Distinguishes "still working" from "stuck" — the hardest agent failure mode.
4. **Circuit breakers for external dependencies are part of the sleep-test discipline** (from §4). Especially the LLM provider. A retry storm against a dead dependency while the human sleeps is exactly the failure mode the sleep test exists to prevent. Circuit breakers belong in the same architectural commitment list as `fsync` and write-through caching.
5. **Bulkheads are essentially free under event sourcing** (from §5). Shadow clones as consumer groups on the event log are natural resource bulkheads, *as long as per-clone resource limits are explicit* (memory, CPU, token budget).
6. **Token budget is a first-class rate-limited resource and a sleep-test concern** (from §6). The most important rate-limiting decision the orchestrator makes. Runaway spending under autonomous operation is a primary failure mode; explicit budget caps with backpressure are the defense.
7. **Explicit graceful degradation paths for budget, time, and context** (from §7). Cheaper-model fallback is the most important. The architecture should name these fallback modes explicitly rather than letting them emerge as ad-hoc emergency responses. Requires loops to be able to function with multiple model tiers.
8. **Failures are first-class events, not exceptional states** (from §8). Dead letter queues become "events with failed flag" in the canonical log. Composes cleanly with event sourcing — failures are queryable, replayable, auditable.

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This entry is roughly 50/50 split between resolution-confirmation and genuinely new architectural material**, which is a different shape from observability (which was ~80% confirmation). The resolution-confirmation parts (idempotency, sagas, dead letters, replay, bulkheads via consumer groups) all dissolve under event sourcing as expected. **The new architectural material is concentrated where the orchestrator interacts with the *external* world** — circuit breakers for the LLM provider, token budget as rate-limited resource, heartbeat-based timeouts for long-running operations, cheaper-model fallback for graceful degradation.

**This suggests a refinement of the convergent-pattern resolution observation**: event sourcing dissolves *internal* concerns (state, persistence, observability, multi-agent coordination) but the orchestrator still has *external* concerns (dependencies, budgets, time, model capacity) that need their own resilience patterns. The external surface is where event sourcing stops helping and where pattern-specific architectural moves become necessary. **This is a useful framing for future entries**: when walking a new system design concept, ask "is this about the orchestrator's internal state, or its external surface?" Internal concerns are mostly already resolved; external concerns still generate new architectural commitments.

**The other observation**: several of the new threads from this entry (circuit breakers, token budget, heartbeats, cheaper-model fallback) are all variations on a single theme: **the sleep test discipline extended to external resources**. The sleep test was originally about persistence (don't lose data while the human sleeps); these new threads extend it to economics (don't burn budget while the human sleeps), liveness (don't hang while the human sleeps), and capability (don't fail to make progress while the human sleeps). **This might be worth eventually consolidating as "the sleep test as an architectural discipline that applies to every external boundary, not just persistence."**

---

## Saga orchestration

Eight dimensions of saga orchestration. For each one, what the options are and how it applies to the orchestrator.

This entry is heavily **resolution-confirmation** as predicted. Sagas have been a one-off thread since Transactions, touched in Replication, and called out in Resilience patterns. Most of this walk confirms what's already established. The genuinely new material is concentrated in two places: **compensation as part of the skill contract** (a discipline that needs explicit naming) and **the harness as a saga framework** (an existing reality the architecture should name explicitly).

### 1. Saga structure — what a saga is

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Saga** | A sequence of local transactions, each with a compensating action | Long-running multi-step workflows with side effects | **A loop execution IS a saga** |
| Step | A single local transaction within the saga | Discrete unit of work | **Each phase/skill is a step** |
| Compensating action | The "undo" for a step, defined alongside the step | Required for backward recovery | **Each skill needs one defined as part of its contract** |
| Linear vs branching | Sequential steps vs parallel/conditional | Workflow shape | Linear is the common case; branching emerges in complex loops |
| Saga participants | The services or modules the saga touches | Distributed transactions | The skills, tools, and external dependencies a loop touches |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Loops ARE sagas**, and the architecture should model them as such explicitly. Each phase/skill in a loop is a saga step. Each step has side effects in the real world (files written, APIs called, state mutated). When a later step fails, the architecture needs to know how to compensate for the earlier steps. **The current loop model treats this implicitly** — recovery is ad-hoc, compensation is whatever the gate or skill author happens to think of in the moment. **Naming loops as sagas would force the discipline to be explicit**: every skill must define its compensating action as part of its contract, and the loop machinery must be able to invoke compensation in reverse order on failure. This is more discipline than the orchestrator currently has, and it's load-bearing for autonomous reliability.

### 2. Choreography vs orchestration — who coordinates

| Pattern | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Orchestration** | Central coordinator tells each participant what to do | Easier to reason about, single point of decision | **The orchestrator's natural model — it's literally in the name** |
| Choreography | Each participant knows what to do next; no central coordinator | More decentralized, harder to debug | Anti-pattern within an org — would violate skill isolation |
| Hybrid | Orchestration at the top level, choreography within sub-flows | Sometimes used for very complex flows | Possibly relevant for very long compound loops |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The orchestrator is centrally coordinated within an org by design**, and this is the right pattern. Choreography would require each skill to know about other skills, which violates skill isolation (skills should be independent units that the harness composes). The harness coordinates; the skills don't talk to each other. **This is consistent with the broader architectural pattern**: centralized within an org (single coordinator, single source of truth via the event log), decentralized across orgs (no cross-org coordinator, no shared state). Within-org orchestration + across-org sovereignty is the architecture's core coordination model, and saga orchestration is just the within-org realization of it.

### 3. Compensation patterns

| Pattern | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Semantic compensation** | Undo the *business effect*, not the technical operation | Real-world transactions | **The right model for the orchestrator** |
| Technical compensation | Rollback database rows | RDBMS transactions | Too narrow for agent work — most effects are external |
| Compensating events | Issue a counter-event that reverses the effect | Event sourcing | Composes with the event log model |
| Idempotent compensation | Compensation can be retried safely | Required for reliable recovery | Required at every level |
| **Non-compensatable operations** | Some side effects can't be undone (sent emails, deleted files past recovery window) | Hard problem | **Need to be flagged at design time, require explicit human approval** |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Compensation is semantic, not technical**, and each skill needs to define its own compensating action as part of its contract. A skill that creates a file knows how to delete it; a skill that sends a message knows how to send a correction; a skill that calls an external API knows whether the call is reversible. **The architecture should make compensation a first-class concern in skill design**: no skill should ship without an answer to "how do I undo this?" — even if the answer is "this is non-compensatable, escalate to human." The non-compensatable case is important: some operations genuinely cannot be undone (sent emails, deleted files past recovery window, money transferred). **These should be flagged at design time, surfaced to the human at execution time, and require explicit approval before running**. The architecture should never silently execute a non-compensatable operation in a saga that might need to roll back.

### 4. Failure modes and rollback strategies

| Strategy | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Backward recovery (compensation)** | Undo what was done by triggering compensating actions in reverse | Saga default | The fallback for catastrophic cases |
| **Forward recovery** | Keep going past the failure; accept partial completion | When backward recovery is too expensive | **More natural for agent work** |
| **Pivot recovery** | Change strategy mid-saga based on what failed | Adaptive workflows | **The most interesting pattern for agent loops** |
| Mixed strategies | Combine backward, forward, and pivot per step | Complex sagas | The realistic answer for the orchestrator |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Forward and pivot recovery are more natural for agent loops than backward recovery**, because agent operations often have side effects that can't be cleanly undone. When a skill fails, the natural responses in order of preference are: (1) retry the same skill (forward), (2) try a different approach to the same goal (pivot), (3) compensate the earlier steps and abandon the loop (backward). **The architecture should privilege forward and pivot strategies**; backward is the fallback for catastrophic cases. This connects to the existing dream state thread "compound reliability is front-loaded" — the front-loaded design is what makes forward recovery viable, because the loop already knows enough to attempt alternative approaches without rolling back. **Pivot recovery is the most interesting pattern for the orchestrator** because it maps directly to "the navigator's choice when a sub-problem turns out harder than expected" — try a different angle, not abandon the goal.

### 5. Saga state management — where saga state lives

| Approach | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dedicated saga state store | Separate database for saga state | Saga frameworks (Temporal, etc.) | Anti-pattern under event sourcing |
| Application database | Saga state in the same DB as business state | Application-managed sagas | Possible but adds another store |
| **Event log** | Saga state IS the fold of events | Event sourcing | **The right answer under the event log resolution** |
| In-memory only | Saga state lives in process memory | Lossy, doesn't survive crashes | Anti-pattern under sleep test |
| Workflow engine state | External engine manages state (Temporal, AWS Step Functions) | Externalized workflow | Anti-pattern for sovereignty |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Saga state lives in the event log, not in a separate store**, under the event sourcing resolution. Each step completion (or failure, or retry, or compensation) is an event; the saga's current state at any point is the fold of events with the saga's correlation ID. This means **no separate saga state store is needed** — one more place where event sourcing dissolves a concern that traditional architectures keep separate. Recovery from crash is trivial: replay events from the saga's start, find the last completed step, resume from there. **This is one of the strongest "free byproduct" cases of the event sourcing model.**

### 6. Retry and timeout within sagas

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Per-step retries | Each step has its own retry policy | Standard | Already established |
| Saga-level timeout | The whole saga has a budget | Bounded execution | The loop has a wall-clock budget |
| **Retry-until-compensation** | Retry until compensation budget exhausted, then compensate | Saga-aware retry | **Already established (Resilience patterns §2)** |
| Heartbeat-based saga timeouts | Watcher checks for progress events | Long-running sagas | **Already established (Resilience patterns §3)** |
| Compensation retries | Compensation can fail too; retry it | Recursive resilience | Required, often forgotten |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Already covered.** Retry-until-compensation is the saga-level retry policy. Heartbeat-based timeouts apply to saga steps. The one thing worth adding: **compensation itself needs retry logic** — if a compensating action fails, the architecture has to retry it (since the alternative is leaving the system in a partial state). This is one of the easy-to-forget cases. The discipline: **every compensation must be idempotent and retriable**, because compensating actions are themselves operations and inherit the same idempotency-and-retry constraints as primary operations.

### 7. Concurrent sagas — when multiple sagas operate at once

| Concern | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resource conflicts | Two sagas try to modify the same thing | Distributed systems concern | Mostly avoided by single-writer-per-resource + event log; becomes a real concern as multi-clone sagas grow common |
| Locking | Pessimistic locking on shared resources | Heavyweight, can deadlock | Anti-pattern |
| Optimistic concurrency | Detect conflicts at commit time | Lighter-weight | Natural with event log |
| Saga boundaries align with consistency boundaries | Sagas don't span consistency boundaries | Distributed sagas | The org boundary is the natural saga boundary |
| **Coordination via event log** | Concurrent sagas serialize through the log | Event sourcing | **The right model for shadow clones running multiple sagas** |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Concurrent sagas within an org are coordinated through the event log.** Each saga is a sequence of events with a correlation ID; conflicts surface when two sagas try to write events with the same key. Idempotent producers + the event log's natural ordering handle most conflicts automatically. The unresolvable conflicts (two sagas with genuinely incompatible goals operating on the same state) surface as compensation triggers — the second saga sees that the state is no longer what it expected and pivots or compensates. **This is the third or fourth time the event log has resolved a concern that would normally need its own coordination mechanism.** Concurrent saga coordination dissolves into "consumer groups on the event log with idempotent producers" — same answer as multi-agent coordination from the Event streaming entry.

### 8. Saga frameworks vs hand-rolled — build or buy

| Approach | Examples | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| External framework | Temporal, Cadence, AWS Step Functions, Camunda, Netflix Conductor | Production workflows | **Anti-pattern for sovereignty** |
| Hand-rolled in application | Custom saga code in the app | When framework cost is too high | The current orchestrator pattern |
| **Built-in to the harness** | The harness IS the saga framework | When the application is the orchestrator | **The right framing — the orchestrator's loop machinery is structurally a saga framework** |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The harness IS a saga framework**, and the architecture should name this explicitly. The orchestrator already has all the components a saga framework provides: **durable state** (the event log), **retries** (via resilience patterns), **compensation hooks** (via gates and skill rollback contracts), **monitoring** (via execution traces and the dashboard), **timeout management** (via heartbeats), **concurrent saga coordination** (via consumer groups on the event log). The only thing missing is the explicit naming. **The dream state should treat loops-as-sagas and the-harness-as-saga-framework as architectural commitments**, and skill contracts should explicitly include compensation actions as a first-class field. This isn't building new infrastructure; it's recognizing that the existing infrastructure has a name in the saga literature, and that the discipline implied by that name is worth adopting.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Five things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or disciplines:

1. **Loops ARE sagas; the architecture should model them as such explicitly** (from §1). Each skill is a step; each step needs a compensating action defined as part of its contract; failure triggers compensation of earlier steps. This forces the discipline that's currently implicit to be explicit.
2. **Compensation as a first-class concern in skill design** (from §3). No skill should ship without an answer to "how do I undo this?" — even if the answer is "this is non-compensatable, escalate to human." Non-compensatable operations need to be flagged at design time and require explicit human approval before execution. The architecture should never silently execute a non-compensatable operation in a saga that might need to roll back.
3. **Forward and pivot recovery as the default; backward recovery as the catastrophic-case fallback** (from §4). Privilege "retry the same skill" or "try a different approach to the same goal" over "compensate and abandon." Pivot recovery maps directly to "the navigator's choice when a sub-problem turns out harder than expected." This sharpens the existing "compound reliability is front-loaded" thread by adding a clear preference order for failure responses.
4. **Compensations themselves must be idempotent and retriable** (from §6). Easy-to-forget case: if a compensating action fails, the architecture has to retry it. Compensations inherit the same idempotency-and-retry constraints as primary operations.
5. **The harness IS a saga framework, and the architecture should name this explicitly** (from §8). Durable state, retries, compensation hooks, monitoring, timeout management, concurrent saga coordination — all already exist in the orchestrator. The architectural move is naming this and adopting the discipline implied: skill contracts explicitly include compensation actions as a first-class field.

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This entry is a clean "convergent-pattern resolution confirmation" — most of its dimensions either dissolve under event sourcing or were already covered by previous entries.** Saga state lives in the event log (no separate store). Retry-until-compensation already established. Heartbeat-based timeouts already established. Concurrent saga coordination dissolves into consumer-groups-on-the-event-log (same resolution as multi-agent coordination). Sagas are natural for the orchestrator because loops have always been long-running multi-step operations with side effects.

**The genuinely new material is concentrated in three places**: (1) **compensation must be a first-class field in skill contracts** — every skill defines its compensating action or flags itself non-compensatable, (2) **forward/pivot recovery should be privileged over backward recovery** — adds a preference order to the existing reliability thread, (3) **the harness should be named as a saga framework** — recognizing existing infrastructure rather than building new.

**This is the second resolution-confirmation entry in a row** (after observability), and it has roughly the same shape: ~70-80% confirmation, ~20-30% new material concentrated in specific disciplines. **If this pattern continues across the remaining gap entries (consensus/coordination, API design, auth), the doc is mostly converged** — new entries will sharpen rather than expand. The convergence is becoming stable.

---

## API design

Eight dimensions of API design. For each one, the option space and the contexts where each option could fit.

This entry presents the API design option space rather than claiming specific architectural commitments. Most of its dimensions offer multiple valid choices depending on client type, or extend existing architectural patterns (typed schemas, event log, content-addressable versioning) to a new layer. The new material is concentrated in option-space awareness itself — knowing what styles exist, what tradeoffs each carries, and when each could be appropriate.

### 1. API style / paradigm

| Style | Mechanism | Standard fit | Where it could fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **REST** | Resource-oriented, HTTP methods, stateless, hypermedia | The web's default | Human-facing endpoints (dashboards, monitoring), where HTTP middleware and caching are valuable |
| GraphQL | Query language, single endpoint, type system | Complex frontends, mobile | Frontends with deeply nested data needs and a single client team |
| gRPC | Binary, protobuf, streaming, RPC-style, HTTP/2 | Internal microservice APIs | High-performance internal interfaces; trades sovereignty (binary lock-in) for speed |
| **MCP (Model Context Protocol)** | Tool-oriented, JSON-RPC, schema-driven, AI-client-first | LLM agent integration | Any surface where the primary consumer is an LLM agent |
| RPC (general) | Function call abstraction | Internal services | Cases where the client wants to invoke a specific operation rather than read/write a resource |
| Pub/Sub | Event-driven, asynchronous | Decoupled services | Subsystem communication where producers and consumers shouldn't be tightly coupled |
| Hypermedia (HATEOAS) | Self-describing, client navigates by following links | Pure REST | Theoretical purity; rarely worth the engineering cost in practice |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **A system with diverse client types may benefit from multiple API styles rather than a single primary one.** Each client type has different needs: a human operating a dashboard wants HTTP and JSON responses; an LLM agent invoking tools wants schema-driven discoverability; a streaming consumer wants event delivery. **Forcing every client through one style creates friction at the boundaries** — REST is awkward for LLM tool invocation, MCP is awkward for human dashboard access, gRPC is awkward for human inspection. **The architectural option: pick the style that matches the client type, accept that the system has multiple surfaces.** This trades the simplicity of one style for the ergonomics of multiple right-sized surfaces.

### 2. Transport layer

| Transport | Mechanism | Standard fit | Where it could fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, HTTP/3 | Request/response over TCP | Web standard | Any networked API; the lowest common denominator |
| WebSocket | Full-duplex bidirectional | Real-time, chat | Live dashboard updates, bidirectional command channels |
| Server-Sent Events (SSE) | Server → client streaming | Streaming responses | Streaming LLM responses, log tails, event log subscriptions |
| gRPC | HTTP/2 with protobuf framing | High-performance internal | Internal service-to-service calls when sovereignty isn't load-bearing |
| **stdio** | Local process IPC over standard input/output | Local CLI tools, MCP default | Local subprocess communication; cheap, no network, no auth |
| Unix domain sockets | Local IPC, no network | Same-host services | Inter-process communication on the same machine |
| Custom binary | Application-specific | Specialized cases | Generally an anti-pattern (lock-in, opacity) |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Different transports fit different contexts and that's fine.** Local subprocesses can use stdio (cheap, no network, no auth needed). Remote services can use HTTP. Streaming responses can use SSE. **The discipline: pick the simplest transport that meets the latency, bandwidth, and trust requirements for each context, don't over-engineer to a single grand transport.** A system can comfortably have multiple transports as long as the choice is deliberate per context rather than an accident of history.

### 3. Schema and contract definition

| Format | Mechanism | Standard fit | Where it could fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| OpenAPI (Swagger) | YAML/JSON spec for REST APIs | REST documentation | REST endpoints that need machine-readable contracts |
| GraphQL SDL | Schema definition language for GraphQL | GraphQL | GraphQL APIs |
| Protobuf | Binary schema for gRPC | gRPC | gRPC services; trades sovereignty for speed |
| **JSON Schema** | Generic schema language for JSON | Cross-format use | Any JSON-based API (including MCP tool definitions) |
| **MCP tool definitions** | JSON Schema-based, standardized format | MCP servers | Tool-oriented APIs for LLM agent clients |
| **Runtime schema libraries** | Application-language schema libraries (Zod for TypeScript, Pydantic for Python, etc.) | Application validation | Internal validation that bridges to external schemas |
| AsyncAPI | Event-driven API spec | Pub/sub, message brokers | Documenting event streams and message contracts |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Typed schemas at API boundaries compose with typed schemas at persistent boundaries** — the same discipline of "every boundary is typed and validated by code" extends from persistence to APIs. **A runtime schema library (Zod, Pydantic, etc.) can bridge the gap between internal validation and external API contracts** when both are derivable from the same source. The principle: the more boundaries that share the same schema definition, the lower the coordination cost when things change.

### 4. Versioning and evolution

| Strategy | Mechanism | Standard fit | Where it could fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| URL versioning | `/v1/resource`, `/v2/resource` | REST default | REST APIs with breaking changes |
| Header versioning | `Accept: application/vnd.api+json;version=2` | More flexible REST | When URL space should stay clean |
| Content-type versioning | Different content types per version | Strict REST | Rare in practice |
| **Operation-name versioning** | `do_thing@1.2.0` as part of the tool/operation name | Content-addressable | Systems where operation identifiers can carry version semantics |
| Breaking vs additive | Distinguish backward-compatible from breaking changes | Standard discipline | Required everywhere |
| Deprecation periods | Old version warns for N months before removal | Standard | Required for graceful upgrade |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Content-addressable versioning generalizes beyond any single layer.** If a system uses versioned identifiers for some kinds of artifacts (skills, models, tools), the same model can apply to API operations. **The advantage: old versions and new versions can coexist by name, and consumers can migrate at their own pace.** This composes cleanly with content-addressable caching (because cache keys include the version) and with event sourcing (because events reference versioned operations). The choice between URL/header/operation-name versioning is mostly stylistic; the load-bearing decision is "are old versions kept available during deprecation."

### 5. Query patterns — pagination, filtering, sorting

| Pattern | Mechanism | Standard fit | Where it could fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Offset-based pagination** | `?offset=100&limit=20` | Common but fragile | Acceptable for static data; fragile under concurrent writes |
| **Cursor-based pagination** | `?cursor=abc&limit=20` | Stable under concurrent writes | The right model when underlying data is changing |
| Filtering | Query parameters, JSON body, GraphQL where clauses | Standard | List views with multiple filter dimensions |
| Sorting | `?sort=created_at:desc` | Standard | List views with multiple sort criteria |
| Search | Keyword, full-text, semantic | Standard | Discoverability over large corpora |
| Field selection (sparse fieldsets) | Client picks which fields to return | Reduce payload | When payload size matters |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Cursor-based pagination via stable identifiers (like event log offsets) is robust by construction under concurrent writes.** Offset-based pagination is fragile — items shift between pages as the underlying data changes — and the failure mode is silent (clients see duplicates or skipped items without errors). **A system that already has stable monotonic identifiers (event log sequence numbers, timestamps with tiebreakers) gets cursor-based pagination almost for free.** This composes with the event sourcing pattern when applicable.

### 6. Error semantics

| Concern | Mechanism | Standard fit | Where it could fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| HTTP status codes | 200, 400, 404, 500 | REST standard | HTTP-based APIs |
| **Structured errors** | Machine-parseable error envelopes with type, code, message | Production APIs | Required when clients need to react programmatically |
| Error codes vs messages | Codes are machine-parseable; messages are human | Standard | Both, with codes as primary |
| Stack traces vs sanitized | Internal vs external errors | Security concern | Sanitize at trust boundaries |
| **Errors as events** | Every error is an event in the canonical log | Event sourcing | When the system has an event log to write to |
| MCP error model | JSON-RPC error codes | MCP standard | MCP tool implementations |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Errors that are structured, machine-parseable, and persistently logged are reactable.** A client (or internal harness) that can read structured error fields can branch on specific error types programmatically rather than parsing strings or guessing from status codes. **In a system with an event log, treating errors as events extends the failures-as-events pattern** — every error becomes queryable, replayable, and auditable in the same way as successful events. The cost is some discipline in error structure design; the benefit is that error handling becomes a use of existing infrastructure rather than a separate concern.

### 7. API layer concerns — idempotency, rate limiting, auth

| Concern | Mechanism | Standard fit | Where it could fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Idempotency keys | Client provides a key; server de-duplicates | Stripe-style | Operations that need exactly-once semantics |
| Rate limit headers | `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, `X-RateLimit-Reset` | Standard | APIs that enforce rate limits |
| Retry-after headers | `Retry-After: 30` | Standard | Required when returning 429 (Too Many Requests) |
| Authentication | Tokens, sessions, OAuth, capability tokens | Standard | APIs with multiple users or trust boundaries |
| Authorization | RBAC, ABAC, policy engines | Standard | APIs with differentiated access |
| API gateway | Centralized middleware (Kong, Tyk, AWS API Gateway) | Production scaling | Multi-service systems; trades sovereignty for centralization |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The API layer is where resilience patterns become visible to clients**, and the discipline is to expose them consistently. If a service returns a rate-limit error, it should include a retry-after header. If an operation requires idempotency, the requirement should be in the schema. If an operation is rate-limited, the rate limit info should be in response headers. **This is mostly application of resilience patterns at the API boundary**, not new material — but the API design lens confirms the patterns need to surface at the edge, not be hidden inside the implementation.

### 8. Multi-surface API architecture — when one style isn't enough

| Pattern | Mechanism | Standard fit | Where it could fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single API surface | One style for all clients | Small systems with one client type | Simpler operationally, friction at client mismatches |
| **Multiple surfaces, picked per client type** | Different styles for different client needs | Systems with diverse clients | More API surface area, but each surface fits its clients |
| API gateway layer | Centralized routing across multiple backends | Large microservice deployments | Adds a coordination point |
| **No gateway, surfaces are direct** | Each backend exposes its own surface | Sovereign / decentralized systems | More direct, no central dependency |
| Bridging layer | One style on top of another (REST → MCP, etc.) | When migration is in progress | Transitional |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Real systems often have multiple API surfaces because different client types have different needs.** A system with both human dashboard users and AI agent clients can't comfortably serve both through a single style — the discoverability and schema model that AI agents want is overkill for a human dashboard; the HTTP-and-JSON model that humans want is awkward for AI tool invocation. **The architectural option: name each surface and the client type it serves, accept that the system has multiple surfaces.** The discipline this requires: each surface should be the simplest fit for its client type, not a compromise meant to serve multiple types. Forcing one style on every client creates friction; multiple surfaces accept some surface-area cost in exchange for ergonomic fit.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Seven things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or options:

1. **Multi-surface API architecture is a valid pattern when client types diverge** (from §1, §8). A system with human dashboard clients AND AI agent clients can pick a different style per client type rather than forcing one style on everyone. The cost is more surface area; the benefit is ergonomic fit per client.
2. **Multi-transport is fine; transport choice should match context** (from §2). stdio for local subprocess, HTTP for remote services, SSE for streams. The discipline is making the choice deliberate per context rather than defaulting.
3. **Typed schemas at API boundaries compose with typed schemas at persistent boundaries** (from §3). Same principle, different layer. A runtime schema library (Zod, Pydantic) can bridge internal validation and external API contracts.
4. **Content-addressable versioning generalizes beyond any single layer** (from §4). If versioned identifiers work for one kind of artifact, the same model can apply to API operations, cache keys, event references, etc.
5. **Cursor-based pagination via stable monotonic identifiers** (from §5). Robust by construction under concurrent writes. Composes naturally with event sourcing if the architecture has an event log.
6. **Errors as events at API boundaries** (from §6). Extends the failures-as-events pattern from internal state to API surfaces. Every error becomes queryable, replayable, auditable.
7. **API layer surfaces resilience patterns to clients** (from §7). Idempotency keys in schemas, rate limit headers in responses, retry-after on 429. Mostly application of existing patterns at the boundary; the discipline is consistency.

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This entry presents the API design option space rather than claiming specific architectural commitments.** Most of its dimensions either offer multiple valid choices for different client types or extend existing architectural patterns (typed schemas, event log, content-addressable versioning) to a new layer. **The new material is concentrated in option-space awareness itself**: knowing what styles exist, what tradeoffs each carries, and when each could be appropriate.

**This is roughly 80% confirmation of existing threads and 20% option-space mapping**, similar in shape to the observability and saga orchestration entries. The convergence is stable — new entries are sharpening existing patterns and surfacing valid options rather than producing new dominant patterns. **The doc is now in its "fluency and option-space" phase** rather than its "discover new convergent patterns" phase, which is the natural arc for a brainstorm/aggregation document.

---

## CAP theorem and consistency tradeoffs

Eight dimensions of CAP-related decisions. For each one, the option space and the contexts where each option could fit, with explicit attention to **the multi-agent layer where CAP-style tradeoffs become load-bearing in-process**, not just cross-machine.

This entry is more applicable than the original (incorrect) framing credited it. The classical CAP theorem is about distributed systems with network partitions between machines — a scenario the orchestrator deliberately avoids. **But the in-process equivalent — many agents reading and writing a shared event log, with agent-to-agent communication failures or slowdowns — is structurally the same problem.** "Partitions" become "agent isolation events" (a clone hangs, a clone dies, a clone is slow). The C vs A vs P tradeoff still applies, just at the in-process scale.

### 1. The CAP theorem itself — the fundamental tradeoff

| Property | What it guarantees | Standard fit | Where it could apply in-process |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Consistency (C)** | Every read sees the latest write | Strong-consistency systems (Postgres, Spanner) | When agents must see the latest state from each other (rare, but real for some coordination tasks) |
| **Availability (A)** | Every request gets a response | High-availability systems (Cassandra, Dynamo) | When the loop must keep making progress even if some agents are unreachable |
| **Partition tolerance (P)** | System works despite network failures | Required for any distributed system | **At the in-process scale: the system works despite individual agents hanging, dying, or being slow** |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **CAP applies in-process when the agent layer becomes many-many.** The classical framing (network partitions between machines) doesn't apply because the orchestrator is local-first. But the structural tradeoff — when you can't reach every component, do you wait (favor consistency) or proceed (favor availability) — applies whenever a clone hangs, dies, or is slow. The "partition" is the fact that some agent isn't responding; the "tolerance" is whether the rest of the system continues to make progress. **The decision the architecture has to make: when an agent isn't responding, does the loop wait for it (CP), continue without it (AP), or fail entirely (CA, which CAP says is impossible under partitions)?** This is a real architectural question once shadow clones are stateful and parallel.

### 2. CP vs AP — picking a side under partition

| Choice | Meaning | Standard fit | Where it could apply |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CP (Consistency + Partition tolerance)** | Refuse to respond when consistency can't be guaranteed | Banking, ledgers, anything requiring exactness | When the loop's correctness depends on every agent's contribution being visible |
| **AP (Availability + Partition tolerance)** | Always respond, accept stale or inconsistent data | Social feeds, caches, anything where eventual is OK | When making progress matters more than perfect agreement |
| **CA (Consistency + Availability)** | The "no partitions" assumption — only fully valid when nothing else is running concurrently | Single-machine RDBMS without replication | The default that breaks down as soon as multiple shadow clones are running, which is already the case |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The orchestrator's CAP posture has been implicitly CA at low parallelism**, but shadow clones (already real via Mechanism 1 and Mechanism 2) introduce in-process partition events as soon as one clone can't reach another's state. The architecture has to decide: when that happens, is it CP (wait until they can reach each other) or AP (proceed with what's available)? **Most agent work is probably AP** — the loop should keep making progress even if one clone hangs — but some operations need CP (gate decisions, dream-state edits, anything where stale state would corrupt the architecture). The decision is per-operation, not system-wide.

### 3. Consistency models within the C side

| Model | What it guarantees | Standard fit | Where it could apply in-process |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linearizability / strict serializable | Real-time global ordering | Strongest, most expensive | When agents need to see each other's writes in real time (rare) |
| Sequential consistency | Same order seen by everyone, but not necessarily real-time | Strong but weaker than linearizable | A reasonable default for high-stakes shared state |
| **Snapshot isolation** | Each transaction sees a consistent snapshot | Postgres default; sufficient for most workloads | The natural default for agents reading the event log |
| **Read-your-writes** | A client always sees its own writes | The minimum for sane single-client behavior | The minimum for an agent to operate sensibly on its own state |
| **Causal consistency** | Causally related events seen in order | Distributed systems with causality tracking | When agent A's action depends on agent B's prior action being visible |
| Eventual consistency | Will converge eventually, no other guarantees | High-availability systems | The default for low-stakes propagation |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **At the in-process multi-agent scale, the consistency model becomes a per-resource decision.** Different kinds of state want different models. The dream state and design principles want strong consistency (linearizable or near-it) because they're foundational. Skill versions want monotonic + read-your-writes because consumers need to see new versions. Calibration data is fine with eventual consistency. Trace events are causally consistent by construction (they have a sequence number per stream). **The architecture should not pick one consistency model for everything; it should specify per resource type.** This is the existing "conflict resolution split by stakes" thread from Replication §7, sharpened for the multi-agent in-process case.

### 4. The PACELC refinement — even without partitions, latency vs consistency

| Dimension | Mechanism | Standard fit | Where it could apply |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PA / EL** | Under partition, favor availability; else favor latency | DynamoDB, Cassandra | When responsiveness matters more than freshness |
| **PC / EC** | Under partition, favor consistency; else favor consistency | Spanner, BigTable | When correctness matters more than speed |
| **PA / EC** | Under partition, favor availability; else favor consistency | Mixed-mode systems | When the partition case is rare and you want strict otherwise |
| **PC / EL** | Under partition, favor consistency; else favor latency | Rare in practice | Edge case |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **PACELC is the refinement that says "even without partitions, you have a latency vs consistency tradeoff."** Strong consistency requires coordination overhead; relaxed consistency allows cheaper reads. **For the orchestrator's in-process multi-agent case, the tradeoff is real even without "partitions" in the classical sense.** When agent A needs to see the latest events written by agent B, the question is whether to (a) wait until B has flushed to the event log and A has caught up (consistency, latency cost), or (b) proceed with the last snapshot A has (availability, freshness cost). **Per-operation choice again**: high-stakes operations should pay the latency cost for consistency; high-throughput agent work should accept stale reads for speed.

### 5. Quorum and read/write tradeoffs (R + W > N)

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit | Where it could apply |
|---|---|---|---|
| `R + W > N` | Read and write sets overlap → strong consistency | Quorum-based distributed databases | Currently N/A — no multi-replica setup |
| Tunable consistency | Pick R and W per operation | Cassandra | If multiple replicas of the event log exist (e.g., for backup) |
| Sloppy quorum | Relaxed when nodes are unreachable | Availability over consistency | Future, if backup replicas exist |
| Hinted handoff | Temporary delegation when target node is down | Eventual consistency mechanism | Future |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Classical quorum math is N/A for the current orchestrator** because there are no replicas to coordinate within a single org instance. **It is already partially relevant for servicegrid** (where 3 humans run their own org instances and need to reconcile event logs) and would become more relevant under (a) sovereign cloud, where the event log is replicated across machines for backup/availability, or (b) more multi-human orgs. The quorum framing gives a precise vocabulary for "how many copies need to agree before a read or write counts" — useful for any situation where multi-human consensus is needed before a high-stakes change becomes canonical.

### 6. The "no real partitions in single process" myth

| Failure mode | Mechanism | Why it looks like a partition | Where it could apply |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Clone hangs** | An agent stops responding but doesn't crash | The rest of the system can't reach it | At any moment when many agents are running |
| **Clone dies** | Process terminates unexpectedly | The rest of the system has to handle missing state | Crash recovery is a partition-recovery problem |
