| **Wait-free vs lock-free** | Wait-free: every operation completes in bounded steps; lock-free: at least one operation makes progress | Theoretical guarantees | N/A directly; conceptually the event log is lock-free by construction |
| **The ABA problem** | A value changes from A to B and back to A; CAS thinks nothing changed | Classic lock-free bug | **Avoided by the event log's monotonic sequence numbers** — every event has a unique offset, so CAS-style comparisons can't be fooled |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The event log is structurally a lock-free queue — append-only, totally ordered per partition, multiple readers consuming without coordination, single-writer-per-clone meaning no CAS contention.** This is one of the cleanest examples of "the architecture inherits a known concurrency property by structural choice rather than careful primitive use." **The ABA problem in particular is dissolved** because every event has a monotonic sequence number — there's no way for an event at offset N to be "the same as" an event at offset N+1, even if the payload happens to match. **The architecture should name this property explicitly**: monotonic sequence numbers in the event log are not just an audit feature, they're an ABA-defense mechanism that makes optimistic concurrency on event-log offsets safe by construction.

### 5. Higher-level concurrency primitives

| Primitive | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Futures / Promises** | A handle for a value that doesn't exist yet | Async/await foundations | **The fundamental unit of harness concurrency** |
| **Channels (CSP-style)** | Typed pipes for sending values between goroutines/coroutines | Go, Rust crossbeam | Conceptually maps to event log streams |
| **async / await** | Syntactic sugar over promises/futures | Modern languages | The TypeScript surface in the harness |
| **Coroutines / fibers / green threads** | Userspace lightweight threads, cooperatively scheduled | Go goroutines, Lua coroutines, Python greenlets | Implicit via async/await; not exposed directly |
| **Reactive streams** | Backpressure-aware async sequences | RxJS, Reactor, Akka Streams | Adjacent to event log consumer groups |
| **Software Transactional Memory (STM)** | Atomic compound operations on shared state | Clojure, Haskell, some research languages | N/A — too exotic; the equivalent is the event log + projection model |
| **Promise combinators** | `Promise.all`, `Promise.race`, `Promise.allSettled` | Composing concurrent operations | Heavy use in the harness for parallel LLM calls and tool calls |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The orchestrator's concurrency surface is async/await + Promise combinators.** That's it. Not channels, not actors directly, not STM, not reactive streams libraries. The simpler the concurrency surface, the fewer ways it can go wrong, and async/await is the simplest concurrency surface that handles the harness's actual workload (I/O-bound LLM calls and file operations). **The architectural commitment**: **don't introduce more concurrency primitives than the actual workload requires.** When parallelism is needed, use `Promise.all` over independent operations; when sequencing is needed, use `await`. Any temptation toward channels, actors, or STM should be checked against "what does this give us that async/await + the event log don't already give us?"

### 6. Concurrent data structures

| Structure | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Concurrent hash map** | Lock-striped or lock-free | Java ConcurrentHashMap, Go sync.Map | N/A within Node; equivalents are plain Maps within a single event-loop tick |
| **Concurrent queue** | Lock-free or fine-grained locking | Producer-consumer | The event log IS this primitive at a different level |
| **Concurrent set** | Lock-striped or lock-free | Membership tracking | N/A directly |
| **Copy-on-write structures** | Mutations create new versions; readers see snapshots | Functional persistent data structures | The right model for the harness's projection layer |
| **Immutable data structures** | Mutation produces new structure, original unchanged | Clojure, Immer, Immutable.js | Aligns with event sourcing — events are immutable, projections are derived |
| **Concurrent linked list** | Fine-grained locking per node | Specialized | N/A |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The orchestrator's "concurrent data structures" are the projection layer over the event log, plus immutable values everywhere.** Plain JavaScript Maps and Objects work fine because access is serialized by the event loop within a process; immutability via spread operators / structuralSharing libraries (Immer, Immutable.js) handles the case where multiple consumers want consistent snapshots. **The classic "concurrent collections" library doesn't apply** because the architecture doesn't have true in-process parallelism that would race on shared collections. The conceptual lineage is right (concurrent data structures → MVCC → snapshot isolation → event sourcing) — the orchestrator just operates several abstraction levels above the lock-striped hash map.

### 7. Parallel execution patterns

| Pattern | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fork-join** | Split work into N pieces, run in parallel, join results | Java fork-join pool, parallel for-each | Maps to Promise.all over independent operations |
| **Work stealing** | Idle workers steal from busy workers' queues | Modern parallel runtimes | N/A within Node; relevant for shadow clone task distribution |
| **MapReduce** | Map → shuffle → reduce stages | Big data processing | N/A at the orchestrator's data scale |
| **Pipeline parallelism** | Different stages of a pipeline run concurrently | Stream processing | Maps to consumer groups on the event log |
| **Data parallelism** | Same operation applied to many independent data items | SIMD, GPU, parallel for | Promise.all over array of items |
| **Task parallelism** | Different operations run concurrently | Multi-threaded servers | Promise.all + Promise.race for independent operations |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The orchestrator's parallel execution surface is Promise.all + the shadow clone topology**, and beyond that nothing applies until you get to the multi-clone scale where work-stealing-style task distribution becomes interesting. **Work stealing specifically transfers as a future architectural pattern** for shadow clone task distribution — when the harness spawns N clones to handle M tasks (M > N), idle clones could "steal" tasks from busy clones rather than the harness statically partitioning the work. This is currently not implemented but is a natural extension once parallelism density grows. **The architecture should know it's available as a primitive even if it's not used today.**

### 8. Failure modes and pitfalls

| Failure mode | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deadlock** | Cycle of locks where each holder waits for another | Lock-based concurrent code | **Dissolved** — no locks |
| **Livelock** | Threads keep retrying but never make progress | Optimistic concurrency under contention | Possible if retry storms are misconfigured; defended by exponential backoff (Resilience patterns §2) |
| **Starvation** | One actor never gets resources because others always win | Unfair scheduling | Possible at multi-clone scale; defended by per-clone resource limits (Resilience patterns §5) |
| **Race condition** | Outcome depends on timing of unrelated operations | Classic concurrency bug | **Mostly dissolved** — single-threaded event loop + event log ordering |
| **ABA problem** | CAS sees value unchanged but it actually changed twice | Lock-free structures | **Dissolved** — monotonic event log offsets prevent it |
| **False sharing** | Two threads contending on the same cache line for unrelated data | CPU-level performance bug | Below the architecture's design space |
| **Priority inversion** | High-priority task blocked by low-priority task holding a lock | Real-time systems | N/A — no locks, no priorities |
| **Lost wakeup** | A signal arrives before the receiver is waiting | Condition variable misuse | N/A — no condition variables |
| **Memory ordering bugs** | Naive code assumes sequential consistency where the runtime gives weaker | Low-level concurrent code | N/A — JS memory model is sequentially consistent within a single isolate |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The orchestrator's concurrency commitments dissolve most classical concurrency bugs by structural choice rather than careful primitive use.** Deadlock is impossible without locks. Race conditions are impossible without true shared-memory parallelism. ABA is impossible with monotonic sequence numbers. False sharing is below the design space. Priority inversion requires priorities and locks, neither of which exists. Lost wakeup requires condition variables, which don't exist. **The classical concurrency bug list is almost entirely N/A**, and the few that remain (livelock from misconfigured retries, starvation from unfair resource allocation) are defended by the resilience patterns and bulkhead disciplines from earlier entries. **This is one of the strongest "the architecture's primitives are doing the work" stories in the doc** — it didn't arise from careful concurrency engineering, it arose from picking primitives (single-threaded event loop, event log, single-writer-per-resource, no in-process parallelism) that make whole classes of bugs structurally impossible.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Seven things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **The orchestrator's concurrency model is hybrid: event loop + multi-process + actor coordination via event log** (from §1). Each part has different semantics and failure modes; naming the hybrid explicitly is necessary for failure-mode reasoning across Mech-1 vs Mech-2 clones.
2. **The harness avoids true in-process parallelism** (from §2). Mechanism-1 sub-agents share memory but execute serially within the parent's event loop; real parallelism is deferred to Mechanism-2 separate processes. This is one of the architecture's quietest but most load-bearing concurrency choices.
3. **The entire locking primitive layer is unused and unneeded** (from §3). Single-writer-per-resource + event log + MVCC reads dissolves the need. This is an architectural decision worth naming explicitly because most production systems spend significant engineering on locks.
4. **The event log is structurally a lock-free queue** (from §4). Append-only, totally ordered per partition, multiple readers without coordination, single-writer-per-clone. The architecture inherits a known concurrency property by structural choice.
5. **Monotonic sequence numbers in the event log are an ABA-defense mechanism** (from §4). Worth naming explicitly — they're not just an audit feature.
6. **Don't introduce more concurrency primitives than the workload requires** (from §5). async/await + Promise combinators is enough for the harness's I/O-bound workload. Any temptation toward channels, actors, or STM should be checked against "what does this give us that async/await + the event log don't?"
7. **Work stealing as a future pattern for shadow clone task distribution** (from §7). When clone count grows past harness static partitioning, idle clones stealing tasks from busy clones is the natural extension. Worth naming as available even if not implemented.

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This entry is structurally a foundation-layer entry like Disk I/O and System models — most of its dimensions are inherited rather than chosen, and the architectural value is naming what's been inherited and what bugs are dissolved by choice of primitive.** The most generative observation is §8 (failure modes): **the orchestrator's concurrency commitments dissolve most classical concurrency bugs structurally, not through careful primitive use.** Deadlock, race conditions, ABA, false sharing, priority inversion, lost wakeup, memory ordering bugs — all N/A. The few that remain (livelock from misconfigured retries, starvation from unfair resource allocation) are handled by the resilience patterns and bulkhead disciplines from earlier entries.

**This validates several existing architectural commitments via their concurrency consequences**: single-threaded Node, single-writer-per-resource, event log with monotonic offsets, no distributed locks, deferring real parallelism to Mechanism-2 separate processes. Each looks innocuous individually; together they make whole classes of concurrency bugs impossible to write. **The dream state should probably acknowledge this explicitly** — the absence of locks is architecturally load-bearing, not an accident of single-threaded JavaScript.

**Layer note**: this entry is harness-layer primary. App-layer concurrency depends on the language and runtime each app is built in. Apps using Node share the harness's event-loop model; apps using Go, Rust, or Java will engage with the broader concurrency primitive space. The vocabulary in this entry is universal even if the specific dissolutions don't transfer.

---

## Authentication and authorization

Eight dimensions of authentication (who is calling) and authorization (what they're allowed to do). This entry is **app-layer primary** — the harness has a single human user and no auth model is needed beyond OS file permissions, but every app each org ships has real users with real authorization needs. The navigator needs the vocabulary even though the harness doesn't directly use it.

This is a **choice-layer entry** for the app layer with limited harness application. The architectural value is naming the option space and the canonical decisions (which auth provider, what authorization model, how to handle secrets) so that app-layer designs can pick deliberately rather than reactively.

### 1. Authentication factors and credentials

| Factor | Mechanism | Standard fit | App fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Knowledge factor** ("something you know") | Password, PIN, security question | Universal baseline | Required minimum but increasingly insufficient alone |
| **Possession factor** ("something you have") | Phone (TOTP/SMS), hardware key (YubiKey, FIDO2), email account | MFA second factor | Strongly recommended for any production app |
| **Inherence factor** ("something you are") | Fingerprint, face, retina | Mobile device unlock, high-security | Rare for web apps; common for mobile |
| **Behavioral factor** | Typing patterns, mouse movement, location | Fraud detection, adaptive auth | Used by banks; rare elsewhere |
| **Single-factor (password only)** | One factor | Insecure baseline | Anti-pattern for any app handling real data |
| **Multi-factor authentication (MFA)** | Two+ factors required | Best practice | Required for any serious app |
| **Passwordless** | Magic link, WebAuthn, passkeys | Modern best practice | The right default for new apps |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **For the apps each org ships, the right default in 2026 is passwordless or password-with-mandatory-MFA, using a managed auth provider (Clerk, Auth0, Supabase Auth, NextAuth) rather than rolling your own.** Rolling your own auth is one of the most common production failures — password hashing, MFA flows, session management, password reset flows, account recovery, brute force defense, credential stuffing defense are all hard to get right and the cost of getting them wrong is severe. **The architectural commitment for app-layer auth: managed auth provider by default, custom auth only with explicit justification.** This is not a sovereignty compromise in the same sense as managed databases — auth providers are well-bounded (they handle credentials and identity, nothing else) and their failure modes are well-understood.

### 2. Session management

| Approach | Mechanism | Standard fit | App fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Server-side sessions** | Session ID in cookie, state in server-side store | Traditional web apps | The classic stateful approach |
| **JWT (JSON Web Tokens)** | Self-contained signed tokens, stateless on the server | Modern stateless services | Common but has known footguns (revocation, leakage) |
| **Opaque token + server lookup** | Random token, server checks against database | Stripe-style API authentication | Robust; harder to leak useful info |
| **Refresh + access token pattern** | Short-lived access token + long-lived refresh token | OAuth 2.0 standard | The right pattern for most production apps |
| **Cookie-based sessions** | Browser-managed cookies, HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite | Web apps | Standard for web; SameSite=Strict prevents most CSRF |
| **Bearer tokens in headers** | Token in `Authorization: Bearer X` header | API clients | Standard for API access |
| **Long-lived sessions vs short-lived** | Tradeoff: convenience vs blast radius on leak | Per-app decision | Short-lived for sensitive apps; longer for low-stakes |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The right default for app-layer session management is the access + refresh token pattern**, which gives short-lived access tokens (15 min - 1 hour) plus longer-lived refresh tokens (days to weeks) that can be rotated and revoked. **JWTs are common but have known footguns**: revocation requires a stateful blocklist (which defeats the "stateless" advantage), token leakage exposes the user until expiration, and naive implementations skip critical validation steps (algorithm confusion, missing exp checks). **Opaque tokens with server lookup are safer for high-stakes APIs** because revocation is trivial (delete the token row) and the token itself reveals nothing. Choose JWT only when stateless validation across many services is genuinely required; otherwise opaque tokens are simpler and safer.

### 3. OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC)

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit | App fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **OAuth 2.0** | Authorization framework — third-party apps get scoped access without user credentials | "Sign in with Google", API integrations | The standard for delegated authorization |
| **OpenID Connect (OIDC)** | Authentication layer on top of OAuth 2.0 — adds identity (who the user is) | "Sign in with X" flows | The standard for federated authentication |
| **Authorization Code flow** | Browser redirect + server-side code exchange | Web apps, mobile | The right default for confidential clients |
| **PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange)** | Code flow + dynamic secret to prevent code interception | SPAs, mobile, public clients | Required for any public client (no client secret) |
| **Client Credentials flow** | Server-to-server, no user involved | Service-to-service auth | The right pattern for backend-to-backend |
| **Implicit flow** | Token returned directly in URL fragment | Legacy SPAs | **Deprecated** — use Code + PKCE instead |
| **Resource Owner Password Credentials flow** | App handles password, exchanges for token | Legacy migrations | **Anti-pattern** — defeats the point of OAuth |
| **Device Authorization flow** | Devices without browsers (TVs, CLIs) | IoT, CLI tools | Useful for terminal apps |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **OAuth 2.0 and OIDC are the canonical federated identity protocols and any app each org ships should use them via a managed provider rather than implementing the flows directly.** The flows have known footguns (code interception, redirect URI manipulation, state parameter omission, PKCE skipping) that managed providers handle correctly. **PKCE is non-negotiable** for any public client (SPAs, mobile, CLIs). **Implicit flow and ROPC flow are anti-patterns** that should never appear in new code. The Device Authorization flow is the right primitive for any CLI tool or device without a browser — useful for orchestrator's own auth flows if it ever needs to authenticate to external services on the navigator's behalf.

### 4. Authorization models

| Model | Mechanism | Standard fit | App fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Access Control List (ACL)** | Per-object list of who can do what | File systems, simple permissions | Trivial cases |
| **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** | Users have roles; roles have permissions | Most enterprise apps | The default for most apps |
| **Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)** | Permissions based on attributes of user, resource, action, environment | Complex policy needs | When RBAC is too coarse |
| **Relationship-Based Access Control (ReBAC) / Zanzibar** | Permissions derived from graph relationships ("user is a member of organization that owns resource") | Modern multi-tenant apps | The right model for graph-shaped permissions |
| **Policy-as-code** | Policies expressed in a DSL (Rego/OPA, Cedar) | Cross-cutting policy enforcement | Useful when policies span many services |
| **Capability-based security** | Possession of an unforgeable token grants the capability | Object-capability systems | Rare but elegant for some patterns |
| **Discretionary vs Mandatory** | Who controls the access — owner (DAC) or system (MAC) | Operating systems | Conceptual; rarely a direct choice in apps |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **For most apps, RBAC is enough; for multi-tenant apps with complex sharing semantics, Zanzibar-style ReBAC is the right model.** Google's Zanzibar paper describes a graph-based authorization system where permissions derive from relationships — "this user can read this document because they're a member of a folder that's shared with their team." **Servicegrid is a strong candidate for ReBAC** because the permissions are inherently graph-shaped: a contractor's owner can see all jobs; a technician can see only their assigned jobs; an office manager can see all jobs in their territory. RBAC would force flat role assignments; ReBAC expresses the actual permission structure. **Open-source Zanzibar implementations (SpiceDB, OpenFGA, Permify, Topaz) are mature enough to use today.** This is a real architectural decision worth surfacing: **Zanzibar-style ReBAC is the modern best-in-class model for multi-tenant app authorization, and it's particularly aligned with the "everything is a graph" pattern the orchestrator already uses.**

### 5. Capability-based security and trust delegation

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Capability tokens** | Possession of an unforgeable token grants the capability | Capability OSes, some web APIs | Aligns with the orchestrator's content-addressable thread |
| **Confused deputy problem** | A privileged service is tricked into using its privilege on behalf of a less-privileged caller | Classic security bug | Defended by capability-passing instead of identity-passing |
| **Macaroons** | Bearer tokens with caveats that can be added but not removed | Distributed authorization | Underrated; useful for delegation chains |
| **Biscuits** | Datalog-based macaroon successors with policy attestation | Newer; gaining adoption | Worth knowing for complex delegation cases |
| **Object capabilities** | Holding a reference to an object IS the permission to invoke it | Capability languages (E, Pony) | The orchestrator's MCP tool model is structurally similar |
| **Delegation** | Granting a subset of one's permissions to another actor | OAuth scopes are the canonical example | Required for any agent-on-behalf-of-user pattern |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Capability-based security is undersold in mainstream auth literature but is structurally the cleanest model for agent systems.** When a shadow clone needs to act on behalf of the navigator (or on behalf of another clone), the capability-based pattern is "pass an unforgeable token that authorizes exactly the operations the clone is allowed to do" rather than "pass the navigator's identity and let downstream services check permissions on every call." The latter is the **confused deputy problem** in action: a service with broad permissions can be tricked into using them on behalf of a caller who shouldn't have them. **Capability passing avoids this entirely** because the token IS the permission — there's no identity-based escalation path. **The orchestrator's MCP tool model is already structurally capability-based**: holding a reference to a tool IS the permission to invoke it, and the tool's permissions are bounded by what the harness allows it to do. **Naming this explicitly matters** because the capability framing extends naturally to "shadow clones get capability tokens for the operations they're authorized to perform, not the navigator's identity." Macaroons specifically are worth knowing as a delegation primitive: a token can be narrowed (by adding caveats) but never broadened, which is exactly the property you want when handing capability to a sub-agent.

### 6. Secrets management

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit | App fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Environment variables** | Secrets in env vars at runtime | The minimum baseline | OK for development; risky for production without rotation |
| **Secrets manager** | Centralized service with versioning, rotation, audit (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Doppler) | Production best practice | The right answer for any production app |
| **Encrypted secrets in repo** | Secrets committed in encrypted form (git-crypt, sops, sealed-secrets) | GitOps workflows | Acceptable when paired with proper key management |
| **Hardcoded secrets** | Secrets in code | **Anti-pattern** | Never; will leak |
| **Secret rotation** | Periodic credential rotation | Compliance, incident response | Required for production; the harder the rotation, the more important it is |
| **Just-in-time secrets** | Secrets generated per-request, short-lived | High-security environments | Specialized; cloud IAM does this for cloud APIs |
| **Workload identity** | Workloads authenticated by identity, no static credentials | Cloud-native pattern (AWS IAM Roles, GCP Workload Identity, Kubernetes service accounts) | The right pattern when cloud-native |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Secrets management is one of the highest-leverage app-layer disciplines and the most commonly fucked up.** The default failure mode is "secrets in environment variables in a `.env` file that gets committed by accident," and the resulting credential leak is one of the top causes of production breaches. **The architectural commitment for app-layer apps: use a secrets manager from day one, not after the first leak.** Doppler, AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, or even just GitHub Secrets + environment-injection are all acceptable; rolling your own is anti-pattern. **The orchestrator's `distribute` skill should enforce this** — any new app deployment should be configured to pull secrets from a managed provider rather than reading them from local env files at deploy time. **Workload identity is the gold standard** when running on a cloud platform: the workload's identity itself is the credential, no static secrets exist, no rotation required.

### 7. Failure modes and attack vectors

| Attack / failure | Mechanism | Defense |
|---|---|---|
| **Credential stuffing** | Reuse of leaked passwords from other breaches | Rate limiting, MFA, breached-password detection (Have I Been Pwned API) |
| **Brute force** | Many guesses per user | Rate limiting per-user, exponential backoff, CAPTCHA |
| **Phishing** | Trick the user into giving credentials to an attacker | WebAuthn / passkeys (origin-bound) |
| **Session fixation** | Attacker sets the user's session ID | Regenerate session ID on login |
| **Session hijacking** | Stealing a valid session token | HttpOnly + Secure cookies, short TTLs, IP/device fingerprinting |
| **CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery)** | Trick browser into making authenticated requests | SameSite cookies, anti-CSRF tokens |
| **JWT algorithm confusion** | "alg: none" or HS256 vs RS256 confusion | Strict algorithm allow-listing in validation |
| **Privilege escalation** | User obtains permissions they shouldn't have | Least privilege, regular access reviews |
| **Confused deputy** | Privileged service tricked into using its privilege wrongly | Capability passing instead of identity passing |
| **Insider threat** | Malicious or compromised authorized user | Audit logs, least privilege, separation of duties |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Auth failure modes are the failure modes most commonly missed in design reviews because they look like edge cases until they're catastrophic.** The architectural defense is twofold: (1) use managed auth providers that handle these failures correctly out of the box, and (2) maintain an explicit threat model for each app that names which of these attacks are in scope and how they're defended against. **The threat model should be a step in the system design walk template** (and indeed it is — Step 11). **The most underrated defense is least privilege**: even if every other defense fails, an attacker who compromises a low-privileged session can do less damage than one who compromises a high-privileged session. The discipline: every user, every service account, every API key should have the minimum permissions needed for its job — and access should be audited and reviewed regularly.

### 8. The harness layer's auth posture

| Concern | Standard answer | Harness answer |
|---|---|---|
| **Identity** | Username + credential | Single human; OS user is the identity |
| **Authentication** | Passwords / MFA / passkeys | OS file permissions; no app-level auth |
| **Authorization** | RBAC / ABAC / ReBAC | Trust model: trust within an org, untrust across orgs (System models §4) |
| **Sessions** | Cookies / tokens | N/A — no user-session model |
| **Secrets** | Secrets manager | Local filesystem with `.env` files; **could be improved with a local secrets store** |
| **Verification of LLM output** | N/A in standard auth | **Verification gates ARE the BFT mechanism** for soft-Byzantine agents (System models §2) |
| **Cross-org trust** | Federation / SSO | No federation; orgs are sovereign and don't share state |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The harness layer doesn't need traditional auth because it has one human user and operates within OS file permissions.** But there are two harness-level concerns that the standard auth literature doesn't cover and that the orchestrator should name explicitly:

1. **Verification gates are the harness's BFT mechanism** for soft-Byzantine agent output (System models §2). When an LLM clone produces output, the harness can't trust it the same way a traditional system trusts an authenticated user — the LLM might hallucinate. Gates check the output before it commits to canonical state. **This is the harness's "authorization" layer**, even though the mechanism is verification rather than identity. The dream state should name this connection: gates serve the same role for agent output that authorization checks serve for user requests.

2. **Local secrets management could be improved.** The orchestrator currently reads external service credentials (LLM API keys, MCP server tokens, channel webhooks) from environment variables or config files. A local secrets store (something as simple as macOS Keychain via the `keychain` Node module, or a dedicated tool like `pass` or `gopass`) would be more sovereign-aligned and less leak-prone than `.env` files. **This is a small but real near-term unlock.**

**Cross-org trust** is already named in System models §4 — the architecture trusts within an org, untrusts across orgs. **No federation, no SSO, no shared identity across orgs.** This is consistent with the cosmos boundary as the sovereignty boundary.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **App-layer auth uses managed providers by default** (from §1, §3, §6). Clerk, Auth0, Supabase Auth, NextAuth + a secrets manager. Rolling your own auth is anti-pattern. The architectural commitment is "managed by default, custom only with explicit justification."
2. **Access + refresh token pattern as the session default; opaque tokens for high-stakes; JWT only when stateless validation is genuinely required** (from §2). JWTs have known footguns; opaque tokens are simpler and safer for most cases.
3. **PKCE is non-negotiable for public clients; implicit flow and ROPC flow are anti-patterns** (from §3). Modern OAuth practice; needs to be in the architecture's app-layer design defaults.
4. **Zanzibar-style ReBAC is the modern best-in-class authorization model for multi-tenant apps, and it aligns with the orchestrator's graph-of-typed-nodes model** (from §4). Servicegrid is a strong candidate; the permission structure is inherently graph-shaped. SpiceDB / OpenFGA / Permify are mature open-source implementations.
5. **Capability-based security is structurally the cleanest model for agent systems** (from §5). The orchestrator's MCP tool model is already structurally capability-based; the framing should be named explicitly. Macaroons are worth knowing for delegation chains. Confused deputy is the bug capability passing prevents.
6. **Secrets manager from day one for app-layer apps; the distribute skill should enforce it** (from §6). Workload identity is the gold standard when running on a cloud platform; secrets in `.env` files is the most common production failure.
7. **Verification gates are the harness's BFT mechanism for soft-Byzantine agent output** (from §8). The dream state should name the connection between gates and authorization — they serve the same role for agent output that authz checks serve for user requests.
8. **Local secrets store as a near-term harness unlock** (from §8). macOS Keychain or `pass` instead of `.env` files. Small change, more sovereign-aligned, less leak-prone.

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This is the doc's first explicitly app-layer-primary entry**, and it sets the pattern for several subsequent entries (Payment, Data governance, Geographic distribution, Mobile, A/B testing, Notification systems for the user-facing parts). The harness section is small (1 of 8 dimensions); the bulk of the entry is option space and recommendations for the apps each org ships.

**The most generative parts** are §4 (Zanzibar-style ReBAC as the right model for multi-tenant graph-shaped permissions) and §5 (capability-based security as the natural fit for agent systems). The first is a concrete app-layer recommendation for servicegrid; the second extends a primitive the orchestrator already uses (MCP tools as capabilities) into a more general framing that should inform how shadow clones get authorization to act on the navigator's behalf.

**The harness-layer connection** (§8) — verification gates as the BFT/authorization layer for soft-Byzantine agent output — is the load-bearing harness contribution from this entry. It validates an existing commitment (gates) by naming it in standard auth vocabulary, which makes the commitment legible to readers coming from a security background.

**Layer note**: app-layer primary, with limited but real harness-layer applications (verification gates as BFT, local secrets store as near-term unlock).

---

## Data governance, compliance, and privacy

Eight dimensions of data governance — the discipline of managing data lifecycle, access, retention, and regulatory obligations. **Heavily app-layer primary**: servicegrid handles real customer data (small contractors and their customers' jobs, schedules, payment info), which puts it squarely in the regulated-data world. The harness has limited governance concerns within an org but needs the vocabulary for app-layer designs.

This is a **choice-layer entry** with strong legal and operational consequences. Most decisions here are downstream of the data being handled and the jurisdictions it crosses. The architectural value is naming the option space, the canonical regulations, and the disciplines that prevent the most common failures.

### 1. Personally identifiable information (PII) and sensitive data classification

| Category | Examples | Treatment |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct identifiers (PII)** | Name, email, phone, government ID, SSN | Encrypted at rest, access-controlled, audited, deletable |
| **Indirect identifiers (quasi-PII)** | IP address, device ID, browser fingerprint, ZIP code + DOB + gender (re-identifiable in combination) | Same as PII when combined; treated as PII by GDPR |
| **Sensitive personal data** | Health, biometric, sexual orientation, political views, religion, race | Higher protection; GDPR Article 9 special categories |
| **Financial data** | Credit card numbers, bank accounts, payment history | PCI-DSS scope (see Payment systems entry); minimize handling |
| **Medical data** | Diagnoses, prescriptions, medical history | HIPAA scope (US); strict access controls |
| **Customer business data** | Contracts, customer lists, internal documents | App-specific; usually not legally regulated but business-critical |
| **Operational data** | Logs, traces, metrics, errors | Often contains incidental PII; needs the same treatment by default |
| **Anonymized data** | Aggregated counts, irreversibly hashed identifiers | Lower protection if truly anonymized; re-identification risk is real |
| **Pseudonymized data** | Replaced identifiers with stable pseudonyms | GDPR considers this still personal data, just lower risk |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Data classification is the discipline that determines everything downstream — encryption, access control, retention, deletion rights, audit obligations.** The most common production failure is treating operational data (logs, traces, error reports) as non-sensitive when in practice they contain incidental PII (IP addresses, user IDs, sometimes full request payloads). **The architectural commitment for any app handling real users: classify every data category at design time, treat operational data as PII by default, and apply the same protections.** Servicegrid will handle direct PII (contractor info, customer info), financial data (job pricing, payment info), and operational data — all three categories need the full discipline.

### 2. GDPR, CCPA, and other regulations

| Regulation | Scope | Key obligations |
|---|---|---|
| **GDPR (EU)** | Any service processing EU residents' data | Lawful basis, data subject rights (access, deletion, portability, rectification), 72-hour breach notification, DPO for high-risk processing, DPIAs for new features |
| **CCPA / CPRA (California)** | Businesses meeting threshold serving California residents | "Do not sell my data," right to know, right to delete, right to correct, opt-out for sensitive PI |
| **HIPAA (US health)** | Health data and covered entities | BAAs with vendors, security safeguards, breach notification, minimum necessary disclosure |
| **PCI-DSS** | Payment card data (see Payment systems entry) | Scope reduction, encryption, access controls, regular audits |
| **SOX** | Financial reporting integrity for public companies | Audit trails, change controls, separation of duties |
| **PIPEDA (Canada)** | Commercial activity in Canada | Consent, accountability, similar to GDPR but lighter |
| **LGPD (Brazil)** | Brazilian residents' data | GDPR-shaped but Brazil-specific |
| **State-by-state US laws** | Various US states (Colorado, Virginia, Connecticut, etc.) | Mostly GDPR-shaped |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The architectural commitment for any app each org ships: design for the strictest regulation in scope, even if not all users are in that jurisdiction.** GDPR is usually the strictest baseline for SaaS apps, so designing GDPR-compliant defaults handles most other regulations as a side effect. **Servicegrid is interesting** because the *direct* customers (small contractors) may not be in GDPR jurisdictions, but the contractors' customers (whose data servicegrid stores indirectly) may be — which puts servicegrid in scope as a *data processor* even if not as a *data controller*. **The architecture should make GDPR-shaped commitments by default**: lawful basis tracking, data subject rights endpoints, breach notification readiness, DPIAs for new features, data retention policies. None of these are expensive to build in from day one and all are expensive to retrofit.

### 3. Data subject rights and the deletion problem

| Right | Mechanism | Architectural challenge |
|---|---|---|
| **Right to access** | Export all data we hold about this user | Requires knowing what data exists and where |
| **Right to deletion (right to be forgotten)** | Delete all data we hold about this user | **The hardest one — see below** |
| **Right to rectification** | Correct inaccurate data | Requires user-facing edit flows + propagation to derived data |
| **Right to portability** | Export in machine-readable format | JSON/CSV export; usually trivial if data model is clean |
| **Right to restrict processing** | Stop processing this user's data without deleting | State flag + downstream filters |
| **Right to object** | Opt out of specific processing types | Granular consent flags |
| **Right to not be subject to automated decisions** | Human review of significant automated decisions | Required for credit decisions, hiring, etc. |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The right to deletion is the hardest data subject right to implement, and event-sourced architectures make it especially hard because events are immutable by design.** When a user requests deletion, you need to delete them from the projection layer (current state), but the historical event log still contains their data. **There are several approaches**:

1. **Crypto-shredding** — encrypt PII fields with per-user keys; on deletion, destroy the key. The events still exist but the PII is unreadable. This is the cleanest approach for event-sourced systems and is what most modern compliant systems use.
2. **Tombstone events** — emit a "user deleted" event; consumers respect it by removing the user from projections and refusing to expose past events. Events still exist but are masked.
3. **Genuine deletion** — actually delete the events from the log. Breaks immutability and audit guarantees; usually unacceptable.
4. **Partition-per-user** — each user has their own event log partition that can be physically deleted. Works for low-cardinality user counts; doesn't scale to millions of users.

**Crypto-shredding is the right default for the orchestrator's event log architecture if any app each org ships needs to delete user data.** It preserves the audit trail and immutability guarantees while making the PII genuinely unreadable. **The architectural commitment**: PII fields in events should be encrypted with per-user keys from day one, even if deletion isn't required immediately, because adding crypto-shredding retroactively requires re-encrypting historical data. **This is a real architectural decision the dream state should make for any app handling user data**.

### 4. Consent management

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit | App fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lawful basis tracking** | Per-user, per-purpose flag for why you're processing their data | GDPR Article 6 | Required for GDPR; useful even outside GDPR |
| **Granular consent** | User opts in to specific processing types separately | Cookie banners, preference centers | Required for marketing, analytics, third-party sharing |
| **Withdrawal of consent** | User can revoke consent at any time | GDPR right | Must propagate to all downstream processors |
| **Consent receipts** | Auditable record of what the user consented to and when | Compliance evidence | Useful for any app handling sensitive data |
| **Default opt-out vs opt-in** | The default position when user doesn't choose | Opt-in is the legal default in GDPR; opt-out is common in US | Choose based on jurisdiction and ethics |
| **Cookie consent** | Banner asking for cookie consent | Required by ePrivacy Directive in EU | Required for any EU traffic |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Consent management is mostly a UX problem with an architectural shadow.** The architectural shadow is "every user has a consent state that needs to be readable from anywhere processing their data, and changes need to propagate immediately." **The right pattern is a consent table indexed by user and purpose, with a cache layer for fast read access from the data processing path.** Consent changes are events in the canonical event log; consumers respect the latest consent state. **For servicegrid specifically**: the data subjects are the contractors AND their customers, and both may need consent flows. The contractors consent at signup; the contractors' customers consent... when? This is a real architectural decision that depends on the user flows servicegrid offers. **Worth flagging as an open question for the servicegrid app design.**

### 5. Audit trails and access logging

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit | App fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Access logs** | Who accessed what, when | Compliance, security forensics | Required for any app handling sensitive data |
| **Change logs** | Who changed what, when, what the change was | Compliance, debugging | Required for SOX, HIPAA |
| **Audit log immutability** | Audit logs cannot be modified or deleted | Strong compliance requirement | Append-only event log handles this naturally |
| **Audit log retention** | How long to keep audit logs | Per-regulation; often 6-7 years | Long retention is the default for high-stakes events |
| **Audit log access controls** | Only specific roles can read audit logs | Separation of duties | Audit log readers are often separate from operational readers |
| **Tamper evidence** | Cryptographic chains (hash chains, Merkle trees) | High-assurance environments | Useful for non-repudiation |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Audit trails are essentially free under event sourcing** (already named in Event streaming §7 and WAL §7) — every state change is automatically recorded, immutable, and replayable. **The orchestrator's event log doubles as an audit log without additional infrastructure.** The remaining work is at the consumer layer: surfacing audit views, controlling who can read them, configuring retention policies per event class. **For servicegrid specifically, this is one of the architectural advantages of going event-sourced from day one** — the compliance audit story is "we have an immutable log of every state change, queryable by user, time range, or operation type." That's the answer regulators want and most production systems have to retrofit painfully. **The dream state should name this as one of event sourcing's load-bearing benefits for app-layer compliance**: it's not just a persistence pattern, it's a compliance story.

### 6. Data residency and sovereignty

| Concern | Mechanism | Standard fit | App fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Geographic data residency** | Data physically stored in a specific region | GDPR encourages EU data in EU; some countries require it | Increasingly important for SaaS |
| **Cross-border transfers** | Moving data across jurisdictions | GDPR Schrems II requires safeguards (SCCs, adequacy decisions) | Compliance burden for any cross-region SaaS |
| **Sovereign cloud** | Cloud providers offering jurisdictional isolation (AWS GovCloud, Azure Sovereign) | Government, regulated industries | Specialized; expensive |
| **On-prem / self-hosted option** | Customers can run the software in their own infrastructure | Enterprise sales | Adds operational burden but unlocks regulated customers |
| **Data localization laws** | Some jurisdictions require local data storage (China, Russia, India) | Country-specific | Restricts where SaaS can serve |
| **Multi-region deployment** | App runs in multiple regions, each with its own data store | Global SaaS scaling | Adds complexity but enables data residency compliance |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Data residency is increasingly a hard constraint for SaaS apps**, and the architectural pattern is "per-region data stores with no cross-region replication of user data, just metadata." **For the orchestrator's apps**, this matters most for any app that goes international or sells into regulated industries. Servicegrid serving small contractors in the US is single-region by default; servicegrid serving European contractors would need EU data residency. **The architectural commitment for new apps**: design the data model to be regionalizable from day one — every user record carries a region, every data store is per-region, no cross-region writes for user data. **This is much cheaper to do at design time than to retrofit.** Connects to the Sharding entry's "cosmos boundary as natural partition" thread — the same partition discipline that makes sharding clean also makes regional isolation cleanish.

### 7. Data retention and deletion policies

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit | App fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Retention period** | How long to keep data | Per-data-class policy | Required for compliance; required by sleep test for high-stakes events |
| **Automated deletion** | Scheduled jobs that delete data past retention | Operational discipline | Required; manual deletion doesn't scale |
| **Soft delete vs hard delete** | Mark as deleted vs actually delete | Recovery vs compliance tradeoff | Soft delete is risky for GDPR (data still exists) |
| **Data minimization** | Collect only what you need | GDPR principle | Architectural discipline, not just policy |
| **Purpose limitation** | Use data only for stated purposes | GDPR principle | Reflected in consent management |
| **Storage limitation** | Don't keep data longer than needed | GDPR principle | Reflected in retention policies |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Retention policy needs to be classified per data type, not system-wide.** Some data is high-stakes and needs to be kept forever (audit logs for SOX compliance: 7 years; medical records for HIPAA: varies by state). Other data should be deleted aggressively (raw API request payloads, debug logs, temporary upload files). **The right pattern is a per-event-class retention policy table** with the default being conservative (keep) and exceptions being explicit (delete after N days). **This composes with the high-stakes-vs-low-stakes split** already named in Event streaming §5 retention. **The architectural commitment**: every event class in the canonical log has a retention policy declared at schema time, and the retention enforcement runs as a background job that compacts the log (or marks events for crypto-shredding). **For app-layer apps**: data minimization at collection time is more powerful than retention at deletion time — the data you don't collect is the data you can't accidentally leak.

### 8. The harness layer's governance posture

| Concern | Standard answer | Harness answer |
|---|---|---|
| **PII** | Encrypt, control access, audit, allow deletion | The navigator IS the only user; their own data is theirs |
| **Compliance** | GDPR / CCPA / HIPAA / etc. | N/A for the navigator's own data; relevant when crossing into apps |
| **Audit trail** | Immutable log of access and changes | Event log already provides this for the navigator's own work |
| **Cross-org data sharing** | Federation, contracts, lawful basis | **Anti-pattern** — orgs are sovereign and don't share user data |
| **Backup and DR for compliance** | Regular tested backups | See Backup and DR entry |
| **Right to deletion (for the navigator's own data)** | N/A — the navigator owns it | The navigator can delete their own org state at any time |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The harness layer doesn't have meaningful data governance obligations because the navigator IS the data subject** for everything in their own org. There's no second party whose data the navigator is processing. **The governance question becomes load-bearing the moment an app each org ships starts processing other people's data**, which is the case for servicegrid (contractors' customers' data), pilgrimage (players' game data), dreamtime (whatever autonomous service users it serves). **The architectural commitment**: the harness layer is governance-free because the cosmos boundary is the data sovereignty boundary; the app layer inherits the full standard production governance burden. **No partial coverage in between** — either you're processing your own data (harness) or you're processing other people's data (app), and the legal obligations are radically different.

The one harness-level governance concern worth naming: **the navigator's interactions with external services (LLM provider, MCP servers, web fetches) may transmit data the navigator is processing on behalf of an org's users**. If servicegrid uses LLM clones to draft customer-facing messages, the customer data passes through the LLM provider. **This requires the navigator to ensure the LLM provider is GDPR-compliant** (Anthropic publishes a DPA) and that the data sent to the LLM is appropriate for that processing context. **This is a real harness-level governance concern that should be named in the dream state**: the navigator is responsible for ensuring all external services the harness uses meet the compliance requirements of any app that's funneling data through the harness.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **Treat operational data as PII by default** (from §1). Logs, traces, error reports contain incidental PII; same protections should apply unless explicitly classified otherwise. The most common compliance failure mode.
2. **Design for the strictest regulation in scope, even if not all users are subject to it** (from §2). GDPR-shaped defaults handle most other regulations as a side effect. Cheap at design time, expensive to retrofit.
3. **Crypto-shredding is the right default for event-sourced architectures handling user data** (from §3). PII fields encrypted with per-user keys; deletion = destroy the key. Preserves immutability and audit while making PII genuinely unreadable.
4. **Audit trails are essentially free under event sourcing** (from §5). The orchestrator's event log doubles as an audit log. This is one of event sourcing's load-bearing app-layer compliance benefits and should be named in the dream state.
5. **Design data models to be regionalizable from day one** (from §6). Per-user region, per-region data store, no cross-region writes for user data. Connects to the cosmos-boundary-as-partition thread.
6. **Per-event-class retention policies declared at schema time** (from §7). Composes with the high-stakes-vs-low-stakes retention split already in Event streaming §5.
7. **Data minimization at collection time > retention at deletion time** (from §7). The data you don't collect is the data you can't leak.
8. **The navigator is responsible for ensuring external services meet compliance requirements of apps funneling data through the harness** (from §8). LLM provider DPA, MCP server data handling, web fetch destinations. A real harness-level concern that should be named.

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This is the second app-layer-primary entry** after Authentication, and it reinforces the pattern: the harness section is small (1 of 8 dimensions), the bulk is option space and recommendations for the apps each org ships. The most generative parts are §3 (crypto-shredding for event-sourced deletion) and §5 (event log as audit log for free) — both validate the event sourcing resolution by surfacing compliance benefits that traditional architectures have to retrofit.

**The most load-bearing app-layer design decision this entry surfaces** is **crypto-shredding as the default PII storage pattern**. Because servicegrid will handle real customer data and likely needs to support GDPR-style deletion eventually, the architectural commitment should be made up front — encrypt PII fields with per-user keys from day one, even before deletion is required, because retrofitting crypto-shredding to existing data is expensive.

**Layer note**: app-layer primary, with one meaningful harness-layer concern (external service compliance for data funneled through the harness).

---

## Payment and billing systems

Eight dimensions of payment and billing systems — the discipline of handling real money in production. **Heavily app-layer primary**: servicegrid is the live load-bearing case (small contractors are paying customers, the platform handles their billing relationships with their own customers, and revenue collection is core to the business). The harness has no payment surface.

This is a **choice-layer entry** with severe consequences for getting it wrong. Money is one of the few system design domains where a small bug can result in immediate, unrecoverable financial loss or regulatory action. The architectural value is naming the canonical patterns and the disciplines that prevent the most common payment failures.

### 1. The payment landscape — providers and abstractions

| Layer | Examples | What it does |
|---|---|---|
| **Payment processor** | Stripe, Adyen, Braintree, Square | Connect merchants to card networks, handle PCI scope, expose APIs |
| **Card networks** | Visa, Mastercard, Amex, Discover | Route authorization requests, settle funds, set rules |
| **Issuing banks** | The cards' banks | Approve/decline charges, manage chargebacks |
| **Acquiring banks** | The merchant's bank | Receive funds, handle settlement |
| **Alternative payment methods** | PayPal, Apple Pay, Google Pay, ACH, SEPA, Klarna | Non-card rails |
| **Marketplace / Connect platforms** | Stripe Connect, Adyen for Platforms | Multi-party payments (platform + sellers + buyers) |
| **Subscription managers** | Stripe Billing, Chargebee, Recurly, Paddle | Recurring billing on top of payment processors |
| **Tax engines** | Stripe Tax, TaxJar, Avalara | Sales tax / VAT calculation and remittance |
| **Crypto rails** | On-chain transfers, custodial wallets | Specialized; usually overkill for SaaS |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **For the apps each org ships, use a managed payment processor (Stripe is the default) rather than implementing card processing directly.** Implementing PCI-DSS compliance from scratch is enormously expensive and risky; managed processors absorb the compliance scope and provide well-tested SDKs. **Servicegrid specifically is a marketplace/platform pattern** — the platform takes payments on behalf of contractors who serve their own customers — and **Stripe Connect (or Adyen for Platforms) is the right primitive for this.** The architectural commitment: **payment processor by default; the apps don't touch raw card data.** The processor handles tokenization, the app handles tokens.

### 2. PCI-DSS scope minimization

| PCI level | Triggered by | Compliance burden |
|---|---|---|
| **Level 1** | >6M transactions/year | Annual on-site audit, quarterly scans, penetration tests |
| **Level 2** | 1-6M transactions/year | Annual self-assessment, quarterly scans |
| **Level 3** | 20K-1M e-commerce transactions/year | Annual self-assessment |
| **Level 4** | <20K e-commerce or <1M total | Annual self-assessment, quarterly scans |
| **SAQ-A** | App never sees card data (uses Stripe Elements / hosted fields) | Lightest |
| **SAQ-A-EP** | App processes card data via JavaScript that includes scripts from outside PCI scope | Slightly heavier |
| **SAQ-D** | App stores, processes, or transmits card data directly | Heaviest |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The architectural commitment for any app handling payments: minimize PCI scope to SAQ-A by using hosted payment fields (Stripe Elements, Checkout) or redirect-based flows.** The app never sees card data; the user enters it directly into the processor's iframe; the processor returns a token that the app stores and uses. **This reduces the PCI compliance burden from "annual audit + quarterly scans + pen tests" to "annual self-assessment questionnaire."** The cost difference is staggering — SAQ-A is hours of paperwork; SAQ-D is hundreds of thousands of dollars per year in audit and tooling costs. **Never store raw card data unless you have an extremely strong business reason and the budget for full compliance.** Servicegrid should be SAQ-A from day one.

### 3. Idempotency in payment flows

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit | Payment fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Idempotency keys** | Client-generated unique key per logical operation; server deduplicates | Stripe-style API design | **Required for all payment operations** |
| **Operation logs** | Immutable record of every payment attempt | Auditing, dispute resolution | Required for chargebacks |
| **Replay-safe operations** | Calling twice has the same effect as calling once | Distributed systems baseline | Required to survive retries from network failures |
| **Network failure recovery** | "Did the charge succeed before the network died?" | Common production case | Idempotency keys make this safe |
| **Duplicate webhook delivery** | Payment processors retry webhooks until acknowledged | Standard | Webhook handlers must be idempotent too |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Idempotency is not optional in payment systems — it's the difference between "we charged the customer twice" and "we didn't."** Every payment operation must have an idempotency key; every webhook handler must be idempotent; every retry must be safe. **The idempotency thread from Transactions §8 and Resilience patterns §1 applies with extra force here**: in normal systems, an idempotency bug causes duplicate emails or duplicate database rows; in payment systems, it causes duplicate charges that customers will (rightfully) dispute. **Architectural commitment for any payment-handling code path: idempotency key required, idempotency window long enough to survive network failures (24 hours minimum), idempotency keys persisted before the upstream call completes.**

### 4. The ledger pattern

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Double-entry bookkeeping** | Every transaction debits one account and credits another; books always balance | Accounting since 1494 | The mathematical guarantee that money isn't created or destroyed |
| **Append-only ledger** | Transactions are immutable; corrections are reversal entries | Standard accounting | Audit trail, regulatory compliance |
| **Account balance as projection** | Balance is computed by summing all transactions, never stored as the source of truth | Event-sourced accounting | Avoids state-vs-event drift |
| **Reconciliation** | Periodically verify ledger against external sources (bank statements, processor reports) | Operational discipline | Catches bugs before they compound |
| **Multi-currency** | Each account has a currency; cross-currency transactions need explicit FX records | International commerce | Required for any non-US-only app |
| **Deferred revenue / accruals** | Recognize revenue when earned, not when cash is received | GAAP / IFRS accounting | Required for SaaS revenue recognition |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The ledger pattern is the architectural foundation for any system handling money, and it's structurally event-sourced — every transaction is an immutable event, every balance is a projection.** This is one of the cleanest cases where the orchestrator's existing event log resolution (Event streaming §1-§8) directly applies to an app-layer pattern: **the same primitives that store the orchestrator's events store the financial transactions of the apps each org ships, with the same guarantees (immutability, replay, audit, time travel).** **For servicegrid specifically**: every job, every payment, every refund, every payout to the contractor should be a ledger entry in an event-sourced ledger. The contractor's "balance" is computed by summing entries, never stored. Reconciliation jobs verify the ledger against Stripe's records nightly. **The architectural commitment: all financial state lives in an append-only ledger with double-entry semantics; balances are projections; corrections are new entries (reversals), never edits.** This is the same commitment the orchestrator makes for its own state, applied to money.

### 5. Subscription lifecycle and dunning

| Stage | Mechanism | Edge cases |
|---|---|---|
| **Trial** | Free period, often credit card optional | Trial-to-paid conversion, trial extensions, trial abuse (multiple trials per user) |
| **Active** | Recurring charge succeeds | Plan changes, prorations, add-ons |
| **Past due** | Charge failed, customer notified | Dunning emails, retry strategy |
| **Canceled** | Customer or system canceled | Reactivation, data retention, refund handling |
| **Paused** | Customer pauses but doesn't cancel | Pause limits, billing implications |
| **Refunded** | Money returned | Partial refunds, refund disputes, reversal of revenue recognition |
| **Chargeback** | Customer disputes the charge with their bank | Evidence collection, chargeback rate monitoring, fee handling |
| **Plan changes** | Upgrade or downgrade | Proration math, immediate vs scheduled |
| **Prorations** | Charging only for time used in a billing period | Easy to get wrong; let the processor handle it |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Subscription lifecycle is more complex than most teams expect, and the right answer is "use a subscription manager like Stripe Billing rather than implementing the lifecycle from scratch."** The edge cases (proration math, plan change timing, refund tax handling, dunning retry logic, trial abuse defense) are individually straightforward but collectively a year of engineering work. **Stripe Billing handles all of this correctly out of the box.** **Architectural commitment for any subscription-based app: managed subscription manager by default.** Custom billing logic is anti-pattern unless the business model is genuinely unusual.

**Dunning specifically** — the process of recovering failed payments — is one of the most underrated revenue levers in SaaS. A typical SaaS loses 5-10% of revenue to involuntary churn (failed cards, expired cards). **Smart dunning** (retry on optimal day-of-month, send recovery emails, offer card update flows, escalate to manual outreach for high-value accounts) can recover most of that revenue. **Stripe Smart Retries, ChargifyKB Recovery, Recurly Revenue Recovery do this; Stripe is the default.**

### 6. Marketplace / multi-party payments (Stripe Connect pattern)

| Concept | Mechanism | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct charges** | Customer pays seller directly; platform takes a fee | Sellers handle their own PCI scope and disputes |
| **Destination charges** | Platform charges customer; routes funds to seller | Platform owns the customer relationship |
| **Separate charges and transfers** | Platform charges customer; transfers money to seller as separate operations | Maximum flexibility |
| **Connected accounts** | Sellers create their own (sub-)accounts with the processor | Required for marketplaces |
| **Onboarding (KYC)** | Sellers complete identity verification before they can receive payouts | Required by regulations |
| **Payouts** | Money moves from platform balance to seller bank account | Daily, weekly, on-demand |
| **Fees** | Platform takes a percentage of each transaction | The marketplace's revenue |
| **Refunds across parties** | Refund from customer triggers reversal of fees and payouts | Easy to get wrong |
| **Tax handling** | Sales tax, VAT, GST on cross-party transactions | Often the responsibility of the seller, but the platform may be liable |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Servicegrid is structurally a marketplace pattern** — the platform sits between contractors (sellers of services) and the contractors' customers (buyers). **Stripe Connect is the canonical implementation** of this pattern and provides all the primitives: connected accounts for contractors, KYC onboarding flows, charge-and-transfer operations, fee handling, payout scheduling, refund propagation. **Architectural commitment for servicegrid: Stripe Connect from day one with destination charges or separate charges-and-transfers** (depending on whether servicegrid wants to own the customer relationship or let contractors own it). **The marketplace pattern has known footguns** that any custom implementation will hit: cross-party refunds, fee reversals on disputes, onboarding state management, payout timing, multi-currency settlement. Stripe Connect handles all of these correctly; rolling your own is years of work.

### 7. Tax, compliance, and international concerns

| Concern | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Sales tax (US)** | Per-state rates, nexus thresholds, exemption certificates | Stripe Tax, TaxJar, Avalara automate |
| **VAT (EU)** | Per-country rates, reverse charge for B2B, OSS scheme | Same automation tools |
| **GST (other countries)** | Per-country rates | Same |
| **Tax IDs and exemptions** | B2B customers may be tax-exempt; need to validate VAT IDs | Stripe Tax handles validation |
| **Withholding tax** | Some jurisdictions require platforms to withhold taxes from sellers | Increasingly common |
| **AML / KYC** | Anti-money-laundering and know-your-customer requirements | Stripe Connect / Adyen handle |
| **Sanctions screening** | Blocking transactions with sanctioned individuals or countries | Required by OFAC, EU sanctions, etc. |
| **1099 reporting (US)** | Marketplaces issue 1099s to sellers earning over thresholds | Stripe Connect 1099 reporting |
| **PSD2 (EU)** | Strong Customer Authentication for EU payments | 3D Secure flows |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Tax and international compliance is a domain where the regulations change faster than any reasonable team can track, and the right architectural commitment is "use a compliance engine, not a custom implementation."** Stripe Tax + Stripe Connect handle most of this for any SaaS app each org ships. **The architectural value is knowing this is hard and not trying to be clever about it.** Specifically: **don't implement sales tax calculation, don't implement VAT validation, don't implement 1099 generation, don't implement sanctions screening yourself.** Use the tooling. Save the engineering effort for things that differentiate the product.

### 8. Failure modes and operational concerns

| Failure | Mechanism | Defense |
|---|---|---|
| **Duplicate charges** | Network retry causes double charge | Idempotency keys |
| **Lost charges** | Charge succeeded but app didn't record it | Reconciliation against processor records |
| **Reconciliation drift** | Internal ledger disagrees with processor ledger | Daily reconciliation jobs, alerts on discrepancies |
| **Webhook misses** | Processor retried webhooks but they all failed | Periodic polling as backup; manual recovery flows |
| **Race conditions in subscriptions** | Subscription state updated while a charge is in flight | Optimistic locking on subscription version, idempotency keys |
| **Refund failures** | Refund initiated but failed at the processor | Retry with backoff, fall back to manual process |
| **Chargeback fraud** | Customer disputes a legitimate charge to get free service | Evidence collection, chargeback rate monitoring, account flags |
| **Currency conversion errors** | FX rates change between auth and capture | Authorize in customer currency, capture quickly, accept FX risk explicitly |
| **Test mode contamination** | Test transactions in production data | Strict separation of test and live keys; environment checks in code |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Reconciliation is the single most important operational discipline in payment systems and the most commonly skipped.** The discipline: **every day, compare the internal ledger against the payment processor's records; alert on any discrepancy.** Most production payment systems run for years with quiet reconciliation drift before someone notices and discovers the system has been losing or double-counting money. **Architectural commitment for any payment-handling app: reconciliation job from day one, with alerting on discrepancies above a threshold, and a manual recovery flow for the discrepancies that do occur.**

The other underrated discipline is **chargeback rate monitoring**. Card networks impose penalties on merchants whose chargeback rate exceeds 1% (and severe penalties at 1.5%); processors will close the merchant account at higher rates. **The architecture should track chargeback rate per customer cohort, per product, per geography**, and alert when any segment is approaching the threshold. Smart marketplaces dispute chargebacks aggressively and screen high-risk transactions before charging.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **Managed payment processor by default; apps don't touch raw card data** (from §1, §2). Stripe is the default; PCI scope minimized to SAQ-A via hosted fields. The cost difference between SAQ-A and SAQ-D is staggering and the right architectural commitment is to never leave SAQ-A.
2. **Idempotency is non-negotiable for every payment operation** (from §3). Stronger than the general idempotency thread because the cost of getting it wrong is duplicate charges, not duplicate emails.
3. **The ledger pattern is the architectural foundation for any system handling money, and it's structurally event-sourced** (from §4). Same primitives as the orchestrator's event log resolution; balances are projections; corrections are reversal entries.
4. **Managed subscription manager (Stripe Billing) for any subscription app** (from §5). Custom billing logic is anti-pattern unless the business model is genuinely unusual. Subscription lifecycle has more edge cases than teams expect.
5. **Smart dunning recovers most involuntary churn** (from §5). Failed-payment recovery is the most underrated SaaS revenue lever; managed processors handle it well.
6. **Stripe Connect for servicegrid's marketplace pattern** (from §6). Platform takes payments on behalf of contractors who serve their own customers. This is the canonical marketplace pattern and Stripe Connect is the canonical implementation.
7. **Use compliance engines (Stripe Tax) for tax and international concerns** (from §7). Don't implement sales tax, VAT, 1099 generation, or sanctions screening yourself. Save engineering for things that differentiate the product.
8. **Reconciliation from day one, with alerts on discrepancies** (from §8). The most important operational discipline and the most commonly skipped. Internal ledger vs processor ledger, daily comparison, alerts on drift.

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This is the most app-layer-specific entry in the doc** — the harness has zero payment surface and the entire walk is about apps each org ships. **The most generative connection to existing architecture is §4 (the ledger pattern as event sourcing applied to money)** — it's another validation that the event log resolution generalizes to app-layer concerns, not just harness concerns. The same primitives that make the orchestrator's audit story free also make the apps' financial accounting story clean.

**The most load-bearing servicegrid-specific recommendation** is **Stripe Connect for the marketplace pattern, with crypto-shredded PII (from the Data governance entry), event-sourced ledger, daily reconciliation, and Stripe Tax for compliance.** That's roughly the entire payment architecture for servicegrid — and almost all of it is "use the managed primitives, don't roll your own."

**Layer note**: app-layer only. The harness has no payment surface; this entry exists for the navigator's app-layer design vocabulary.

---

## ML systems infrastructure

Eight dimensions of machine learning systems infrastructure — the platform layer that supports training, serving, monitoring, and improving ML models in production. **This entry is complementary to `REFERENCE-scaling-laws.md`**: scaling-laws covers ML capability scaling (the why and how-far of model size, data, compute); this entry covers the system design view of ML infrastructure (the engineering substrate that runs the models). Both apply at both the harness and app layers.

This is a **choice-layer entry** for any system that uses ML in production. The orchestrator uses ML extensively at the harness level (LLM calls, calibration, scoring, leverage estimation are all ML-adjacent), and the apps each org ships will likely use ML for app-specific features (servicegrid scheduling optimization, predictive maintenance; pilgrimage matchmaking; dreamtime is plausibly ML-driven end to end). The architectural value is naming the canonical ML infrastructure components and where the orchestrator already has them, where it needs them, and where the boundary lives.

### 1. Model lifecycle stages

| Stage | What happens | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Data collection** | Training data ingested, labeled, quality-checked | Upstream of training |
| **Feature engineering** | Raw data transformed into model inputs | Where most of the work lives |
| **Training** | Model parameters fit to data | Compute-intensive, often offline |
| **Validation** | Model performance evaluated on held-out data | Before deploying anywhere |
| **Model registry** | Trained model stored with metadata, versioned | Central catalog |
| **Deployment** | Model loaded into a serving environment | Online (real-time), batch (offline), or streaming |
| **Serving** | Inference on incoming requests | Where production traffic hits |
| **Monitoring** | Track model performance, drift, errors in production | Continuous |
| **Retraining** | Model updated based on new data and observed drift | Periodic or triggered |
| **Rollback** | Revert to previous model version on regression | Required defensive capability |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The model lifecycle is structurally a saga over many independent stages**, and the orchestrator's existing saga + event log primitives apply directly. **Each stage is an event in the canonical log** (training started, training completed with metrics, model registered with version, model deployed to environment, model serving traffic, monitoring detected drift, retraining triggered, model rolled back). **The model registry is a projection of these events** — current state of each model, version history, deployment status. **For the orchestrator's harness ML usage** (LLM calls, calibration, scoring), the lifecycle is partially externalized to Anthropic (Anthropic owns training, validation, model registry for the LLMs); the orchestrator owns deployment-time choice (which model tier), monitoring (cost, latency, output quality), and the equivalent of "retraining" via prompt iteration. **For the apps each org ships using ML**, the lifecycle is fully internal and the saga model gives them the same audit + replay + recovery properties the orchestrator has for everything else.

### 2. Feature stores

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Online feature store** | Low-latency lookup of features at serving time (Redis, DynamoDB, Cassandra) | Real-time inference |
| **Offline feature store** | Historical features for training, point-in-time correct (Parquet, Delta, Iceberg) | Training and backfills |
| **Online-offline parity** | Same feature definition computed the same way in both stores | Critical for model accuracy |
