| **Feature engineering pipelines** | Code that computes features from raw data | Maintained once, used in both stores |
| **Feature versioning** | Feature definitions versioned like code | Required for reproducibility |
| **Feature monitoring** | Track feature distributions for drift | Catches input drift before model drift |
| **Point-in-time correctness** | Features used for training reflect what was known at the time of the prediction | Prevents data leakage |
| **Vendor solutions** | Tecton, Feast, Hopsworks, AWS SageMaker Feature Store, Vertex AI Feature Store | Managed feature stores |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Feature stores solve a problem most ML teams discover painfully**: training-serving skew. The model is trained on features computed one way (offline, batch, on historical data) but served on features computed differently (online, real-time, on live data) — and the divergence causes the model to perform worse in production than in validation. **Feature stores enforce online-offline parity** by making feature computation a single pipeline that writes to both stores. **For the apps each org ships**: if any app trains its own ML models on user behavior, a feature store is the right primitive even if it's just a thin wrapper around a database. **Feast (open-source) is the lightest option**; managed feature stores (Tecton, Hopsworks) are heavier but more featureful. **The architectural commitment**: any ML feature should be defined once and used in both training and serving via the same code path. Don't rewrite feature logic between training and production.

### 3. Model serving patterns

| Pattern | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Online serving (real-time)** | Synchronous prediction per request | Recommendation, fraud, ranking, ad targeting |
| **Batch serving** | Predictions computed offline, stored, looked up at serving time | Email campaigns, daily reports, slow-changing predictions |
| **Streaming serving** | Predictions computed on streaming events as they arrive | Real-time anomaly detection, IoT |
| **Edge serving** | Model runs on user device or edge node | Mobile inference, low-latency, offline |
| **Self-hosted** | Model runs on your own infrastructure | Sovereignty, latency, cost control |
| **Managed inference** | Cloud provider hosts the model (Vertex AI, SageMaker, Hugging Face Inference) | Operational simplicity |
| **API-based (LLM providers)** | Inference via external API (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) | The orchestrator's current pattern |
| **vLLM, TensorRT, Triton** | High-throughput serving frameworks | Self-hosted high-volume |
| **ONNX Runtime, ggml/llama.cpp** | Cross-framework inference | Edge deployment |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The orchestrator is currently in the API-based serving pattern for its primary models (LLMs via Anthropic).** This is the right call today — the operational complexity of self-hosting is not worth the savings at the orchestrator's scale, and Anthropic's models are the best available. **The deferred trajectory toward self-hosting** is named in the dream state as the sovereign model registry / vLLM thread. **For the apps each org ships using ML**: the right serving pattern depends on the workload. Real-time apps need online serving; analytics apps can use batch serving; mobile apps may want edge serving. **vLLM is the right primitive for any future self-hosted LLM serving** because it's 20x faster than Ollama and is the de-facto standard for high-throughput LLM inference. **The architectural commitment for the apps**: pick the serving pattern that matches the actual latency and throughput requirements; don't default to managed inference if cost or sovereignty is a concern.

### 4. Training infrastructure

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Distributed training** | Training across multiple GPUs/machines | Large models, large datasets |
| **Data parallelism** | Same model on different data shards | Most common pattern |
| **Model parallelism** | Different parts of the model on different devices | Very large models |
| **Pipeline parallelism** | Different layers on different devices, pipelined | Combined with the others |
| **Training orchestration** | Workflow tools that launch training jobs (Kubeflow, Metaflow, Flyte, Argo) | Production training pipelines |
| **Experiment tracking** | Log hyperparameters, metrics, artifacts per training run (MLflow, Weights & Biases, Comet) | Reproducibility |
| **Hyperparameter optimization** | Automated search for best hyperparameters (Optuna, Ray Tune, KerasTuner) | Model tuning |
| **GPU/TPU pools** | Shared compute resources for training | Cost efficiency |
| **Spot instances** | Cheaper preemptible compute | Cost savings; requires checkpoint discipline |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The orchestrator doesn't currently train its own models** — Anthropic trains the LLMs, and the orchestrator is the consumer. **The future trajectory** (sovereign model registry, fine-tuning on accumulated traces) would put the orchestrator into the training infrastructure space, and at that point the canonical primitives become relevant: training orchestration (Kubeflow or simpler), experiment tracking (MLflow as the open-source standard), GPU pools (probably rented from Lambda Labs or RunPod). **For the apps each org ships**: most apps don't train their own models from scratch but may fine-tune existing ones. **Fine-tuning infrastructure** is much lighter than from-scratch training and is the realistic path for app-level ML. **The architectural value**: knowing what training infrastructure exists so the future move (when the orchestrator or an app starts training its own models) is informed by canonical patterns rather than reinvented.

### 5. Model monitoring and drift detection

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Performance monitoring** | Accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUC, etc. on production data | Offline evaluation against ground truth |
| **Prediction drift** | Distribution of predictions changes over time | Catches model degradation |
| **Input drift (data drift)** | Distribution of features changes over time | Catches changes in the world |
| **Concept drift** | Relationship between features and labels changes | The hardest kind to detect |
| **Latency monitoring** | p50/p95/p99 inference latency | Standard SLO monitoring |
| **Cost monitoring** | Compute cost per prediction, per training run | The token economy framing applied to ML |
| **Bias and fairness monitoring** | Performance across demographic groups | Required for regulated decisions (credit, hiring) |
| **Adversarial monitoring** | Detect adversarial inputs or model abuse | Security |
| **Ground truth collection** | Get labels for production predictions to evaluate | The hardest operational discipline |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Model monitoring is the discipline that catches when a model has stopped working before users notice**, and it's the most commonly skipped step in ML system design. The most important kinds of drift are **input drift** (the world changed; your features look different from training) and **concept drift** (the relationship changed; the same features now mean something different). **The orchestrator already has the primitive that monitoring needs**: the event log captures every prediction and (via gates and feedback) every outcome, so drift detection is a projection over the event log. **The orchestrator's calibration system is a form of model monitoring** applied to its own scoring/leverage models. **Connects to the existing drift-detection-as-first-class-concern thread** from Indexes §4 and §8 — this is the same discipline applied to ML models. **For the apps each org ships using ML**: drift monitoring is non-negotiable; the model that worked at launch will degrade as the world changes, and you need to know before the users do.

### 6. Online vs offline evaluation

| Mode | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Offline evaluation** | Test the model on historical data with known outcomes | Pre-deployment; cheap; fast iteration |
| **Holdout sets** | Reserve some training data for unbiased evaluation | Standard practice |
| **Cross-validation** | Multiple train/test splits to estimate generalization | Smaller datasets |
| **Backtest** | Replay model on historical production data | Approximates production behavior |
| **Shadow deployment** | Run new model alongside old one; compare predictions on live traffic | No user impact, full real-world signal |
| **A/B test** | Split traffic between old and new model; measure user-facing metrics | Required for validating real-world impact (see A/B testing entry) |
| **Multi-armed bandit** | Adaptive traffic allocation based on observed performance | Faster convergence than fixed A/B |
| **Online learning** | Model updates in real time from production feedback | Specialized; risky; requires safety nets |
| **Counterfactual evaluation** | Estimate what would have happened with a different model | Logged-bandit-feedback methods |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Offline evaluation is necessary but never sufficient for ML models** — the offline metrics (accuracy on a held-out set) often don't predict online metrics (whether users actually click, convert, or come back). **Shadow deployment is the underrated primitive** that bridges them: run the new model in parallel with the old one on real traffic, log both predictions, compare. No user impact, full real-world signal, perfect ground truth for the comparison. **For any ML-using app**: shadow deployment should be the default before promoting a new model to production. **For the orchestrator's calibration system**: the equivalent of shadow deployment is "score the same execution with the new calibration AND the old calibration, log both, compare" — and the event log makes this trivial because both scores are just events on the same execution. **The architectural commitment**: every model rollout passes through shadow deployment + A/B test before becoming the default, and the rollout state itself is event-sourced so it's auditable and rollback-able.

### 7. Vector databases and embedding infrastructure

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Embedding generation** | Text/image/audio → fixed-size vector | Foundation of semantic search |
| **Vector index** | Approximate nearest neighbor (HNSW, IVF, PQ) | Fast retrieval over millions of vectors |
| **Vector databases** | Pinecone, Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus, ChromaDB, pgvector | Managed or embedded |
| **Hybrid search** | Combine dense (embedding) and sparse (BM25) retrieval | Better than either alone |
| **Re-ranking** | Cheap retrieval + expensive re-rank of top results | Quality + cost balance |
| **Embedding model selection** | Different models for different domains and languages | Important quality lever |
| **Chunking strategy** | How to split documents into embeddable units | Underrated lever; affects retrieval quality dramatically |
| **Metadata filtering** | Filter retrieval by structured metadata (date, type, user) | Standard requirement |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Vector databases are the foundation of retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and semantic search**, both of which are likely future capabilities for the orchestrator and for any app each org ships. **The orchestrator's current knowledge graph + grep approach is non-vector** — it works because the corpus is small and the retrieval is mostly graph traversal rather than semantic similarity. **As the corpus grows past where grep is comfortable**, vector retrieval becomes the right primitive (already named in Indexes §1). **For the apps**: any app that does semantic search, RAG, or retrieval over user content needs a vector store. **pgvector is the right default** if Postgres is already in the stack — it's a Postgres extension that adds vector indexing without requiring a separate service. **Qdrant is the right default** if a dedicated vector store is justified — it's open-source, single binary, and competitive on quality and speed. **Pinecone is anti-pattern for sovereignty reasons** even though it's the most polished offering. **Hybrid search (dense + sparse)** is the right quality move — pure vector search underperforms on exact-match queries; hybrid handles both.

### 8. The orchestrator's existing ML infrastructure

| Concern | Standard ML answer | Orchestrator's current state |
|---|---|---|
| **Models** | Trained in-house | LLMs from Anthropic; calibration models in-house (simple statistics) |
| **Feature store** | Tecton/Feast/Hopsworks | The event log (features = events) |
| **Training infrastructure** | Kubeflow/Metaflow/Flyte | None (Anthropic trains; orchestrator doesn't) |
| **Model registry** | MLflow / SageMaker Model Registry | None (Anthropic owns LLM versions; orchestrator references them by name) |
| **Serving** | vLLM / Triton / SageMaker | Anthropic API (managed) |
| **Monitoring** | Production drift, performance, cost | Calibration system (in-house, partial) + cost tracking via token budget |
| **Experiment tracking** | MLflow / Weights & Biases | Loop runs in the event log; calibration runs serve this purpose |
| **Vector store** | Pinecone / Qdrant / pgvector | Knowledge graph + grep (no vectors today) |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The orchestrator already has primitive versions of most ML infrastructure components, just unnamed**: the event log is the feature store, calibration runs are experiment tracking, the token budget is cost monitoring, the LLM fallback model tier is rollout control. **The architectural commitment worth making explicit**: **the orchestrator IS an ML system, and the standard ML infrastructure components apply, just at a different abstraction level than typical ML systems.** Calling these out explicitly serves two purposes: (1) the navigator can recognize when an existing primitive is the answer to an ML question rather than reinventing one, and (2) the deferred trajectory toward sovereign model serving is informed by canonical ML infrastructure patterns rather than reinvented from scratch.

The biggest gap in the current ML infrastructure is **vector retrieval** — the knowledge graph + grep approach works today but the inverted-index thread from Indexes §1 and the vector retrieval need for RAG over the event log are both surfacing this gap. **pgvector or Qdrant is the right unlock** when the corpus grows past the size where grep is comfortable.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **The model lifecycle is structurally a saga over many independent stages, and the orchestrator's existing saga + event log primitives apply directly** (from §1). Each stage is an event; the model registry is a projection.
2. **Online-offline feature parity is non-negotiable for any system that trains and serves its own models** (from §2). Define features once, use the same code path in both training and serving. Feast is the lightest open-source option.
3. **vLLM is the right primitive for any future self-hosted LLM serving** (from §3). Already named in the dream state as part of the sovereign model registry trajectory.
4. **The orchestrator's calibration system is structurally a model monitoring system applied to its own scoring/leverage models** (from §5). This connects to the drift-detection-as-first-class-concern thread from Indexes §4 and validates the existing commitment via canonical ML vocabulary.
5. **Shadow deployment is the underrated primitive for ML rollouts** (from §6). Bridges offline and online evaluation. The orchestrator's calibration system can do this for free via the event log.
6. **pgvector or Qdrant for any future vector retrieval need** (from §7). pgvector if Postgres is already in the stack; Qdrant if a dedicated store is justified. Pinecone is anti-pattern for sovereignty.
7. **Hybrid search (dense + sparse) is the right quality move** (from §7). Pure vector search underperforms on exact-match queries; hybrid handles both. Connects to the inverted-index thread from Indexes §1.
8. **The orchestrator IS an ML system; standard ML infrastructure components apply at a different abstraction level** (from §8). The event log is the feature store; calibration runs are experiment tracking; the token budget is cost monitoring; the model fallback tier is rollout control. Naming this explicitly helps the navigator recognize existing primitives instead of reinventing them.

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This entry is complementary to `REFERENCE-scaling-laws.md`** — scaling-laws covers the why and how-far of ML capability; this entry covers the system design view of the infrastructure that runs the models. Both apply at both layers and they're meant to be read together when designing anything ML-shaped.

**The most generative observation is §8 (the orchestrator IS an ML system, with primitive versions of most ML infrastructure components already in place)**. The event log as feature store, calibration runs as experiment tracking, token budget as cost monitoring, model fallback tier as rollout control — these are all unnamed but real. Naming them in canonical ML vocabulary makes the existing commitments legible to readers from an ML background and prevents the navigator from reinventing primitives that already exist.

**The most load-bearing app-layer recommendation** is **pgvector for any app needing vector retrieval** — it's the lightest path to RAG and semantic search, doesn't require a new service, and is open-source. Combined with the inverted-index thread from Indexes §1 (BM25 or similar sparse retrieval), apps can have hybrid search from day one without specialized vector infrastructure.

**Layer note**: applies to both layers. Harness uses ML extensively (LLMs, calibration, scoring); apps will use ML for app-specific features. The infrastructure components are the same; the abstraction levels differ.

---

## Backup and disaster recovery

Eight dimensions of backup and disaster recovery — the discipline of surviving the failure modes that single-system durability primitives don't cover. **Touched in Replication §6 (RTO/RPO framing) and Disk I/O §5 (fsync lying defense)** but not walked as its own topic. This entry walks it as a first-class operational discipline because the sleep test discipline applied to the substrate IS backup and disaster recovery, and the orchestrator's local-first commitment puts the navigator in the role of their own backup operator.

This is a **choice-layer entry with severe consequences for getting it wrong**. Most production systems run for months or years without exercising their backup procedures, and most discover at the worst possible moment that the backups don't actually work. The architectural value is naming the canonical patterns and the disciplines that prevent the most common failures.

### 1. Backup strategies — what gets backed up and how

| Strategy | Mechanism | Standard fit | Orchestrator fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full backup** | Complete copy of all data | Periodic baseline | Once a day or week, depending on size |
| **Incremental backup** | Only data changed since the last backup | Daily/hourly, smaller and faster | Standard pattern combined with full |
| **Differential backup** | Data changed since the last full backup | Middle ground | Less common today |
| **Snapshot-based** | Filesystem or volume snapshot | apfs, btrfs, zfs, EBS snapshots | Free if the filesystem supports it |
| **Continuous backup (CDP)** | Every change shipped immediately | Mission-critical data | Aligned with the sleep test |
| **Log-based backup** | Backup the WAL / event log; restore via replay | Event-sourced systems | **The orchestrator's natural fit** |
| **Application-consistent** | Backup is taken at a known consistent point (quiesce or snapshot) | Database backups | Required for any database with in-flight transactions |
| **Crash-consistent** | Backup represents the state if the system had crashed | Cheaper, may need recovery on restore | Acceptable when WAL replay handles consistency |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **For an event-sourced architecture, log-based backup is the natural fit and gives backup-and-restore properties for free.** Ship the event log to a backup location continuously (or at high frequency); restore by replaying the events on a fresh substrate. **This composes cleanly with the WAL entry's replication discussion** — the same primitive that handles replication also handles backup, because replication IS backup-with-zero-RPO. **For the orchestrator specifically**: the event log per org is the backup unit; the backup destination is anything that can store an append-only log durably (S3/R2/Backblaze, a second local disk, a NAS). **Architectural commitment: backup discipline is "ship the event log + the snapshot of the latest projection state to a sovereign backup location, continuously."** No separate backup tooling needed — the event log primitive does the work.

### 2. The 3-2-1 rule and backup storage

| Concept | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **3 copies** | Original + 2 backups |
| **2 different media** | Local disk + cloud, or local SSD + external HDD |
| **1 offsite** | At least one copy not in the same physical location |
| **Air-gapped** | Disconnected from the network entirely (defense against ransomware) |
| **Immutable backups** | Backup destination doesn't allow modification (object lock, WORM storage) |
| **Encrypted backups** | Backup data encrypted at rest in the destination |
| **Cross-account / cross-region** | Backup destination is administratively isolated from the source |
| **Versioned backups** | Multiple historical versions retained, not just the latest |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The 3-2-1 rule is the canonical backup discipline and the orchestrator should follow it** even at the harness layer. **Concrete instantiation for the harness**: original event log on the local SSD, backup to a second local destination (external drive or NAS), backup to a sovereign cloud destination (Backblaze B2 or Cloudflare R2 — both are cheaper than S3 and don't require AWS account). That's three copies, two media, one offsite. **Object lock / WORM storage** is the modern defense against ransomware — even if an attacker compromises the credentials, they can't delete or modify backups within the lock period. **Backblaze B2 and S3 both support object lock**; most other backup destinations do not. **Architectural commitment for any production app each org ships**: 3-2-1 with object lock on the offsite copy, encrypted in transit and at rest, with a documented restore procedure. For the harness: same rules, just at the navigator's machine scale.

### 3. RTO and RPO — what you're optimizing for

| Concept | Definition | Typical values |
|---|---|---|
| **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** | How much data you can afford to lose | Zero (continuous) to 24 hours (daily backup) |
| **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** | How long you can afford to be down | Seconds (hot standby) to days (rebuild from cold backup) |
| **MTTR (Mean Time To Recover)** | Statistical average; different from RTO target | Operationally tracked |
| **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** | How often failures happen | Statistical baseline |
| **The cost-of-RTO/RPO curve** | Lower RTO/RPO = exponentially more expensive | The hard tradeoff |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **RTO and RPO need to be deliberate architectural choices, not defaults of the backup tool.** This is already named in Replication §4 (RPO as a deliberate architectural choice) — the backup entry sharpens it with the cost framing: **achieving zero RPO (continuous backup) costs significantly more than hourly RPO, which costs more than daily, which costs more than weekly.** The architecture should pick the RPO target per data class: high-stakes events (decisions, dream-state edits, financial transactions) want zero RPO; trace events and calibration data can tolerate hours of loss. **For the orchestrator's harness**: the sleep test argues for very tight RPO on high-stakes events because the user is absent during the most critical operations and losing a night of work is exactly the failure mode the sleep test exists to prevent. **Concrete commitment: continuous backup of high-stakes events to the offsite destination; hourly backup of trace and calibration data**.

**RTO is dominated by setup time** (already named in Replication §6) rather than restoration speed. Investment in fast restore procedures doesn't lower RTO unless the new-machine setup is also automated. **The architectural move for low RTO**: make the orchestrator setup so fast and scripted that "disaster recovery" is just "restore the event log on a new machine and resume the loop where it left off." This is event sourcing's standard answer.

### 4. Restore testing — the most-skipped discipline

| Concept | Mechanism | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Restore drill** | Periodically restore from backup to verify it works | The only way to know backups work |
| **Synthetic restore** | Restore to a sandbox environment, validate state | Cheap, doesn't disrupt production |
| **Game days / chaos engineering** | Deliberately simulate failures and exercise recovery | Reveals gaps in runbooks and tooling |
| **Backup verification jobs** | Automated jobs that read backup data, replay events, compare to source | Detects corruption early |
| **The "untested backup" trap** | Backup runs every night for years; nobody ever restored from it; restore fails | The most common backup failure mode |
| **Documentation of restore procedures** | Step-by-step runbook for restore | Required because you'll be panicking when you need it |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **An untested backup is an imaginary backup.** The single most important backup discipline is **periodic restore testing** — at least quarterly, restore from backup to a sandbox environment and verify the restored state matches the source. **This is the discipline that 90% of teams skip and 100% of teams who skip it eventually regret.** **For the orchestrator's harness**: the navigator should run a restore drill at least monthly — start a fresh orchestrator instance from backup data on a clean directory or VM, replay the event log, verify the projection state matches expectations. Connects to the **`fsync` lying defense** thread from Disk I/O §5: verification beyond a single layer is the same discipline at a different level. **For the apps each org ships**: restore drills should be part of CI/CD if possible — automated restore-from-backup-to-staging, validate, tear down. Anything less and you're flying blind. **Architectural commitment: monthly restore drill at the harness layer; automated restore validation in CI for any production app**.

### 5. Point-in-time recovery (PITR)

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Snapshot + log replay** | Snapshot at time T, replay log forward to time T+N | Postgres, MySQL, modern databases |
| **Continuous archiving** | WAL shipped continuously; restore to any point | Production database default |
| **Recovery target time** | Specify the exact time to restore to | Required for "undo the bug from 2pm yesterday" |
| **Recovery target LSN/offset** | Specify the exact log position to restore to | More precise than time-based |
| **Recovery target name** | Named recovery points (savepoints) | Useful for known-good states |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Point-in-time recovery is the killer feature of WAL-based backup**, and the orchestrator gets it for free under event sourcing — already named in Event streaming §7 (time travel via event replay) and WAL §6 (recovery via replay). **The backup entry sharpens this**: PITR is not just a debugging tool, it's a disaster recovery tool. When something goes catastrophically wrong (a bug corrupts state, an automation runs amok, a ransomware attack encrypts files), the recovery procedure is "restore the event log from before the incident, replay forward to just before the bad operation, resume." **For the apps each org ships**: PITR is non-negotiable for any database holding financial or compliance data. Postgres provides PITR via continuous archiving + base backups; managed providers (Neon, Supabase) handle this for you. **The architectural commitment: event-sourced or PITR-capable substrate for any data the system can't afford to lose to a bug.**

### 6. Disaster scenarios and threat models

| Scenario | What fails | Defense |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-disk failure** | One disk dies | RAID, replication, frequent backups |
| **Single-machine failure** | One server dies | Backup to a different machine |
| **Site failure** | Entire data center fails (fire, flood, power) | Offsite backups, different geographic region |
| **Region failure** | Cloud region goes down | Multi-region backups |
| **Provider failure** | Cloud provider has account issue, billing dispute, account suspension | Cross-provider backups |
| **Ransomware** | Attacker encrypts all data and demands payment | Air-gapped or immutable backups |
| **Insider sabotage** | Authorized user maliciously deletes data | Object lock on backups, separation of duties |
| **Software bug corrupts data** | Bad code writes invalid state | PITR + restore drills |
| **Accidental deletion** | Human error deletes critical data | Soft delete + backup retention |
| **Compliance event** | Regulator demands data restoration to a specific point | PITR + retained backups |
| **Total user loss** | The user's machine is destroyed (theft, fire, hardware failure) | The 3-2-1 rule with offsite copy |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The threat model for backups should explicitly enumerate which failures are in scope and which aren't.** Most teams design for single-disk failure (RAID is everywhere) and skip the rest, then discover they're not protected against the failures that actually happen (ransomware, insider sabotage, software bugs corrupting state). **Architectural commitment: every system has an explicit backup threat model that names the top 5-10 failure scenarios and the defenses for each.** **For the orchestrator's harness**: the most relevant scenarios are total user loss (the navigator's machine fails), accidental deletion (the navigator deletes something they shouldn't have), and software bug corrupting state (the harness writes invalid events). The defenses are the 3-2-1 rule, retention long enough to recover from accidental deletion, and PITR via event replay. **Air-gapped backups** matter most for systems that handle real money or sensitive data, which means they matter for servicegrid and any future financial app — much less for the harness itself.

### 7. The application-consistent backup problem

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **In-flight transactions** | Backup taken while transactions are open | Crash-consistent if the WAL is included |
| **Quiesce + snapshot** | Pause writes, take snapshot, resume writes | Brief downtime, simple consistency |
| **Pre-backup hooks** | App-specific scripts to flush state before snapshot | Standard for databases |
| **Multi-volume consistency** | Snapshot multiple disks atomically | Required when state spans volumes |
| **Database-aware backup tools** | Tools that understand database semantics | pg_basebackup, Percona XtraBackup, Wal-G |
| **Application checkpointing** | App writes a known-good checkpoint before backup | The cleanest pattern |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Application-consistent backup is hard for systems with many independent state stores (multiple databases, files, caches) and easy for systems with one canonical state primitive.** The orchestrator's event log makes this trivial: **the event log offset is the consistency boundary — backup the log up to offset N, and any restore can replay to exactly offset N and reconstruct a consistent state.** No quiesce needed, no multi-volume coordination needed, no application-specific hooks needed. **This is one of the load-bearing benefits of event sourcing for backup**, and the dream state should name it. **For the apps each org ships using event-sourced state**: same benefit transfers. **For apps using traditional databases**: the database-aware backup tools (pg_basebackup, Wal-G for Postgres) handle the consistency problem correctly; rolling your own backup-via-pg_dump is anti-pattern because it doesn't scale and doesn't handle large databases well.

### 8. Operational discipline and cultural patterns

| Discipline | Mechanism | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Backup runbook** | Documented restore procedure | Required because you'll be panicking when you need it |
| **Backup ownership** | Named owner accountable for backup health | "Everyone owns it" = nobody owns it |
| **Backup monitoring** | Alerts when backups fail or skip | Backups are only useful if they actually run |
| **Backup retention policy** | How long to keep backups, when to expire | Compliance + cost balance |
| **Backup encryption key management** | Where the keys live, how they rotate | Lost keys = lost backups |
| **Cross-region restore drills** | Test restoring to a different region than the source | Catches region-specific assumptions |
| **The "two people who know" rule** | At least two humans understand the restore procedure | Bus factor |
| **Documenting what's NOT backed up** | Explicit list of what's intentionally excluded | Prevents surprise |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Backup is more of an operational discipline than an architectural pattern**, and the discipline failures are more common than the architectural failures. **The most common backup failures**:

1. **Backup ran but never actually copied the data** — silent failure, not noticed until restore is needed
2. **Backup data is corrupted** — fsync lying or other low-level failures
3. **Backup encryption key was lost** — backup is intact but unreadable
4. **Restore procedure was never documented** — humans can't remember under pressure
5. **Restore procedure was documented but never tested** — works in theory, fails in practice
6. **Critical data was excluded from backup** — by accident or oversight

**The architectural commitments that prevent these**: monitored backups (alert on failure or unexpected backup size), per-record checksums (already named in WAL §8 — closes the corruption-detection loop), key escrow (redundant copies of encryption keys, ideally in a hardware token AND a sealed envelope), restore runbooks tested quarterly, explicit "what's NOT in backup" documentation. **For the orchestrator's harness specifically**: the navigator should have a personal "if my laptop dies tomorrow, here's how I rebuild my orchestrator state" runbook and should test it at least once. **For the apps each org ships**: backup runbooks are a CI gate — no app ships to production without a tested restore procedure.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **Log-based backup is the natural fit for event-sourced architectures and gives backup-and-restore for free** (from §1). Ship the event log to a backup destination continuously; restore by replay. The same primitive that handles replication handles backup.
2. **The 3-2-1 rule with object lock on the offsite copy** (from §2). Three copies, two media, one offsite, immutable on the offsite. The defense against ransomware and credential compromise.
3. **RPO and RTO need to be per-data-class, not system-wide** (from §3). High-stakes events want zero RPO; trace data can tolerate hours of loss. Sharpens the existing RPO-as-deliberate-choice thread from Replication §4.
4. **An untested backup is an imaginary backup; restore drills are the most important backup discipline** (from §4). Monthly at the harness layer; automated in CI for production apps. Connects to the `fsync` lying defense — verification beyond a single layer.
5. **PITR via event replay is event sourcing's standard answer to disaster recovery** (from §5). Already a property the architecture wants; the backup entry sharpens it as a disaster recovery primitive, not just a debugging tool.
6. **Every system has an explicit backup threat model naming top 5-10 failure scenarios and defenses** (from §6). Most teams design for single-disk failure and skip the failures that actually happen.
7. **Application-consistent backup is trivial under event sourcing because the event log offset IS the consistency boundary** (from §7). One of the load-bearing benefits worth naming explicitly.
8. **Backup is mostly an operational discipline, not an architectural pattern; the discipline failures are more common than architectural failures** (from §8). Monitored backups, per-record checksums, key escrow, tested runbooks, explicit "what's NOT backed up" docs.

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This entry sharpens existing threads from Replication, Disk I/O, WAL, and Event streaming rather than introducing brand-new patterns** — most of the architectural moves were already in place; the backup framing makes them legible as a coherent disaster recovery story. **The most generative observation is §7 (application-consistent backup is trivial under event sourcing)** — this is one more load-bearing benefit of the event log resolution that hasn't been explicitly named, and it should be added to the dream state's "why event sourcing" justification list.

**The most operationally important commitment** is **the monthly restore drill at the harness layer**. It's the cheapest possible safeguard against the most common backup failure (untested backups don't work) and it's the only way to know whether the architecture's backup story is real or aspirational. If the navigator doesn't restore from backup at least once a month, they don't actually know what their RTO is.

**Layer note**: applies to both layers. Harness backup is the navigator's personal responsibility; app-layer backup is standard production operational discipline. The patterns are the same; the operators differ.

---

## Search and information retrieval

The discipline of returning relevant items from a large corpus given a query — the substrate behind every "find me the thing I'm thinking of" moment. Search is interesting for the orchestrator because the harness already has multiple latent search problems (skills, memory, inbox, knowledge graph nodes, past executions, conversation history) and each one has been answered ad-hoc with substring grep so far.

The entry exists because search is the bridge between **what's stored** (Indexes, Open Table Formats, Knowledge graph) and **what's reachable in practice** — without good retrieval the storage primitives are inert. It also names the on-ramp to RAG when the harness eventually wants it.

This is a **convergent-pattern resolution** entry for **both layers**.

### 1. Tokenization and analysis

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Tokenizer** | Splits raw text into terms | Standard (whitespace, Unicode-aware, language-specific) |
| **Stemmer / lemmatizer** | Reduces inflected forms to a base | Snowball, spaCy lemmatizer; tradeoff between recall and precision |
| **Stopword removal** | Drops `the`, `a`, `of`, etc. | Standard but increasingly skipped in modern IR |
| **N-gram generation** | Indexes 2- and 3-grams as tokens | Useful for substring search and typo tolerance |
| **Subword tokenizers** | BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece | Required for dense retrieval; bridges to LLM tokenizers |
| **Code-aware tokenization** | Splits on camelCase, snake_case, dots, slashes | The right default for code search; ripgrep gets this right |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** Tokenization is the place where the corpus and the query meet, and it's where most search systems quietly break — a query tokenized one way and a corpus tokenized another way produces empty results for reasons no user will ever understand. **Architectural commitment: any search subsystem in the harness must commit to a single tokenization pipeline that runs identically at index time and query time, and must be tested with a "round-trip" check (every document is findable by a query made from its own contents).** The orchestrator's existing skill search is substring-only and doesn't have this problem because there's no analysis layer to disagree with itself; the moment a real inverted index lands, the round-trip check becomes load-bearing. **Code-aware tokenization is the right default at the harness layer** — most of what the navigator searches for is identifiers, file paths, and structured strings, not natural-language prose.

### 2. The inverted index

| Concept | Mechanism | Tradeoff |
|---|---|---|
| **Term → posting list** | For each term, the set of documents containing it | The defining structure of IR |
| **Postings with positions** | Each posting also stores positions for phrase queries | More space, enables proximity scoring |
| **Compressed postings** | Variable-byte, Roaring, Frame-of-reference | Critical at scale; postings dominate index size |
| **Skip pointers** | Allow jumping forward in long postings during AND queries | Speeds up intersection |
| **Term dictionary** | The lookup from term string to posting list | Often a finite-state transducer (Lucene) or B-tree |
| **Segment-based writes** | Immutable segments, periodic merge | The same LSM pattern as in storage engines |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The inverted index is structurally an LSM tree applied to a different access pattern**, and it shares the same merge-on-write discipline. This is the same convergence already named in Indexes §1 (LSM is the right default for write-heavy workloads). **For the orchestrator's harness specifically, the inverted index is the natural answer to the skill search problem already named in Indexes §1 as a near-term unlock** — substring grep over `skills/` does not scale past a few hundred skills, and the orchestrator is already approaching that count. **The architectural commitment**: when the substring grep starts to hurt, the answer is an inverted index over skill text (description, when-to-use, examples) maintained as a derived projection from the skill registry, rebuilt on skill version bumps. SQLite FTS5 is the right substrate at the harness layer because it's already in process and already aligned with the local-first commitment. **Tantivy** is the next-tier answer if the harness ever outgrows FTS5 (Rust, embeddable, Lucene-class quality, single-binary-friendly).

### 3. Ranking functions

| Function | What it captures | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **TF-IDF** | Term frequency × inverse document frequency | The classic baseline; still strong |
| **BM25** | TF-IDF with length normalization and saturation | The default scoring function for modern IR |
| **BM25F** | BM25 with per-field weights | When documents have structured fields (title, body, tags) |
| **Learning-to-rank** | ML model produces scores from many features | Standard at large web search scale |
| **Vector cosine / dot product** | Similarity between dense embeddings | The dense-retrieval default |
| **Hybrid score fusion** | Combine sparse and dense scores | Reciprocal rank fusion is the simple default |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **BM25 is the strongest sparse-retrieval baseline and the right default for the harness's first real search** — it's well understood, deterministic, requires no training data, and SQLite FTS5 implements it natively. The temptation in 2026 is to skip directly to dense retrieval because embedding-based search is "modern", but **the empirical record is that BM25 alone outperforms naive dense retrieval on short, keyword-heavy queries**, and most harness searches are short and keyword-heavy. **Architectural commitment: BM25 first, dense retrieval second, hybrid (BM25 + dense, fused via reciprocal rank fusion) as the eventual destination once both sides exist.** This sequencing matters because the temptation to leapfrog is strong and the failure mode (dense retrieval with poor recall on exact-name queries) is invisible until users notice they can't find things they know exist.

### 4. Query parsing and query expansion

| Concept | Mechanism | Tradeoff |
|---|---|---|
| **Boolean query syntax** | `AND`, `OR`, `NOT`, parentheses, field qualifiers | Power-user surface |
| **Natural-language parser** | Free-text query, system infers intent | Lower friction, less precision |
| **Query rewriting** | Stemming, synonym expansion, spell correction | Boosts recall at the cost of precision |
| **Query suggestion / autocomplete** | Live completions from prefix queries | Standard UX expectation; needs its own index |
| **LLM-based query expansion** | Model generates related queries | Recent pattern; verifier-of-last-resort risk |
| **Filter pushdown** | Structured filters applied before scoring | Critical for performance with many filters |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The orchestrator's existing knowledge graph and structured skill metadata mean filter pushdown will be more load-bearing than query expansion.** Most real harness queries have a natural filter dimension already (skill phase, skill category, execution phase, inbox source, time range), and the right architecture pushes those filters down to the index rather than scoring everything and filtering after. **This is the same filter-pushdown discipline named in Query Engines §3** and it's the kind of cross-entry convergence the doc should reinforce. **LLM-based query expansion is interesting but should be opt-in, not default**: it's the kind of "helpful" rewriting that quietly destroys precision when the user typed exactly what they meant. **The architectural commitment**: harness search treats the user's literal query as authoritative, and any expansion is shown as a separate "did you also mean" path the user can engage with explicitly.

### 5. Dense retrieval and embeddings

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Bi-encoder** | Encode query and documents independently, compare | The default for retrieval |
| **Cross-encoder** | Encode query + document together, score | Much better quality, much slower; used for re-ranking |
| **ANN index** | HNSW, IVF-PQ, ScaNN | Required at scale; exact search tops out fast |
| **Vector DB** | pgvector, Qdrant, Weaviate, LanceDB | The persistence layer for vectors |
| **Embedding refresh** | Rebuild vectors on model upgrade | Often forgotten until search quality regresses |
| **Hybrid sparse+dense** | Combine BM25 with dense scores | The state of the art for general retrieval |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Dense retrieval is the bridge between Search and the ML systems infrastructure entry** — embeddings are an ML model artifact and inherit all the lifecycle problems named there (versioning, online-offline parity, refresh on model upgrade). **The orchestrator's specific bet** named in ML systems §3: pgvector if the substrate is already Postgres, Qdrant or LanceDB if a sovereign single-binary store is wanted. LanceDB is interesting because it stores vectors alongside columnar data (Lance/Arrow format) and aligns with the sovereign-binary, file-format-first commitment from Open Table Formats. **Embedding refresh is the operational trap**: the model upgrades, the corpus needs to be re-embedded, the refresh is batchy and slow, and during the refresh window queries hit a mixed-version index that scores incoherently. **Architectural commitment: dense retrieval must be deployed with a versioned embedding namespace, and queries must specify (or default to) a single embedding version for the duration of the query.** This is the same versioning discipline as ML feature stores (ML systems §4) applied to retrieval indices.

### 6. Re-ranking

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **First-stage retrieval** | Cheap recall (BM25, ANN) returns top-K | The candidate set |
| **Second-stage re-ranker** | Expensive cross-encoder reorders top-K | The quality stage |
| **LLM as re-ranker** | The LLM scores or reorders candidates | Slow but high-quality, increasingly common |
| **Diversity-aware re-ranking** | MMR, submodular selection | When result variety matters |
| **Personalization layer** | Per-user features applied at re-rank time | Standard at consumer scale |
| **Position bias correction** | Account for click-through bias in training data | Required for any LTR pipeline using clicks |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The two-stage retrieval pattern (cheap recall → expensive rerank) is the right default for any harness search that touches the LLM**, because the LLM is structurally a high-quality but expensive reranker and the BM25/dense first stage is structurally a cheap but lower-quality recall layer. **This is one of the most natural fits between the orchestrator's existing infrastructure and IR theory**: the LLM is already in the loop, already invoked per query, and already capable of judging relevance — so the only architectural question is how big the candidate set is and how many tokens get spent on the rerank. **Architectural commitment: harness search that returns to the LLM uses a two-stage pipeline, with the first stage tuned for high recall (top-50 or top-100) and the rerank step constrained by token budget.** This connects to the cost-engineering thread that will land in entry 22.

### 7. Search at scale and operational concerns

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Sharding by document ID** | Documents partitioned across nodes; queries fan out | Standard at scale (Elasticsearch, Solr) |
| **Sharding by term** | Postings partitioned across nodes | Rare; bandwidth-heavy for AND queries |
| **Replica fan-out** | Read replicas serve query load independently | Standard read-scaling pattern |
| **Hot-segment caching** | Recent or frequently-accessed segments in RAM | The query latency floor |
| **Index freshness lag** | Time between write and queryability | Standard tradeoff (real-time vs near-real-time) |
| **Query and click logging** | Logs of what users searched and clicked | Required for relevance evaluation |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Most of the scale-out concerns in this dimension don't apply at the harness layer** — the harness corpus (skills, memory, inbox, executions) fits comfortably in a single SQLite database for the foreseeable future, and sharding is solving a problem the harness doesn't have. **The concerns that DO apply at the harness layer**: index freshness lag (when a skill is updated, how soon is it findable?), hot-segment caching (the LSM-like merge structure of FTS5 segments), and query logging. **The query log is the most interesting harness-relevant artifact** — every search the navigator runs is signal for what skills are missing, what mental models the navigator has, and where the corpus is failing them. **Architectural commitment: harness search logs every query (with the user's consent, since this is the navigator's own data) and surfaces "queries that returned nothing" as an inbox-style stream of skill-creation prompts.** This is **the harness query log as a learning loop into skill creation** — a small but high-signal feedback path that doesn't exist yet. **At the app layer**: servicegrid's customer-facing search will hit the standard scaling concerns the moment it has real customers, and the architectural answer is "use a managed search service (Algolia, Typesense Cloud, Elasticsearch on AWS) until the cost crosses the run-it-yourself line."

### 8. The harness search surfaces

| Surface | Current state | Target architecture |
|---|---|---|
| **Skill search** | Substring grep over `skills/` | FTS5 inverted index, BM25 ranking, code-aware tokenization, optional dense reranking via LLM |
| **Memory search** | Direct file reads, no index | FTS5 over memory entries, faceted by type (user/feedback/project/reference) |
| **Inbox search** | List view, manual scanning | FTS5 over inbox content, time-bucketed |
| **Knowledge graph query** | Direct node/edge access by ID | Already covered in Indexes §5 / Query Engines §8; search is the text layer over it |
| **Past execution search** | Substring grep over execution logs | FTS5 over execution logs, faceted by phase/skill/loop |
| **Conversation history search** | None | The eventual long-context retrieval surface; touches dense retrieval |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The harness has six latent search surfaces and only one of them is acknowledged in the existing infrastructure**, and the gap between "we have storage" and "we have retrieval" is exactly the architectural shortfall this entry is naming. **The most generative move** is to recognize that all six surfaces share the same primitive (FTS5 inverted index, BM25 ranking, optional dense rerank) and can be served by a single "harness search service" rather than reinventing each one. **Architectural commitment: a unified harness search service exposing one query API that takes a corpus selector (skills/memory/inbox/executions/conversations) and a query, returning ranked results — backed by FTS5 indices that are derived projections from the canonical event-sourced state.** This is **the search service as a derived projection of the event log**, which keeps it consistent with the existing event-sourcing commitment: when the event log is the source of truth, search indices are just another consumer that rebuilds on event replay. **The orchestrator's existing skill search IS the harness's search service** — it just needs to be promoted from substring grep to a real index, and then the same primitive serves the other five surfaces.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **The inverted index is structurally an LSM tree applied to a different access pattern** (from §2). Same merge-on-write discipline as the storage layer; the convergence is worth naming.
2. **Code-aware tokenization is the right default at the harness layer** (from §1). Most harness queries are identifiers and paths, not prose.
3. **BM25 first, dense second, hybrid as destination** (from §3). Sequencing matters because leapfrogging to dense produces invisible recall failures on exact-name queries.
4. **Filter pushdown is more load-bearing than query expansion at the harness layer** (from §4). Same discipline as Query Engines §3; the orchestrator's structured metadata is the reason.
5. **Versioned embedding namespaces and query-pinned embedding versions** (from §5). The same versioning discipline as ML feature stores; the operational trap is mixed-version retrieval during refresh.
6. **Two-stage retrieval (cheap recall + LLM rerank) is the right default for any harness search that returns to the LLM** (from §6). The LLM is already a high-quality reranker; only the candidate-set size and token budget are tunable.
7. **The harness query log as a learning loop into skill creation** (from §7). Queries returning nothing are skill-creation prompts; the feedback path doesn't exist yet.
8. **A unified harness search service serving six latent surfaces from a single FTS5-backed primitive, derived from the event log** (from §8). The architectural commitment that closes the storage-vs-retrieval gap.

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This entry is a convergent-pattern resolution entry**: it doesn't introduce a new substrate, it names that the substrate (SQLite FTS5, optional pgvector/LanceDB for dense, the event log as source of truth) is already in place, and it surfaces six latent surfaces that should all be served by a single search service. The most generative move is **recognizing that the harness has been quietly accumulating search problems and answering each with substring grep**, and that the moment to consolidate is now — before the sixth surface gets its own ad-hoc answer.

**The most concrete near-term unlock** is **promoting the existing skill search from substring grep to FTS5 with BM25 ranking**. This is small enough to ship as a single skill execution and unlocks the unified-search-service framing for the other five surfaces. **The most architecturally interesting thread** is **the harness query log as a skill-creation feedback loop** — it's a small change to a logging pipeline with a disproportionately large effect on the orchestrator's self-improvement cycle.

**Layer note**: applies to both layers. Harness search is the unified service over six latent surfaces (skills, memory, inbox, executions, knowledge graph, conversations), backed by FTS5 and the event log. App-layer search (servicegrid customer search, dreamtime content search) is standard production retrieval — managed service first, self-hosted Tantivy or Elasticsearch later if cost or sovereignty demands it.

---

## Stream processing

The discipline of computing over unbounded sequences of events as they arrive — windowing, aggregation, joining, and stateful transformation on data that never stops. Stream processing is interesting for the orchestrator because **the harness is already a stream processor of its own event log** without ever having named itself one, and many of the operational questions (windowing, watermarks, late-arriving events, exactly-once) apply directly even at the single-process scale.

The entry exists because the gap is glaring: the doc has Event streaming as a separate entry covering the transport substrate, but the *processing model* on top of streams has been implicit. Streams are not just a transport pattern; they're a computational pattern with its own theory.

This is a **resolution-confirmation** entry for **both layers**.

### 1. Stream vs batch as a continuum

| Dimension | Batch | Streaming |
|---|---|---|
| **Input** | Bounded dataset | Unbounded sequence |
| **Latency** | Minutes to hours | Milliseconds to seconds |
| **Failure model** | Re-run the job | Recover from checkpoint |
| **Complexity** | Lower (one snapshot) | Higher (state + time) |
| **Mental model** | "Process this file" | "Process forever" |
| **Replay** | Re-read the file | Re-read the log from offset |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The batch-vs-streaming distinction has collapsed into a continuum at modern systems**: a streaming job replaying from an old offset is structurally equivalent to a batch job over the data that arrived since that offset, and a batch job over an append-only log is structurally a streaming job with infinite latency tolerance. The Lambda architecture (separate batch and speed layers) was the response to a temporary technology gap; the Kappa architecture (single stream pipeline that supports replay) is the resolution. **The orchestrator already lives in the Kappa world by accident**: there is no batch layer because there's no separate batch substrate, and there's no streaming layer because the event log is the only substrate. **Architectural commitment: the orchestrator commits to Kappa-style unification by default — every computation over event-log data is a stream replay, even if the "stream" is the entire log from offset zero.** This dissolves a category of architectural decisions (when to batch, when to stream, how to reconcile) that other systems spend significant effort on.

### 2. Time semantics: event time vs processing time

| Concept | Definition | When it matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Event time** | When the event actually occurred in the source domain | Almost always, for correctness |
| **Processing time** | When the event arrived at the stream processor | When you don't care about correctness, only throughput |
| **Ingestion time** | When the event was first received by the system | A middle ground; rarely the right answer |
| **Watermark** | A bound on how late an event can still arrive | Required for closing windows correctly |
| **Allowed lateness** | Grace period after watermark before window finalizes | Tradeoff between correctness and latency |
| **Late-arriving events** | Events that arrive after the watermark | Must be either dropped, side-outputted, or trigger window updates |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Event time vs processing time is the single most load-bearing distinction in stream processing**, and it's where the hardest bugs live in every real system. The classic failure: a daily aggregation job using processing time, a network outage delays a batch of events by an hour, the events arrive in the next day's window, the report for the prior day is wrong forever. **The orchestrator's event log is event-time native by design** — every event carries an explicit timestamp from when it occurred (when the skill was completed, when the gate was approved), and the processing time (when a downstream consumer reads it) is structurally separate. **This is one of the load-bearing benefits of event sourcing for stream processing** that hasn't been named explicitly: the event-time/processing-time distinction is already resolved in favor of event time, and any harness computation that aggregates over the event log is doing event-time processing for free. **Watermarks** matter less at the harness layer because the orchestrator has a single producer and effectively no out-of-order delivery; they matter much more at the app layer where servicegrid will eventually have mobile clients writing offline and syncing later.

### 3. Windowing

| Window type | Definition | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Tumbling** | Fixed-size, non-overlapping (e.g., every 5 minutes) | Periodic aggregations, billing, reports |
| **Sliding** | Fixed-size, overlapping (e.g., 5 minutes, slide every 1 minute) | Smoothed metrics, moving averages |
| **Session** | Bounded by inactivity gaps | User sessions, agent task sessions |
| **Global** | One window over all time | Stateful aggregation with no time bound |
| **Custom triggers** | Windows fire on conditions, not just time | Alerting, complex patterns |
| **Punctuation-based** | Windows close on explicit marker events | When the producer knows session boundaries |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Session windows are the most interesting window type for the orchestrator** because the harness's natural unit of work is a *session* (a navigator working session, an agent execution session, a loop run from start to complete) and these sessions have natural punctuation events (loop START, loop COMPLETE) rather than arbitrary time bounds. **Architectural commitment: harness aggregations over the event log default to session windows defined by punctuation events, not tumbling time windows.** The session is more naturally semantic than the time bucket, and the punctuation events already exist in the log. **This is the same insight that the WAL entry §7 named about checkpoints** — the right boundary for harness operations is usually a semantic event, not a clock tick. **Tumbling time windows are still the right answer for app-layer billing** (Stripe usage records, monthly invoices) where the time bucket *is* the semantic unit.

### 4. Stateful stream processing

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Local state per key** | Each key's state lives on the worker handling that key | The default for keyed aggregations |
| **State backend** | Where state is persisted (RocksDB on disk, memory) | Flink uses RocksDB; Kafka Streams uses RocksDB |
| **State checkpointing** | Periodic snapshot of state for recovery | Required for fault tolerance |
| **State migration** | Moving state when partitioning changes | The hardest operational concern |
| **State TTL** | Automatic expiration of stale state | Required for unbounded state |
| **Materialized views** | State that is itself queryable | Kafka Streams interactive queries; Materialize |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Stream processing state is structurally the same problem as derived projections from an event log**, and the harness has already committed to event-sourced derived projections as the answer (Event streaming entry, WAL §6). **The convergence**: a Kafka Streams app maintaining a keyed state store is structurally equivalent to a harness consumer maintaining a SQLite-backed projection of the event log filtered to the relevant key. The vocabulary differs but the architecture is identical. **Architectural commitment: the orchestrator's stateful computations over the event log are derived projections, persisted in SQLite (or a per-projection store), rebuilt on full replay when the projection logic changes.** This is the **kept-state-as-projection pattern** — the projection's state IS the stream processor's state, and the same mechanism (event replay from offset zero) handles both rebuilding-after-schema-change and recovery-after-failure. **State TTL** is the operational concern that matters most at the harness layer for any projection over high-volume event types (executions, sub-agent calls); the projection should bound its retention explicitly rather than growing without limit.

### 5. Exactly-once semantics

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **At-most-once** | Drop on failure | Throughput-critical, loss-tolerant |
| **At-least-once** | Retry on failure, duplicates possible | The default for most stream systems |
| **Exactly-once (consumer-side)** | Idempotent consumers + dedup | The pragmatic answer |
| **Exactly-once (transactional)** | Atomic write of output + offset commit | Kafka transactions, Flink checkpoints |
| **End-to-end exactly-once** | Source → processor → sink, all transactional | Hardest; requires sink cooperation |
| **Effectively-once via idempotent sinks** | At-least-once + idempotent writes | The most common practical pattern |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Exactly-once is the most over-marketed property in stream processing**, and the honest answer is almost always "at-least-once delivery + idempotent consumers". The orchestrator's commitment to monotonic event sequence numbers (named in Concurrency primitives §3 and WAL §3) makes idempotent consumers structurally easy: every consumer tracks the highest event offset it has processed, and re-processing an event with offset ≤ the last-processed offset is a no-op. **Architectural commitment: the orchestrator's stream consumers are at-least-once + idempotent by sequence number, and "exactly-once" is achieved as an emergent property rather than as a transactional primitive.** This avoids the complexity of distributed transactional commit (which would conflict with the local-first single-process commitment anyway) and gets the same observable behavior. **The classical exactly-once trap** — where the system claims exactly-once but the sink isn't idempotent and a retry causes a duplicate side effect — doesn't apply because the harness structurally cannot have non-idempotent sinks for log-replay computations.

### 6. Joins on streams

| Join type | Definition | Tradeoff |
|---|---|---|
| **Stream-stream join** | Two streams joined within a time window | Requires buffering both sides; window size = memory cost |
| **Stream-table join** | Stream enriched against a slowly-changing table | The most common practical pattern |
| **Temporal join** | Join with a versioned table at the event-time of each stream record | The "correct" answer; harder to implement |
| **Interval join** | Join only when timestamps fall within a window | Used for matching related events |
| **Windowed join** | Both streams windowed; join the windows | Subset of stream-stream join |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Stream-table joins are the most important join type for the harness**, because most of what the harness needs is "enrich this event with the current state of skill X / loop Y / org Z". The lookup side (skill registry, loop registry, org metadata) is slowly-changing relative to the event stream, and the join is structurally a hash lookup against the latest known version. **Architectural commitment: the harness's projection consumers do stream-table joins by reading the current registry state at the moment of event processing, accepting that this is a "processing-time" join that may give a different answer if replayed against a future registry state.** This is **a deliberate trade against temporal correctness** in exchange for implementation simplicity, and it's the right call at the harness layer because the registries change rarely. **At the app layer, temporal joins matter more** — servicegrid pricing rules at the moment a job was created should not be retroactively changed by a later pricing-rule update, which means the pricing-rule lookup must be temporal (versioned by the event time of the job-creation event). This is the same versioning discipline as ML feature stores (ML systems §4) and embedding namespaces (Search §5).

### 7. Backpressure and flow control

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Pull-based consumption** | Consumer requests events at its own pace | Kafka, Pulsar; the modern default |
| **Push-based with credit** | Producer pushes; consumer grants credits | Reactive Streams, gRPC streaming |
| **Buffering with bounded queues** | Drop or block when queue fills | The simple answer; correctness depends on which |
| **Adaptive batching** | Larger batches under load, smaller under idle | Throughput optimization |
| **Source throttling** | Producer slows down when consumer can't keep up | Required for steady-state stability |
| **Dead-letter routing** | Events the consumer can't process go to a side stream | The right answer for poison messages |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Backpressure dissolves at the harness layer because the consumer (the navigator's session and its sub-agents) is structurally slower than the producer by orders of magnitude** — events are produced at human and LLM speeds, not machine speeds, and there is no risk of an in-process consumer falling behind an in-process producer. **At the app layer the backpressure question is real**: servicegrid will have webhook ingestion from external systems, mobile sync, and customer events, all of which can outpace the consumer. **The architectural commitment for the app layer: managed message brokers (Kafka, SQS, Pub/Sub) handle backpressure correctly out of the box, and the right answer is to use them rather than rolling a homegrown bounded queue.** **Dead-letter routing** is the operational pattern that matters most at the app layer because poison messages (malformed events the consumer cannot process) will appear and the system must not block the entire stream while a single bad event is investigated.

### 8. The harness as a stream processor

| Property | Standard stream processor | Orchestrator harness |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Kafka topic | The event log (SQLite-backed today) |
| **Processing model** | DAG of operators | Sequential consumers reading from offsets |
| **State** | RocksDB per-operator | SQLite-backed derived projections |
| **Time semantics** | Event time + watermarks | Event time native; no watermark complexity |
| **Exactly-once** | Transactional commit | At-least-once + idempotent by sequence number |
| **Replay** | From earliest committed offset | From offset zero, full replay always available |
| **Fault tolerance** | Checkpoint and resume | Checkpoint and resume (event log persists offset-per-consumer) |
| **Operators** | Map, filter, window, aggregate, join | Whatever the consumer code does |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The harness IS a stream processor**, structurally — the same way the Saga orchestration entry named "the harness IS a saga framework" and the WAL entry named "the event log IS a WAL applied at application-event granularity". **The architectural recognition**: the harness already has a source (the event log), already has consumers reading from offsets, already has stateful projections, already has fault tolerance via offset persistence, and already has replay from any offset. The only thing it's missing is the *vocabulary* — calling these "stream processors" rather than "consumers" — and the only thing missing operationally is the discipline of treating consumer state as derived projections rather than ad-hoc files. **Architectural commitment: the harness names itself a stream processor and adopts the discipline of windowing, stateful projection, and idempotent-by-sequence-number consumption as first-class patterns, even though there is no Flink or Kafka Streams in the substrate.** This is one more **"X is structurally Y"** recognition that the doc should add to the convergent list.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **The Kappa unification of batch and streaming is already in place at the harness, by accident** (from §1). Every computation over event-log data is a stream replay; there is no separate batch layer.
2. **Event-time vs processing-time is already resolved in favor of event time by event sourcing** (from §2). Every event carries its event-time timestamp; processing time is structurally separate.
3. **Session windows defined by punctuation events are the right default for harness aggregations** (from §3). Loop START / loop COMPLETE define semantic sessions better than arbitrary time buckets.
4. **Stateful stream processing state IS derived projections from the event log** (from §4). The vocabulary differs from Kafka Streams but the architecture is identical.
5. **At-least-once + idempotent-by-sequence-number gives exactly-once semantics as an emergent property** (from §5). No need for distributed transactional commit; the sequence number is the dedup key.
6. **Stream-table joins read current registry state at event time at the harness layer; temporal joins matter at the app layer for pricing/rules versioning** (from §6). The same versioning discipline as ML feature stores and embedding namespaces.
7. **Backpressure dissolves at the harness layer because human/LLM-speed producers cannot outpace in-process consumers** (from §7). At the app layer, managed brokers handle it; dead-letter routing is the operational pattern that matters.
8. **The harness IS a stream processor — structurally** (from §8). Source, consumers, offsets, projections, replay, fault tolerance — the only missing piece is the vocabulary and the discipline of naming consumer state as projections.

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This entry is a resolution-confirmation entry**: most of stream processing's conceptual apparatus is already structurally present in the orchestrator's event-sourced architecture, and the walk's job is to recognize the equivalence and adopt the vocabulary rather than to introduce new substrates. The single most generative observation is **§8: the harness IS a stream processor**, which adds another entry to the "X is structurally Y" pattern list and unlocks borrowing IR theory wholesale for harness consumer design.

**The most concrete near-term unlock** is **adopting the discipline of session windows defined by punctuation events for harness aggregations** — currently, any "give me stats over the last N executions" question gets answered by an ad-hoc query, and the better answer is a derived projection that maintains a session-keyed aggregate updated by the event stream. **The most architecturally interesting thread** is the convergence between stateful stream processing state and event-sourced derived projections (§4) — they are the same thing under different names, and naming the equivalence makes the borrowing of patterns from Kafka Streams / Flink legitimate.

**Layer note**: applies to both layers. Harness stream processing is already in place structurally; the work is recognition and vocabulary adoption, not new substrate. App-layer stream processing (servicegrid real-time job updates, dreamtime event aggregations) follows standard production patterns: managed brokers (Kafka or Pub/Sub), Flink or Kafka Streams for real stateful processing if it ever scales beyond what the application can handle in-process.

---

## Notification systems

The discipline of getting messages to people on the channels they want, at the times they want, without losing messages or sending duplicates. Notifications are interesting for the orchestrator because the harness already has a live notification surface (the Slack proactive-messaging service that delivers loop status, gate prompts, and questions to the navigator's phone) and the app layer will eventually have full multi-channel customer notifications for servicegrid.

The entry exists because notifications are deceptively complex — what looks like "send a message" is actually a fan-out problem, a templating problem, a deduplication problem, a preferences problem, a delivery-tracking problem, and a rate-limiting problem all at once, and most teams underestimate it until production.

This is a **choice-layer** entry for **both layers**, with current load-bearing infrastructure at the harness layer.

### 1. Channels and reach

| Channel | Latency | Reach | Cost | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **In-app** | Real-time | High when user is in-app | Free | Default for engaged users |
| **Push (mobile)** | Seconds | High; delivery to lock screen | Free (APNS/FCM) | Mobile apps |
| **SMS** | Seconds | Highest delivery rate | $$ per message | Critical alerts, 2FA |
| **Email** | Minutes | High but variable | Cheap | Detailed updates, receipts |
| **Voice** | Real-time | Highest urgency signal | $$$ | Pager-grade alerts |
| **Slack / Teams / Discord** | Real-time | High for ops/dev users | Free with app | Internal ops, dev workflows |
| **Webhooks** | Real-time | System-to-system | Free | B2B integrations |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The orchestrator's harness already commits to Slack as the primary mobile notification channel** (CLAUDE.md names the proactive-messaging service explicitly), and this is the right call for the navigator-as-sole-user model: Slack reaches the navigator's phone with rich interactive buttons, threads notifications per execution, and supports the @mention discipline that distinguishes "you should look at this now" from "fyi". **The architectural commitment**: Slack is the default channel at the harness layer because it's the only one that satisfies all four properties (real-time, mobile-reachable, interactive, threadable). Email is the fallback for non-urgent summaries. SMS is reserved for the case where Slack itself is broken (a meta-alert, not a primary channel). **At the app layer**: the channel choice is per-customer-segment. servicegrid's contractor-facing notifications will need SMS (contractors are field workers, often without push notifications enabled) and email (for compliance trails). The customer-facing notifications can default to email with SMS for high-urgency events.

### 2. Templating and personalization

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Static templates** | String with placeholders, e.g. Mustache | Simple cases; doesn't compose |
| **Component-based templates** | Reusable blocks (header, body, button) | Email tools (MJML, React Email) |
| **Per-locale templates** | Translated copies of each template | I18n requirement |
| **Per-channel templates** | Same notification, different format per channel | Required for multi-channel |
| **LLM-generated copy** | Model produces channel-appropriate message | Recent pattern; precision risk |
