| **Template versioning** | Track template changes for A/B and rollback | Required for analytics |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Per-channel templating is the load-bearing requirement that most homegrown notification systems get wrong** — they start with one channel, build a template system that assumes that channel's format, then add a second channel and discover the template system can't accommodate both without rewriting. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: any notification system that will eventually serve more than one channel commits to a "notification intent" abstraction at the template layer** — the template defines what the notification *means* (recipient, urgency, payload, action), and per-channel renderers translate the intent into channel-specific format. **At the harness layer**: the proactive-messaging service is currently Slack-only and the templates can be Slack-shaped without penalty, but the moment a second channel is added (email summaries, SMS for the "Slack is down" meta-alert) the abstraction needs to land. **LLM-generated copy** is interesting for novel notifications but should not replace deterministic templates for transactional messages — the precision risk on payment confirmations and password resets is unacceptable.

### 3. Fan-out patterns

| Pattern | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Fan-out on write (push model)** | Compute recipient list, write to per-recipient inbox | Standard for low-recipient notifications |
| **Fan-out on read (pull model)** | Recipient queries shared feed at read time | Standard for high-recipient feeds (Twitter timelines) |
| **Hybrid (write for active, read for inactive)** | Write fanout for active users, read fanout for the long tail | Twitter, Instagram default |
| **Per-channel queue** | One queue per channel, workers consume independently | Standard at scale |
| **Per-recipient preference resolution** | Look up recipient's channel preferences before delivery | Required for opt-out |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Fan-out is mostly a non-problem at the harness layer** because the recipient set is always exactly one (the navigator), so there's no fan-out at all — it's a single-recipient direct delivery. **Architectural recognition: the harness's "notification system" is structurally simpler than real fan-out systems by exactly the factor of `1 / number_of_recipients`**, which is to say, infinitely simpler. **At the app layer the fan-out problem is real**: servicegrid will have notifications that go to the contractor, the customer, the platform admin, and possibly multiple stakeholders per job. **The architectural commitment for the app layer**: fan-out on write (compute the recipient list at the moment of the triggering event, enqueue per-recipient delivery jobs) is the right default until per-event recipient counts cross ~100, at which point the read-fanout question starts to apply. servicegrid will not approach that boundary in the foreseeable future, so fan-out on write is the long-term answer.

### 4. Deduplication, digesting, and rate limiting

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Idempotency keys** | Same notification with same key delivered at most once | Required for at-least-once event sources |
| **Deduplication windows** | Suppress duplicates within a time window | "Don't send the same alert twice in 10 min" |
| **Digesting** | Combine many notifications into a single summary | Default for non-urgent feeds (daily digest) |
| **Per-user rate limits** | Cap on notifications per user per time window | Defense against notification fatigue |
| **Priority lanes** | Critical messages bypass rate limits | Required to keep urgent alerts reachable |
| **Quiet hours** | Defer non-urgent notifications during sleep windows | Standard UX expectation |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Rate limiting and digesting are the most underrated parts of notification design** — a system that sends every event as its own notification will train the user to ignore it, and once trained the system is broken even if every individual message is correct. **The orchestrator has already committed to a primitive form of this**: the proactive-messaging service threads per-execution Slack notifications so that the navigator sees a single conversation per loop run rather than dozens of disconnected messages. **The architectural commitment to extend this**: the harness should adopt explicit notification priority levels (`critical` / `attention` / `fyi`) and apply different rate-limit budgets per level. Critical messages always reach the navigator immediately. Attention-level messages can be batched into a digest if more than N arrive within a window. FYI messages collapse into a periodic summary. **The "sleep test" connection**: notifications during the navigator's sleep window should respect quiet hours by default, with critical-level the only exception, and the quiet-hours window is part of the navigator profile rather than per-channel configuration. This is the same sleep-test framing extended one more layer beyond persistence, economics, and liveness — now into attention discipline.

### 5. Delivery guarantees and retry

| Guarantee | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Best-effort** | Try once, drop on failure | Throughput-critical, loss-tolerant |
| **At-least-once with retries** | Retry on transient failure, possibly duplicate | The default |
| **Exactly-once via idempotency keys** | Idempotency at the channel level | The pragmatic answer for transactional messages |
| **Persistent queue with checkpointing** | Notification durably stored until acked by channel | Required for non-loss guarantees |
| **Dead-letter for permanent failures** | Failures after N retries route to a dead-letter queue | Standard operational pattern |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Notification delivery guarantees are usually weaker than transactional guarantees because the channel itself is unreliable** — Slack APIs go down, email gets bounced, SMS providers throttle, push tokens expire. **Architectural commitment: notifications are persisted durably before any delivery attempt, and the delivery worker treats the channel as a flaky external dependency**: retries with exponential backoff, dead-letter routing after N failures, observable per-notification status (queued / sent / delivered / failed / dead-lettered). **The harness's proactive-messaging service should track per-notification delivery status as events in the event log** — this is the same event-log-as-audit-log pattern named in Data governance §5 and Event streaming §7, applied to a new domain. **The connection to the sleep test**: a notification queued at 3am that fails delivery at 3am must still reach the navigator at 7am when they wake up, which requires durable persistence + retry rather than fire-and-forget. The current harness implementation may or may not satisfy this; the architectural commitment names that it must.

### 6. Preferences and opt-out

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Per-channel opt-out** | User disables a single channel | Standard preference UI |
| **Per-category opt-out** | User disables a notification category | "Marketing emails off, transactional on" |
| **Global do-not-disturb** | Temporary suppression of all non-critical | Standard mobile pattern |
| **Compliance-mandated unsubscribes** | One-click unsubscribe for marketing | CAN-SPAM, GDPR requirement |
| **Required transactional categories** | Cannot be opted out (receipts, security) | The category that overrides preferences |
| **Preference migration on channel changes** | User changes phone or email; preferences carry over | Often broken |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The transactional-vs-marketing distinction is a legal requirement, not just a product preference** — CAN-SPAM, CASL, and GDPR all require one-click unsubscribe for marketing messages and forbid hiding the unsubscribe link, while transactional messages (order confirmations, password resets, security alerts) are exempt. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: every notification is tagged at the source with a category (transactional / operational / marketing / engagement), and the delivery layer enforces opt-out rules per category, with marketing-category messages physically incapable of bypassing the unsubscribe-respect check.** This is the same defense-in-depth discipline as the data governance entry's commitment to enforcing PII handling at the storage layer rather than relying on every consumer to do the right thing. **At the harness layer**: opt-out is trivially implemented by the navigator turning off the proactive-messaging service entirely, but a finer-grained opt-out (silence FYI messages but keep critical ones) is the right next step.

### 7. Observability and feedback

| Signal | Mechanism | What it tells you |
|---|---|---|
| **Sent rate** | Notifications attempted per unit time | Throughput baseline |
| **Delivery rate** | Notifications confirmed delivered by the channel | The first quality metric |
| **Open / read rate** | Recipient opened the message | Engagement signal |
| **Click-through rate** | Recipient took the linked action | Conversion signal |
| **Bounce / hard-fail rate** | Permanently failed deliveries | Recipient list quality |
| **Unsubscribe rate** | Users opting out per notification | Negative engagement signal |
| **Time-to-read** | Latency from send to user-engagement | Sleep test signal |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Notification observability is the only way to know whether the system is working in the way that matters (the recipient actually saw and acted on the message), as opposed to the way that's easy to measure (the system attempted delivery).** **The orchestrator's harness specifically should track per-notification "did the navigator engage with this within N minutes" as a signal that proactive-messaging is succeeding at its goal** — if a "gate is waiting" notification sits unacknowledged for an hour, the system has failed even if the API call to Slack returned 200. **Architectural commitment: harness notifications are tracked for engagement, and the engagement signal feeds back into priority calibration** — if a "critical" notification consistently sees long delays, it should be downgraded; if an "fyi" notification sees fast engagement, it might be miscategorized. This is the same calibration discipline as the learning loop's effort estimates. **At the app layer**: the standard email/push observability stack (SendGrid event webhooks, Firebase delivery callbacks) handles this, and the architectural commitment is to wire those callbacks back into the application's event log so that delivery status is queryable like any other domain event.

### 8. The scheduler and deferred delivery

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Scheduled delivery** | Notification queued for a future time | Standard for reminders, digests |
| **Recurring schedules** | Daily/weekly/monthly digests | Cron-like primitive |
| **Time-zone-aware scheduling** | Deliver at recipient-local time | Required for global audiences |
| **Conditional delivery** | Deliver only if precondition still holds | "Reminder in 1h if not yet acted on" |
| **Cancellation on event** | Cancel a scheduled notification when its trigger resolves | The "you don't need this anymore" case |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Conditional and cancellable scheduled notifications are the highest-leverage primitive for the harness specifically**, because the orchestrator's notification needs are dominated by "remind the navigator about this gate in 30 minutes if they haven't acted on it" rather than "send this immediately". **Architectural commitment: the harness adopts a scheduled-with-cancel notification primitive — a notification can be queued for future delivery and then cancelled by a later event before its delivery window opens.** This is structurally a saga step: schedule the notification as one step, observe the trigger event (gate approved, question answered), cancel the scheduled notification as the compensating action. The Saga orchestration entry already names "the harness IS a saga framework", and this is one more saga the framework should support natively. **At the app layer**: this same primitive serves servicegrid's "remind the contractor 30 min before the appointment unless they've already checked in" pattern, which is one of the most common operational notifications in field-service workflows.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **Slack as the harness's primary mobile notification channel because it's the only one that satisfies real-time + mobile + interactive + threadable** (from §1). Already committed to in CLAUDE.md.
2. **Per-channel "notification intent" abstraction that separates meaning from rendering** (from §2). The trap that catches most homegrown systems on the second channel.
3. **Fan-out is structurally a non-problem at the harness layer because the recipient set is always one** (from §3). Single-recipient delivery is infinitely simpler than real fan-out.
4. **Notification priority levels (critical/attention/fyi) with per-level rate-limit budgets and quiet-hours respect** (from §4). The sleep test extended one more layer into attention discipline.
5. **Notifications persisted durably before delivery; per-notification status tracked as events in the event log** (from §5). Same event-log-as-audit-log pattern as data governance.
6. **Transactional-vs-marketing category enforced at the delivery layer, not relied on per-consumer** (from §6). Defense-in-depth for legal compliance.
7. **Per-notification engagement tracking that feeds back into priority calibration** (from §7). Same calibration discipline as the learning loop.
8. **Scheduled-with-cancel notifications as a saga primitive** (from §8). The harness IS a saga framework; this is one more saga.

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This entry is a choice-layer entry that resolves cleanly toward existing harness infrastructure**: most of the architectural moves either confirm what the proactive-messaging service is already doing (Slack as primary channel, per-execution threading) or extend it with disciplines from app-layer notification practice (priority levels, durable persistence, engagement tracking, scheduled-with-cancel). The most generative move is **§4: notification priority levels with quiet-hours respect**, which extends the sleep test into attention discipline and connects to a class of bugs (notification fatigue) that the harness is already at risk of as it gains more proactive messaging.

**The most concrete near-term unlock** is **adopting the scheduled-with-cancel primitive at the harness layer** — this is the saga pattern applied to notifications and it's the building block that turns the proactive-messaging service from a fire-and-forget channel into a real conversation primitive ("ping me about this in 30 minutes unless I act first"). **The most architecturally interesting thread** is the recognition that notification observability is the calibration loop for notification priority — the same discipline as effort calibration in the learning loop, applied to a new domain.

**Layer note**: applies to both layers. Harness notifications are Slack-primary, navigator-only-recipient, with priority levels and quiet-hours discipline as the near-term work. App-layer notifications (servicegrid contractor and customer messaging, dreamtime engagement messages) follow standard production multi-channel discipline: managed providers (SendGrid, Twilio, Firebase), per-channel renderers from a notification-intent abstraction, durable persistence, opt-out enforcement at the delivery layer.

---

## File storage and large object handling

The discipline of storing, serving, and managing files that don't fit cleanly into a row of a database — images, videos, documents, generated artifacts, model weights, exports, archives. Object storage is interesting for the orchestrator because the local-first commitment puts files on the navigator's disk first by default, and the sync-to-cloud question only arises for collaboration, distribution, or backup.

The entry exists because file handling is the dimension where "just put it in the database" stops working earliest, and the architectural choices (object storage backend, naming scheme, lifecycle, access control, CDN) are mostly orthogonal to the rest of the storage stack and need their own resolution.

This is a **choice-layer** entry for **both layers**.

### 1. Object storage backends

| Backend | Sovereignty | Pricing model | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Local filesystem** | Full | Disk cost | The local-first default |
| **AWS S3** | None (managed) | Storage + egress + requests | The industry default |
| **Google Cloud Storage** | None | Similar to S3 | GCP-aligned stacks |
| **Cloudflare R2** | None | Storage only, no egress fee | Cost-optimized for read-heavy |
| **Backblaze B2** | None | Cheapest storage tier | Long-term archives |
| **MinIO** | Self-hosted | Hardware cost | S3-compatible, sovereign |
| **SeaweedFS** | Self-hosted | Hardware cost | Small-file optimized |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The orchestrator's local-first commitment makes the local filesystem the canonical store for harness artifacts**, and any cloud object storage is a *sync target* rather than the source of truth. **Architectural commitment: at the harness layer, files live on the navigator's disk by default, with optional sync to a cloud bucket configured per-org for backup or sharing.** The S3-API standard (which R2, B2, MinIO, and most others implement) means the choice of backend is reversible — code written against the S3 API can target any of them. **At the app layer**: managed object storage is the right default for any app shipping to customers. **Cloudflare R2 is the most interesting commercial option** because the no-egress-fee pricing eliminates the dominant cost of read-heavy workloads (image and video CDNs in particular) and aligns with the cost-engineering thread that will land in entry 22. **The substrate-sovereignty escape valve**: MinIO is the right answer if a future shipped app needs S3 semantics under full sovereignty (e.g., for compliance reasons), and the same code that targets R2 in production can target MinIO in a private cloud without changes.

### 2. Naming, content addressing, and immutability

| Scheme | Mechanism | Tradeoff |
|---|---|---|
| **Path-based names** | `users/123/avatar.jpg` | Mutable; overwrites lose history |
| **Content-addressed** | `sha256/{hash}` | Immutable, deduplicated, cache-friendly |
| **UUID-based** | `uploads/{uuid}.jpg` | Unique, opaque, no information leak |
| **Time-bucketed** | `2026/04/10/{hash}.jpg` | Cleanup-friendly; supports lifecycle policies |
| **Versioned** | `users/123/avatar.jpg?version=N` | Mutable interface with version-aware reads |
| **Composite** | UUID-named in the bucket, semantic mapping in DB | Best of both; the standard pattern |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Content-addressed storage is the right default for any artifact that should be immutable and deduplicated** — the hash is the name, two identical files share storage automatically, and cache invalidation is structurally impossible because the URL refers to exactly one byte sequence forever. **Architectural commitment for the harness specifically: artifacts that should be immutable (skill snapshots, execution logs at completion, generated app outputs, model checkpoints) are stored content-addressed, and the database holds the mapping from semantic name to content hash.** This is the same Merkle-tree pattern as Git, IPFS, and Nix, applied to harness artifacts. **The composite pattern (UUID in bucket + semantic mapping in DB) is the right default for mutable user-uploaded content** because it lets the application rename, replace, or hide content at the database layer without rewriting bucket contents. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: putting user-supplied filenames or paths directly into the bucket key — this leaks information, breaks on Unicode or special characters, and creates collision risks.

### 3. Upload paths

| Pattern | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct server upload** | Client → backend → object store | Simple; backend bandwidth bottleneck |
| **Presigned URL upload** | Backend signs upload URL; client uploads directly to bucket | Standard at scale; backend out of the data path |
| **Multipart upload** | Client splits file into parts, uploads in parallel, finalizes | Required for files > a few MB |
| **Resumable upload** | Client can pause and resume after network failure | Required for large files on flaky networks |
| **Browser fetch with CORS** | Browser uploads directly given correct CORS config | Standard for SPA uploads |
| **Server-side fetch from URL** | Client gives a URL, backend fetches | When the source is already on the public internet |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Presigned URLs are the load-bearing pattern that takes the application backend out of the data path for uploads**, and the cost savings (and reliability improvements) are large enough that any upload-heavy app should adopt them by default. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: any user-facing upload uses presigned URLs by default, with multipart for files over 5MB and resumable for files over 100MB.** servicegrid will need this for job photos (typically a few MB each, dozens per job), dreamtime will need it for any user-uploaded content, and all of them benefit from getting the application server out of the upload data path. **At the harness layer**: uploads are mostly file-to-disk operations and presigned URLs are not relevant; the equivalent discipline is "harness writes go to disk via the local filesystem API, and any sync-to-cloud step happens asynchronously after the local write succeeds" — which is the local-first commitment applied to file storage.

### 4. Serving and CDN integration

| Pattern | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct bucket reads** | Public bucket; clients fetch directly | Static sites, public images |
| **Presigned download URLs** | Time-limited URLs for private content | Standard for protected files |
| **CDN-fronted bucket** | CDN caches bucket reads at the edge | The default for any read-heavy public content |
| **Edge transform** | CDN-resizing/converting on the fly | Cloudflare Images, imgix |
| **Range request support** | Clients fetch byte ranges | Required for video, large file streaming |
| **Cache-control discipline** | Long max-age with versioned URLs | The cache-friendliness contract |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **CDN-fronted object storage is the architectural pattern that absorbs essentially all serving load for public read-heavy content** — the origin bucket sees almost no traffic once the CDN warms, and the egress cost (which is the dominant cost for image and video apps) collapses by 95%+. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: any public file content is served via a CDN with long max-age headers and content-addressed (or version-stamped) URLs**, so cache invalidation is never needed. **Cloudflare R2 + Cloudflare CDN is the most cost-aligned combination** because R2 has no egress fees in the first place and the CDN absorbs reads anyway — the failure mode where egress becomes the dominant bill is structurally impossible. **At the harness layer**: there is no CDN concern because there are no public reads; harness files are read by the navigator from local disk. **The cache-control discipline is non-negotiable**: any file with a mutable URL must have short max-age (or no caching), any file with an immutable URL (content-addressed or version-stamped) must have long max-age and immutable directive — and the rule must be enforced uniformly to avoid the "stale image after upload" class of bugs.

### 5. Access control

| Pattern | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Public bucket** | Everything readable by anyone | Public sites only |
| **Bucket-level IAM** | Access controlled at the bucket | Coarse-grained |
| **Object-level ACLs** | Per-object permissions | Fine-grained but expensive |
| **Presigned URLs** | Time-limited per-request access tokens | The standard pattern for private content |
| **Application-mediated reads** | App backend proxies all reads | Required when reads need business logic |
| **Signed cookies (CDN)** | Cookie grants access to a URL prefix | Streaming services |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Presigned URLs are the standard pattern for private file access, and the time-bound nature (typically minutes to hours) is the right default**: short enough that a leaked URL has limited blast radius, long enough that legitimate use cases work. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: long-lived presigned URLs (days or weeks) that get embedded in long-running processes or shared in chat — these are credentials in disguise and leak the same way. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: presigned URLs default to ≤ 1 hour expiry, and any longer-lived access requires application-mediated reads where the auth check happens per-request.** **At the harness layer**: file access is filesystem-permission based (the navigator owns the files), and the only adjacent concern is "what happens when an in-process sub-agent needs to read a file the parent has access to" — the answer is the agent reads via the parent's filesystem permissions, which is the same trust model as everything else in the harness.

### 6. Lifecycle and storage tiers

| Tier | Latency | Cost | Use case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hot (standard)** | Milliseconds | Highest | Frequently-accessed files |
| **Warm (infrequent access)** | Milliseconds, charged per read | Medium | Backups, occasional access |
| **Cold (archive)** | Minutes to hours to retrieve | Lowest | Long-term retention, compliance |
| **Glacier / Deep Archive** | Hours to days | Cheapest | Legal hold, never-expected-to-read |
| **Lifecycle rules** | Auto-tier or auto-delete by age/access pattern | Operational hygiene |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Lifecycle rules are the operational discipline that prevents object storage costs from growing without bound** — the failure mode is "we never deleted anything and now we're paying $10K/month for files no one has read in two years". **Architectural commitment for the app layer: every bucket has explicit lifecycle rules from the moment of creation**, defining (1) when objects move to cheaper tiers, (2) when objects expire (if at all), (3) which prefixes are exempt. **The connection to data governance**: per-data-class retention from the data governance entry §3 is implemented at the file storage layer via lifecycle rules — if a category of files is on a 30-day retention policy, the lifecycle rule deletes them at 30 days, full stop. **At the harness layer**: the lifecycle question is "how long does the navigator keep old execution logs, generated apps, snapshots", and the answer is configurable per-org with sensible defaults (forever for snapshots and important artifacts; some bounded retention for verbose debug logs). **The forbidden anti-pattern**: assuming "disk is cheap, just keep everything" — this works until it doesn't, and the moment of failure is exactly the moment when finding anything in the bloat is hardest.

### 7. Processing pipelines for media

| Step | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Content type detection** | Magic bytes, not file extension | Required; user-supplied extensions are unreliable |
| **Virus / malware scanning** | ClamAV, managed service (e.g., Lambda + ClamAV) | Required for any user-upload path |
| **Image transformation** | Resize, format conversion, EXIF stripping | Standard for any image upload |
| **Video transcoding** | Encoder (FFmpeg), format conversion, HLS chunking | Standard for video apps |
| **Thumbnail generation** | On upload or on first request | Required for any image gallery |
| **EXIF and metadata stripping** | Remove location data, camera metadata | Privacy requirement |
| **Watermarking** | Add visible or invisible mark | Brand protection, authenticity |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **EXIF stripping is a privacy requirement that most apps forget**, because user-uploaded photos contain GPS coordinates, camera serial numbers, and timestamps that the user did not realize they were sharing. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: every user-uploaded image has EXIF metadata stripped before it's served back, except for fields the user has explicitly opted into preserving.** servicegrid in particular needs this discipline because contractor job photos may contain customer-property GPS data that the customer has not consented to share with the contractor's other parties. **The processing pipeline question (synchronous vs async)**: synchronous processing on upload is simpler and gives the user immediate feedback; async processing via a queue is required for expensive operations (video transcoding) and is the right default for any pipeline that exceeds ~1 second of work. **At the harness layer**: media processing is mostly relevant for screenshots and generated artifacts, and the harness has very little work here — most artifacts are text or structured data, not media.

### 8. Deduplication and storage efficiency

| Mechanism | How it works | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Whole-file dedup** | Hash the file, store once if hash already exists | Standard for content-addressed stores |
| **Block-level dedup** | Split files into blocks, store unique blocks once | Backup systems (Restic, Borg) |
| **Compression** | gzip, zstd, or format-specific | Standard for text and structured data |
| **Format-specific compression** | WebP for images, AV1 for video | Modern format upgrades |
| **Cold-tier compression** | Heavier compression for rarely-read files | Cost optimization |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Whole-file dedup via content addressing is the natural fit for harness artifacts**, and §2's commitment to content-addressed storage gives this for free — two skills that emit identical output share the same byte sequence in the store, two execution logs that contain identical fragments don't share but the larger immutable artifacts do. **Architectural commitment: the harness's content-addressed artifact store is structurally a deduplicating store**, and the dedup ratio is one of the things the harness should track as a storage health metric. **Format-specific compression matters at the app layer**: serving WebP instead of JPEG for images cuts bandwidth by 30-50% and is a one-line change at the CDN layer. AV1 for video is the same story but bigger. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: rolling a custom compression layer when the format itself has a modern replacement — the modern format is almost always better and is supported by every browser the app cares about.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **Local-first as the default for harness artifacts; cloud object storage as a sync target, not source of truth** (from §1). The local-first commitment applied to file storage.
2. **Content-addressed storage for immutable harness artifacts; composite naming (UUID + DB mapping) for mutable user-uploaded content** (from §2). Same Merkle-tree pattern as Git.
3. **Presigned URLs default for app-layer uploads, with multipart for >5MB and resumable for >100MB** (from §3). Takes the application backend out of the data path.
4. **CDN-fronted object storage with content-addressed or version-stamped URLs and long max-age** (from §4). Cache invalidation becomes structurally unnecessary.
5. **Presigned URLs default to ≤1 hour expiry; longer-lived access requires application-mediated reads** (from §5). Long-lived presigned URLs are credentials in disguise.
6. **Every bucket has explicit lifecycle rules from the moment of creation; per-data-class retention from data governance is implemented here** (from §6). Connects to data governance §3.
7. **EXIF stripping as a privacy requirement; servicegrid in particular because of customer-property GPS leakage** (from §7). The forgotten privacy default.
8. **The harness's content-addressed artifact store is structurally a deduplicating store; dedup ratio is a storage health metric** (from §8).

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This entry is a choice-layer entry that resolves cleanly toward the local-first commitment at the harness layer and toward standard managed-provider patterns at the app layer**. The most generative move is **§2: content-addressed storage for immutable artifacts**, which connects the harness's growing artifact store (skills, snapshots, generated apps, model checkpoints) to the broader Merkle-tree pattern shared with Git, IPFS, and Nix — and which gives deduplication for free. The second most generative move is **§6: lifecycle rules as the implementation of per-data-class retention from data governance**, which makes the data governance commitment concrete at a layer that previously only had abstract policy.

**The most concrete near-term unlock** is **adopting content-addressed storage for harness artifacts that should be immutable** — this is small enough to ship as a single skill execution and unlocks dedup, cache-friendly serving, and the composite naming pattern for everything else. **The most architecturally interesting thread** is the recognition that the harness's local-first commitment maps cleanly onto file storage by treating cloud object storage as a sync target rather than a source of truth — the same pattern as the event log, applied to a different data type.

**Layer note**: applies to both layers. Harness file storage is local-first, content-addressed for immutable artifacts, with optional cloud sync per-org. App-layer file storage (servicegrid job photos, dreamtime user content) follows standard production patterns: managed object storage (R2 preferred for cost), CDN-fronted, presigned URL uploads, lifecycle rules, EXIF stripping, format-specific compression at the CDN.

---

## Multi-tenancy

The discipline of serving many independent customers from a single shared system without leaking data between them, without one customer degrading another, and without giving up the operational simplicity of a single deployment. Multi-tenancy is the dimension where SaaS architecture differs most sharply from single-customer architecture, and getting it wrong is one of the most expensive classes of mistake in production software.

The entry exists because multi-tenancy is **app-layer primary** for the orchestrator: servicegrid is the live multi-tenant case (small contractors as paying tenants, each with their own customers, jobs, schedules, and pricing), and the architectural choices made now will determine what's possible later. The harness layer has only adjacent concerns — the orchestrator is more "many workspaces for one navigator" than SaaS multi-tenant — and the entry calls this out explicitly to avoid the over-claim of treating orgs as paying tenants.

This is a **choice-layer** entry **app-layer primary, with limited harness adjacencies**.

### 1. The isolation spectrum (silo / pool / bridge)

| Model | Definition | Cost per tenant | Blast radius |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Silo** | One full stack per tenant (DB, compute, storage) | Highest | Contained per tenant |
| **Bridge** | Shared compute, isolated data store per tenant | Medium | Compute-shared, data-isolated |
| **Pool** | Shared everything, tenant ID is a row attribute | Lowest | Smallest blast contained by code |
| **Hybrid (tier-based)** | Pool for free tier, bridge for paid, silo for enterprise | Mixed | Tier-dependent |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The pool-vs-silo decision is the foundational multi-tenancy choice**, and most teams overestimate the value of silo isolation while underestimating its operational cost. Silos are expensive at every layer (provisioning, deployment, schema migration, monitoring, billing per tenant), and the operational overhead grows linearly with tenant count — a 1,000-tenant silo deployment is 1,000 things to monitor, migrate, and debug. **Architectural commitment for servicegrid: pool-by-default with bridge-tier escape valves for enterprise customers who require dedicated infrastructure for compliance or contract reasons.** The pool model is the only one that scales operationally to thousands of small contractors at the price point servicegrid will charge. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: starting with silos because "isolation feels safer" and discovering at 100 tenants that the operational burden is unsustainable. **The harness adjacency**: the orchestrator's per-org workspaces are structurally siloed (one filesystem directory per org, one event log per org), but the cost is one navigator's disk space rather than one company's cloud bill, so the silo model is fine here for reasons that don't transfer.

### 2. Data isolation in pool tenancy

| Mechanism | Enforcement | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Tenant ID column on every row** | Application-enforced filtering | Simple but fragile |
| **Row-level security (RLS)** | Database-enforced filtering | Postgres RLS, the standard answer |
| **Schema-per-tenant** | One schema per tenant in shared database | Bridge variant; harder ops |
| **Database-per-tenant** | Full database isolation | Silo variant |
| **Connection-pooler tenant routing** | Pooler routes to per-tenant database | Citus, PgBouncer with tenant-aware config |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Row-level security (RLS) in Postgres is the load-bearing mechanism for safe pool tenancy**, and the architectural commitment is to push tenant isolation down to the database layer rather than relying on every query in application code to include the right tenant filter. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: "we'll just remember to add `WHERE tenant_id = ?` to every query" — every team that has tried this has had a tenant data leak within 18 months because one query, one new feature, one ORM lazy-load forgot the filter. **Architectural commitment for servicegrid: Postgres RLS enforced at the row level, with a session-level `SET app.current_tenant_id = ?` that the application sets at request boundary**, and every table that holds tenant-scoped data has an RLS policy. The application code is no longer responsible for getting the filter right — the database refuses to return rows from other tenants regardless of what the query asks for. **This is the same defense-in-depth principle as data governance**: the storage layer enforces the boundary, not the consumer.

### 3. Noisy neighbor mitigation

| Concern | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU starvation** | Per-tenant CPU quotas, fair scheduling | Cgroups, k8s resource limits |
| **Database connection exhaustion** | Per-tenant connection limits | PgBouncer, connection pooling |
| **Slow query monopolization** | Per-tenant query timeouts | Statement timeout, long-query killer |
| **Storage growth runaway** | Per-tenant storage quotas, alerting | Disk quota, billing-driven enforcement |
| **API rate exhaustion** | Per-tenant rate limits | Token bucket per tenant ID |
| **Background job queue starvation** | Per-tenant fair queue | Sidekiq pro fair, custom fair-share schedulers |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Noisy neighbor protection is the operational discipline that pool tenancy requires to actually deliver on its cost promises** — without it, one runaway tenant degrades every other tenant and the cost-of-failure becomes "all customers angry simultaneously". **Architectural commitment for servicegrid: every shared resource (CPU, database connections, query time, storage, API requests, background jobs) has per-tenant limits and the limits are observable and adjustable per tenant.** The default limits are sized for the typical small contractor; enterprise tenants can be granted higher limits as a contract feature. **The connection to cost engineering**: per-tenant resource accounting is the input to per-tenant cost attribution, which is the input to honest pricing. A multi-tenant SaaS that doesn't measure per-tenant cost is essentially flying blind on unit economics. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: setting limits high enough that no one ever hits them, then discovering during the first noisy-neighbor incident that there were effectively no limits. The right limits *will* be hit by some legitimate usage and require adjustment; that's how you know they're real.

### 4. Tenant onboarding and provisioning

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Self-service signup** | Sign up flow that creates a tenant | Standard for product-led growth |
| **Tenant creation API** | Programmatic tenant creation | Required for sales-led, ops-led onboarding |
| **Default seed data per tenant** | Sample data, default configurations | Improves time-to-value |
| **Tenant migration on plan change** | Move tenant from pool to bridge to silo as they grow | Required for tiered offerings |
| **Tenant import / export** | Bulk data import on signup, full export on cancel | Migration in and out |
| **Tenant cloning** | Duplicate a tenant for staging or testing | Required for safe schema migrations |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Tenant provisioning is the operation that gets exercised most often during the early growth phase, and the speed and reliability of provisioning is one of the load-bearing onboarding metrics.** **Architectural commitment for servicegrid: tenant provisioning is a single transactional operation that creates the tenant record, sets up RLS context, seeds default data, sends the welcome email, and is fully rollback-able if any step fails.** This is structurally a saga, and the harness's saga framework would be the right substrate if servicegrid were running on the harness — at the app layer it can be implemented as a database transaction with side-effect orchestration. **Tenant cloning is the underrated feature** because it unlocks safe testing of schema migrations against realistic data, and any pool-tenancy SaaS that doesn't have it will have a production incident from a migration tested against synthetic data that didn't reproduce a real tenant's edge case.

### 5. Per-tenant customization

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Configuration per tenant** | Tenant settings table | Standard, low-risk |
| **Feature flags per tenant** | Flag service with tenant context | Standard SaaS pattern |
| **Custom branding** | Per-tenant theming, logos, domains | Required for white-label |
| **Custom domains** | Tenant uses their own DNS name | Requires SSL provisioning automation |
| **Custom integrations** | Tenant-specific webhooks, OAuth apps | Per-tenant configuration storage |
| **Code-level customization** | Per-tenant code branches, plugins | The forbidden anti-pattern |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Per-tenant code customization is the most expensive multi-tenancy mistake** because it transforms the SaaS into a custom-development shop where every release requires per-tenant testing and every bug fix requires per-tenant porting. **Architectural commitment: customization is configuration, never code.** Anything a tenant needs that isn't already in the configuration vocabulary becomes a feature request that lands in the shared codebase as a configurable option, controlled by a flag or setting. **Custom domains are the highest-leverage customization for servicegrid** because contractors want their customer-facing booking pages to look like their own brand — and the right architecture for this is **per-tenant SSL certificate automation via Let's Encrypt + a wildcard reverse proxy that handles the dynamic cert provisioning**, not per-tenant deployment of separate environments. **The integration story**: tenant-specific webhook destinations and OAuth credentials live in per-tenant configuration storage, and the application reads them at request time to fan out to the tenant's external systems. The same code path serves all tenants; only the configuration differs.

### 6. Per-tenant billing and metering

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Per-seat pricing** | Charge per user per tenant | Simple, well-understood |
| **Usage-based pricing** | Charge per unit of consumption | Requires accurate metering |
| **Tiered plans** | Free / Pro / Enterprise with feature gates | Standard SaaS structure |
| **Usage reporting to Stripe** | Send metering events to subscription manager | Stripe Billing usage records |
| **Tenant cost attribution** | Internal: how much does this tenant cost us | Required for honest unit economics |
| **Bill-shock prevention** | Spending caps, alerts, hard limits | Required for usage-based |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The billing entry (#4) committed to Stripe Billing for subscriptions and Stripe Connect for marketplace patterns, and multi-tenancy is where these commitments cash out.** servicegrid's pricing model is two-sided: the contractor pays a subscription to use the platform (Stripe Billing, monthly recurring), and the platform takes a transaction fee on jobs that flow through the platform (Stripe Connect, per-payment marketplace fee). **Architectural commitment**: the tenant ID at the application layer maps directly to a Stripe customer ID, every billable usage event is sent to Stripe Billing as a usage record, and the per-job marketplace fee is collected via Stripe Connect at the moment of payment. **Per-tenant cost attribution** is the operational discipline required to know whether a tenant is profitable: per-tenant resource accounting (from §3) feeds a daily cost-attribution job that produces "this tenant cost us $X this month, generated $Y of revenue" rows. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: shipping to multi-tenant production without per-tenant cost visibility and discovering a year later that the largest tenants are unprofitable.

### 7. Tenant lifecycle: suspension, deletion, export

| Operation | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Tenant suspension** | Read-only or fully blocked, recoverable | Payment failure, abuse |
| **Tenant deletion** | Hard delete with grace period | GDPR, customer offboarding |
| **Tenant data export** | Full data dump in standard format | GDPR, customer right to data |
| **Tenant data import** | Bulk import on migration in | Migration from competing platforms |
| **Soft delete with retention** | Mark deleted, purge after N days | Standard pattern |
| **Compliance hold** | Override delete for legal reasons | Required for litigation, audit |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Tenant deletion is where multi-tenancy meets data governance**, and the implementation shortcuts (delete by tenant ID across every table, hope nothing was missed) are exactly the failure mode that gets a SaaS sued under GDPR. **Architectural commitment for servicegrid: tenant deletion is a documented procedure that enumerates every store touched and verifies completeness via a post-deletion audit query** ("count rows where tenant_id = X across every table; should be zero"). **The crypto-shredding pattern from data governance §3 transfers**: if PII is stored under a per-tenant key, deletion is destroying the key, and the post-deletion audit becomes "key is destroyed; data is structurally unreadable." **Tenant export is the underrated lifecycle operation** because it's the guarantee that customers can leave, which is the guarantee that lets them trust signing up — a SaaS that makes export hard is signaling that it's planning to lock customers in, and savvy buyers detect this. **Architectural commitment: tenant export is a single operation that produces a complete, machine-readable dump of all tenant data in a standard format (JSON, Parquet, or both), tested as part of the regular release cycle.**

### 8. The harness adjacency: org workspaces are not SaaS tenants

| Concern | App-layer (servicegrid) | Harness (orchestrator) |
|---|---|---|
| **Recipient set** | Many independent paying customers | One navigator with many workspaces |
| **Trust boundary** | Cross-tenant data leakage is catastrophic | Cross-org leakage is the navigator confusing themselves |
| **Noisy neighbor** | One tenant degrades others | One org consumes the navigator's whole budget |
| **Billing** | Per-tenant subscription + usage | Aggregate cost to the navigator |
| **Compliance scope** | Each tenant's data subject to their jurisdiction | The navigator's data only |
| **Provisioning** | Self-service signup, instant | Manual setup by the navigator |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The harness's per-org workspaces are not SaaS tenants in the architecturally meaningful sense**, and the entry exists in part to call this out so the conversational framing doesn't get retroactively imported. **The legitimate harness adjacencies**: (1) **token budget allocation across orgs** — the navigator has a finite monthly LLM budget, and if one org's runaway loop consumes it, the other orgs can't run; the architectural commitment is per-org budget caps that the harness enforces at the model-call layer (this is the same noisy-neighbor pattern, just with the navigator as the only customer). (2) **Compute fairness across orgs** — sub-agents from one org should not starve sub-agents from another org of the navigator's local CPU; the in-process serial-execution model from Concurrency primitives §1 makes this easier, but a long-running org task can still block other orgs from progressing. (3) **Shared dependency failures** — when an external API the harness depends on goes down, all orgs are affected simultaneously; this is structurally a multi-tenant outage even though there's only one customer. **The architectural commitment**: the harness adopts the per-tenant resource accounting discipline from §3 *as if* the orgs were tenants, even though they're not, because the same observability and limit-enforcement primitives are useful for the navigator's own resource hygiene. **The thing the harness explicitly does NOT adopt**: the rest of the multi-tenancy apparatus (RLS, isolation models, per-tenant billing, tenant lifecycle as customer flow), because the trust boundary, the recipient set, and the compliance scope are all single-actor.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **Pool tenancy by default for servicegrid; bridge/silo escape valves only for enterprise customers with explicit contractual reasons** (from §1). Silo-by-default does not scale operationally.
2. **Postgres RLS as the load-bearing mechanism for safe pool tenancy; tenant filtering enforced at the database, not in application code** (from §2). Defense-in-depth — the same principle as data governance.
3. **Per-tenant resource accounting across CPU, DB connections, query time, storage, API requests, and background jobs** (from §3). Without it, pool tenancy fails its cost promise.
4. **Tenant provisioning as a single saga-like transactional operation, fully rollback-able** (from §4). The harness's saga framework would fit if servicegrid were on the harness.
5. **Customization is configuration, never code; custom domains via Let's Encrypt + wildcard reverse proxy** (from §5). Per-tenant code branches are the most expensive multi-tenancy mistake.
6. **Tenant ID maps directly to Stripe customer ID; per-tenant cost attribution is required for honest unit economics** (from §6). Connects to the payment entry's commitments.
7. **Tenant deletion is a documented procedure with post-deletion audit queries; crypto-shredding from data governance transfers** (from §7). Enumerate every store touched.
8. **Harness orgs are not SaaS tenants; the harness adopts per-tenant resource accounting (token budget, compute fairness, shared dependency failures) but not the rest of the apparatus** (from §8). Explicit calibration against the conversational over-claim.

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This entry is a choice-layer entry that resolves toward standard SaaS multi-tenancy patterns at the app layer and toward an explicit non-claim at the harness layer**. The most generative move is **§2: RLS as the database-layer enforcement of tenant boundaries**, because it's the single architectural commitment that prevents the most dangerous class of multi-tenancy bug (cross-tenant data leakage from a missed query filter), and it's enforceable with a one-time setup rather than ongoing code review discipline. The second most generative move is **§8: the explicit framing that harness orgs are not SaaS tenants**, which prevents the conversational over-claim from contaminating the doc and clarifies which multi-tenancy primitives transfer to the harness (resource accounting) and which don't (the rest).

**The most concrete near-term unlock for servicegrid** is **adopting Postgres RLS from day one** — it's much cheaper to set up at the start than to retrofit, and the retrofit failure mode (one missed table for years) is silent until catastrophic. **The most architecturally interesting thread** is the recognition that per-tenant cost attribution (§6) is the input to the cost-engineering entry that will land in entry 22, and that running multi-tenant SaaS without it is structurally flying blind.

**Layer note**: app-layer primary, with limited harness adjacencies. servicegrid is the live multi-tenant case (small contractors as paying tenants, each with their own customers). The full multi-tenancy apparatus (RLS, isolation, per-tenant billing, lifecycle) applies at the app layer. The harness adopts per-tenant resource accounting (token budget, compute fairness, shared dependency failures) for the navigator's own orgs, but does NOT claim to be multi-tenant in the SaaS sense.

---

## A/B testing and experimentation infrastructure

The discipline of running controlled experiments on a live system to learn whether a change is actually an improvement, rather than relying on intuition, opinion, or aggregate metric drift. Experimentation is interesting for the orchestrator because **the harness's learning loop is already a form of online experimentation** — calibration captures comparisons between skill versions, between prompt variants, between model choices — and the standard A/B testing apparatus has primitives the harness can borrow even though the population (N=1 navigator) is unusual.

The entry exists because experimentation is the bridge between "we made a change" and "we know whether the change worked", and most teams either skip it (and ship harm they can't see) or roll their own primitive version (and draw conclusions from underpowered data). The right answer is borrowed wholesale from the population-experimentation literature even when the population is small.

This is a **convergent-pattern resolution** entry for **both layers**.

### 1. Assignment and bucketing

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Deterministic hash** | `hash(user_id + experiment_id) → bucket` | The standard primitive |
| **Sticky assignment** | A user gets the same bucket for the experiment's lifetime | Required for valid analysis |
| **Mutually exclusive experiments** | Layered assignment to avoid interference | Required when running multiple experiments |
| **Holdout populations** | A reserved fraction never sees any experiment | Required for long-term effect measurement |
| **Cluster assignment** | Bucket by team/account, not user | When the experiment unit is a group |
| **Salted bucketing** | Per-experiment salt to randomize independently | Avoids cross-experiment correlation |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Deterministic hash-based assignment is the load-bearing primitive** because it requires no central state — any service that knows the user ID and experiment ID can compute the bucket independently and get the same answer. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: bucketing is computed at the request boundary using a single shared hash function (xxhash or murmur3), salted per experiment, and the resulting bucket is logged with every event downstream.** This means the analysis layer doesn't need to "remember" who was in what bucket — it can recover the assignment from the user ID alone. **The harness adjacency**: with N=1 navigator, traditional bucketing doesn't apply, but the same primitive works for **per-execution variant assignment** — the harness can hash `(execution_id + experiment_id)` to deterministically pick which variant of a skill to run for that execution, and the assignment persists across replays. This is **the bucketing primitive applied to executions instead of users**, and it gives the harness deterministic, replay-stable variant selection without any extra state.

### 2. Exposure logging

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Exposure event** | Log when a user actually sees the variant, not when assigned | Required for valid analysis |
| **Lazy exposure** | Log on first interaction, not on page load | More accurate cohort definition |
| **Per-experiment opt-out** | Some users excluded by policy (employees, internal traffic) | Required for clean populations |
| **Exposure deduplication** | Same user, same experiment, log once | Avoids inflating the denominator |
| **Cross-device exposure** | Stitching exposures across user's devices | Required for any login-aware analysis |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The exposure event vs. the assignment event distinction is the most common source of invalid A/B test results** — analyzing by "who was assigned" instead of "who actually saw the variant" inflates the denominator with users who never reached the variant code path, dilutes the effect, and produces underpowered tests that look like negative results. **Architectural commitment: every experiment fires an explicit exposure event the first time the user actually reaches the variant code, and analysis uses exposure events as the cohort boundary, not assignment.** **The harness equivalent**: every execution that *actually runs* a variant skill emits a `skill_variant_exposed` event into the event log, and the calibration analysis uses exposure events as the cohort boundary. Executions that were assigned a variant but didn't reach the relevant skill don't count.

### 3. Metrics: primary, secondary, guardrail

| Type | Purpose | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary metric** | The single metric the experiment is trying to move | Pre-registered, one per experiment |
| **Secondary metrics** | Supporting metrics that help interpret the primary | Multiple, lower priority |
| **Guardrail metrics** | "Don't make this worse" checks (latency, errors, revenue) | The safety net |
| **Long-term metrics** | Metrics that lag the experiment (retention, LTV) | Require holdout populations |
| **Quality / counter metrics** | Metrics that catch gaming the primary metric | Required for incentive alignment |
| **Per-segment breakdowns** | Metric impact split by user segment | Required for heterogeneous effects |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The pre-registered primary metric is the discipline that prevents post-hoc fishing for significance** — without it, every experiment can be reported as "successful" by picking whichever of the 50 metrics happened to move. **Architectural commitment: every experiment names its primary metric before launch, and post-experiment analysis reports the primary metric first regardless of what the secondary metrics say.** **Guardrail metrics are the most underrated category** because they catch the failure mode where the experiment moved the primary metric *and* broke something else — a checkout button color change that improved click-through but increased payment errors should not ship. **The harness equivalent**: skill version experiments have a primary metric (e.g., "rubric quality score"), guardrail metrics (e.g., "execution latency", "token cost", "error rate"), and the upgrade-proposal pipeline must surface all three — not just the primary — before the navigator approves the upgrade.

### 4. Statistical methods

| Method | Mechanism | Tradeoff |
|---|---|---|
| **Frequentist (t-test, z-test)** | Null hypothesis significance testing, p-values | Standard, well-understood, pitfalls well-documented |
| **Bayesian** | Posterior distribution over effect size | Better for small samples, harder to communicate |
| **Sequential testing** | Continuously valid p-values, early stopping | Required for "should we stop the experiment now?" |
| **CUPED (variance reduction)** | Use pre-experiment data as a covariate to reduce noise | 30-50% sample size reduction, well-established |
| **Bonferroni / FDR correction** | Adjust for multiple comparisons | Required when testing many metrics |
| **Bootstrap** | Resampling-based confidence intervals | When parametric assumptions don't hold |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Sequential testing is the load-bearing primitive that makes "stop the experiment early when it's clearly working or clearly harmful" statistically valid** — naive p-value-based early stopping is invalid because peeking inflates false positive rate, and the right answer is a sequential test (e.g., mSPRT, always-valid p-values from confidence sequences) that supports continuous monitoring. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: any experimentation system that allows operators to monitor results during the experiment must use sequential testing, not naive frequentist tests.** **CUPED is the cheapest variance reduction available** and should be the default for any metric where pre-experiment data exists — it's a one-time engineering investment that effectively reduces sample size requirements by 30-50% across every future experiment. **The harness adjacency**: with N=1, the calibration system is structurally a sequential decision problem — every execution updates a posterior estimate of skill quality, and the decision "is this version better than the previous one" is naturally Bayesian. The frequentist apparatus mostly doesn't apply at the harness layer; the Bayesian apparatus does.

### 5. Sample size and statistical power

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Power calculation** | Required N to detect effect of size E with confidence C | Pre-experiment hygiene |
| **Minimum detectable effect** | Smallest effect the experiment can reliably detect | Used to set realistic expectations |
| **Sample ratio mismatch (SRM)** | Bucket sizes deviate from expected | Indicates a bug in assignment |
| **Variance estimation** | Standard deviation of the metric | Input to power calculation |
| **Relative vs absolute effect** | "% improvement" vs "X more units" | Communication choice |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Power calculation done before the experiment is the discipline that prevents the most common waste**: running an experiment for two weeks and finding "no significant effect" when the experiment was always too small to detect anything but a huge effect. **Architectural commitment: every experiment includes a pre-launch power calculation that names the minimum detectable effect and the required sample size**, and the experiment only launches if those numbers are achievable in the available time. **SRM checks** are the operational early-warning that catches most assignment bugs — if the bucket sizes deviate from expected by more than chance, something is wrong with the assignment layer (often a bug in the hashing, exclusion logic, or exposure logging). **The harness adjacency dissolves the population concern entirely**: with N=1, the relevant sample is "executions of the affected skill", and the statistical question is "how many executions before the calibration signal is reliable" — which is the same power question, just with executions as the unit instead of users. **The right framing: the harness's calibration system is doing N-of-1 sequential experimentation, and the statistical primitives transfer with `executions` substituted for `users`.**

### 6. Experiment lifecycle

| Stage | Mechanism | Gate |
|---|---|---|
| **Proposal** | Hypothesis, primary metric, expected effect, power calculation | Pre-launch review |
| **Implementation** | Variant code, feature flag wiring, exposure logging | Code review |
| **A/A test** | Compare two identical variants to verify the pipeline | Validates assignment + analysis |
| **Ramp-up** | Start at 1% traffic, expand if no guardrail violations | Safety phase |
| **Full launch** | 50/50 split or specified ratio | Main experiment |
| **Decision** | Ship, kill, iterate | Pre-registered decision criteria |
| **Holdout monitoring** | Long-term effect measurement against holdout | Quarterly or longer |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The A/A test is the most underrated lifecycle stage** because it catches infrastructure bugs before they corrupt real experiments — running two identical variants and finding a "significant effect" between them means the analysis pipeline is broken, not that the variants differ. **Architectural commitment: every new experimentation infrastructure ships with an automated A/A test as a smoke test, and the smoke test runs on every deploy of the analysis pipeline.** **The ramp-up stage is the experimentation equivalent of the canary deploy** named in Resilience patterns and the rollout patterns from CI/CD: start at low traffic, monitor for harm, expand only if guardrails hold. **Pre-registered decision criteria** matter because "we'll decide what to do based on the results" is post-hoc and lets motivated reasoning win — the right discipline is "if primary metric improves by ≥X% with no guardrail violations, ship; otherwise kill".

### 7. The relationship to feature flags

| Distinction | Feature flag | A/B test |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Toggle features on/off, gradual rollout | Measure causal effect of a change |
| **Assignment** | Often manual, percentage-based | Randomized, hash-based |
| **Analysis** | None or post-hoc | Pre-registered, statistically rigorous |
| **Sticky** | Sometimes | Always |
| **Mutually exclusive layering** | Rare | Required when running multiple experiments |
| **Lifecycle** | Indefinite (until cleaned up) | Bounded (end at decision time) |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Feature flags and A/B tests are different things that share infrastructure** — both need a way to assign users to a variant at request time, but feature flags are mostly an operational tool (gradual rollout, kill switch, dark launch) while A/B tests are a learning tool (causal inference). **Architectural commitment: the app layer adopts a single feature flag service (LaunchDarkly, GrowthBook, or Unleash) that supports both feature flagging and experimentation as different modes of the same primitive**, rather than separate systems. **GrowthBook is the most aligned commercial option** because it's open-source, self-hostable, and supports both modes; it also supports CUPED out of the box. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: treating feature flags as A/B tests by setting a 50/50 percentage and reading the metrics later — without sticky assignment, exposure logging, and pre-registration, this is post-hoc analysis dressed up as an experiment, and the conclusions are nearly always wrong. **The harness equivalent**: the configuration management entry (entry 14) will resolve the harness's flag layer; experimentation at the harness layer reuses the same primitive with deterministic per-execution assignment.

### 8. The harness as N-of-1 experimentation

| Concept | Standard A/B test | Harness equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Population** | Many users | One navigator with many executions |
| **Unit of randomization** | User | Execution |
| **Assignment** | Hash user ID | Hash execution ID |
| **Exposure** | First page view | First skill invocation in execution |
| **Primary metric** | Conversion, retention, click | Rubric quality score from `complete_skill` |
| **Guardrail metrics** | Latency, errors, revenue | Execution latency, token cost, error rate |
| **Statistical method** | Frequentist t-test | Bayesian sequential, posterior over quality |
| **Decision** | Ship to all users | Promote skill version via upgrade proposal |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The harness's calibration and learning system is structurally an N-of-1 experimentation platform**, and naming this explicitly unlocks borrowing the rest of the experimentation apparatus (deterministic assignment, exposure logging, primary/guardrail metric distinction, sequential analysis, A/A smoke tests). **The architectural commitment**: the harness's skill upgrade pipeline adopts the experimentation lifecycle as a discipline — proposal includes a primary metric and guardrails, implementation includes exposure logging, ramp-up runs the new variant on a fraction of executions before full promotion, decision criteria are pre-registered, and the upgrade proposal surfaces the full primary+guardrail picture for the navigator's approval. **This is the experimentation lifecycle applied to the harness's self-improvement loop**, and it tightens the calibration discipline in exactly the place where motivated reasoning is most dangerous (deciding whether your own changes were improvements). **The connection to ML systems infrastructure**: the calibration system already named in ML systems §6 IS the N-of-1 experimentation system, and the experimentation entry sharpens what good calibration looks like. **The connection to cost engineering**: every harness experiment has a token-cost guardrail because skill quality improvements that triple the cost are not always net-positive, and the only way to know is to measure both.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **Deterministic hash-based bucketing computed at the request boundary, salted per experiment** (from §1). The same primitive applies to per-execution variant assignment at the harness layer.
2. **Exposure events as the cohort boundary, not assignment events** (from §2). Most invalid A/B results come from this distinction being missed.
3. **Pre-registered primary metric + guardrail metrics; upgrade proposals surface all three** (from §3). Prevents post-hoc fishing and prevents the "improved primary, broke something else" failure mode.
4. **Sequential testing for any monitorable experiment; CUPED as default variance reduction; Bayesian methods for the harness's N-of-1 case** (from §4). Naive peeking is statistically invalid; CUPED is free.
5. **Pre-launch power calculations and SRM checks as required hygiene; the same primitives transfer to N-of-1 with executions as the unit** (from §5). Don't run experiments that can't detect realistic effects.
6. **A/A test as a smoke test on the analysis pipeline; ramp-up as the experimentation equivalent of canary deploys** (from §6). Catches infrastructure bugs before they corrupt real experiments.
7. **Single feature flag service supporting both flags and experiments (GrowthBook is the most aligned option)** (from §7). They're different things but they share infrastructure.
8. **The harness's calibration system IS an N-of-1 experimentation platform; the upgrade proposal pipeline adopts the experimentation lifecycle as discipline** (from §8). Borrowed wholesale from population experimentation with executions substituted for users.

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This entry is a convergent-pattern resolution entry**: most of the experimentation apparatus already has a structural analog in the harness's calibration and learning loop, and the walk's job is to recognize the equivalence and adopt the discipline. The most generative move is **§8: the harness as N-of-1 experimentation** — once this framing is adopted, the entire experimentation literature becomes legitimate prior art for the calibration system, and the upgrade proposal pipeline gets a clear architectural shape (proposal → A/A smoke → ramp-up → decision → holdout monitoring).

**The most concrete near-term unlock** is **adding guardrail metrics to the upgrade proposal pipeline** — currently, the primary signal is the rubric quality score, but execution latency, token cost, and error rate are not consistently surfaced in proposals, which means the navigator can approve quality improvements that are net-negative on cost or speed without seeing the tradeoff. **The most architecturally interesting thread** is the **mapping from population experimentation primitives onto N-of-1 sequential calibration**, which is a small literature gap (population statistics dominates the field) that the harness happens to need.

**Layer note**: applies to both layers. Harness experimentation is N-of-1 sequential, with executions as the unit, Bayesian methods, and the existing calibration system as the substrate. App-layer experimentation (servicegrid pricing tests, dreamtime onboarding tests) follows standard population experimentation: GrowthBook or LaunchDarkly as the flag/experimentation service, deterministic assignment, exposure logging, primary + guardrail metrics, sequential analysis with CUPED, A/A smoke tests, ramp-up rollouts.

---

## Networking and protocols at depth

The discipline of moving bytes between processes across unreliable networks — TCP, UDP, TLS, HTTP versions, QUIC, connection pooling, congestion control, MTU, NAT, DNS resolution. This entry sits one layer below the existing API protocols entry: that one is about *application-level shape* (REST, gRPC, GraphQL, WebSockets, SSE), this one is about *what's happening underneath* and where the failure modes live.

The entry exists because every distributed system spends compute and complexity on the network layer whether it acknowledges it or not, and the choices that look like "we'll just use HTTP" actually carry decisions about head-of-line blocking, connection reuse, TLS handshake cost, name resolution latency, and congestion behavior. Most of these don't matter at the harness layer because the harness has no networking — but they matter enormously at the moment the harness reaches out to the LLM API, and they matter at every layer of any app the orchestrator ships.

This is a **doesn't-apply-but-transfers** entry **for both layers, mostly app-layer relevant**.

### 1. TCP vs UDP and the transport choice

| Protocol | Guarantees | Cost | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TCP** | Ordered, reliable, congestion-controlled | Handshake + ack overhead | Almost everything by default |
| **UDP** | Unordered, unreliable, no congestion control | Minimal | DNS, real-time media, custom protocols on top |
| **QUIC (over UDP)** | Reliable, congestion-controlled, multiplexed, 0-RTT | Designed-in efficiency | The new HTTP/3 default |
| **SCTP** | Multi-stream, message-oriented, reliable | Limited support | Telecom, mostly historical |
| **WebRTC data channel** | Application-level reliable-or-unreliable, P2P | Browser-specific | Real-time peer apps |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **TCP is the right default for almost everything** and the choice to deviate should be deliberate, not aesthetic — UDP-based custom protocols are appealing in benchmarks and dangerous in production because everything TCP gives you (ordering, reliability, congestion control, NAT traversal, middlebox compatibility) has to be re-implemented or accepted as broken. **QUIC is the interesting modern exception** because it's UDP-based but provides everything TCP provides plus connection migration and 0-RTT resumption. **At the harness layer**: there is essentially no transport choice to make because the harness doesn't expose network services, and its outbound calls (to the LLM API) use whatever HTTP version the SDK provides. **At the app layer**: TCP is the default; QUIC matters once HTTP/3 is the default browser behavior, and the right answer is to terminate HTTP/3 at the CDN layer (Cloudflare, Fastly) and let the origin remain HTTP/2 or HTTP/1.1.

### 2. The HTTP version stack: HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, HTTP/3

| Version | Transport | Multiplexing | Head-of-line blocking | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **HTTP/1.1** | TCP | None (one request per connection) | At the connection level | Universal compatibility |
| **HTTP/1.1 with pipelining** | TCP | Limited (rarely deployed) | At the connection level | Effectively dead |
| **HTTP/2** | TCP + TLS | Stream-multiplexed within one connection | At the TCP level (one lost packet stalls all streams) | Most modern stacks |
| **HTTP/3** | QUIC over UDP | Stream-multiplexed, no TCP head-of-line | None at transport | Modern, gaining adoption |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **HTTP/2 vs HTTP/1.1 is the most underrated upgrade for any API client that makes many requests to the same origin** — HTTP/1.1 requires opening multiple TCP connections (and multiple TLS handshakes) in parallel, while HTTP/2 multiplexes everything over one connection with one handshake. **The harness specifically benefits**: every LLM API call to the same provider can share a single connection, eliminating per-call TLS overhead (which can be tens to hundreds of milliseconds per request). **Architectural commitment for the harness: HTTP/2 (or HTTP/3 if the SDK supports it) is the default for outbound API calls, with explicit connection pooling tuned to keep one warm connection per origin.** **The HTTP/2 head-of-line blocking trap**: a single dropped packet at the TCP layer stalls every multiplexed stream until retransmission, which means HTTP/2 can be *worse* than HTTP/1.1 on lossy networks. HTTP/3 was designed specifically to fix this by moving multiplexing into the QUIC layer where each stream is independent. **At the app layer**: HTTP/3 at the CDN edge with HTTP/2 at the origin is the standard 2026 deployment.

### 3. TLS: handshake, certificates, performance

| Concept | Mechanism | Cost / risk |
|---|---|---|
| **TLS 1.3 handshake** | One round-trip in the common case | Faster than TLS 1.2 |
| **0-RTT resumption** | Resume previous session with no handshake | Replay attack risk; should not be used for state-changing requests |
| **Certificate chain validation** | Walk chain to a trusted root | Network-dependent (OCSP), often skipped or stapled |
| **OCSP stapling** | Server attaches revocation status | The right answer for revocation freshness |
| **mTLS** | Client also presents a certificate | Required for service-to-service trust |
| **Certificate transparency** | All issued certs published to public logs | Catches mis-issuance |
| **Let's Encrypt + ACME** | Automated certificate provisioning | The right default for any public service |
