**Architectural inspiration thread:** **TLS handshake cost dominates short-lived HTTP requests**, and the architectural answer is connection reuse rather than handshake optimization — keep connections alive long enough that the TLS cost amortizes across many requests. **Architectural commitment: every HTTP client in the harness or any orchestrator-built app uses persistent connections with explicit keep-alive timeouts long enough for the workload's request pattern.** **mTLS matters at the app layer** for service-to-service trust (e.g., servicegrid mobile clients authenticating to the API beyond just bearer tokens), and the right substrate is the auth entry's commitment to managed providers — modern identity providers handle mTLS as a feature. **Let's Encrypt + ACME automation is the only acceptable answer for public-facing certs in 2026** — manual cert provisioning is the source of every "cert expired in production" outage in history. **The harness layer is essentially TLS-free internally** because there's no service-to-service traffic; only outbound calls to managed APIs that handle TLS as part of the SDK.

### 4. Connection pooling and reuse

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Per-origin pool** | Reusable connections grouped by destination | Standard HTTP client default |
| **Idle timeout** | Connections closed after N seconds idle | Tradeoff: reuse vs server resources |
| **Max connections per origin** | Cap on parallel connections | Prevents overwhelming the server |
| **Connection eviction** | Remove unhealthy connections from pool | Required for long-lived processes |
| **Pool warm-up** | Pre-establish N connections at startup | Eliminates first-request latency |
| **Server-initiated close detection** | Detect FIN before reusing a stale connection | Required for correctness |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Connection pool tuning is one of the highest-leverage performance optimizations available to any HTTP-heavy service**, and most teams accept default pool settings that are wrong for their workload. **The harness specifically**: the LLM API call pattern is "many requests to one origin per session", which is exactly the pattern HTTP/2 + connection pooling was designed for; the architectural commitment is to verify that the LLM SDK is reusing one HTTP/2 connection per provider rather than opening fresh connections per call (which would impose tens of ms of TLS handshake on every call). **The forbidden anti-pattern**: "we have 1000 worker threads each with their own HTTP client and their own pool of 100 connections" — this creates 100,000 potential connections to the upstream service and exhausts ports, file descriptors, and the server's connection table. The right answer is one shared HTTP client across all workers with a sensibly-sized pool. **Connection eviction matters for long-lived processes** because TCP connections can become "half-open" in ways that aren't visible until the next read or write fails — health checks on idle connections catch this.

### 5. Congestion control and the network as a shared resource

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **TCP slow start** | Begin with small congestion window, ramp up | Default TCP behavior |
| **TCP Reno / NewReno** | Classic congestion avoidance | Default until BBR |
| **CUBIC** | Modern default in Linux | Most current servers |
| **BBR (Google)** | Bottleneck bandwidth + RTT-based | Better on lossy / long-fat networks |
| **Buffer bloat** | Excessive buffering hides congestion signal | The dominant tail-latency cause |
| **ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification)** | Routers signal congestion before dropping | Underdeployed |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Buffer bloat is the dominant cause of bad tail latency on consumer networks**, and the architectural answer at the application layer is mostly to use protocols that have congestion-aware behavior built in (HTTP/2, HTTP/3) rather than rolling custom transports. **At the harness layer**: the navigator's local network conditions affect every LLM API call, and the practical defense is the resilience patterns entry's commitment to retries with backoff plus the local context size discipline (smaller requests = less sensitivity to bandwidth dips). **At the app layer**: BBR is the right default congestion control on the server side; most modern Linux distributions ship with CUBIC and switching to BBR is a one-line sysctl change that meaningfully improves tail latency on lossy paths. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: assuming the network is reliable and high-bandwidth in user-facing applications — most users on most networks most of the time are not.

### 6. DNS: name resolution as a load-bearing dependency

| Concept | Mechanism | Failure mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Recursive resolver** | Local DNS server walks the chain | Cache poisoning, slow resolution |
| **Authoritative server** | The source of truth for a zone | DDoS target |
| **TTL** | Cache lifetime per record | Tradeoff: freshness vs query load |
| **DNS over HTTPS / TLS** | Encrypt resolver queries | Privacy + censorship resistance |
| **Anycast DNS** | Same IP served from many locations | Standard for major resolvers |
| **DNS round-robin** | Multiple A records, client picks | Crude load distribution |
| **GeoDNS** | Different answers per client location | Edge routing primitive |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **DNS is the most under-monitored load-bearing dependency in most production systems** — slow DNS resolution adds latency invisibly, DNS outages take down everything that resolves names, and DNS configuration mistakes (wrong TTL, missing records) are among the most common production incidents. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: DNS for production services lives at a managed authoritative provider with anycast** (Cloudflare DNS, Route53, NS1) **rather than self-hosted, and TTLs are deliberately chosen per-record-type** — long TTLs for static records, short TTLs for failover targets. **DoH / DoT** is the right client-side default for any application that cares about query privacy and censorship resistance. **The harness adjacency**: the harness depends on DNS only at the moment of API calls, and the resilience patterns entry's defense (retries with backoff) covers transient DNS failures; the more interesting harness-relevant question is whether the navigator's local resolver introduces latency, and the answer is "use 1.1.1.1 or 8.8.8.8 as the local resolver because they're faster than most ISP defaults".

### 7. Load balancing: L4 vs L7

| Layer | Mechanism | Tradeoff |
|---|---|---|
| **L4 (TCP)** | Forwards TCP connections without inspecting payload | Fast, protocol-agnostic, no application awareness |
| **L7 (HTTP)** | Inspects and routes by HTTP headers, paths, methods | Application-aware, slower, protocol-specific |
| **DSR (Direct Server Return)** | LB receives request, server replies directly to client | Removes LB from response path; eliminates a bottleneck |
| **Consistent hashing** | Same client routes to same backend | Required for sticky cache patterns |
| **Health-checked pools** | LB removes unhealthy backends automatically | Required for any real deployment |
| **Weighted routing** | Send X% to backend A, Y% to backend B | Canary deployments, gradual rollout |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **L7 load balancing is the right default for most modern applications** because path-based routing, header-based routing, and HTTP-aware features (compression, request retries, circuit breaking) are all worth more than the small performance penalty over L4. **The architectural commitment for the app layer**: managed L7 load balancing (Cloudflare, AWS ALB, GCP HTTPS LB) is the default, with weighted routing as the canary primitive and consistent hashing where session-affinity matters. **At the harness layer**: there is no load balancer because there are no peer instances to balance across; the harness is one local process serving one navigator. **The relationship to service discovery**: the service discovery entry's commitment to in-process service registry plus failure-by-stakes split means the harness has no need for the load-balancing apparatus at all — load balancing is structurally about distributing traffic across redundant peers, and the harness has no redundant peers.

### 8. NAT, MTU, and the operational gotchas

| Concept | Mechanism | Failure mode |
|---|---|---|
| **NAT traversal** | Translation between private and public addresses | Breaks long-lived connections, P2P |
| **NAT timeout** | Stateful NAT entries expire | Long-idle connections drop silently |
| **Path MTU discovery** | Find the smallest MTU on the path | Broken when ICMP is filtered (often) |
| **MTU mismatch fragmentation** | Packets too large get fragmented or dropped | Mysterious connection failures |
| **TCP keep-alive** | Periodic empty packets to keep NAT open | Required for long-lived connections |
| **ICMP filtering** | Common at firewalls; breaks PMTUD | Causes the "everything works except large transfers" bug |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **NAT timeout and MTU discovery failures are the source of an enormous category of "intermittent and mysterious" network bugs** that nobody believes are network bugs because the network "obviously works". **Architectural commitment: any long-lived connection over the public internet enables TCP keep-alive at the application layer** with intervals shorter than the typical NAT timeout (~5 minutes). **The harness adjacency**: the harness's outbound LLM API calls are short enough that NAT timeout doesn't matter, but any long-running streaming session (an SSE stream from the LLM API, for example) needs keep-alive to survive transient NAT cleanup. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: building peer-to-peer features that assume direct connectivity will work — without WebRTC (which handles NAT traversal via STUN/TURN/ICE), direct P2P is broken for the majority of consumer networks. **At the app layer**: any P2P feature means adopting WebRTC or accepting that it only works for the subset of users with cooperative NAT.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **TCP is the default; QUIC matters at the CDN edge for HTTP/3 termination, with HTTP/2 at the origin** (from §1). The architectural choice is "where in the stack does HTTP/3 enter".
2. **HTTP/2 default for outbound API calls; one warm connection per origin** (from §2). The harness's LLM API calls benefit specifically from HTTP/2 multiplexing.
3. **TLS handshake cost is amortized by connection reuse, not handshake optimization; Let's Encrypt + ACME is the only acceptable cert automation** (from §3).
4. **One shared HTTP client with a sensibly-sized pool, not per-worker pools; connection eviction for half-open connections in long-lived processes** (from §4).
5. **BBR as the default congestion control; buffer bloat is the dominant tail-latency cause; smaller requests are more network-resilient** (from §5).
6. **DNS at a managed authoritative provider with anycast, deliberate per-record TTLs, DoH/DoT for client privacy** (from §6).
7. **L7 load balancing default at app layer; the harness has no load balancer because it has no redundant peers** (from §7).
8. **TCP keep-alive at the application layer for any long-lived connection over the public internet; NAT timeout is the source of "intermittent and mysterious" bugs** (from §8).

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This is a doesn't-apply-but-transfers entry**: most of the networking apparatus has no direct application at the harness layer (no servers, no load balancers, no peer-to-peer, no service mesh) but the parts that do apply (HTTP/2 connection reuse to the LLM API, TLS handshake amortization, application-layer keep-alive on streaming connections) are load-bearing for the navigator's experienced latency. The most generative observation is **§4: connection pool tuning as a high-leverage harness optimization** — the navigator's LLM API latency is largely TLS handshake + connection establishment, and getting the SDK to reuse one HTTP/2 connection per provider eliminates that overhead per call.

**The most concrete near-term unlock at the harness layer** is **verifying that the LLM SDK reuses HTTP/2 connections rather than opening fresh per call** — this is a one-time investigation with potentially large per-request latency improvements. **The most architecturally interesting thread** is the recognition that the harness's near-zero networking surface area is itself a load-bearing simplification: no L4/L7 load balancers, no service mesh, no DNS to operate, no NAT traversal, no TCP keep-alive on internal traffic, no congestion control to tune. The networking entry's biggest architectural insight is **how much complexity the local-first commitment dissolves** — every section in this entry is much simpler at the harness layer than at the app layer, by design.

**Layer note**: applies primarily to the app layer. Harness networking surface area is essentially zero except for outbound calls to managed APIs (LLMs, payment processors, etc.), where the relevant disciplines are connection reuse, HTTP/2, TLS amortization, and application-layer keep-alive on long-lived streams. App-layer networking (servicegrid customer traffic, dreamtime user requests) follows standard production discipline: HTTP/3 at the CDN edge, HTTP/2 at origin, managed L7 load balancing, BBR congestion control, managed authoritative DNS, automated cert provisioning.

---

## Configuration management and feature flags

The discipline of getting the right values into a running system without hardcoding them, without restart, and without losing track of who changed what when. Configuration is interesting for the orchestrator because the harness already has config for everything from API keys to skill paths to slack channels to per-org settings, and the question of "where does config live and how does it change" is currently answered ad-hoc per subsystem.

The entry exists because configuration is the dimension that quietly accumulates the most operational debt — every "small flag added for the experiment" stays in the codebase forever, every secret in a config file becomes a credential leak risk, every drift between environments becomes the source of "works on my machine" bugs. The right architecture treats config as a first-class subsystem with the same rigor as code.

This is a **choice-layer** entry for **both layers**.

### 1. The configuration taxonomy

| Type | Example | Mutability | Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Build-time constants** | API version, feature compile flags | Immutable per build | Low |
| **Environment config** | DB URL, API endpoints, log level | Per-environment, restart-required | Low to medium |
| **Secrets** | API keys, OAuth client secrets, signing keys | Per-environment, rotation-required | High |
| **Runtime config** | Throttle rates, timeouts, sampling rates | Hot-reloadable | Low |
| **Feature flags** | Enable/disable code paths | Per-request, per-user | Low |
| **User preferences** | Theme, notifications, locale | Per-user | Low |
| **Tenant config** | Per-tenant customization values | Per-tenant | Medium |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The most common configuration mistake is treating all of these as the same thing**, dumping them all into one giant `config.yml` or environment-variable namespace, and then suffering the union of all their failure modes. **Architectural commitment: every value gets classified into exactly one of these categories before it's stored, and each category has its own storage mechanism, change discipline, and audit trail.** **Build-time constants live in code**, environment config lives in a per-environment file or environment variables, secrets live in a managed secrets store (never in environment variables checked into git), runtime config lives in a hot-reloadable store with audit logs, feature flags live in a flag service, user preferences live in a database table, tenant config lives in per-tenant settings tables. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: `.env` files containing both database URLs and OAuth secrets and feature flags and user preferences — because the appropriate change discipline differs by category, mixing them means the most strict discipline (secrets rotation, audit) is either applied uniformly (pain) or selectively (drift).

### 2. Where configuration lives

| Storage | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Code constants** | `const X = 5` | Build-time only |
| **Environment variables** | Set by the process launcher | Twelve-factor default; deployable but ephemeral |
| **Config files** | YAML, TOML, JSON loaded on startup | Local-first, version-controllable |
| **Config service** | Centralized config server (Consul KV, etcd) | Distributed systems with many services |
| **Database table** | Settings stored alongside data | App-specific, tenant-specific config |
| **Secrets manager** | AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Doppler | Required for high-sensitivity values |
| **Feature flag service** | LaunchDarkly, GrowthBook, Unleash | Flag-specific |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Twelve-factor "config in environment variables" is the right default for cloud-deployed apps but the wrong default for local-first systems** because environment variables are process-scoped, lose their values on restart, and don't compose well with file-based persistence. **Architectural commitment for the harness: configuration lives in version-controlled config files at the org level, with environment variable overrides for ephemeral experiments and a managed secrets store for sensitive values.** **The harness's existing pattern** of `.claude/CLAUDE.md` for behavior config, `data/proactive-messaging/proactive-messaging-config.json` for service config, and environment variables for secrets is structurally close to right but inconsistent — the architectural discipline is to make this pattern explicit rather than implicit. **At the app layer**: managed secrets stores (Doppler is the most ergonomic option, AWS Secrets Manager the most enterprise-aligned) are non-negotiable for production secrets; rolling your own is the path to credential leaks.

### 3. The secret-management substrate

| Mechanism | Sovereignty | Trust model |
|---|---|---|
| **Plaintext file** | Full | Anyone with disk access has secrets |
| **OS keyring** | Full | Process running as user has access |
| **`age` / `sops` encrypted files** | Full | Encrypted-at-rest, decrypt key required |
| **HashiCorp Vault** | Self-hostable | Centralized secret service with policies |
| **Cloud secrets manager** | Cloud-dependent | Managed service trust |
| **Hardware security module (HSM)** | Full | Keys never leave hardware |
| **Encrypted env files via direnv** | Full | Per-directory secret loading |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Secrets in plaintext config files are the failure mode that catches every "we'll fix it later" team**, and the moment of failure is usually a public git push. **Architectural commitment for the harness: secrets are stored in a sovereign-compatible encrypted store (`sops` with `age` keys, or the macOS keychain) and never written to disk in plaintext.** **`sops` is the most aligned tool** because it lets the secrets file live in version control (encrypted) while keeping the decryption key on the navigator's machine. The harness's near-term unlock named in Auth §6 (a local secrets store) is exactly this commitment. **At the app layer**: managed secrets stores integrated with the deployment platform (Doppler ↔ Railway, AWS Secrets Manager ↔ AWS apps) are the right answer; the local-first pattern doesn't transfer because the threat model is different (the deployed app's process is the only consumer, not a human navigator).

### 4. Schema validation and config-as-code

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Schema definition** | JSON Schema, Zod, Pydantic | Catch typos at load time |
| **Type-safe config loaders** | Generated types from schema | Standard for typed languages |
| **Config validation in CI** | Lint configs against schema before deploy | Catches bad configs before rollout |
| **Config diffing in PRs** | Show config changes alongside code | Required for review |
| **Default values + override layering** | Defaults → env → user override | Standard pattern |
| **Required vs optional fields** | Loader fails fast on missing required | Better than discovering at use time |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Schema-validated, type-safe configuration is the discipline that catches the largest class of config bugs** — typos in field names, wrong types, missing required values — at load time rather than at use time. **The orchestrator already commits to Zod for runtime validation** (CLAUDE.md names this), and the architectural commitment is to extend that discipline to every config file: every config has a Zod schema, the loader fails fast if the file doesn't validate, and the schema is the single source of truth for what valid config looks like. **At the app layer**: the same discipline applies, with schema validation in CI to catch bad configs before deploy. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: untyped config maps (`config["foo"]["bar"]`) that throw at use time instead of load time — these spread the failure point across the codebase and make debugging take orders of magnitude longer than it should.

### 5. Hot reload and dynamic configuration

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **File-watcher reload** | Re-read config on file modification | Standard for dev; risky for prod |
| **SIGHUP-triggered reload** | Send signal to process to reload | Classic Unix pattern |
| **Polling-based reload** | Periodically refetch from a config service | Standard for distributed systems |
| **Push-based reload** | Config service notifies subscribers | Lower latency, more complex |
| **Atomic swap** | New config validated before replacing old | Required for safety |
| **Versioned config snapshots** | Roll back to previous config on failure | Required for safe rollback |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Hot-reload is most useful for runtime tuning (rate limits, sampling rates, timeouts) and most dangerous for structural changes (database URLs, schema definitions)**, and the architectural commitment is to draw the line explicitly: hot-reloadable values are a deliberate subset of all config, not a default for everything. **At the harness layer**: the relevant hot-reloadable values are token budget caps, model selection, slack notification preferences, and logging verbosity — small enough to enumerate, useful enough to support live changes, low-risk enough that a bad change is recoverable. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: hot-reload everything and discover that a typo in a database URL takes the system down silently because the loader picked up the bad value on the next file watch. **Atomic swap with validation** is the safety mechanism: the new config is parsed and validated in full before being installed, and the old config remains active if the new one fails to validate.

### 6. Feature flag taxonomy

| Type | Lifetime | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Release flag** | Days to weeks | Gate code that's not ready for everyone |
| **Experiment flag** | Weeks to months | A/B test infrastructure (covered in entry 12) |
| **Permission flag** | Permanent | Access control by user/tier/role |
| **Ops flag (kill switch)** | Permanent | Emergency disable for runaway features |
| **Long-term toggle** | Indefinite | Never-cleaned-up flag (the debt category) |
| **Dynamic config flag** | Runtime tuning | Effectively a hot-reloadable config value |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Distinguishing flag types matters because each has a different lifecycle and a different cleanup discipline.** **Release flags MUST be deleted within weeks of full rollout** — a release flag that survives past its purpose is technical debt that compounds with every code path that depends on it. **Experiment flags belong to the experimentation infrastructure** named in entry 12 and have their own bounded lifecycle (the experiment itself). **Permission flags are permanent by design** and behave more like access control than feature flags. **Ops flags / kill switches are permanent by design** and exist for emergencies — they should be testable in production via deliberate exercise, not "hopefully it works when we need it". **Long-term toggles** are the failure category: flags that were release flags or experiment flags and never got cleaned up. **Architectural commitment: every flag is created with an explicit type tag and an explicit owner; release flags have a deletion deadline and the deadline is enforced by tooling that fails CI when the deadline is past.**

### 7. Flag debt and the cleanup discipline

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Flag inventory** | Generated list of all flags in the codebase | Required to see the debt |
| **Flag age reporting** | How long has each flag been live | The cleanup signal |
| **Stale flag warnings** | CI fails when flags exceed age limits | Enforces deletion |
| **Dead flag detection** | Flags whose code paths are never reached | The garbage to collect |
| **Flag cleanup as code review check** | "Did you delete the flag for the feature you're shipping?" | Cultural reinforcement |
| **Flag service usage analytics** | Which flags are checked, by whom, how often | Operational signal |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Flag debt is the dimension where the experimentation entry's discipline pays back**: once flags are typed with explicit lifecycles, the cleanup signal is automatable (every release flag past its deadline shows up in CI as a failure), and flag debt stops being a thing teams hand-wave at and starts being a thing the tooling enforces. **The most common organizational failure**: a feature flag service with thousands of flags, hundreds of which are dead, dozens of which are mistakenly checked from code that's also dead — and nobody is responsible for cleanup because cleanup feels like overhead with no upside. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: flag inventory is automated, age limits are enforced in CI, and flag deletion is part of every release that depends on a flag transition.** **The harness adjacency**: the harness's flag surface area is small (currently dominated by per-org config that isn't really flag-shaped), and the discipline matters less in absolute terms but transfers in principle.

### 8. Configuration drift, audit, and GitOps

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Configuration drift** | Per-environment configs diverge over time | The bug source |
| **Configuration audit log** | Every change tracked with who/when/why | Required for compliance and debugging |
| **GitOps for config** | Config in git, deploys triggered by commits | Standard 2026 pattern |
| **Per-environment overlays** | Base config + per-env overrides (Kustomize, Helm) | Standard for k8s deployments |
| **Config rollback** | Revert to previous config snapshot | Required for incident response |
| **Drift detection** | Compare running config to source-of-truth | Catches manual edits |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Configuration drift between environments is the source of nearly every "works in staging, broken in prod" incident**, and the architectural answer is to make config a single source of truth in git, with environment overlays rather than full separate copies. **GitOps is the right default for any deployed system**: config lives in git, changes are PRs reviewed like code, deploys are triggered by merges, and the running configuration is verifiable against the git source of truth at any time. **The audit trail**: config changes recorded as git commits inherit the entire git audit infrastructure (who, when, why, reviewed-by) for free, which is one more reason to prefer git-backed config over UI-driven config services that don't have equivalent audit. **The harness adjacency**: harness config already lives in version-controlled files (`.claude/`, per-org config directories), and the discipline is mostly already in place; the architectural commitment is to ensure that *all* harness config follows this pattern rather than having some values escape into UI-edited stores. **The connection to the event log**: any harness config change should be an event in the log so that the navigator can replay "what was my config when this thing broke" — this is the same audit-log-from-event-log pattern as data governance §5.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **Every config value classified into exactly one of seven categories (build-time / env / secret / runtime / flag / user pref / tenant); each gets its own storage and discipline** (from §1). Mixing categories is the dominant config mistake.
2. **Harness config lives in version-controlled files with environment variable overrides for ephemeral experiments and a managed secrets store for sensitive values** (from §2). Make the existing pattern explicit.
3. **`sops` + `age` for sovereign-compatible encrypted secret storage at the harness layer; managed secrets stores at the app layer** (from §3). Aligns with the auth entry's local secrets store unlock.
4. **Every config file has a Zod schema; loader fails fast on validation; schema is the single source of truth for valid config** (from §4). Extends the existing Zod discipline to config.
5. **Hot-reload is opt-in for a deliberate subset of config (rate limits, model selection, slack prefs); atomic swap with validation as the safety mechanism** (from §5).
6. **Flag taxonomy with explicit type tags (release / experiment / permission / ops / long-term); release flags have deletion deadlines enforced by CI** (from §6).
7. **Flag inventory automated; age limits enforced in CI; flag cleanup is part of every release** (from §7). The discipline that prevents flag debt.
8. **GitOps for config: config in git, environment overlays, audit trail from git history; harness config changes recorded as events in the event log** (from §8). Same audit-log-from-event-log pattern as data governance.

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This is a choice-layer entry that mostly resolves toward existing harness patterns made explicit and existing app-layer patterns adopted from convention.** The most generative move is **§1: the seven-category configuration taxonomy**, because it surfaces that the dominant configuration mistake is *putting things in the wrong category*, and once each value is categorized correctly the storage, discipline, and audit fall out per-category rather than uniformly. The second most generative move is **§3: `sops` + `age` as the harness's sovereign secret store**, which is small enough to ship as a single skill execution and unlocks the rest of the auth entry's near-term commitments.

**The most concrete near-term unlock at the harness layer** is **adopting `sops`-encrypted secrets in the existing config directories** — this gets secrets out of plaintext config files and out of environment variables, into a version-controllable encrypted store, with the decryption key on the navigator's machine. **The most architecturally interesting thread** is the recognition that the harness's existing pattern (version-controlled config files, environment overrides for experiments, secrets in `.env`) is structurally close to the right answer but inconsistent — the architectural commitment is consistency, not new mechanisms.

**Layer note**: applies to both layers. Harness config is version-controlled files + `sops`-encrypted secrets + Zod schema validation + hot-reload for a deliberate subset of values + GitOps via the existing repo + audit via event log. App-layer config (servicegrid, dreamtime) follows standard production discipline: managed secrets stores (Doppler / AWS Secrets Manager), GitOps with environment overlays, schema validation in CI, single feature flag service for both flags and experiments (GrowthBook), automated flag inventory and age enforcement.

---

## CI/CD, infrastructure-as-code, and container orchestration

The discipline of getting code from a developer's machine into production safely, repeatably, and reversibly. This entry covers continuous integration (build + test on every change), continuous deployment (automated rollout to environments), infrastructure-as-code (the substrate the apps run on, defined in version-controlled declarative files), and container orchestration (running many container instances reliably across many machines).

The entry exists because the orchestrator already has a distribution skill and a distribution loop, and the architectural choices baked into them (GitHub Actions, Vercel for web apps, Railway for persistent servers, tarball releases as the third target) have implications worth naming explicitly. It also exists to draw the line between what the harness layer needs (essentially nothing) and what the app layer needs (the full apparatus, but with managed-provider defaults that absorb most of the complexity).

This is a **choice-layer** entry **for both layers, mostly app-layer relevant**.

### 1. The CI pipeline

| Stage | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Push, PR, schedule, manual | Standard |
| **Checkout** | Fetch code at the triggering commit | Standard |
| **Dependency install** | Cached when possible | Standard |
| **Lint** | Fast, fail-early style checks | Standard |
| **Type check** | Compiler-level correctness | Standard for typed languages |
| **Unit tests** | Fast, isolated correctness checks | The first quality gate |
| **Integration tests** | Real dependencies (DB, etc.) | The second quality gate |
| **Build artifacts** | Containers, tarballs, binaries | Cached and reusable |
| **Security scans** | Dependency vulnerabilities, secret leaks | Required for any public release |
| **SBOM generation** | Bill of materials for the artifact | 2026 baseline expectation |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The CI pipeline is structurally a graph of dependencies between stages**, and the architectural commitment is to make stages fail as fast and as cheaply as possible — lint before unit tests, unit tests before integration tests, integration tests before build artifacts. Every stage that fails late wastes the compute and time of the stages before it. **Architectural commitment**: pipelines are ordered cheapest-first, with the expensive stages gated on the cheap stages passing. **The harness's distribution loop already follows this pattern** for the tarball release path; the architectural commitment is to make sure every app the orchestrator ships inherits the same discipline. **SBOM generation is a 2026 baseline expectation** because supply chain attacks (compromised npm packages, malicious dependencies) are now common and the SBOM is the artifact that lets downstream consumers verify what they're actually running. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: a 30-minute pipeline where lint runs at minute 25 and fails the build for a missing semicolon — this is a productivity disaster and a sign that the pipeline graph is wrong.

### 2. CD: deployment strategies

| Strategy | Mechanism | Tradeoff |
|---|---|---|
| **Recreate** | Stop old, start new | Downtime; simplest |
| **Rolling** | Replace instances one at a time | No downtime; mixed-version state during rollout |
| **Blue-green** | New version deployed in parallel, traffic switched atomically | Fast rollback; double the resource cost |
| **Canary** | New version gets small fraction of traffic, expanded if healthy | Real-traffic validation, fine-grained rollback |
| **Shadow** | New version receives mirrored traffic, doesn't serve responses | Risk-free correctness validation |
| **Feature flag rollout** | Code shipped to all, flag controls exposure | Decouples deploy from release |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Canary deployments are the right default for any change with non-trivial risk** because they expose the change to real production traffic at small scale before committing to the full rollout, and the rollback is "stop sending traffic to the canary" rather than "redeploy the previous version". **The relationship to feature flags**: shipping code behind a flag is the same pattern as canary deployment but at a finer granularity — the code is in production for everyone, but exposure is controlled by flag percentage rather than traffic percentage. **The architectural commitment**: any high-risk change (database migration, payment-path change, auth change) ships behind a flag with a canary rollout. **At the harness layer**: the navigator IS the canary by definition (one user, no fan-out), so the canary apparatus is unnecessary; the equivalent discipline is "ship to a dev branch first, exercise it for a session, then merge to main". **Shadow deployment is the underrated strategy** for changes where correctness is hard to verify in tests — mirror real traffic to the new version, compare its responses to the old version's, and ship only when divergence is acceptable.

### 3. Infrastructure-as-code

| Tool | Approach | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Terraform** | Declarative HCL, multi-cloud | The industry default |
| **OpenTofu** | Open-source Terraform fork | The sovereign Terraform replacement |
| **Pulumi** | IaC in real programming languages (TS, Python, Go) | When you want loops, types, abstractions |
| **AWS CDK** | Pulumi-style for AWS specifically | AWS-aligned |
| **Crossplane** | Kubernetes-native IaC via CRDs | When everything else is k8s |
| **Ansible** | Imperative configuration management | Legacy or simple cases |
| **Nix** | Reproducible environments via the Nix language | Sovereign-aligned, steep learning curve |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Infrastructure-as-code is non-negotiable for any production deployment in 2026** — manual cloud console clicks are the source of every "we changed something six months ago and nobody remembers what" incident, and the architectural commitment is that every piece of cloud infrastructure is described by a file in version control. **OpenTofu over Terraform** is the right sovereign-aligned default because the Terraform license change in 2023 made Terraform's future uncertain and OpenTofu is the open-source fork. **Pulumi is the right answer when you want types, loops, and abstractions** in your IaC and your team is already comfortable with TypeScript or Python; the learning curve is lower than HCL for engineers who already write code. **At the harness layer**: there is no cloud infrastructure to describe because the harness runs locally; the equivalent discipline is "the navigator's local environment is described in version-controlled config and dotfiles", which is a much smaller version of the same idea. **The connection to the configuration entry**: IaC is structurally configuration, and the same disciplines apply (schema validation, version control, audit trail, GitOps, drift detection).

### 4. Container orchestration: when and what

| Approach | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **No containers** | Bare binary on a host | Local-first systems, simple tools |
| **Single container per host** | Docker run, systemd-managed | Small services |
| **Docker Compose** | Multi-container app on one host | Dev environments, small deployments |
| **Managed PaaS (Vercel, Railway, Fly)** | Containers as a primitive, no cluster | The right default for most apps |
| **Nomad** | Lightweight orchestrator | Mid-scale, alternative to k8s |
| **Kubernetes** | The full orchestrator | Large deployments, multi-team |
| **ECS / Cloud Run / GKE Autopilot** | Managed k8s-or-equivalent | When you want k8s without operating it |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Kubernetes is the right answer to a problem most teams don't have**, and the operational cost (cluster maintenance, networking complexity, RBAC, ingress controllers, certificate manager, secret integration, monitoring stack) dominates the technical benefit until the team is large enough to have dedicated platform engineers. **Architectural commitment for any orchestrator-built app**: managed PaaS (Vercel for web apps, Railway for persistent servers, Fly for global distribution) is the default, and Kubernetes is reserved for the case where the PaaS abstractions become the limiting factor — which for the foreseeable scale of servicegrid and dreamtime, they will not. **The harness layer needs no orchestration at all** because there are no containers and no peers — it's a single local process. **The CLAUDE.md commitment** (GitHub Actions → Vercel for web apps, GitHub Actions → Railway for persistent servers) is exactly this pattern, and it's the right default for the scale the orchestrator is operating at. **The escape valve**: if a future scale demands self-hosted orchestration, **Nomad over Kubernetes** is the right choice because it's an order of magnitude simpler operationally and covers 80% of the same use cases.

### 5. Build artifacts and reproducibility

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Immutable artifacts** | Build once, deploy the same artifact to every env | Required for honest "deployed the tested version" |
| **Reproducible builds** | Same source → identical bytes | Supply chain integrity |
| **Content-addressed artifacts** | Artifact name is the hash | Immutability + dedup |
| **Multi-arch builds** | One artifact, multiple CPU architectures | Required for ARM64 + x86_64 |
| **Layered Docker images** | Cached layers across builds | Standard build optimization |
| **Distroless / minimal base images** | Smaller attack surface | Standard for production containers |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Immutable artifacts are the discipline that makes "the version we tested IS the version we deployed" actually true** — without immutability, the dev/staging/prod artifacts can drift from each other in subtle ways (different dependency resolutions, different build environments, different timestamps in the binary) and the testing-to-production bridge becomes unverifiable. **Architectural commitment: every release builds one artifact, that artifact gets a content hash, and every environment deploys that same artifact by hash.** **Reproducible builds** are the next level: same source must produce the same bytes, even built on a different machine, which is the only way to verify that nothing was injected into the build. **Distroless / minimal base images** are the right default because they remove tools (shells, package managers, compilers) that an attacker would otherwise have available if they got code execution inside the container. **The connection to file storage §2**: content-addressed artifacts are the same Merkle-tree pattern named there, applied to build outputs.

### 6. Supply chain security

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Dependency pinning** | Exact versions, not ranges | Required for reproducibility |
| **Lock files** | Generated full dependency tree | npm, cargo, poetry; standard |
| **Dependency vulnerability scans** | Match deps against CVE databases | Standard CI step |
| **License compliance scans** | Catch incompatible licenses | Required for commercial software |
| **Sigstore / cosign** | Cryptographic signing of artifacts | Modern supply chain integrity |
| **SLSA framework** | Levels of supply chain integrity | The 2026 baseline expectation |
| **Renovate / Dependabot** | Automated dependency updates | Standard ongoing hygiene |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Supply chain attacks are the dominant 2026 threat for any project that uses third-party dependencies**, and the defense is multi-layered: pinned versions (so the build is reproducible), vulnerability scans (so you notice when dependencies become known-bad), signed artifacts (so consumers can verify authenticity), and automated update tooling (so dependencies don't fall behind to the point where upgrading is dangerous). **Architectural commitment: every orchestrator-built app uses lock files, runs vulnerability scans in CI, signs release artifacts with `cosign`, and runs Dependabot or Renovate for automated dependency updates.** **The forbidden anti-pattern**: `npm install` with version ranges, no lock file, no scans, no signing — every step in this pipeline is exposed to a different supply chain failure mode. **At the harness layer**: the same discipline applies but the surface area is smaller (the harness has a small dependency footprint compared to most apps), and the architectural commitment is to keep it small as a defense in depth.

### 7. Environment promotion

| Pattern | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Trunk-based development** | One main branch, short-lived feature branches | The 2026 default |
| **Environment per branch** | Ephemeral preview environments per PR | Vercel preview deployments, Neon branches |
| **Promotion through environments** | Build once, promote dev → staging → prod | Standard for high-risk apps |
| **Direct-to-prod with fast rollback** | No staging; rely on canary + monitoring | Frontline web apps with strong observability |
| **GitOps environment management** | Each environment is a git directory | Standard for k8s |
| **Schema migration ordering** | Migrate schema before code that depends on it | Required for zero-downtime deploys |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Trunk-based development is the right default for any team smaller than ~50 engineers**, because long-lived feature branches are the source of merge conflicts, integration debt, and the "we'll merge it after the demo" trap that turns into months of drift. **Architectural commitment**: trunk-based, with feature flags or branch-by-abstraction for unfinished work. **Environment per branch (preview deployments) are the highest-leverage CI feature for app development** because they let reviewers see the actual change running without a local checkout, and they catch deployment-specific bugs (env var differences, build failures, asset path issues) before they reach staging. **Vercel + Neon's preview branch integration** is the most aligned commercial pattern because the preview environment includes a database branch that doesn't pollute production data — this is the right default for any orchestrator-built web app with a database. **Schema migration ordering is the load-bearing discipline for zero-downtime deploys**: schema changes must be additive-then-cleanup (add the new column, deploy code that writes both, deploy code that reads only the new, drop the old column) rather than one-shot, and the discipline is enforced at the migration tool layer (tools like `pgroll`, `ariga/atlas`, or hand-rolled multi-phase migrations).

### 8. Observability of the deploy pipeline itself

| Signal | Mechanism | What it tells you |
|---|---|---|
| **Pipeline duration** | Time from trigger to completion | Productivity metric |
| **Pipeline failure rate** | % of pipelines that fail | Reliability metric |
| **Time-to-rollback** | How fast can you revert a bad deploy | Recovery metric |
| **Deploy frequency** | How often you ship | Velocity metric (DORA) |
| **Lead time for changes** | Time from commit to production | Velocity metric (DORA) |
| **Change failure rate** | % of deploys that cause incidents | Quality metric (DORA) |
| **Mean time to recover** | Time from incident to resolution | Quality metric (DORA) |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The DORA four key metrics (deploy frequency, lead time, change failure rate, MTTR) are the load-bearing measure of CI/CD health**, and they're the only metrics that have been empirically linked to organizational performance in research. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: track all four metrics from day one**, even at small scale, because it's much easier to maintain good values than to recover them once they've degraded. **Time-to-rollback is the most operationally important metric** because the worst outage is the one you can't recover from quickly — every architectural choice that makes rollback faster (immutable artifacts, blue-green, canary, feature-flag-controlled exposure) pays back during the incident. **At the harness layer**: the equivalent metric is "how fast can the navigator revert a bad change to a skill or loop", and the answer is "git revert + restart" — fast enough that the architectural complexity of more sophisticated rollback isn't justified.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **CI pipelines ordered cheapest-first; SBOM generation as a 2026 baseline expectation** (from §1).
2. **Canary deployments as the default for high-risk changes; feature flags as finer-grained canary; shadow deployment for hard-to-verify correctness** (from §2).
3. **OpenTofu over Terraform for sovereign-aligned IaC; Pulumi when you want types; harness equivalent is version-controlled local environment config** (from §3).
4. **Managed PaaS (Vercel / Railway / Fly) by default; Kubernetes is the right answer to a problem most teams don't have; Nomad as the escape valve before k8s** (from §4). Aligns with the existing distribution-skill commitment.
5. **Immutable, content-addressed artifacts; reproducible builds; distroless base images** (from §5). Same Merkle-tree pattern as file storage §2.
6. **Pinned dependencies + lock files + vulnerability scans + cosign signing + Renovate; supply chain attacks are the dominant 2026 threat** (from §6).
7. **Trunk-based development with preview environments per PR (Vercel + Neon branches as the canonical pattern); schema migration ordering for zero-downtime** (from §7).
8. **DORA four key metrics tracked from day one; time-to-rollback is the most operationally important metric** (from §8).

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This is a choice-layer entry that resolves cleanly toward the existing distribution skill's commitments and toward managed-provider defaults at the app layer.** The most generative move is **§4: managed PaaS as the default and Kubernetes as the wrong answer for the orchestrator's scale** — this is the architectural commitment that the existing distribution skill already encodes, and naming it explicitly defends against the recurring temptation to "graduate" to k8s when the actual scale doesn't demand it. The second most generative is **§7: trunk-based development with preview environments**, because the workflow improvements compound across the project's lifetime.

**The most concrete near-term unlock at the app layer** is **adopting Vercel + Neon preview branches for any web app with a database** — this is a configuration-only change with large workflow benefits. **The most architecturally interesting thread** is the recognition that **the harness layer needs essentially none of the CI/CD apparatus** because there is no production environment to deploy to, no containers to orchestrate, no infrastructure to provision; the equivalent disciplines (version-controlled config, fast rollback via git revert, dependency hygiene via the small footprint) all exist in primitive form.

**Layer note**: applies to both layers, primarily app-layer relevant. Harness CI/CD is essentially the existing distribution loop (GitHub Actions → tarball release + Vercel for the dashboard), and the discipline transfers to any orchestrator-built app via the distribution skill. App-layer CI/CD follows standard production patterns: trunk-based development, preview environments, canary or feature-flag rollouts, OpenTofu IaC, managed PaaS deployment, immutable artifacts with cosign signing, DORA metrics from day one.

---

## Geographic distribution, edge, and CDN

The discipline of putting compute and data physically close to users by spreading them across the globe — CDNs, edge functions, regional databases, geo-routing, and the hard problem of multi-region consistency. Geography is interesting for the orchestrator because **local-first dissolves most of it at the harness layer** (the navigator's data is on the navigator's machine; the network distance is near-zero) and shapes most of it at the app layer (servicegrid customers are physically distributed; dreamtime users are global from day one).

The entry exists because latency to users is one of the few experienced metrics that compounds with every UX choice, and the architectural decisions around geographic distribution determine whether the application feels instant or laggy at the moment of first contact. Most of the trade-offs are well-resolved by managed providers in 2026, and the entry's job is to name which decisions are still genuine choices vs which have been absorbed by the substrate.

This is a **doesn't-apply-but-transfers** entry **for both layers, primarily app-layer relevant**.

### 1. Why latency to users matters

| Distance | Round-trip latency | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Same datacenter** | < 1 ms | LAN performance |
| **Same metro** | 1–5 ms | City-local users |
| **Same country** | 10–50 ms | Country-local users |
| **Same continent** | 50–150 ms | Continent-local users |
| **Cross-continental** | 150–300 ms | Global users (especially APAC ↔ Europe/Americas) |
| **Satellite** | 600+ ms | Worst case for non-LEO satellite |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The speed of light is a hard constraint** — at ~200,000 km/s in fiber, a New York ↔ London round trip is at least ~56 ms before any processing, and a Tokyo ↔ São Paulo round trip is at least ~190 ms. **The architectural commitment**: any application serving global users must place compute and data physically close to those users for the request paths that need to feel instant, because the bandwidth-latency product means even a small number of round trips dominates the user-experienced latency. **The harness layer dissolves this entirely**: the navigator's data is on the navigator's machine, and the only "network" is the loopback interface, so geographic distribution is structurally a non-problem. **The app layer cannot dissolve it**: servicegrid contractors, dreamtime users, and any future orchestrator-built consumer app will have users distributed across geographies, and the latency budget for "instant" is around 100 ms total round trip — which means the back-end work has to happen close to the user.

### 2. CDN: edge caching for static and semi-static content

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Edge cache** | Content cached at PoPs near users | The standard CDN primitive |
| **Origin shield** | A second-tier cache that protects origin from cache misses | Standard for high-traffic CDNs |
| **Push CDN** | Origin pushes content to CDN ahead of demand | Static sites, controlled releases |
| **Pull CDN** | CDN fetches from origin on first miss | The standard pattern |
| **Stale-while-revalidate** | Serve stale, refresh in background | Standard for slowly-changing content |
| **Surrogate cache control** | Cache directives for CDN that differ from browser cache | Required for fine-grained control |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **CDNs are the highest-ROI infrastructure investment for any read-heavy application** — they absorb 95%+ of read traffic before it ever reaches the origin, they place content within 50 ms of essentially every user on Earth, and they're cheap to operate via managed providers. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: every static asset (images, CSS, JS, fonts), every semi-static API response (catalog data, configuration), and every immutable artifact (file storage entry §4) is served via a CDN with content-addressed or version-stamped URLs and long max-age directives.** **Cloudflare's CDN is the cost-aligned default** because the no-egress-fee model on R2 + CDN means the dominant cost dimension is structurally absent. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: serving images directly from the origin server, paying egress on every request, and discovering at scale that the bandwidth bill exceeds the compute bill by an order of magnitude. **The harness layer has no CDN concern** because there is no public read traffic.

### 3. Edge compute

| Platform | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Cloudflare Workers** | V8 isolates running JS/Wasm at PoPs | The most-deployed edge runtime |
| **Vercel Edge Functions** | Same V8-isolate model, integrated with Vercel | When already on Vercel |
| **AWS Lambda@Edge / CloudFront Functions** | AWS-integrated edge | AWS-aligned stacks |
| **Fastly Compute@Edge** | WebAssembly at PoPs | Wasm-first; high performance |
| **Deno Deploy** | Deno runtime at edge | Sovereign-friendly Deno stacks |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Edge compute is the bridge between "static CDN content" and "full origin compute"** — it lets dynamic logic run at the PoP (so the user-experienced round trip is to the nearest PoP, not to the origin region), at the cost of a constrained execution environment (limited CPU per request, limited memory, limited libraries, no persistent local state). **Architectural commitment for the app layer: edge compute is the right substrate for the request-handling layer that touches authentication, geo-routing, A/B test assignment, and other "make a fast decision and either return or proxy to origin" patterns.** **The forbidden anti-pattern**: trying to run full application logic at the edge — the constraints (CPU, memory, library limits) make this painful, and the right answer is "edge for the routing layer, regional origin for the heavy work". **The harness layer has no edge concern** because the harness is the local process; there's no remote anything to push toward the user.

### 4. Multi-region databases

| Pattern | Mechanism | Tradeoff |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-region with read replicas elsewhere** | Writes to one region, reads anywhere | Simplest; cross-region read lag |
| **Active-active multi-region** | Writes to any region; conflicts must be reconciled | Lowest write latency; conflict-resolution complexity |
| **Geo-partitioned (sharded by user region)** | Each user's data lives in one region | No conflicts; cross-region queries hard |
| **Globally-replicated managed (Spanner, CockroachDB, FaunaDB)** | Multi-region SQL with strong consistency | Pay for the consistency in latency |
| **Edge-replicated KV (Cloudflare D1, Workers KV, Durable Objects)** | KV storage replicated to edge | Eventual consistency at the edge |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Multi-region databases are where the CAP entry's commitments cash out** — every choice in this dimension is a CAP trade-off in disguise, and the right answer depends on whether the application can tolerate eventual consistency for cross-region reads and which operations need strong global consistency. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: start with single-region writes + cross-region read replicas, and only adopt active-active when the write latency from the most distant region becomes a real product problem.** This is the same "start simple, escalate when forced" sequencing as the database selection entry's commitment. **For servicegrid specifically**: the contractor and customer are usually in the same region (small contractors serve local customers), so geo-partitioning by tenant region is the natural fit — a contractor's data lives in their region, customers booking jobs with that contractor read from the same region, and there are essentially no cross-region transactional operations. **For dreamtime**: the cross-user collaboration patterns determine the right answer, and the simplest place to start is a single global write region with edge caching for read-heavy public content.

### 5. Geo-routing and request placement

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Anycast** | Same IP routed to nearest server via BGP | The CDN and DNS standard |
| **GeoDNS** | DNS returns different answers per client location | When you need DNS-level routing |
| **Latency-based routing** | Route to the lowest-measured-latency origin | Common at CDN edge |
| **Geofencing** | Block or route by geographic region | Compliance, licensing |
| **Sticky regional routing** | Once a user is routed to a region, keep them there | Required for write affinity |
| **Failover routing** | Route to backup region if primary unhealthy | DR primitive |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Anycast routing is the load-bearing primitive** that makes "the user gets routed to the nearest replica without any application-level decision" actually work — the same IP is announced from multiple locations and BGP picks the topologically-nearest one. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: managed CDN and managed DNS providers handle anycast routing as a default**, and rolling your own is rarely justified. **Sticky regional routing matters when there's regional write affinity**: if a user's data lives in region A, the user's writes need to go to region A, and the routing layer needs to know this — the application layer typically embeds the region into the user's session token or cookie so the routing layer can read it without a database lookup. **The harness layer has no routing concern** because there's no fan-out across regions.

### 6. Data residency and sovereignty

| Concern | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **GDPR data residency** | EU users' personal data stays in EU regions | Required for EU services |
| **Schrems II / data export rules** | Restrictions on US-based processors of EU data | Legal complexity |
| **Sectoral regulation (HIPAA, PCI)** | Geographic + access constraints on regulated data | Required for regulated industries |
| **Government data sovereignty** | Some data must stay in specific jurisdictions | Government contracts, defense |
| **Backup region choice** | Backups must respect residency too | Often forgotten |
| **Encryption with regional keys** | Keys held in the residency region | Defense in depth |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Data residency is the dimension where geographic distribution meets the data governance entry**, and the architectural commitment is to design the data model so that geo-pinning is possible per-record, not just per-region as a deployment-time decision. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: every record carries a residency tag determined at creation time (typically by the user's region), and the storage layer enforces that records with EU residency live in EU storage, US in US storage, etc.** This is the same "design the data model for regionalization" commitment named in data governance §6, made concrete in the file storage layer. **The connection to crypto-shredding from data governance §3**: per-region key storage adds a second layer to the data residency story — the encrypted PII can technically live in any region, but the decryption keys are physically held in the required residency region, which means access to decrypted data can only happen through systems in that region. **Backup region choice is the most commonly-forgotten residency concern**: it's easy to remember that the primary copy needs to be in-region and forget that the backup is in a different region by default.

### 7. The CDN as a programmable platform

| Capability | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Edge functions** | Run code at PoPs (covered in §3) | Modern CDN feature |
| **Edge KV / D1** | Persistent storage at the edge | Low-latency reads of small data |
| **Edge AI inference** | Run small models at the edge | Recent CDN expansion |
| **Image transformation** | Resize, format-convert at the CDN | Standard for image-heavy apps |
| **Bot detection** | Identify and handle automated traffic | Standard CDN security feature |
| **DDoS mitigation** | Absorb large attack volumes at the edge | The dominant DDoS defense |
| **WAF (Web Application Firewall)** | Rule-based request filtering | Standard CDN security feature |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The CDN has evolved from "cache for static content" to "programmable platform that runs application logic at the edge"**, and treating it as just a cache leaves significant capability on the table. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: CDN-level features (image transformation, bot detection, DDoS mitigation, WAF, edge functions for routing) are adopted by default, and the application logic above the CDN assumes these features exist rather than re-implementing them.** **Cloudflare is the most feature-rich commercial option** in this category and the most aligned with the other choices already made (R2 for storage, Workers for edge compute, D1 for edge KV) — choosing Cloudflare as the unified edge platform reduces integration complexity vs picking different vendors for each capability. **The harness layer has no CDN concern**, but **the orchestrator's dashboard (deployed to Vercel per CLAUDE.md) does benefit from Vercel's CDN as a side effect**.

### 8. The harness layer dissolution

| Concern | App layer (servicegrid, dreamtime) | Harness (orchestrator) |
|---|---|---|
| **User location** | Distributed globally | One navigator on one machine |
| **Network distance** | 10–300 ms RTT typical | < 1 ms (loopback) |
| **CDN benefit** | 95%+ traffic absorption | None (no public reads) |
| **Edge compute benefit** | Major (auth, routing, A/B at PoP) | None (no remote peers) |
| **Multi-region database** | Real architectural choice | Not applicable (single local store) |
| **Data residency** | Real legal constraint | Single-jurisdiction by definition |
| **Geo-routing** | Required for global users | Not applicable |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The local-first commitment dissolves nearly every concern in this entry at the harness layer**, and the dissolution is one of the load-bearing simplifications of the architecture. **The architectural recognition**: the harness avoids the entire geographic-distribution apparatus (CDNs, edge runtimes, multi-region databases, anycast routing, residency engineering) not by solving it cleverly but by making the apparatus structurally unnecessary — there is one navigator, one machine, one jurisdiction, and one storage location. **This is the local-first commitment paying back at the geographic dimension**, and it's worth naming explicitly because the dissolution is the architecture's reward for the upstream choice. **The app layer cannot use this dissolution** because the apps serve external users, but the harness can — and the architectural commitment is to keep it that way: any future feature that would require geographic distribution at the harness layer should be challenged on whether it should live at the app layer instead.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **The speed of light is a hard constraint; latency budgets for "instant" require compute and data physically close to users** (from §1). The harness dissolves this; the app layer cannot.
2. **CDN is the highest-ROI infrastructure investment for read-heavy applications; every static asset and semi-static API response goes through it with content-addressed URLs and long max-age** (from §2). Cloudflare cost-aligned default.
3. **Edge compute as the right substrate for the request-handling layer (auth, geo-routing, A/B assignment); regional origin for heavy work** (from §3). Don't try to run full application logic at the edge.
4. **Single-region writes + cross-region read replicas first; geo-partitioning by tenant region for servicegrid; active-active only when forced** (from §4). The same start-simple sequencing as database selection.
5. **Anycast routing as the load-bearing primitive; managed CDN and DNS providers handle it; sticky regional routing for write affinity** (from §5).
6. **Per-record residency tags at creation time; per-region key storage for crypto-shredded PII; backup region must respect residency** (from §6). Connects to data governance §6.
7. **CDN as programmable platform (image transformation, bot detection, DDoS, WAF, edge functions); Cloudflare as unified vendor for storage + compute + edge** (from §7).
8. **Local-first dissolves the entire geographic-distribution apparatus at the harness layer; this is the load-bearing simplification of the architecture** (from §8).

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This is a doesn't-apply-but-transfers entry where the most generative observation is the dissolution itself**: §8 names that local-first eliminates the entire geographic-distribution category at the harness layer, and that this is one of the architecture's load-bearing simplifications (alongside the dissolution of cross-machine concurrency from Concurrency primitives §1, the dissolution of distributed coordination from System models, and the dissolution of load balancing from networking §7). The pattern is **"local-first eliminates a class of concerns entirely"**, and it's worth tracking as a recurring convergent thread in the meta-section.

**The most concrete near-term unlock at the app layer** is **adopting Cloudflare as the unified edge platform** for any orchestrator-built app — R2 for storage, Workers for edge compute, D1 or Workers KV for edge state, the CDN for cache, the WAF for security, all managed under one vendor with one billing relationship. **The most architecturally interesting thread** is the recognition that the geographic-distribution dimension is one of several where the local-first commitment continues to pay back across the doc — and that the dissolution should be named as a convergent pattern in the meta-section, not just observed entry-by-entry.

**Layer note**: applies primarily to the app layer. Harness geographic distribution dissolves entirely under local-first (one navigator, one machine, one jurisdiction). App-layer geographic distribution (servicegrid customers, dreamtime users) follows standard production patterns: managed CDN with edge caching, edge compute for routing/auth/A/B, single-region writes with cross-region read replicas (escalating only when forced), per-record residency tags, Cloudflare as the unified edge platform.

---

## SLIs, SLOs, SLAs, and error budgets

The discipline of defining what "working" means quantitatively, agreeing on a target for it, and using the gap between the target and reality to make engineering trade-offs. SLI/SLO is interesting for the orchestrator because **the harness has structurally different reliability needs than a multi-tenant SaaS** (one user, no contractual obligations, no compensation clauses) but the underlying discipline — measure what matters, target a level, treat the gap between target and reality as the budget for risk-taking — transfers cleanly to the navigator's experience of the harness.

The entry exists because reliability without a numerical definition of reliability is just hand-waving, and the standard SLO methodology from the SRE literature is the most-validated way to make reliability decisions. Most teams either skip it (and ship blind) or adopt SLA targets without SLOs (and confuse customer commitments with internal targets). The right answer is the full hierarchy: SLIs measure, SLOs target, error budgets convert the gap into engineering decisions, SLAs are the customer-facing subset.

This is a **resolution-confirmation** entry for **both layers**.

### 1. The hierarchy: SLI, SLO, SLA, error budget

| Concept | Definition | Audience |
|---|---|---|
| **SLI (Service Level Indicator)** | A measurement of service behavior (latency, availability, error rate) | Engineering |
| **SLO (Service Level Objective)** | A target value for an SLI over a time window | Engineering |
| **SLA (Service Level Agreement)** | Customer-facing contractual commitment with penalties | Legal + sales |
| **Error budget** | The allowable amount of unreliability before intervention | Engineering |
| **SLO target window** | The rolling time period the SLO applies over (28 days standard) | Engineering |
| **SLA target window** | Often calendar-month for billing alignment | Legal |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The hierarchy distinction matters because confusing SLA with SLO is the most common reliability-discipline mistake**: an SLA is a contract that says "we'll refund you if uptime drops below 99.9%", while an SLO is an internal target that says "we'll make engineering trade-offs to keep uptime above 99.95%, leaving margin for the SLA". **Architectural commitment for the app layer: SLOs are always stricter than SLAs, the gap is the safety margin, and the SLA is never the engineering target.** **At the harness layer**: there is no SLA because there's no customer to contract with, but there ARE SLIs (the navigator can measure execution latency, gate response time, notification delivery time) and there CAN BE SLOs (the navigator's expectation of "the gate prompt arrives within 5 seconds of the gate firing 99% of the time"). **The architectural commitment**: the harness adopts internal SLOs against navigator-experience SLIs even though there's no SLA, because the discipline of measuring against a target is more important than the contract.

### 2. Choosing SLIs that matter

| Type | Example | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Latency** | P50, P95, P99 of request duration | Almost every service |
| **Availability** | % of requests that succeed | Almost every service |
| **Error rate** | % of requests that return errors | Almost every service |
| **Throughput** | Requests per second served | Backpressure-bounded systems |
| **Freshness** | How stale is the data being served | Replicated reads, batch outputs |
| **Correctness** | % of responses that are factually correct | ML systems, search |
| **Coverage** | % of expected work that was done | Batch jobs, scheduled tasks |
| **Quality** | A composite domain-specific quality metric | Domain-specific |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The most important SLI is the one that, when it degrades, the user notices first** — and this is rarely the obvious one (CPU utilization, memory usage) and usually a user-experience metric (latency at high percentiles, success rate of the critical path). **Architectural commitment**: SLIs are chosen by working backwards from the user experience, not by enumerating what's easy to measure. **Latency at the high percentile (P99 or P99.9) matters more than the median** because the median user is fine and the long-tail user is the one calling support. **At the harness layer**: the most important SLI is the navigator's experience of "did the loop complete without me having to babysit it", which is a composite of (1) execution latency vs estimate, (2) gate prompt response time, (3) error/retry rate, (4) notification delivery success. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: tracking 50 SLIs and acting on none of them — the right number of SLOs per service is small (3–5), and each one corresponds to a user journey that the team commits to caring about.

### 3. Setting SLO targets

| Approach | Mechanism | Tradeoff |
|---|---|---|
| **Aspiration-based** | "We want to be 99.99% available" | Often unrealistic; ignores cost |
| **Historical-based** | "Look at recent reality, set the target near it" | Pragmatic; doesn't drive improvement |
| **Customer-expectation-based** | "Survey what customers consider acceptable" | Hard to measure |
| **Cost-aware** | "Each nine costs ~10x the previous nine" | Forces explicit cost-reliability trade-off |
| **Tiered SLOs** | Different SLOs for different user tiers / journeys | Required at multi-tier offerings |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **SLO targets are an explicit cost-reliability trade-off**, and the architectural commitment is to make the cost dimension visible so the trade-off is honest. **The "more nines costs ~10x" heuristic** is the load-bearing rule: going from 99% to 99.9% requires roughly 10x the engineering investment of the prior tier, and going from 99.9% to 99.99% requires another 10x. This is the same "exponential cost for marginal reliability" pattern that shows up in backup storage, code review thoroughness, and most engineering quality dimensions. **Architectural commitment**: SLO targets are set by deciding "what level of unreliability is acceptable for this user journey", not by defaulting to "as high as possible". **At the harness layer**: 99% SLO targets are usually fine because the cost of a single failure is "the navigator notices and re-runs", which is much smaller than the cost of failure at the app layer where "the customer notices and complains". The harness can tolerate lower nines because the recovery cost is lower. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: setting 99.999% targets without measuring whether the resulting cost is justified — and discovering that the team is spending all their time on reliability work for a level the user couldn't distinguish from 99.9%.

### 4. Error budgets

| Concept | Mechanism | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Budget = 1 - SLO** | The allowable failure fraction | The total budget for the window |
| **Budget consumption** | How much of the budget has been used | The current state |
| **Budget exhaustion** | Budget reaches zero before window end | Trigger for intervention |
| **Budget burn rate** | Rate at which budget is being consumed | Predictive signal |
| **Budget reset** | Window rolls forward, budget refreshes | Standard 28-day rolling window |
| **Budget freeze policy** | Stop launches when budget is exhausted | The reliability-velocity trade |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The error budget is the load-bearing concept that converts SLOs from "a target nobody acts on" into "a quantitative budget for risk-taking"**, and it's the single most important contribution of the SRE methodology. **The mechanism**: when the error budget has plenty of remaining headroom, the team can take on risky launches, deploy faster, ship experimental features. When the error budget is exhausted, the team focuses on reliability work and freezes risky launches. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: every SLO has an associated error budget policy that names what changes when the budget is exhausted (typically: launch freeze, focus on reliability, postmortem the consumption).** **The connection to feature flags**: budget exhaustion can trigger automatic flag rollbacks for features that are consuming the budget. **At the harness layer**: the equivalent is "if the harness has been failing the navigator's expectations recently, the navigator should pause new feature work and focus on reliability", and the discipline is the same even though it's a self-imposed pause rather than a team agreement.

### 5. Burn rate alerting

| Pattern | Mechanism | What it catches |
|---|---|---|
| **Threshold alert** | Alert when SLI exceeds bound | Single-point spikes |
| **Slow burn alert** | Alert when budget consumes faster than sustainable | Long-running degradation |
| **Fast burn alert** | Alert when budget consumes very fast over short window | Acute incidents |
| **Multi-window multi-burn-rate** | Combination of fast and slow burn windows | The Google SRE recommendation |
| **Per-journey alerts** | Alert per critical user journey, not per service | Aligns with what users notice |
