**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Multi-window multi-burn-rate alerting is the load-bearing pattern that catches both acute incidents and gradual degradations without flooding pagers with false positives.** **The classical failure mode**: threshold alerts on a single window either fire constantly (window too small, every blip pages) or miss real outages (window too large, alert too late). The multi-window approach uses two or more windows simultaneously — a fast window (5 min) catches acute spikes, a slow window (1 hour) catches gradual burns, and the alert fires only if both indicate consumption is too fast for the budget. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: SLO alerting follows the multi-window multi-burn-rate pattern from the Google SRE workbook, with thresholds calibrated per-SLO based on the budget remaining and the urgency of intervention. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: pager fatigue from threshold-based alerts that fire on every transient blip — this trains the team to ignore alerts and turns the on-call rotation into noise.

### 6. SLOs for specific patterns

| Pattern | Common SLI choice | Common SLO target |
|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous web request** | P99 latency, success rate | Latency < N ms for 99%, success > 99.9% |
| **Async job (queue worker)** | End-to-end completion time, success rate | "Job completes within N min for 99%" |
| **Streaming (SSE / WebSocket)** | Connection establish time, message lag, drop rate | "Lag < N seconds for 99%" |
| **Batch job** | Coverage (% of expected items processed), correctness | "100% coverage daily" |
| **Database read** | Latency, freshness lag | "Replica lag < N seconds for 99%" |
| **Background ML inference** | Throughput, P99 latency, prediction quality | "P99 < N ms; quality > X" |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The right SLI shape depends on the pattern of the work, and applying request-pattern SLOs (latency, availability) to non-request patterns (batch, streaming, background work) is the most common wrong default.** **Architectural commitment**: SLOs are defined per work-pattern, with batch jobs measured by coverage and correctness, streaming measured by lag and drop rate, and background ML inference measured by throughput + quality alongside latency. **At the harness layer**: the relevant SLOs are mostly request-shaped (gate response time, execution latency) but also include batch-shaped (skill calibration coverage: did every executed skill have its rubric scored?) and quality-shaped (rubric quality scores trending correctly over time).

### 7. The harness as a navigator-experience SLO system

| SLI | Target (illustrative) | What it measures |
|---|---|---|
| **Gate prompt arrival time** | 99% within 5s of gate firing | Notification path latency |
| **Execution latency vs estimate** | 90% within 1.5x of calibrated estimate | Calibration accuracy |
| **Skill error rate** | < 2% per skill version | Skill quality |
| **Sub-agent completion success** | > 95% of spawned sub-agents complete | Mech-1 reliability |
| **Loop completion rate** | > 90% of started loops reach COMPLETE | End-to-end discipline |
| **Notification delivery success** | 99% of critical notifications acknowledged within 30 min during waking hours | Sleep test integration |
| **Calibration coverage** | 100% of executed skills have rubric scores | Learning loop hygiene |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The harness IS a system with implicit SLOs**, and naming them explicitly turns the navigator's vague sense of "the harness is feeling slow" into measurable degradations that can be acted on. **Architectural commitment: the harness adopts a small set of navigator-experience SLOs** (the table above is the candidate list, to be calibrated by the navigator), tracks them as derived projections from the event log, and surfaces them in a dedicated terminal view. **The most generative thread**: the calibration system from the learning loop is structurally an SLI tracker for skill quality, and the upgrade-proposal pipeline is structurally an error-budget-based decision: "if the new skill version is regressing the calibrated quality SLO, don't promote". **The connection to notification systems §7**: the engagement signal from notification observability IS a notification-delivery SLI, and the priority-calibration loop named there is structurally a slow-burn alert for notification reliability.

### 8. SLO discipline and the post-incident loop

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Incident postmortem** | Structured review of SLO-impacting events | Required for learning |
| **Blameless culture** | Postmortems focus on systemic causes, not individuals | Required for honest reporting |
| **SLO regression hunting** | Find changes that degraded the SLO | Standard SRE practice |
| **Runbook generation from postmortems** | Each postmortem produces actionable runbooks | The compounding return |
| **SLO retrospective** | Periodic review: are the targets still right? | Required to keep SLOs honest |
| **Customer apology / credits** | When SLA breached, what does the customer get | Standard for SLA-bearing services |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The post-incident loop is where SLOs become a learning system rather than just a monitoring system** — every incident that consumes error budget is an opportunity to extract a runbook, fix a class of failure, and improve the SLO for the next window. **Architectural commitment: every SLO-impacting incident generates a postmortem with named follow-up actions, and the follow-up actions are tracked to completion the same way feature work is tracked.** **The connection to the learning loop**: the harness's existing learning-loop infrastructure (improvement capture, skill upgrade proposals, calibration regressions) is structurally a postmortem-and-followup pipeline, and the architectural commitment is to make sure SLO-impacting events are explicit inputs to it. **Blameless culture is the prerequisite**: if reporting an incident has personal cost, incidents go unreported, and the SLO data is structurally biased toward "everything is fine".

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **SLOs always stricter than SLAs; the gap is safety margin; the SLA is never the engineering target** (from §1). The harness has SLOs without SLAs.
2. **SLIs chosen by working backwards from user experience; small number (3–5) per service; high-percentile latency over median** (from §2).
3. **SLO targets are explicit cost-reliability trade-offs; "more nines costs ~10x" heuristic; harness tolerates lower nines because recovery cost is lower** (from §3).
4. **Error budgets convert SLOs from targets into a budget for risk-taking; budget exhaustion triggers launch freeze** (from §4). The single most important contribution of SRE methodology.
5. **Multi-window multi-burn-rate alerting from the Google SRE workbook; per-journey alerts not per-service** (from §5).
6. **SLOs defined per work-pattern (request, batch, streaming, ML inference); request-pattern SLOs applied to non-request work is the common wrong default** (from §6).
7. **Harness adopts a small set of navigator-experience SLOs; calibration system IS structurally an SLI tracker; upgrade pipeline IS error-budget-based** (from §7).
8. **Every SLO-impacting incident generates a postmortem with tracked follow-ups; harness learning loop IS structurally a postmortem pipeline** (from §8).

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This is a resolution-confirmation entry**: the SRE methodology's apparatus (SLI/SLO/SLA/error budget hierarchy, multi-window alerting, post-incident learning) maps cleanly onto both the harness's existing learning-loop infrastructure and the app layer's standard production discipline. The most generative move is **§7: the harness as a navigator-experience SLO system** — once this framing is adopted, the calibration system gets a clearer architectural shape (it IS an SLI tracker), the upgrade-proposal pipeline gets a clearer decision rule (error budget consumption blocks promotion), and the navigator gets a measurable answer to "is the harness getting better or worse" instead of an aesthetic one.

**The most concrete near-term unlock at the harness layer** is **adopting the seven SLIs in the §7 table as derived projections from the event log, surfaced in a dedicated terminal view** — this is small enough to ship as one or two skill executions and gives the navigator an instrumented answer to a question that's currently answered by feel. **The most architecturally interesting thread** is the recognition that the harness's learning loop is structurally an SLO-driven post-incident loop, and naming the equivalence makes it legitimate to borrow alerting and burn-rate methodology from the SRE workbook.

**Layer note**: applies to both layers. Harness adopts navigator-experience SLOs (gate response, execution latency vs estimate, skill error rate, sub-agent success, loop completion, notification delivery, calibration coverage) tracked as derived projections from the event log, with the learning loop as the post-incident response mechanism. App-layer follows standard SRE discipline: per-journey SLIs, SLOs stricter than SLAs, error budgets with explicit policies, multi-window multi-burn-rate alerting, blameless postmortems with tracked follow-ups.

---

## Consensus and distributed coordination

The discipline of getting multiple processes to agree on a single value, despite some of them being slow, crashed, or malicious. Consensus is the deepest theoretical territory in distributed systems — the FLP impossibility result, the Paxos/Raft families, leader election, distributed locks, fencing tokens, group membership, split-brain prevention — and it's the territory where the local-first commitment most dramatically dissolves the apparatus.

The entry exists because consensus algorithms are the dimension where the orchestrator most cleanly avoids a class of problems that other systems spend years on, and naming what's been avoided makes the architecture's choices legible. Originally planned as two separate entries (consensus protocols + distributed coordination primitives), they've been merged because the harness needs neither: at the harness layer, consensus is a non-problem because there's nothing to coordinate, and at the app layer the right answer is "delegate to a managed service that already implemented Paxos correctly".

This is a **doesn't-apply-but-transfers** entry **for both layers, with most of the value in dissolution recognition**.

### 1. The FLP impossibility result and what it forces

| Concept | Definition | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **FLP (Fischer-Lynch-Paterson) impossibility** | No deterministic consensus algorithm can guarantee both safety and liveness in an async network with even one crash | Every real algorithm trades one for the other |
| **Safety-favoring (CP) algorithms** | Block on uncertainty rather than risk inconsistency | Paxos, Raft |
| **Liveness-favoring (AP) algorithms** | Make progress under partition; reconcile later | Gossip protocols, CRDTs |
| **Failure detector assumption** | Algorithms typically assume "eventually accurate" failure detection | Required to escape FLP in practice |
| **Synchronous network assumption** | Some algorithms require bounded message delays | Rarely true in practice |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **FLP is the load-bearing theoretical result that constrains every consensus algorithm**, and the practical escape is to assume "partial synchrony" — periods of network sanity long enough for an algorithm to make progress, even though the network is async in the worst case. **Architectural recognition: every consensus protocol the orchestrator might encounter is making a partial-synchrony assumption**, and the failure modes (split-brain, deadlock under partition, leader churn) are FLP cashing out in practice. **The harness layer dissolves FLP entirely** because there is exactly one process — there's no possibility of disagreement about state because there's no second observer of state. **The app layer cannot dissolve it**: any multi-region deployment, any HA database, any leader-elected scheduler is making partial-synchrony bets, and the architectural commitment is to delegate those bets to systems that have been tested against the failure modes (managed databases, managed coordination services).

### 2. The Paxos family

| Variant | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Basic Paxos** | One-shot consensus on a single value | Theoretical baseline; rarely used directly |
| **Multi-Paxos** | Optimization for sequences of values, with stable leader | The basis for most production systems |
| **Cheap Paxos** | Reduces replica count using auxiliary acceptors | Niche optimization |
| **Generalized Paxos** | Allows commutative operations to commit in parallel | Niche, rarely deployed |
| **EPaxos (Egalitarian Paxos)** | Leaderless Paxos with command interference graphs | Research; some production deployments |
| **Flexible Paxos** | Quorum intersection requirement relaxed | Theoretical refinement |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Paxos is famously hard to understand, hard to implement correctly, and hard to debug** — the standard description glosses over enough details that "I implemented Paxos" is rarely true and frequently catastrophic. **The architectural commitment**: do not implement Paxos. If you need consensus, use a system someone else has already implemented and battle-tested (Spanner, CockroachDB, FoundationDB, etcd, ZooKeeper). **The harness layer needs no Paxos** because there's no replication; the event log is the source of truth on a single machine. **The app layer should never reach for Paxos directly**; it should reach for managed services that use Paxos under the hood. **Multi-Paxos is the production-relevant variant** because real systems need to agree on a sequence of values (a log of operations), and the stable-leader optimization is what makes it efficient in the common case.

### 3. Raft as the successor

| Concept | Mechanism | Tradeoff |
|---|---|---|
| **Strong leader** | One node leads at a time, drives all writes | Simpler than Paxos; bottleneck on leader |
| **Leader election with terms** | Monotonically-increasing term numbers prevent stale leaders | The main correctness mechanism |
| **Log replication** | Leader appends to its log, replicates to followers | Same shape as a WAL |
| **Membership changes (joint consensus)** | Safe addition / removal of nodes | The hardest part of Raft |
| **Snapshotting** | Compact the log periodically | Required for long-running systems |
| **Pre-vote optimization** | Avoid disrupting a healthy leader during partial partitions | Important production refinement |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Raft was designed to be understandable**, and the design goal was met: most production consensus systems written after 2014 use Raft rather than Paxos because the implementation is tractable. **The architectural recognition**: Raft's log-replication structure is the same shape as the WAL entry §2 — Raft is structurally a WAL with consensus added on top, and the consensus part is mostly about "which writer is allowed to append next". **The harness's event log is structurally a single-writer Raft log without the consensus part**, because there's only one writer (the harness process) and the consensus question is moot. **The architectural commitment**: if the harness ever needs replication for collaboration or sync (multiple navigators editing the same workspace), the right answer is **a managed Raft-backed system (etcd, FoundationDB, or a managed multi-writer database) rather than a custom protocol** — the local-first commitment can extend to "the local copy is authoritative for the local navigator, with a Raft-backed convergence layer for the shared state" without abandoning sovereignty.

### 4. Byzantine fault tolerance

| Algorithm | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **PBFT (Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance)** | Byzantine consensus with O(n²) messages | Permissioned blockchains, high-stakes systems |
| **Tendermint / HotStuff** | BFT with linear message complexity in the common case | Modern permissioned chains |
| **Nakamoto consensus (proof-of-work)** | Probabilistic BFT via cryptographic puzzles | Bitcoin, Ethereum 1 |
| **Proof-of-stake variants** | Economic-security BFT | Ethereum 2, Solana, Cosmos |
| **Soft-Byzantine framings** | Treat output as untrusted; verify externally | The orchestrator's pattern |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **BFT is required when the participants in the consensus might be adversarial** rather than just slow or crashed, and the cost is significantly higher than crash-fault-tolerant consensus (more replicas, more messages, slower commits). **The orchestrator's "soft-Byzantine" framing** named in System models §2 and Auth §8 — agent output is treated as untrusted and verified by gates rather than trusted because of identity — is structurally a BFT pattern adapted to the single-process case: the verifier (the gate) is the BFT mechanism, the agent is the potentially-adversarial participant, and the navigator is the validator of last resort. **Architectural recognition: the harness IS implementing a soft-BFT pattern at the agent layer**, where the assumption "the agent might produce wrong output" is structurally the same as "the participant might be Byzantine", and the response (verification before action) is structurally the same as "BFT consensus before commit". **At the app layer**: full Byzantine consensus is rare and reserved for systems with adversarial participants (permissioned blockchains, multi-organization federations); most app-layer consensus needs are crash-fault-tolerant only.

### 5. Leader election and group membership

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Leader election** | One node designated to coordinate | Required for serializable writes |
| **Heartbeats** | Leader signals liveness to followers | Standard liveness primitive |
| **Lease-based leadership** | Leader holds time-bounded right to lead | Avoids dual-leader during partition |
| **Group membership** | Authoritative list of who's in the cluster | Required for quorum calculation |
| **Failure detector** | Decides when a member has failed | The hardest distributed problem after consensus |
| **Phi accrual failure detector** | Probabilistic failure detection | Used by Cassandra, Akka |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Leader election and failure detection are the operational concerns that consume most of the actual debugging time in real consensus systems** — the algorithmic correctness is largely solved, but "did this node really fail or is it just slow" is the practical problem that produces split-brain incidents. **Lease-based leadership** is the load-bearing pattern that prevents the most dangerous failure mode (two simultaneous leaders during a partition): the leader's right to lead expires after a fixed time, and a new leader cannot be elected until the lease has elapsed plus a safety margin. **The harness layer has no leader to elect** because there's only one process; the architectural commitment is to keep it that way and resist the temptation to add multi-process coordination at the harness layer. **At the app layer**: managed services handle leader election internally; the app layer should never implement a custom leader election protocol.

### 6. Distributed locks and the fencing token problem

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Distributed lock service** | Coordination primitive (Zookeeper, etcd, Redis Redlock) | Mutual exclusion across processes |
| **Lease-based locks** | Lock expires after a duration | Avoids deadlock from crashed lock holder |
| **Fencing tokens** | Monotonic counter passed with each lock acquisition | Required for correctness |
| **The Martin Kleppmann critique** | Distributed locks without fencing tokens are unsafe | Foundational reading |
| **Optimistic concurrency control** | Detect conflict at commit, retry | Often the better answer than locks |
| **CRDTs** | Avoid the lock entirely via conflict-free types | Standard for collaborative editing |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Distributed locks are the coordination primitive that most systems need and most systems implement incorrectly**, and the canonical mistake is forgetting fencing tokens. The failure mode: process A acquires the lock, GCs for 30 seconds, the lock expires, process B acquires the lock, A wakes up and continues operating as if it still held the lock. The fix is fencing tokens — a monotonically-increasing counter that the resource being protected checks before accepting writes, so A's stale token is rejected. **Architectural recognition: the orchestrator's monotonic event-log sequence numbers ARE fencing tokens applied at the application-event layer** — every operation includes the next sequence number, and the event log refuses to accept a write with a stale sequence. This is the same fencing-token discipline named in Concurrency primitives §3. **The harness layer needs no distributed lock** because the single-process model dissolves the mutual-exclusion problem. **At the app layer**: prefer optimistic concurrency control (database row versioning) and CRDTs over distributed locks when possible; reach for a distributed lock service only when the workload genuinely requires mutual exclusion across processes, and always use fencing tokens.

### 7. Coordination services and their substrate role

| Service | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **ZooKeeper** | Hierarchical key-value store with watches, ZAB consensus | Hadoop ecosystem, legacy systems |
| **etcd** | Key-value store with Raft consensus, gRPC API | Kubernetes, modern systems |
| **Consul** | Service discovery + KV + health checking | Multi-DC service mesh |
| **FoundationDB** | Distributed transactional KV store with full ACID | Snowflake, Apple, high-scale systems |
| **Spanner / CockroachDB** | Distributed SQL with strong consistency | When you need SQL semantics globally |
| **Redis Redlock** | Distributed lock pattern over Redis | Discouraged by Kleppmann; unsafe without fencing |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Coordination services are the substrate other systems delegate to for the hard parts of distributed coordination**, and the architectural commitment is to use them rather than reimplement them. **etcd is the right default for any modern system that needs coordination** because it's well-designed, well-tested, and widely deployed. **FoundationDB is the right answer when you need transactional KV at scale** and have the team to operate it. **The harness layer has no coordination service because it has no coordination needs**, which is the dissolution recognized again. **At the app layer**: coordination services are mostly invisible because they're embedded inside the managed databases and orchestration platforms the app is built on (Kubernetes uses etcd internally, managed Postgres handles its own replication consensus, Vercel and Railway handle their own scheduling).

### 8. The harness as the dissolution case study

| Distributed coordination concern | Standard answer | Harness equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Consensus on writes** | Raft / Paxos quorum | Single writer (the harness process) |
| **Leader election** | Raft term-based election | No leader needed; one process |
| **Failure detection** | Heartbeats + phi accrual | The OS process is alive or it isn't |
| **Distributed lock** | etcd / Zookeeper | In-process serial execution |
| **Fencing token** | External token service | Monotonic event-log sequence numbers |
| **Group membership** | Gossip-based or coordinator-based | Single member |
| **Split-brain prevention** | Quorum-based writes | Impossible (no second brain) |
| **CAP trade-off** | C vs A under partition | No partition is possible (single process) |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The harness is the case study for what local-first dissolves**, and this entry's job is to enumerate the dissolutions explicitly so the architectural value is legible. **Architectural recognition: every row in the table above is a category of bugs and operational complexity that the harness avoids by structural choice rather than by correct implementation.** This is one of the most generative observations in the doc, and it should be promoted to a convergent pattern in the meta-section. The pattern is **"local-first eliminates a class of distributed-systems concerns entirely"**, and it's reinforced by:

- Concurrency primitives §1: cross-machine concurrency dissolved
- System models §1-2: distributed assumptions dissolved
- Service discovery §1: discovery dissolved
- Networking §7: load balancing dissolved
- Geographic distribution §8: geographic distribution dissolved
- This entry §1, §3, §5, §6, §8: consensus and coordination dissolved

**The architectural commitment**: any future feature proposal at the harness layer that would require distributed coordination should be challenged with "can this live at the app layer instead?" — because adopting coordination at the harness means giving up the dissolution. **The escape valve**: if multi-navigator collaboration ever becomes a real requirement, the right answer is **a CRDT-based or Raft-backed sync layer at the workspace level**, not a distributed harness; the harness stays single-writer, the sync layer handles convergence.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **FLP forces every consensus algorithm to make a partial-synchrony bet; the harness dissolves FLP entirely by being single-process** (from §1).
2. **Do not implement Paxos; use a system that already did. The architectural commitment is delegation to battle-tested systems** (from §2).
3. **Raft is structurally a WAL with consensus on top; the harness's event log is structurally a single-writer Raft log without the consensus part** (from §3). If multi-writer ever becomes a need, use a managed Raft-backed system.
4. **The orchestrator's soft-Byzantine framing IS a BFT pattern adapted to the single-process case**: agent output as the potentially-adversarial participant, gates as the BFT mechanism (from §4).
5. **Lease-based leadership is the load-bearing pattern preventing dual-leader split-brain; the harness has no leader to elect** (from §5).
6. **Fencing tokens are required for correct distributed locking; the orchestrator's monotonic event-log sequence numbers ARE fencing tokens at the application-event layer** (from §6). Same as Concurrency §3.
7. **etcd as the default coordination service when needed; the harness needs none because it has no coordination needs** (from §7).
8. **The harness is the case study for "local-first eliminates a class of distributed-systems concerns entirely"; this should be promoted to a convergent pattern in the meta-section** (from §8).

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This is a doesn't-apply-but-transfers entry where the dissolution is the entire architectural value**, and §8 is the most generative observation in the entry — the harness's avoidance of every standard distributed-coordination concern is not an accident but a load-bearing consequence of the local-first commitment, and naming the pattern explicitly (with the cross-references to all the entries that reinforce it) is the cleanup-pass-worthy contribution. The pattern **"local-first eliminates a class of distributed-systems concerns entirely"** is currently observed in 6+ entries and is convergent-pattern-worthy.

**The most concrete near-term unlock at the harness layer** is **none** — the architectural value is recognition, not new infrastructure. **The most architecturally interesting thread** is the recognition that the orchestrator's **monotonic event-log sequence numbers serve simultaneously as a WAL primitive (WAL §3), an idempotency mechanism (Stream processing §5), an ABA-defense (Concurrency §3), and a fencing token (this entry §6)** — one primitive serving four roles is the kind of convergence the doc should call out.

**Layer note**: applies to both layers, with most of the value at the harness layer in the form of dissolution recognition. The harness avoids consensus, leader election, distributed locks, coordination services, group membership, split-brain prevention, and the CAP trade-off entirely by structural choice. The app layer delegates consensus to managed services (etcd inside Kubernetes, Raft inside CockroachDB, managed Postgres replication) and never implements consensus protocols directly. The "soft-Byzantine" framing for agent verification transfers BFT concepts to the single-process case.

---

## Message queues and brokers

The discipline of decoupling producers from consumers via a durable intermediary that holds messages until consumers are ready for them. Message queues are interesting for the orchestrator because **the harness's event log already serves as an in-process message bus** between the producer (the running session) and many in-process consumers (projections, triggers, learning loop, calibration), and the standard broker apparatus has primitives that transfer even though the harness has no broker process.

The entry exists because queueing is one of the most reliably load-bearing patterns in distributed systems and one of the easiest to do badly. The doc has Stream processing (entry 8) covering computation over message sequences, and Event streaming as the existing broader entry, but neither focuses specifically on the broker as a coordination primitive: how it decouples producer from consumer, what delivery guarantees it offers, how it handles fan-out, where its operational debt lives.

This is a **resolution-confirmation** entry for **both layers**.

### 1. Queue vs topic vs log

| Pattern | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Work queue (point-to-point)** | One message → one consumer; consumed = removed | Background jobs, task distribution |
| **Topic (pub/sub fan-out)** | One message → many subscribers, each reads independently | Event broadcasting, multi-consumer events |
| **Log-as-broker** | Append-only sequence; consumers track their own offset | Kafka, the modern default for high-throughput events |
| **Priority queue** | Higher-priority messages consumed first | Ops queues, urgent vs background |
| **Delay queue** | Messages become visible after a delay | Scheduled work, retries with backoff |
| **FIFO queue** | Strict ordering preserved per partition / queue | Required for serialized state machines |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The work-queue vs log-as-broker distinction is the foundational queue design choice**, and it determines the operational model for the rest of the system. **Work queues (RabbitMQ classic, SQS, Sidekiq) destroy messages after consumption** — consumers compete for messages, the queue manages "who has what", and replay is structurally impossible because the messages are gone after ack. **Log-as-broker (Kafka, Redpanda, Pulsar) keeps messages in an append-only log** — consumers track their own offset, replay is free, multiple independent consumers can read the same stream. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: log-as-broker is the right default for any system where replay is valuable** (which is most systems), and work-queue is the right answer only for ephemeral background-job patterns where the message is genuinely a one-time task with no reread value. **At the harness layer**: the event log IS a log-as-broker, with the harness process as the producer and the projection consumers as readers tracking their own offsets — the architectural recognition is that this pattern is already in place without ever having been called "a message broker".

### 2. Producers, consumers, and the decoupling pattern

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Asynchronous decoupling** | Producer doesn't wait for consumer | The whole point of queues |
| **Producer back-pressure** | Producer slows down when queue fills | Required for stable systems |
| **Consumer parallelism** | Many workers read from one queue | Standard scale-out |
| **Consumer groups** | Multiple consumers form a group, partition assigned per consumer | Kafka's parallelism primitive |
| **Message acknowledgment** | Consumer confirms processing before removal | At-least-once primitive |
| **Visibility timeout** | Message hidden from other consumers during processing | SQS pattern |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Asynchronous decoupling is the architectural value of message queues** — the producer no longer needs to know whether the consumer is healthy, fast, or even running, and the consumer can be added/removed/scaled without coordinating with the producer. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: any operation that doesn't need to return a result to the user immediately (sending an email, processing a payment webhook, generating a report, indexing for search, sending a notification) goes through a queue rather than running synchronously in the request path.** **The latency win is significant**: a request that fan-outs to 5 async operations responds in 50 ms instead of 500 ms, and the user doesn't care that the work happens after the response. **At the harness layer**: the same pattern applies in-process — the running session emits events to the log, and projection consumers read them asynchronously rather than blocking the session on every projection update. The architectural commitment is to keep the decoupling: any new harness feature that wants synchronous projection updates should be challenged to go through the event log instead.

### 3. Delivery semantics

| Guarantee | Mechanism | Tradeoff |
|---|---|---|
| **At-most-once** | Send and forget | Message loss possible |
| **At-least-once** | Retry until acked | Duplicates possible |
| **Exactly-once (transactional)** | Atomic produce + consume + ack | Hard; requires sink cooperation |
| **Effectively-once via idempotency** | At-least-once + idempotent consumers | The pragmatic answer |
| **Ordered delivery** | Messages delivered in produce order | Per-partition for log brokers |
| **At-least-once + dedup window** | Tolerate brief duplicates within a window | Common compromise |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Exactly-once is the most over-promised property in messaging**, and the honest answer is the same as in stream processing §5: **at-least-once delivery + idempotent consumers is effectively-once with much less complexity**, and the orchestrator's monotonic event-log sequence numbers make idempotency structurally easy. **Architectural recognition: the harness's effectively-once-via-sequence-numbers pattern is the right answer for any in-process or near-process consumer**, and the same pattern works at the app layer for app-internal queues (the consumer tracks the highest processed message ID, ignores any with lower IDs). **At the app layer for cross-system queues** (e.g., a queue feeding a third-party webhook): true exactly-once is impossible because the receiver may process the message and crash before acking, and the architectural commitment is to design for at-least-once with idempotent receivers — never trust an external system's "exactly-once" claim without inspecting the implementation.

### 4. The broker substrate: which one to pick

| Broker | Model | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **RabbitMQ** | Work queue + topic exchanges, AMQP | Classic background jobs, complex routing |
| **Apache Kafka** | Log-as-broker, partitioned, persistent | High-throughput events, replayable |
| **Redpanda** | Kafka-API compatible, single-binary, no JVM | Sovereign-friendly Kafka replacement |
| **Apache Pulsar** | Log + queue hybrid, geo-replicated | Multi-tenancy, geo-distribution |
| **NATS / NATS JetStream** | Lightweight pub/sub + persistence | Cloud-native, low-overhead |
| **AWS SQS / SNS** | Managed work queue + pub/sub | AWS-native, simplest operationally |
| **Google Pub/Sub** | Managed log-as-broker | GCP-native |
| **Cloudflare Queues** | Managed at the edge | When using Cloudflare Workers |
| **PostgreSQL LISTEN/NOTIFY** | Built into Postgres | Small-scale pub/sub without a separate broker |
| **PostgreSQL skip-locked queues** | Use Postgres as a queue with `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` | Avoid the broker entirely for small scale |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **PostgreSQL as a message queue is the most underrated substrate choice for small-to-medium-scale apps** — the `SKIP LOCKED` pattern works correctly, the durability is the same as the rest of your data, and you don't have a separate operational surface to maintain. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: start with Postgres as the queue (via `SKIP LOCKED` for work queues, LISTEN/NOTIFY for pub/sub), and graduate to a dedicated broker only when the scale or feature set forces it. **The graduation thresholds**: more than ~1000 messages/sec sustained, a need for replay across many consumers, a need for consumer groups with rebalancing, or cross-region distribution. **When graduation is forced, Redpanda is the most aligned commercial Kafka replacement** because it's single-binary, no-JVM, and fully Kafka API compatible — the same code that runs against Kafka runs against Redpanda. **Cloud-managed (AWS SQS, GCP Pub/Sub, Cloudflare Queues) is the right answer for cloud-deployed apps that don't have replay needs**. **At the harness layer**: the event log already IS the broker, and the architectural commitment is to recognize this rather than introduce a separate broker process.

### 5. Dead letters and failure handling

| Pattern | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Dead letter queue (DLQ)** | Messages that fail repeatedly go to a side queue | Standard for any persistent queue |
| **Retry with exponential backoff** | Retries spaced increasingly far apart | Standard for transient failures |
| **Poison message detection** | Identify messages that always fail | Required to avoid infinite retry |
| **Replay from DLQ** | Manually re-process DLQ messages after fix | Standard recovery flow |
| **DLQ alerting** | Alert on DLQ growth | The signal that something is wrong |
| **Per-message retry counter** | Track retries to enforce a max | Required for poison detection |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Dead letter queues are the operational discipline that prevents one bad message from blocking the entire queue** — without them, a message that always crashes the consumer will be retried indefinitely, blocking the queue or filling the logs with errors until someone notices. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: every queue has a DLQ, every consumer has a max-retry counter, and DLQ growth is alerted on with the same urgency as a downed service.** **The DLQ replay flow**: the failure mode that sent the message to DLQ is investigated, the bug is fixed, the consumer is updated, and the DLQ is replayed back into the main queue. **At the harness layer**: the event log doesn't have a "dead letter" concept because events are not retried — every event is processed exactly once by each consumer in offset order, and a consumer that fails an event is structurally stalled (which is an alertable state). The harness equivalent of DLQ alerting is "this consumer has been stuck on the same event for N minutes; the navigator should look at why".

### 6. Ordering and partitioning

| Concept | Mechanism | Tradeoff |
|---|---|---|
| **No ordering (parallel consumption)** | Maximum throughput | No state machines |
| **Strict global ordering** | Single partition / single consumer | Throughput bottleneck |
| **Per-key ordering** | Hash key to partition; per-partition strict ordering | The standard compromise |
| **Per-session ordering** | Session ID determines partition | Standard for user-facing apps |
| **Causal ordering** | Messages with happens-before relationship are ordered | Hard; rarely needed in practice |
| **Total order broadcast** | Every consumer sees the same order | Required for state machine replication |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Per-key ordering is the standard compromise**: messages with the same key (user ID, session ID, aggregate ID) go to the same partition and are strictly ordered within it, while different keys can be processed in parallel for throughput. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: any queue carrying state-machine-shaped messages (a sequence of operations on the same aggregate) is partitioned by aggregate ID, and the consumer for each partition processes its messages serially. **At the harness layer**: the event log is a single global log with strict total ordering, which is the simplest and most expensive form — and the simplicity is justified because there's only one producer and the throughput is bounded by human/LLM speeds, never by message production rate. **The architectural recognition**: the harness gets the strongest possible ordering guarantee for free because it's single-producer; no partitioning is needed because there's no throughput problem to solve.

### 7. Backpressure and the stable-system question

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Bounded queue** | Drop or block when queue fills | The simple answer; correctness depends on which |
| **Flow control** | Producer slows down when queue fills | The cooperative answer |
| **Load shedding** | Drop low-priority messages under pressure | The graceful degradation answer |
| **Circuit breaker** | Stop producing when consumer is failing | Defense against feedback loops |
| **Reactive Streams** | Pull-based with credit-based flow control | Library-level standard |
| **Adaptive batching** | Larger batches under load | Throughput optimization |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Backpressure is the property that distinguishes stable systems from systems that work until they don't.** Without backpressure, a faster producer + slower consumer = unbounded queue growth = eventual memory exhaustion or storage exhaustion or both. **Architectural commitment**: every queue in the app layer is bounded, and the bound is enforced — either by blocking the producer (the simple answer) or by load-shedding (the graceful answer). **At the harness layer**: backpressure dissolves because the producer is human/LLM speed and the consumers are in-process; the producer cannot outpace the consumers. **The connection to Stream processing §7**: this is the same dissolution observed there, and it's part of the broader pattern of "single-process producers cannot create the throughput problems that distributed-system queue design exists to solve".

### 8. The harness event log as broker

| Concept | Standard broker | Harness event log |
|---|---|---|
| **Producer** | Many producer processes | One producer (the harness) |
| **Consumer** | Many consumer processes | Many in-process projections + learning loop |
| **Persistence** | Disk-backed (Kafka segments) | SQLite (today); Iceberg (eventual) |
| **Ordering** | Per-partition | Global total order |
| **Delivery** | At-least-once + idempotency | At-least-once + sequence-number idempotency |
| **Replay** | From offset | From offset zero (always) |
| **DLQ** | Side queue | Stalled consumer (alertable state) |
| **Backpressure** | Flow control | Dissolved (single-producer at human speed) |
| **Operational surface** | Cluster (brokers, ZK, monitoring) | Single SQLite file |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The harness's event log IS a message broker, structurally — the same way it IS a WAL, IS a saga framework, IS a stream processor, IS a service registry, IS an audit log.** Naming the equivalence makes the borrowing of patterns from the broker literature legitimate and prevents the temptation to introduce a separate broker process at the harness layer when the existing event log already serves the role. **The architectural commitment**: any feature proposal at the harness layer that wants "a queue" should be challenged with "use the event log" first, because the event log already has all the properties the feature needs (persistence, ordering, replay, idempotency) without the operational cost of a second substrate. **The escape valve**: if the harness ever needs to integrate with an external broker (e.g., consuming events from a third-party webhook delivery system), the integration is one consumer of the external broker that writes the consumed events into the harness event log — preserving the architectural commitment that the event log is the single source of truth.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **Log-as-broker is the right default for any system where replay is valuable; work-queue only for ephemeral background-job patterns** (from §1). The harness event log is structurally a log-as-broker.
2. **Asynchronous decoupling for any operation that doesn't need to return a result to the user immediately; the same pattern applies in-process at the harness layer** (from §2).
3. **At-least-once + sequence-number idempotency gives effectively-once with much less complexity than transactional exactly-once** (from §3). Same as Stream processing §5.
4. **PostgreSQL as a message queue (`SKIP LOCKED` + LISTEN/NOTIFY) is the most underrated substrate; graduate to a dedicated broker only when forced; Redpanda as the sovereign Kafka replacement** (from §4).
5. **Every queue has a DLQ + max-retry counter + DLQ growth alerting; harness equivalent is "stalled consumer" as alertable state** (from §5).
6. **Per-key ordering as the standard compromise for app-layer queues; harness gets strongest ordering for free because it's single-producer** (from §6).
7. **Every queue is bounded; backpressure dissolves at the harness layer because single-producer at human speed** (from §7).
8. **The harness event log IS a message broker — the same convergence as WAL, saga framework, stream processor, service registry, audit log** (from §8).

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This is a resolution-confirmation entry that adds another row to the "X is structurally Y" pattern list**: the event log IS a message broker. The full list of "X IS Y" recognitions is now substantial:

- The harness IS a saga framework (Saga orchestration §8)
- The harness IS the in-process service registry (Service discovery §2)
- The event log IS a WAL applied at application-event granularity (WAL entry)
- The orchestrator IS an ML system (ML systems §8)
- The event log IS the audit log (Data governance §5, Event streaming §7)
- Verification gates ARE the orchestrator's BFT mechanism (System models §2, Auth §8)
- The harness IS a stream processor (Stream processing §8)
- The harness IS an N-of-1 experimentation platform (A/B testing §8)
- The harness IS a navigator-experience SLO system (SLI/SLO §7)
- The event log IS a single-writer Raft log without the consensus part (Consensus §3)
- **The event log IS a message broker (this entry §8)**

This list is itself a convergent observation: the orchestrator's event-sourced architecture has been quietly absorbing the responsibilities of multiple distinct distributed-systems components, and naming the equivalences keeps the doc honest about how much existing infrastructure each new entry is *recognizing* rather than *adding*.

**The most concrete near-term unlock at the harness layer** is **none** — the architectural value is recognition. **The most architecturally interesting thread** is the recognition that the "X IS Y" pattern is itself a convergent pattern worthy of explicit naming in the meta-section, alongside the "local-first eliminates a class of concerns" pattern from entry 18.

**Layer note**: applies to both layers. Harness uses the event log as the in-process message broker, with projections as consumers tracking their own offsets — the existing infrastructure already serves the role. App-layer messaging follows standard patterns: Postgres queues for small scale, Kafka or Redpanda for high throughput, SQS or Pub/Sub for managed cloud, every queue with DLQ and bounded size and backpressure discipline.

---

## Mobile and offline-first sync

The discipline of building applications that work without a network connection, sync changes when connectivity returns, and never lose user data to disconnection — CRDTs, sync engines, optimistic UI, conflict resolution, background sync, local-first databases. Offline-first is interesting for the orchestrator because **the harness IS already a local-first system**, and the architectural lessons of offline-first apply to it as the simplest case (no sync ever needed) while serving as the template for app-layer features that need to survive disconnection.

The entry exists because mobile and offline-first apps have spent the last decade building a sophisticated apparatus for the same problem the harness solves trivially (single-machine local-first), and the field has converged on patterns (CRDTs, sync engines, optimistic UI) that the orchestrator can borrow when it needs to extend beyond a single navigator's machine.

This is a **convergent-pattern resolution** entry **app-layer primary, with the harness as the foundational case**.

### 1. The local-first gradient

| Level | Mechanism | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Pure online** | Server is the source of truth; client is a render layer | Most pre-2015 web apps |
| **Cache-aware online** | Client caches some data for performance | Most 2020s web apps |
| **Read-offline / write-online** | Cached reads work offline; writes require connection | Pinterest, Spotify free tier |
| **Optimistic offline** | Writes accepted locally, retried when online | Most mobile apps |
| **True local-first** | Local database is the source of truth; sync is best-effort | Linear, Figma, Notion (partially) |
| **Local-only** | No sync at all | Single-machine apps |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The local-first gradient is one of the most important design dimensions for any application that touches a user's primary work**, because the level chosen determines how the application behaves under the conditions where it matters most (offline, slow network, contested network, captive portal). **Architectural commitment**: any orchestrator-built app that touches user-authored content (notes, drafts, configurations, decisions) is true local-first or local-only by default — the cloud is an optional sync target, not the source of truth. **The harness already lives at "local-only"** for the navigator's primary work, and the architectural commitment is to keep it there until a real multi-navigator collaboration need forces a move to "true local-first" (which is the level above local-only). **The forbidden anti-pattern**: building a "cloud-first with offline cache" version of features that should be local-first; this gets the failure modes wrong (data loss on disconnection becomes possible), and it's much harder to retrofit local-first onto a cloud-first design than to start local-first from the beginning.

### 2. Local data substrate

| Substrate | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **SQLite** | Embedded SQL database | The dominant local store; available everywhere |
| **IndexedDB** | Browser-native key-value with indexes | Web apps without native code |
| **PouchDB / RxDB** | JavaScript databases with sync built-in | Web/mobile JS apps |
| **WatermelonDB** | React-native-aware lazy database | React Native apps |
| **Realm** | Object database with reactive query | iOS / Android native |
| **Core Data / Room** | Native iOS / Android persistence | Platform-native apps |
| **DuckDB on device** | Embedded analytical SQL | When the local data is analytical, not transactional |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **SQLite is the right local substrate for almost everything** because it's available on every platform, has decades of testing, supports full SQL, and is the most-deployed database in the world. **Architectural commitment**: any orchestrator-built app that needs local persistence uses SQLite as the local substrate, with the schema migration discipline named in CI/CD §7. **DuckDB on device is the interesting modern addition** for any local data that's analytical rather than transactional — read-heavy aggregations, reports, dashboards. **The harness already commits to SQLite** for the event log; the architectural recognition is that this commitment is exactly the right substrate for offline-first because the same SQLite file IS the local source of truth, and "syncing" is just shipping the SQLite file or its derivable contents elsewhere when desired.

### 3. CRDTs and conflict-free replicated types

| Type | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **G-Counter (grow-only counter)** | Add-only counter, merged by max per replica | Like counts, view counters |
| **PN-Counter (positive-negative)** | Two G-Counters for increment/decrement | General-purpose counters |
| **G-Set (grow-only set)** | Set with only adds | Append-only collections |
| **2P-Set (two-phase set)** | Set with adds and tombstone removes | Full set semantics |
| **OR-Set (observed-remove)** | Set with causal removes | The standard CRDT set |
| **LWW-Register (last-write-wins)** | Single value with timestamp | Single-value fields |
| **RGA (replicated growable array)** | Sequence with stable position IDs | Collaborative text editing |
| **Yjs / Automerge document** | Composite CRDT for arbitrary documents | Real-world collaborative apps |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **CRDTs are the right answer when you need conflict-free merge of concurrent changes from multiple replicas without coordination** — and they're the wrong answer when you don't, because the operational complexity is meaningful. **Architectural commitment**: CRDTs are reserved for cases where two users genuinely need to edit the same data simultaneously and the application semantics support automatic merge. **The harness layer needs no CRDTs** because there's only one writer (the navigator), and the architectural commitment is to keep it that way. **Yjs and Automerge are the most production-ready document CRDTs** in 2026, and they're the right starting point if a future orchestrator-built app needs collaborative editing (e.g., a shared deck-loop for multiple navigators editing a presentation together). **The forbidden anti-pattern**: reaching for CRDTs because they sound interesting; the right answer is single-writer-with-explicit-merge unless the application genuinely requires concurrent edits.

### 4. Sync engines as the modern substrate

| Engine | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Replicache** | Client-side state with server reconciliation | Web/mobile apps with custom backend |
| **ElectricSQL** | Postgres ↔ SQLite-on-device sync | Postgres-backed local-first apps |
| **PowerSync** | Same model, different vendor | Postgres / Mongo-backed local-first |
| **Liveblocks** | Hosted real-time collaboration primitives | Cursors, presence, collaborative state |
| **InstantDB** | Reactive database with built-in sync | React/JS apps |
| **Triplit** | Client-server sync with full SQL | Modern alternative |
| **Y-sweet / Tldraw sync** | Yjs-backed managed sync | Document-shaped collaboration |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Sync engines are the highest-leverage substrate addition of the last 5 years** because they take the "I need offline-first" feature and turn it from a multi-month engineering project into a configuration choice. **Architectural commitment for any orchestrator-built app that needs offline-first sync: use a sync engine, do not build sync from scratch.** **ElectricSQL is the most architecturally aligned commercial option** because it syncs Postgres ↔ SQLite-on-device, which means the server side is standard Postgres (which the rest of the app stack is built on) and the client side is SQLite (which the harness already standardizes on). **The harness adjacency**: if multi-navigator collaboration becomes a real need, the right answer is **a sync engine layered on top of the existing event log** — the local event log stays as the source of truth for the local navigator, the sync engine handles convergence with other navigators' event logs, and the architecture preserves single-writer locality at the "current navigator" level. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: building a custom sync protocol for the harness; the field has converged on sync engines as the right substrate, and rolling your own is a large engineering investment with worse failure modes.

### 5. Optimistic UI and the response-before-confirmation pattern

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Optimistic update** | Client applies the change immediately, before server confirmation | Standard for any responsive UI |
| **Optimistic with rollback** | Reverses local change if server rejects | The full pattern |
| **Pending state UI** | Show pending changes with visual indicator | Disambiguates "sent" from "confirmed" |
| **Conflict UI** | Surface server-side conflicts to the user | Required when automatic merge isn't safe |
| **Offline indicator** | Show network status so users can predict behavior | Required for any offline-capable app |
| **Background sync queue** | Pending operations queued for retry | Required for offline writes |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Optimistic UI is the architectural pattern that makes networked applications feel local** — the user sees their action take effect immediately, the network round trip happens in the background, and the rare case of server rejection is handled with a clear rollback. **Architectural commitment**: any user-facing action in an orchestrator-built app applies optimistically by default, with explicit rollback handling for the failure case. **The pending state UI is the underrated detail**: distinguishing "this change is on your screen but hasn't been confirmed by the server" from "this change is permanent" prevents the confusing "did my edit save?" question. **The harness has its own version of this**: when the navigator approves a gate or completes a skill, the action takes effect immediately in the harness (the event log is updated, the next phase begins) and any subsequent persistence to the dashboard or to remote backups is asynchronous. **The connection to the event log**: optimistic UI is structurally "local writes hit the local source of truth immediately", which is exactly what the event log already does for the harness — and the same pattern transfers to any app that uses the event log as its local source of truth.

### 6. Conflict resolution strategies

| Strategy | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Last-write-wins** | Keep the most recent timestamped change | Simple; loses concurrent edits |
| **First-write-wins** | Keep the earliest change | Rare; causes user frustration |
| **Multi-value (siblings)** | Keep all conflicting versions, surface to user | Riak's pattern; harder UX |
| **Manual conflict resolution UI** | Show conflict, let user choose | Required for high-stakes data |
| **CRDT-based automatic merge** | Conflict-free by construction | Standard for collaborative editing |
| **Operational transform (OT)** | Transform concurrent operations to commute | Google Docs (legacy approach) |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Last-write-wins is the simplest conflict resolution and the most dangerous** because it silently discards the loser's changes — the user whose write was overwritten doesn't know, and the data loss is invisible until someone notices much later. **Architectural commitment**: last-write-wins is acceptable only for data where loss is genuinely acceptable (cached preferences, ephemeral state); high-stakes data requires either CRDT-based merge (automatic but constrained semantics) or manual conflict resolution UI (slower but correct). **The harness layer dissolves conflicts entirely** because there's only one writer, and the architectural commitment is to keep it that way; if multi-writer ever becomes a need, the answer is CRDTs (via a sync engine) for data where merge semantics work, and manual resolution for data where they don't.

### 7. Mobile-specific concerns

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Background sync** | OS-scheduled sync when app is backgrounded | Required for "fresh data on launch" |
| **Push notifications as sync triggers** | Server pushes to wake the app for sync | Standard battery-friendly pattern |
| **Network-aware behavior** | Different logic on cellular vs WiFi vs offline | Battery + data plan friendly |
| **Battery-aware behavior** | Defer non-urgent work when battery is low | Standard mobile UX expectation |
| **App lifecycle handling** | Pause/resume work as the app backgrounds/foregrounds | OS-platform requirement |
| **Storage budget pressure** | Evict cached data when device storage is low | OS may force this |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Mobile is where the offline-first apparatus was forged** because mobile users routinely operate in conditions (subways, planes, dead zones, captive portals) that desktop users don't, and the apparatus reflects the constraint. **Architectural commitment for any mobile orchestrator-built app**: background sync via push triggers, network-aware behavior that defers expensive operations on cellular, battery-aware behavior that pauses non-urgent work below a threshold. **The connection to notification systems entry**: push notifications as sync triggers is the same primitive named there, applied to a different purpose. **At the harness layer**: there is no mobile concern because the navigator is on a desktop; the architectural recognition is that this dissolves another category of concerns.

### 8. The harness as the foundational local-first case

| Offline-first concern | App-layer answer | Harness equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Local source of truth** | SQLite-on-device + sync engine | The event log (already SQLite) |
| **Conflict resolution** | CRDT or manual | None (single writer) |
| **Optimistic UI** | Local apply + background sync | Already inherent (local writes are immediate) |
| **Background sync** | OS scheduler + push triggers | Not applicable (always-on local process) |
| **Network awareness** | Defer expensive ops on cellular | Not applicable (no network) |
| **Sync engine** | ElectricSQL, Replicache, PowerSync | None needed (no remote replicas) |
| **Pending state UI** | Visual indicator | Not applicable (no pending state) |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The harness is the foundational local-first case** — the simplest version of the pattern, where there's only one writer, no sync, no conflicts, no pending state, and no network. **Architectural recognition: the entire offline-first apparatus exists to give other applications the properties the harness has by default.** This is one more dissolution that reinforces the convergent pattern from entry 18. **The architectural commitment**: when the harness needs to extend to multiple navigators, the right answer is to layer a sync engine on top of the existing event log rather than rewriting the harness as a cloud-first system; the event log stays as the local source of truth, the sync engine handles convergence. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: rewriting the harness around a remote database with the local SQLite as a cache; this gets the failure modes wrong and abandons the local-first commitment.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **Any orchestrator-built app that touches user-authored content is true local-first or local-only by default; cloud is optional sync target, not source of truth** (from §1).
2. **SQLite as the local substrate for everything; DuckDB on device for analytical local data** (from §2). The harness already commits to this.
3. **CRDTs reserved for cases where users genuinely need concurrent edits with automatic merge; harness needs none because single-writer** (from §3). Yjs / Automerge as the production-ready document CRDTs.
4. **Sync engines as the right substrate for offline-first sync; ElectricSQL most architecturally aligned (Postgres ↔ SQLite); harness multi-navigator answer is sync engine layered on the event log** (from §4).
5. **Optimistic UI by default with explicit rollback; pending state UI distinguishes sent from confirmed; the harness already does this via the event log** (from §5).
6. **Last-write-wins acceptable only for genuinely loss-tolerant data; high-stakes needs CRDT or manual resolution; harness dissolves conflicts entirely** (from §6).
7. **Mobile-specific concerns (background sync, push triggers, network-aware, battery-aware) for any mobile orchestrator-built app** (from §7).
8. **The harness IS the foundational local-first case; the entire offline-first apparatus exists to give other applications the properties the harness has by default** (from §8). Reinforces the dissolution pattern.

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This is a convergent-pattern resolution entry**: most of the offline-first apparatus has a structural analog or dissolution at the harness layer, and the walk's job is to recognize the equivalence. The most generative move is **§8: the harness as the foundational local-first case** — naming that the offline-first field has been building toward properties the harness has by default reframes "local-first" from a buzzword into a load-bearing simplification that the orchestrator pioneered (in its small way) before the field caught up.

**The most concrete near-term unlock at the app layer** is **adopting ElectricSQL as the sync substrate for any orchestrator-built app that needs offline-first**, because the architectural alignment (Postgres ↔ SQLite) matches the rest of the orchestrator's substrate choices and avoids the engineering cost of custom sync. **The most architecturally interesting thread** is the recognition that **the harness's local-first commitment is the same architectural choice as the offline-first movement made**, and naming the equivalence means the orchestrator can borrow patterns from the offline-first literature when extending to multi-navigator scenarios without abandoning the core commitment.

**Layer note**: app-layer primary, with the harness as the foundational case. App-layer offline-first follows standard production patterns: SQLite local + sync engine (ElectricSQL preferred), CRDTs only when needed, optimistic UI with rollback, mobile-specific battery and network awareness. Harness is already local-first by structural choice — single writer, no sync needed, the event log IS the local source of truth, and the entire offline-first apparatus exists to give other applications the properties the harness has by default.

---

## Real-time systems

The discipline of meeting timing constraints — guaranteeing that operations complete within a deadline, that events are observable within a budget, and that the system feels "live" rather than "polled". Real-time is interesting for the orchestrator because **the standard CS definition of "real-time" (hard guarantees on operation timing) almost never applies to web/SaaS systems**, but the colloquial sense (live updates, instant feedback, sub-second response) applies to nearly everything the orchestrator builds, and confusing the two leads to either over-engineering or under-engineering.

The entry exists because "real-time" is one of the most overloaded terms in software, and naming the spectrum (hard real-time → soft real-time → near-real-time → batch) makes the architectural choices legible. Most orchestrator-built features are soft real-time at most, and the right architecture borrows from real-time-systems literature (perceptual thresholds, latency budgets, jitter awareness) without taking on the constraints of true hard real-time.

This is a **choice-layer** entry for **both layers**.

### 1. The real-time spectrum

| Tier | Definition | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Hard real-time** | Missing the deadline is a system failure | Avionics, medical devices, industrial control |
| **Firm real-time** | Missing the deadline makes the result useless | Some financial trading, video frame deadlines |
| **Soft real-time** | Missing the deadline degrades quality | UI responsiveness, video calls, game state |
| **Near-real-time** | Sub-second latency, no per-request deadline | Live dashboards, presence indicators, chat |
| **Batch / not real-time** | Latency measured in minutes to hours | Analytics, reports, ETL |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The single most common mistake in real-time discussions is treating soft real-time as if it were hard real-time** — applying the operational complexity of hard real-time (RTOS, deterministic scheduling, worst-case execution analysis) to a problem where missing a deadline by 50 ms is fine. **Architectural commitment**: every real-time-shaped feature is classified into one of these tiers before architectural decisions are made, and the architecture is sized to the tier — not the worst case the team can imagine. **The orchestrator never builds hard real-time systems** because nothing in its scope has hard deadlines that risk human life or physical equipment; the architectural commitment is that any "real-time" feature in an orchestrator-built system is at most soft real-time, and most are near-real-time. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: building near-real-time features (a chat system, a live dashboard) using the apparatus of soft real-time (low-latency event loops, deterministic schedulers) when standard polling or WebSocket-based push would meet the user's actual expectations.

### 2. Perceptual thresholds and the user-experience budget

| Threshold | Perception | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **< 100 ms** | Feels instant; user perceives no delay | Click feedback, keystroke response |
| **100–300 ms** | Noticeable but acceptable; user attention stays engaged | Page transitions, autocompletion |
| **300 ms – 1 s** | Feels slow; attention may wander | Form submissions, search results |
| **1–10 s** | Feels broken without a progress indicator | Heavy operations, file uploads |
| **> 10 s** | Requires explicit "this will take time" framing | Batch jobs, long-running computations |
| **< 16.7 ms** | One frame at 60 Hz | Animation, game loops |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Perceptual thresholds are the load-bearing input to latency budgets**, and the architecture should be designed around what the user will actually notice rather than the engineer's instinct for "as fast as possible". **Architectural commitment**: every user-facing operation has a target perceptual threshold, and the architecture is sized to meet that threshold under typical conditions (not absolute worst case). **The 100 ms threshold is the most important** because it's the boundary between "feels instant" and "noticeable delay" — if the architecture can hit it, the user perceives the application as responsive; if it can't, no amount of polish makes the application feel snappy. **At the harness layer**: the relevant perceptual threshold is the gate-prompt arrival time (named in SLI/SLO §7) — the navigator's perception of "the harness is responsive" is dominated by how quickly notifications arrive when the harness needs attention, and the SLO target should map to a perceptual threshold (5 seconds is the soft real-time tier, not the < 100 ms tier, because the navigator doesn't expect instant pings).

### 3. Latency budgets and where they're spent

| Component | Typical contribution |
|---|---|
| **Network round trip** | 10–300 ms depending on geography (Networking entry) |
| **TLS handshake** | 0 (pooled) to ~100 ms (cold) |
| **DNS resolution** | 0 (cached) to ~50 ms (cold) |
| **Database query** | 1–100 ms depending on indexing and locking |
| **Application logic** | 1–500 ms depending on complexity |
| **Serialization / deserialization** | 1–50 ms depending on payload size |
| **Garbage collection pauses** | 0 to multiple seconds depending on language and tuning |
| **Render time** | 1–100 ms depending on DOM complexity |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Latency budgets are the engineering discipline that makes "the page feels slow" diagnosable** — without an explicit budget per component, every team blames every other team for the slowness, and nothing improves. **Architectural commitment**: every user-facing operation has an explicit latency budget split across components (network, database, application, render), and the budget is verified in CI via performance tests. **The biggest hidden contributors are usually network round-trips and GC pauses**, and the architectural fixes (connection pooling, batching, GC tuning, switching to a non-GC'd language for hot paths) are discoverable only when the budget makes them visible. **At the harness layer**: most latency comes from the LLM API call itself (typically 100 ms to several seconds), and the architectural commitment is to make sure the harness doesn't add significant latency on top of the unavoidable model latency — connection reuse (Networking §4), efficient serialization, no synchronous projection updates blocking the response.

### 4. Push vs poll for live updates

| Pattern | Mechanism | Tradeoff |
|---|---|---|
| **Polling** | Client periodically asks for updates | Simple; latency ≥ poll interval |
| **Long polling** | Client request held open until update arrives | Mostly obsolete |
| **Server-Sent Events (SSE)** | Server pushes events over HTTP, one-way | The right default for one-way push |
| **WebSocket** | Bidirectional persistent connection | Required for two-way, full-duplex |
| **WebRTC data channel** | Peer-to-peer with low latency | Required for real-time peer scenarios |
| **HTTP/2 server push** | Mostly deprecated | Avoid |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Server-Sent Events are the right default for "the server pushes updates to the client" patterns** — they're simpler than WebSockets, work over standard HTTP, support automatic reconnection, and don't have the protocol complexity of full-duplex. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: SSE for one-way push (live dashboards, status updates, notifications, chat-from-server), WebSocket only when bidirectional is genuinely required (collaborative editing, multiplayer games, voice/video signaling). **The forbidden anti-pattern**: WebSocket for one-way push because "WebSocket is fancier" — this adds complexity and operational debt with no benefit. **At the harness layer**: SSE is already used for the dashboard's live execution updates (CLAUDE.md names `GET /api/executions/:id/stream` as SSE), which is the right pattern; the architectural commitment is to keep SSE as the default for any new live-update surface.

### 5. Jitter, ordering, and the lossy-network question

| Concept | Definition | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Latency** | Time from cause to observation | The headline metric |
| **Jitter** | Variance in latency over time | Often more user-visible than mean latency |
| **Out-of-order delivery** | Events arrive in different order than sent | Standard over UDP; possible over TCP at app layer |
| **Loss tolerance** | How well the system handles dropped messages | Dictates retry / dedup discipline |
| **Bufferbloat-induced jitter** | Network buffers add unpredictable delay | The dominant tail-latency cause (Networking §5) |
| **Adaptive jitter buffer** | Buffer absorbs jitter at the cost of latency | Standard for voice/video |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Jitter is often more user-visible than mean latency** because humans are good at perceiving variance — a stream of events arriving every 100 ms feels smooth, while events arriving 50–200 ms apart feels jerky even though the mean is the same. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: any animation, game loop, or audio stream uses an adaptive jitter buffer to smooth incoming events, accepting a small fixed latency in exchange for predictability. **At the harness layer**: jitter shows up in execution-progress updates and gate prompts; the architectural commitment is to debounce or smooth them so the navigator perceives steady progress rather than bursts of activity followed by silence. **The connection to the bufferbloat thread from Networking §5**: the dominant cause of jitter on consumer networks is the ISP's routers, and the architectural defense is mostly indirect (HTTP/2 + connection reuse + smaller payloads).

### 6. Real-time databases and the live-query pattern

| Pattern | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Polling-based "live"** | Client polls the database every N seconds | Crude but works |
| **Database change feeds (CDC)** | Database streams change events to subscribers | Postgres logical replication, MongoDB change streams |
| **Materialized view + invalidation** | Cached query result with invalidation on source change | Common pattern at scale |
| **Live query** | Database holds open queries; pushes results on change | Firebase Firestore, RethinkDB (legacy), Convex |
| **Subscription-based reads** | Client subscribes to a query; server pushes diffs | Hasura, Supabase Realtime |
| **Reactive ORMs** | Application-layer reactive queries | RxDB, Watermelon, Reactive Postgres |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Live queries are the architectural pattern that makes "the UI updates as the data changes" trivial**, and the right substrate eliminates a class of bugs (forgetting to invalidate caches, race conditions between reads and writes). **Architectural commitment for the app layer: prefer live-query substrates (Supabase Realtime, Convex, ElectricSQL) for any UI that needs to reflect data changes in near-real-time, rather than rolling polling or invalidation logic.** **The connection to the offline-first entry**: ElectricSQL gives both offline-first and live-query in one substrate, which is the architecturally cleanest combination. **At the harness layer**: live queries map to the projection-on-event-log pattern — projections are continuously updated as new events arrive, and any consumer reading from a projection sees the latest state without explicit invalidation. **The architectural recognition**: the harness's projection layer IS a live-query substrate built on the event log, and naming this makes the borrowing of patterns from live-query databases legitimate.

### 7. Presence and collaborative awareness

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Presence (online/offline)** | Heartbeat or connection-based status | Chat, collaborative tools |
| **Cursors / selection** | Other users' cursor positions in the UI | Collaborative editors |
| **Typing indicators** | "X is typing..." | Chat |
| **User avatars in document** | Who's currently viewing this | Collaborative tools |
| **Live count** | "12 people viewing this page" | Social proof, dashboards |
| **Activity feed** | Live stream of user actions | Slack, Notion |
