**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Presence is a separate concern from data sync** — it's high-frequency, low-value-per-message, and tolerates loss in ways that data updates don't. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: presence data goes through a separate, lighter-weight channel (often a managed service like Liveblocks or Pusher) rather than the same substrate as data sync, because the throughput and reliability requirements are different. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: routing presence updates through the same persistent message broker as data updates, which fills the broker with ephemeral data that has no value 30 seconds later. **At the harness layer**: there is no presence concern because there's only one navigator; the architectural recognition is yet another dissolution.

### 8. The harness's near-real-time surfaces

| Surface | Current state | Soft-real-time tier |
|---|---|---|
| **Gate prompt arrival** | Slack notification via proactive-messaging | Soft real-time (target ≤ 5 s) |
| **Execution progress updates** | SSE stream from `/api/executions/:id/stream` | Near-real-time (target ≤ 1 s) |
| **Skill completion feedback** | Logged immediately to event log | Near-real-time (already < 100 ms locally) |
| **Calibration recompute** | Triggered on `complete_skill` | Near-real-time (background) |
| **Sub-agent status** | Polled or via in-process callback | Near-real-time (target ≤ 1 s) |
| **Question/response cycles** | Slack interactive buttons | Soft real-time (target ≤ 5 s) |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The harness has a small set of near-real-time surfaces that are the user-experience load-bearing**, and naming them with explicit soft-real-time tiers makes the architecture's commitments concrete. **Architectural commitment**: each surface gets a target perceptual tier (near-real-time vs soft-real-time vs batch), and the SLI/SLO entry's discipline (entry 17) is applied to track whether the targets are being met. **The connection to SLI/SLO §7**: every row in this table corresponds to an SLI in the navigator-experience SLO list, and the perceptual tiers from §2 of this entry give the targets concrete numerical values. **The architectural recognition**: the harness is already building toward a near-real-time experience for the navigator without ever having explicitly named "real-time" as a concern, and naming it makes the commitments testable.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **The orchestrator never builds hard real-time systems; everything is at most soft real-time** (from §1). Most features are near-real-time.
2. **Every user-facing operation has a target perceptual threshold (100 ms / 300 ms / 1 s); the architecture is sized to meet it under typical conditions** (from §2).
3. **Every user-facing operation has an explicit latency budget split across components; the budget is verified in CI** (from §3). Network round trips and GC pauses are the biggest hidden contributors.
4. **SSE as the default for one-way push; WebSocket only when bidirectional is genuinely required** (from §4). The harness already uses SSE for the dashboard.
5. **Adaptive jitter buffers for any animation, game loop, or audio stream; debounced progress updates at the harness layer** (from §5). Jitter is often more user-visible than mean latency.
6. **Live-query substrates (ElectricSQL, Supabase Realtime, Convex) over rolled polling; the harness's projection layer IS a live-query substrate built on the event log** (from §6).
7. **Presence routed through a separate lighter channel (Liveblocks, Pusher) rather than the data-sync substrate; harness has no presence concern** (from §7).
8. **The harness has 6 near-real-time surfaces with explicit soft-real-time tiers; each corresponds to an SLI in the navigator-experience SLO list** (from §8).

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This is a choice-layer entry that mostly resolves toward "soft real-time, not hard real-time" and toward standard managed-substrate patterns at the app layer.** The most generative move is **§1: classifying every real-time-shaped feature into a tier before deciding architecture** — this prevents the most common over-engineering trap (treating a chat system like an avionics control loop). The second most generative is **§6: the harness's projection layer IS a live-query substrate** — adding another row to the "X is structurally Y" pattern list.

**The most concrete near-term unlock at the harness layer** is **applying explicit perceptual tiers and latency budgets to the six surfaces in §8**, which connects to the SLI/SLO entry's commitments and gives the navigator a measurable answer to "is the harness feeling responsive". **The most architecturally interesting thread** is the recognition that the harness's near-real-time experience is built from primitives (SSE for live updates, projection layer for derived state, event log for ordered events) that the live-query database literature has been independently building toward.

**Layer note**: applies to both layers. Harness has six near-real-time surfaces (gate prompts, execution progress, skill completion feedback, calibration recompute, sub-agent status, question/response) classified into explicit soft-real-time tiers and tracked via the SLI/SLO discipline. App-layer real-time features follow standard production patterns: SSE for one-way push, WebSocket for bidirectional, live-query substrates (ElectricSQL preferred), presence via separate lighter channels, latency budgets verified in CI.

---

## Cost engineering and FinOps

The discipline of treating cloud cost as an engineering metric — measuring it, attributing it, forecasting it, and making engineering decisions that account for it. Cost engineering is interesting for the orchestrator because **the dominant cost line at the harness layer is LLM tokens**, and the sleep test has already been extended to economics (Resilience patterns §6) as the load-bearing defense against runaway spending. At the app layer, cost engineering is the standard FinOps discipline (cloud cost attribution, reserved capacity, egress optimization, unit economics) made concrete by the multi-tenancy entry's commitment to per-tenant cost visibility.

The entry exists because cost is the dimension where the orchestrator's economics are most non-traditional (LLM tokens dominate over compute and storage), where the harness's defenses are most load-bearing (a runaway loop can spend the navigator's monthly budget in hours), and where the sleep test most directly applies. It also exists to give app-layer features explicit cost-engineering discipline before they reach the scale where unit economics become unforgiving.

This is a **choice-layer** entry for **both layers**.

### 1. The cost dimensions of a typical cloud application

| Dimension | Typical share | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Compute** | 30–50% | EC2, Lambda, container hours |
| **Storage** | 5–20% | Object storage, block storage, snapshots |
| **Egress (bandwidth)** | 5–40% | Often dominant for read-heavy apps |
| **Database** | 10–30% | Managed DBs (RDS, Aurora, Spanner) |
| **Managed services** | 5–15% | Auth, payments, observability, search, queues |
| **Third-party APIs** | 5–80% | LLMs, payment processors, geocoding, mapping |
| **Inter-region transfer** | 1–10% | Multi-region or multi-cloud architectures |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The single most underestimated cost dimension is egress (bandwidth out of the cloud)**, and the second most underestimated is third-party API spend. **Architectural commitment**: any cost forecast must explicitly enumerate egress and third-party API costs separately from compute, because both can dominate the total bill in ways that "we're paying for a server" intuitions miss. **For the orchestrator specifically**: the dominant cost dimension is LLM API tokens, which is the third-party-API category, and the cost grows roughly linearly with the navigator's activity (more loops = more model calls = more tokens). **Architectural recognition**: the orchestrator is structurally a high-third-party-API-cost system, and the cost-engineering discipline that matters most is token accounting and budget enforcement, not the EC2-hour-counting discipline that dominates traditional FinOps.

### 2. Unit economics and the cost-per-action question

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost per request** | Total cost / number of requests | The simplest unit |
| **Cost per active user** | Total cost / monthly active users | SaaS standard |
| **Cost per tenant** | Per-tenant cost attribution | Multi-tenancy §6 |
| **Cost per feature** | Allocated cost per feature/code path | Required for build/buy decisions |
| **Margin per customer** | Revenue per customer minus cost per customer | The unit economics question |
| **CAC payback period** | Customer acquisition cost / monthly margin | The growth-economics primitive |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Unit economics is the discipline that tells you whether the business is structurally profitable** — without per-customer cost attribution, the company's "we're growing fast" story can mask the reality that every new customer is losing money. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: per-tenant cost attribution is non-negotiable** (named in Multi-tenancy §6), and the per-tenant cost row feeds a daily margin calculation. **For the harness specifically**: the unit is "per loop" or "per skill execution" rather than "per customer", because there's only one navigator. **The harness equivalent of unit economics**: cost per loop completion, cost per learning-loop iteration, cost per skill execution by version — which connects directly to the calibration system's quality scores to give a "quality-per-dollar" ratio. **The most generative observation**: the harness can rank skill versions not just by quality but by quality-per-token, and the upgrade-proposal pipeline (already named in entry 12) should surface this ratio as a guardrail metric.

### 3. The LLM token economy as the dominant cost dimension

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Input tokens** | Prompt + context paid per call | The bulk of typical LLM cost |
| **Output tokens** | Generated response paid per call | Usually 5–20x more expensive per token |
| **Cached input tokens** | Provider charges less for cached prompt prefixes | Major optimization for repeated context |
| **Reasoning tokens** | Hidden chain-of-thought tokens (some providers) | Can be 10x the visible output |
| **Model tier selection** | Frontier vs mid vs cheap models | Up to 100x cost difference for similar tasks |
| **Batch API discount** | Async batch calls at 50% pricing | Standard for non-interactive workloads |
| **Context window pricing** | Long-context calls cost more per token at scale | Sub-linear at small scale, super-linear at large |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Token economics is the load-bearing cost dimension for the orchestrator**, and the architectural disciplines that matter most are: (1) **prompt caching** — keep the long static prefix in cache so repeated calls only pay for the dynamic suffix; (2) **model tier selection** — use cheap models for tasks that don't need frontier capability, and only escalate when calibration shows the cheap model fails; (3) **batch API for non-interactive work** — the learning loop, calibration recompute, and any background analysis that doesn't need to feel real-time should use batch APIs at 50% discount. **Architectural commitment**: the harness adopts all three as defaults — the cached-prompt-prefix pattern, the cheaper-model-with-fallback pattern (already named in Resilience patterns §7), and the batch API for any work that doesn't have a soft-real-time tier (Real-time §1). **The forbidden anti-pattern**: using the most expensive model for every call because "quality matters" — without measurement, this is throwing money at a problem the cheaper model could have solved.

### 4. The sleep test extended to economics

| Concern | Standard cost defense | Harness equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Runaway loop spending unbounded** | Cloud budget alerts | Token budget caps enforced at the model-call layer |
| **Feedback-loop cost amplification** | Circuit breakers | Per-skill, per-loop, per-org budget enforcement |
| **Sleep-time spending** | "While the human sleeps" defense | Already named in Resilience patterns §6 |
| **Cost-per-loop visibility** | Per-feature cost attribution | Per-loop and per-skill cost reporting |
| **Cheaper-model fallback** | Tier-based degradation | Already named in Resilience patterns §7 |
| **Hard ceiling on monthly spend** | Account-level budget cap | Per-org monthly token budget |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The sleep test extension to economics, named in Resilience patterns §6, is the load-bearing defense against the orchestrator's most dangerous failure mode**: a loop that runs while the navigator sleeps and burns the entire monthly LLM budget by morning. **Architectural commitment: token budget caps are enforced at the model-call layer (not just monitored in dashboards), and the cap structure is hierarchical** — per-skill, per-loop, per-org, per-month — with the most restrictive cap winning. **The defense in depth**: per-call cost prediction (refuse to make a call that would exceed the remaining budget), per-loop cost cumulative tracking (kill the loop when its budget is exhausted), per-org daily and monthly caps, and a hard global ceiling that the navigator can override only manually. **The connection to the multi-tenancy entry's "harness adjacency"**: the harness adopts per-org token budget allocation as a noisy-neighbor defense — one runaway org's loops should not consume the budget that other orgs need. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: budget alerts without enforcement — alerts go to a notification channel the navigator might miss, while enforcement actually stops the spending.

### 5. Cost attribution and tagging

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Resource tagging** | Per-resource labels (team, feature, environment) | Required for any per-thing cost view |
| **Tag-based cost reporting** | Group cloud bills by tag | Standard FinOps practice |
| **Showback** | Show teams their cost without charging them | Cultural step toward accountability |
| **Chargeback** | Actually charge teams for their cost | The strongest accountability |
| **Per-feature cost** | Allocate cost to features for build/buy decisions | Required for honest engineering trade-offs |
| **Per-tenant cost** | Multi-tenancy §6 | Required for unit economics |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Resource tagging is the prerequisite for any cost engineering discipline** — without tags, every line in the cloud bill is "general infrastructure" and no one can be held accountable for their share. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: every cloud resource is tagged at creation time with at least four labels (team, feature, environment, cost-center)**, and the IaC layer enforces this — untagged resources are rejected at the apply step. **The harness equivalent of tagging**: every model call is tagged with the loop, skill, sub-agent, and org that made it, and the cost attribution rolls up by any of those dimensions. **The connection to the event log**: every model call is already an event in the event log, and adding cost as an event field gives per-call attribution for free — the cost reporting is then a derived projection over the event log, not a separate accounting system.

### 6. Pricing model awareness for cloud resources

| Resource | On-demand | Reserved / committed | Spot / preemptible | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Compute (EC2, Compute Engine)** | Highest cost | 30–60% off for 1–3 year commit | 50–90% off, can be killed | Spot for fault-tolerant batch |
| **Lambda / Functions** | Per-invocation pricing | Limited committed-use discount | N/A | Predictable per-request workloads |
| **Database (RDS, Aurora)** | High | Significant savings for committed | N/A | Always reserved at steady state |
| **Storage** | Per GB-month | Tier-based (hot/warm/cold) | N/A | Lifecycle rules from File storage §6 |
| **Egress** | Per GB | Some private interconnect savings | N/A | CDN absorption is the answer |
| **Managed services** | Usage-based | Volume tiers | N/A | Negotiate at scale |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Reserved capacity is the single most impactful FinOps discipline for predictable workloads** — committing to 1-year terms typically saves 30–60% off on-demand pricing, and the commitment is right-sized to a portion of baseline load while leaving the burst capacity at on-demand. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: any workload at steady state for more than 6 months has reserved capacity for the baseline portion. **Spot / preemptible instances are the right answer for any fault-tolerant batch work** (data pipelines, training jobs, async background jobs) at 50–90% off on-demand, accepting that the instance can be killed at any time and the work needs to retry. **At the harness layer**: there is no cloud compute to commit because the harness runs locally; the equivalent discipline is "the navigator's local hardware is paid for once" and the architectural recognition is that local compute has a flat cost while cloud compute scales with usage — this is one more reason the local-first commitment is economically aligned, not just architecturally.

### 7. Forecasting and budget management

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Historical projection** | Extrapolate from recent spend | The simplest forecast |
| **Anomaly detection** | Alert on spend that deviates from baseline | Standard FinOps practice |
| **Per-feature forecasting** | Predict cost impact of new features | Required for product decisions |
| **Budget vs actual tracking** | Compare projected spend to actual | Standard accounting |
| **Cost commit alerts** | Alert when on track to exceed budget | Predictive defense |
| **Hard cost ceilings** | Stop creating resources when budget exhausted | The strongest defense, rarely deployed |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Most teams have budget alerts but very few teams have hard cost ceilings**, and the gap is the difference between "we noticed we were over budget last week" and "we structurally cannot spend more than the budget". **Architectural commitment for the app layer: hard cost ceilings on all non-production environments** (staging, preview, dev) so that one runaway test doesn't blow the monthly budget, with manual override required to exceed. **Production environments can't usually have hard ceilings because the cost of "we ran out of budget and stopped serving customers" exceeds the cost of overspending**, but they should have aggressive anomaly detection. **At the harness layer**: hard ceilings are appropriate because there's no customer to fail — the navigator can set a hard cap and live with the consequences of hitting it, which is "stop running loops until the cap is reset". This is the same discipline as the sleep test extension to economics, applied at the budget-management layer.

### 8. The cost-quality-speed trade-off triangle

| Choice | Cost | Quality | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Frontier model, full context** | Highest | Highest | Slowest (large model latency) |
| **Mid-tier model, full context** | Medium | High | Medium |
| **Cheap model, full context** | Low | Medium | Fastest |
| **Frontier model, batch API** | Half cost | Highest | Hours-deferred |
| **Cheap model + frontier verifier** | Medium | High (verified) | Medium |
| **Cached prompts + cheap model** | Lowest | Variable | Fastest |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The cost-quality-speed triangle is the architectural choice space for any LLM-using system**, and the architectural commitment is to make the choice explicitly per-task rather than defaulting uniformly to "frontier model, every time". **Architectural commitment for the harness**: each skill carries an explicit model-tier preference (frontier / mid / cheap) and a fallback chain, and the calibration system tracks quality-per-dollar across tiers so the choice can be revisited as evidence accumulates. **The cheap-model-plus-frontier-verifier pattern is the most generative**: use a cheap model to generate, use a frontier model to verify or rerank, accept the cheap model's output when the verifier passes. This gives close-to-frontier quality at a fraction of the cost. **The connection to A/B testing §8**: the experimentation framework's primary metric (rubric quality) and guardrail metric (token cost) are exactly the inputs to making this trade-off honestly. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: defaulting to the most expensive model "to be safe" without measuring whether the expense is justified — this is the LLM equivalent of running every workload on the largest EC2 instance because you can.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **The orchestrator is structurally a high-third-party-API-cost system; LLM tokens dominate; cost engineering discipline is token accounting, not EC2-hour counting** (from §1).
2. **Per-loop and per-skill-execution unit economics; harness can rank skill versions by quality-per-token; upgrade pipeline surfaces this ratio** (from §2).
3. **Prompt caching + model-tier selection + batch API for non-interactive work as the three load-bearing token-cost defenses** (from §3).
4. **Token budget caps enforced at the model-call layer (not just dashboarded), with hierarchical cap structure (per-skill / per-loop / per-org / per-month)** (from §4). Sleep test extension to economics.
5. **Resource tagging required at creation time; cost attribution as derived projection over the event log; every model call tagged with loop/skill/sub-agent/org** (from §5).
6. **Reserved capacity for predictable steady-state workloads; spot for fault-tolerant batch; local-first commitment is economically aligned (flat local hardware cost vs scaling cloud cost)** (from §6).
7. **Hard cost ceilings on non-production environments; harness can have hard ceilings because there's no customer to fail** (from §7).
8. **Cost-quality-speed triangle as explicit per-task choice; cheap-model-plus-frontier-verifier as the most generative pattern** (from §8). Connects to A/B testing §8 guardrail metrics.

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This is a choice-layer entry that mostly resolves toward token-economy discipline at the harness layer and standard FinOps discipline at the app layer.** The most generative move is **§4: token budget caps enforced at the model-call layer**, because it converts the sleep-test framing into a load-bearing defense against the harness's most dangerous failure mode, and §8's cheap-model-plus-frontier-verifier pattern is the most generative cost-quality optimization available. The second most generative is **§2: quality-per-token as a calibration metric**, because it gives the upgrade-proposal pipeline a richer trade-off space than quality alone.

**The most concrete near-term unlock at the harness layer** is **adding token cost as an event field on every model call**, which immediately enables per-skill, per-loop, per-org cost attribution as derived projections over the event log — no separate accounting system needed. **The most architecturally interesting thread** is the recognition that the local-first commitment is economically aligned, not just architecturally — local hardware is a flat cost while cloud compute scales with usage, and the harness's local-first choice doubles as a cost-engineering choice that other systems pay for via reserved capacity and spot instances.

**Layer note**: applies to both layers. Harness cost engineering is dominated by LLM tokens, with token budget caps enforced at the model-call layer, hierarchical cap structure, prompt caching, model-tier selection with calibration-driven fallback, batch API for non-interactive work, and per-call cost as an event-log field for attribution. App-layer cost engineering follows standard FinOps: resource tagging, per-tenant attribution (Multi-tenancy §6), reserved capacity for steady state, spot for fault-tolerant batch, hard ceilings on non-production, anomaly detection on production, CDN absorption of egress.

---

## Data integration, ETL, and change data capture

The discipline of moving data between systems — extracting from sources, transforming to a target shape, loading into destinations, capturing changes as they happen, and keeping the systems in sync. Data integration is interesting for the orchestrator because **the harness's event log is structurally a change data capture stream of the orchestrator's own state**, and the same patterns that other systems use to expose database changes to downstream consumers are already in place at the harness layer without ever having been called "CDC".

The entry exists because data integration is the dimension that connects production systems to analytics, downstream applications, and external partners. Most teams build this ad-hoc and discover too late that the integration layer has become a load-bearing piece of infrastructure with no owner. The right architecture treats integration as a first-class concern from the start.

This is a **resolution-confirmation** entry for **both layers**.

### 1. ETL vs ELT vs streaming integration

| Pattern | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **ETL (Extract → Transform → Load)** | Transform on the way to the warehouse | Legacy; expensive on-ramp |
| **ELT (Extract → Load → Transform)** | Land raw, transform in the warehouse | The modern default |
| **Streaming ETL** | Continuous transform-on-the-fly | When freshness matters |
| **Reverse ETL** | Move warehouse data back into operational systems | The "warehouse as source of truth" pattern |
| **Zero-ETL** | Direct query across source and warehouse | AWS pitch; rarely the right answer |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **ELT replaced ETL as the default in the 2020s** because cloud warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks) are cheap enough to land raw data and powerful enough to transform after loading, and the architectural shift unlocked dbt-style transformation as a SQL-first discipline rather than a pipeline-first discipline. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: any orchestrator-built app that needs analytics lands raw data in the warehouse first and transforms with dbt or equivalent, rather than transforming during extract. **The harness adjacency**: the event log is structurally a "raw data lake of orchestrator events" and any analytical consumer reads from it via projections — which is the harness's version of the ELT pattern, where the "warehouse" is the local SQLite (or eventual Iceberg) and the "transformations" are the projection logic. **Reverse ETL is the underrated pattern** for getting analytical insights back into operational systems (e.g., "the warehouse computed each customer's lifetime value; reverse-ETL it back into the CRM so customer support sees it"); for the orchestrator, the equivalent is "calibration insights computed in the analytics layer should flow back into the operational decision layer" — which is structurally what the upgrade-proposal pipeline already does.

### 2. Change data capture

| Mechanism | How it works | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger-based CDC** | Database triggers write changes to a side table | Legacy; high overhead |
| **Log-based CDC (Debezium)** | Read the database's WAL directly | The modern default |
| **Polling-based** | Periodically query for changed rows | Crude but simple |
| **Outbox pattern** | Application writes domain events alongside data changes | The application-managed CDC |
| **Native change feeds** | DB exposes its own change stream API | MongoDB change streams, DynamoDB streams |
| **Built-in Postgres logical replication** | The Postgres-native foundation | What Debezium uses |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Log-based CDC is the load-bearing pattern that connects operational databases to downstream consumers without coupling either side** — the source database doesn't need to know who's reading its changes, the consumers don't need to coordinate with each other, and the transformation can happen anywhere along the pipeline. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: any production database that needs to expose changes to other systems uses log-based CDC (Debezium for self-hosted, the cloud-native equivalent for managed Postgres) rather than polling or trigger-based approaches. **The harness recognition**: **the harness's event log IS log-based CDC, structurally** — every state change in the harness is written to the event log, downstream consumers (projections, learning loop, calibration, dashboard) read from the log without coordinating with the producer, and the same pattern works in-process or out-of-process. **The architectural recognition**: this is the same pattern Debezium implements, but at the application-event layer rather than the database-row layer, and the harness's event log is more sophisticated than CDC because the events are semantically meaningful rather than row-level diffs.

### 3. The outbox pattern

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Dual-write problem** | App writes to DB and to message broker; one fails | The bug source the outbox solves |
| **Outbox table** | App writes events to a DB table in the same transaction as the data change | Atomic with the data change |
| **Outbox poller** | Background process reads outbox, publishes to broker | The publish step |
| **Idempotent publish** | Each outbox row published at most once via dedup | Required for correctness |
| **Cleanup after publish** | Delete or mark outbox rows after broker acks | Standard hygiene |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The outbox pattern is the load-bearing solution to the dual-write problem** — without it, an application writing to both its database and a message broker has a race condition where one succeeds and the other fails, leaving the systems out of sync. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: any application that needs to publish events about its data changes uses the outbox pattern, with the outbox table written atomically alongside the data change and a separate process publishing from the outbox to the broker. **The harness adjacency**: the event log already IS the outbox — every harness operation that produces an event writes it as part of the operation's commit (because the event log is the source of truth, not a side channel), and downstream consumers read from the log. **The architectural recognition**: by making the event log the source of truth rather than a derived stream, the harness avoids the outbox pattern entirely — there's no "primary database plus secondary event stream" to keep in sync, because the event stream IS the primary store. **This is one more dissolution that the local-first + event-sourced commitment provides.**

### 4. Schema evolution and the integration contract

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Schema registry** | Centralized store of message schemas with versioning | Confluent Schema Registry, Apicurio |
| **Backward compatibility** | New schemas readable by old consumers | Required for rolling upgrades |
| **Forward compatibility** | Old schemas readable by new consumers | Required for downgrades |
| **Avro / Protobuf / JSON Schema** | Schema description languages | Avro is the Kafka standard |
| **Field-level evolution rules** | "Adding optional fields is safe; removing fields is not" | Standard per-format rules |
| **Schema-on-read vs schema-on-write** | Validate at read vs write time | Tradeoff between flexibility and safety |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Schema evolution is where data integration meets the configuration entry's discipline** — every schema change is structurally a configuration change that affects multiple systems, and the same disciplines (validation in CI, backward/forward compatibility rules, deprecation deadlines) apply. **Architectural commitment for the app layer: every cross-system message has a schema in a schema registry, every schema change is reviewed in CI for backward compatibility, and breaking changes require a versioned migration with explicit producer/consumer coordination.** **At the harness layer**: the event log's events have an implicit schema (defined by the producer's emit code and the consumers' read code), and the architectural commitment is to make the schema explicit via Zod (already named in Configuration §4) and to add backward-compatibility checks in CI for any event type that has more than one consumer. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: schemaless integration where the producer changes the message shape and the consumer crashes — the consumer should fail at schema-validation time with a clear error, not at use time with a confusing one.

### 5. Integration patterns

| Pattern | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Point-to-point** | A talks directly to B | Simple at small scale; quadratic complexity at scale |
| **Hub-and-spoke** | All integrations through a central hub | Easier to govern; hub becomes bottleneck |
| **Message bus (event bus)** | Pub/sub between many producers and consumers | The modern default |
| **Service mesh** | Sidecar-managed integration with policies | Service mesh entry (#26) |
| **API gateway** | Centralized API surface for external consumers | Standard for B2B APIs |
| **Data mesh** | Decentralized data ownership with shared protocols | The modern data architecture |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Point-to-point integration is the right answer at small scale and the wrong answer at large scale**, and the transition is one of the hardest architectural moves to make retroactively — once N services are integrated point-to-point, the dependencies are too tangled to refactor cleanly. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: any new integration that has a possibility of becoming the third or fourth point-to-point integration goes through a message bus (the event bus) instead. **The harness layer is structurally a message bus** because every consumer reads from the same event log, and the integration pattern is hub-and-spoke with the event log as the hub — and the hub is structurally simple because there's no separate hub process to operate. **Data mesh is the modern data architecture pattern** for decentralizing data ownership across teams while sharing protocols and discovery — relevant at the app layer for any multi-team data architecture, not relevant at the harness layer because there's only one team.

### 6. Managed integration tools

| Tool | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Airbyte** | Open-source ELT, hundreds of connectors | The sovereign-friendly default |
| **Fivetran** | Managed ELT, premium pricing | When ops cost matters more than dollar cost |
| **Stitch** | Older managed ELT | Legacy |
| **dbt** | SQL-first transformation, version-controlled | The standard transformation layer |
| **Dagster / Prefect / Airflow** | Data orchestration (workflow scheduling) | Batch processing entry (#24) |
| **Estuary Flow** | Streaming ETL with both real-time and batch modes | When freshness matters |
| **Hevo Data** | Managed integration with focus on simplicity | Mid-market alternative |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Airbyte + dbt is the sovereign-friendly modern data stack** — Airbyte handles the extract and load, dbt handles the transformation, both are open-source and self-hostable, and the combination covers most of what teams use Fivetran + dbt for at significantly lower cost. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: Airbyte for any data integration needs (operational DBs → warehouse, third-party SaaS → warehouse, warehouse → operational), dbt for transformations on top, with the warehouse as the central analytical store. **The harness adjacency**: there is no traditional ETL because the event log is the source of truth for all operational data, and any analytics is a derived projection over the event log; the dbt-style transformation discipline transfers to the projection layer (named SQL transformations, version-controlled, tested) but the substrate is the harness's projection runtime, not a separate dbt process.

### 7. The reverse ETL question

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Warehouse as analytics-only** | The classic separation | Pre-2020 default |
| **Warehouse as operational source** | Reverse ETL feeds operational systems | The modern pattern |
| **Operational analytics** | Warehouse insights drive operational decisions | The reverse-ETL goal |
| **Hightouch / Census / Polytomic** | Managed reverse-ETL tools | The vendor category |
| **Cohort syncing** | Warehouse computes cohorts, sends to operational tools | Marketing automation pattern |
| **Score syncing** | Warehouse computes scores, sends back to CRM/support | Customer success pattern |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Reverse ETL closes the loop between analytical insight and operational action**, and it's the architectural pattern that justifies treating the warehouse as a first-class system rather than just a reporting backend. **The harness equivalent**: the calibration system computes quality-per-token, error rates, and SLI compliance across many executions, and the upgrade-proposal pipeline feeds those insights back into the operational decision layer (skill version selection, budget allocation, model tier choice). **This is the harness's reverse-ETL loop**: insights computed from the event log flow back into operational decisions about how the harness behaves next. **Architectural recognition**: the harness has been doing reverse-ETL all along under the name "learning loop", and the equivalence is worth noting because it makes the calibration → upgrade-proposal flow legitimate as a category of well-studied data architecture rather than a one-off invention.

### 8. The harness event log as integration substrate

| Integration concern | Standard answer | Harness equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **CDC source** | Debezium reading database WAL | Event log (already CDC-shaped) |
| **Outbox pattern** | Application writes outbox table + poller | Not needed (event log IS the outbox) |
| **Schema registry** | Confluent Schema Registry | Zod schemas in code |
| **Integration pattern** | Message bus + pub/sub | Event log as the bus, projections as subscribers |
| **ELT raw landing** | Snowflake / BigQuery | SQLite or Iceberg projection of event log |
| **Transformation layer** | dbt | Projection logic (SQL or code) |
| **Reverse ETL** | Hightouch / Census | Learning loop feeding upgrade proposals |
| **Schema evolution** | Backward-compatible schema changes | Same discipline applied to event types |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The harness event log is a complete data integration substrate**, and every traditional integration concern has a harness-native equivalent that already exists. **Architectural recognition**: the event log is the source of truth, the projections are the transformation layer, the learning loop is the reverse-ETL flow, and the schema discipline is enforced via Zod. **The architectural commitment**: any future "we need to integrate this" question at the harness layer should be answered first by "what consumer would read from the event log?" before considering external integration tools. **The escape valve**: if the harness needs to integrate with a truly external system (a cloud warehouse for cross-machine analytics, a partner's API for data exchange), the integration is one consumer of the event log that exports to the external system, preserving the event log as the single source of truth.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **ELT replaced ETL as the default; harness's event log + projections IS the ELT pattern with the local SQLite as the warehouse** (from §1).
2. **Log-based CDC as the load-bearing pattern for exposing changes to downstream consumers; the harness event log IS log-based CDC at the application-event layer** (from §2).
3. **Outbox pattern solves the dual-write problem; the harness avoids outbox entirely because the event log IS the source of truth, not a side channel** (from §3). One more dissolution.
4. **Every cross-system message has a schema in a schema registry; harness uses Zod with backward-compat checks in CI** (from §4).
5. **Point-to-point integration is right at small scale, wrong at large scale; harness layer IS structurally a message bus with the event log as the hub** (from §5).
6. **Airbyte + dbt is the sovereign-friendly modern data stack; the harness's projection layer transfers dbt-style transformation discipline** (from §6).
7. **Reverse ETL closes the loop between analytical insight and operational action; the harness has been doing reverse-ETL all along under the name "learning loop"** (from §7).
8. **The harness event log is a complete data integration substrate; every traditional integration concern has a harness-native equivalent already in place** (from §8).

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This is a resolution-confirmation entry that adds significant material to the "X IS structurally Y" pattern list** — the event log IS log-based CDC, the harness IS structurally a message bus, the learning loop IS reverse ETL, and the projection layer IS a dbt-style transformation runtime. The most generative move is **§3 + §8: the harness avoids the outbox pattern entirely** because the event log is the source of truth rather than a derived stream, which dissolves one of the most common integration bugs (the dual-write race) by structural choice.

**The most concrete near-term unlock at the app layer** is **adopting Airbyte + dbt as the standard data integration stack** for any orchestrator-built app that needs analytics, because it's sovereign-friendly and aligns with the rest of the substrate choices. **The most architecturally interesting thread** is the recognition that the harness's learning loop is structurally a reverse-ETL flow — calibration insights computed from the event log flow back into operational decisions — and naming the equivalence makes it legitimate to borrow patterns from the reverse-ETL literature for the upgrade-proposal pipeline.

**Layer note**: applies to both layers. Harness data integration is the event log + projections + learning loop, which structurally implements log-based CDC, the message bus pattern, the ELT pattern, schema evolution discipline, and reverse ETL — all without separate integration tools. App-layer integration follows standard patterns: log-based CDC (Debezium or managed equivalent), Airbyte + dbt for ELT, schema registry with backward-compat checks, outbox pattern for any application that publishes to a broker, reverse ETL (Hightouch or Census) for warehouse-to-operational data flows.

---

## Batch processing and workflow orchestration

The discipline of running computations on bounded data sets, scheduling work that doesn't need to be real-time, and orchestrating multi-step pipelines where each step depends on previous ones — Airflow, Dagster, Prefect, Temporal, Cron, scheduled jobs, DAGs, sensors, backfills. Workflow orchestration is interesting for the orchestrator because **the harness IS structurally a workflow orchestration system**, where loops are the DAGs, phases are the dependencies, gates are the sensors, and the navigator is the operator — and the same patterns that batch and workflow tools encode are already in place at the harness layer in their LLM-native form.

The entry exists because workflow orchestration is one of the most directly applicable categories of distributed-systems engineering to the orchestrator's core mission, and naming the equivalence makes the borrowing of patterns from the workflow literature legitimate. It also exists because the harness's loop runner has operational concerns (retries, idempotency, backfills, scheduling) that are exactly the concerns workflow orchestrators have spent a decade resolving.

This is a **resolution-confirmation** entry for **both layers**.

### 1. Batch vs streaming vs workflow

| Pattern | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Batch processing** | Bounded data, run-to-completion | Reports, ETL, training jobs |
| **Streaming processing** | Unbounded data, continuous (Stream processing entry) | Live analytics, real-time alerts |
| **Workflow orchestration** | DAG of tasks with dependencies, possibly long-running | Multi-step pipelines |
| **Job scheduler** | Time-triggered execution of a single job | Cron, scheduled tasks |
| **Long-running workflows** | Workflows that span hours, days, or human approval steps | Saga-shaped processes |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The lines between batch, streaming, and workflow have blurred in 2026** — modern tools (Dagster, Prefect, Temporal) handle all three modes from the same primitive (a graph of tasks with dependencies and a scheduler), and the architectural choice is mostly which tool's biases align best with the work shape. **Architectural commitment**: choose the orchestration tool by the work shape, not by category — short batch jobs go in Dagster/Prefect, long-running workflows with human steps go in Temporal, simple time-triggered jobs go in Cron or its managed equivalent. **The harness adjacency**: the loop runner is structurally a long-running workflow orchestrator with human approval steps (gates), and the closest commercial equivalent is **Temporal** rather than Airflow or Dagster — Temporal's primitives (workflows, activities, signals, durable state) map onto the harness's primitives (loops, skills, gates, event log) almost one-to-one.

### 2. The DAG as the core abstraction

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Task** | A unit of work | The atom |
| **Dependency edge** | "Task B runs after task A" | The edge type |
| **Static DAG** | Defined at definition time | Standard for batch |
| **Dynamic DAG** | Built at runtime based on data | Required for fan-out patterns |
| **Sub-DAG** | Reusable DAG composed into others | Standard composition |
| **Trigger / sensor** | Wait for an external condition before proceeding | Required for event-driven workflows |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The DAG is the load-bearing abstraction in workflow orchestration**, and most operational concerns (parallelism, failure isolation, partial retries, observability) become tractable once the work is expressed as a DAG. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: any multi-step process with non-trivial dependencies is expressed as a DAG in a workflow tool, not as imperative code with implicit ordering. **The harness adjacency**: the loop's phase structure IS a DAG with linear topology — INIT → SCAFFOLD → IMPLEMENT → TEST → VERIFY → VALIDATE → DOCUMENT → REVIEW → SHIP — and the skills within each phase form sub-DAGs. **Architectural recognition: the loop definition (`loop.json` named in CLAUDE.md) is structurally a DAG specification**, and the loop runner is structurally a DAG executor. **The most generative observation**: the harness has been building toward DAG-structured work for the entire project, and naming the equivalence makes the borrowing of patterns from workflow tools (sensor primitives, dynamic fan-out, sub-DAG composition) legitimate.

### 3. Scheduling and triggers

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Cron schedule** | Time-based recurrence | The classic primitive |
| **Sensor / trigger** | Wait for an external event before running | Event-driven workflows |
| **Backfill** | Run a workflow over historical time periods | Required for reprocessing |
| **Catchup** | Run all missed schedules after downtime | Standard scheduler feature |
| **Manual trigger** | Operator-initiated execution | Required for debugging and ops |
| **Webhook trigger** | External system kicks off workflow | Standard integration pattern |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Sensors and triggers are the load-bearing primitive that makes workflows event-driven rather than time-driven**, and the right architecture lets a workflow start when a precondition is met rather than when a clock fires. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: time-based triggers for genuinely periodic work (daily reports, hourly aggregations), sensor-based triggers for event-driven work (file landed, message received, deadline reached). **The harness adjacency**: the gate primitive IS a sensor — a phase waits at a gate until the navigator approves it, exactly like a workflow waits at a sensor until an external condition is met. **Architectural recognition: the harness's gate IS structurally a workflow sensor with the navigator as the external system**, and the same operational concerns (timeout, default action, escalation) apply. **Backfill is the underrated workflow feature**: the ability to re-run a workflow over historical time periods, which the harness gets for free because of event-log replay (re-execute the loop against a snapshot of the event log at any prior offset).

### 4. Failure handling and idempotency

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Task-level retry** | Failed task retries automatically with backoff | Standard for transient failures |
| **Partial retry (only failed branches)** | DAG continues from the failed task without re-running successful tasks | Required for expensive workflows |
| **Idempotent tasks** | Safe to retry without side effects | Required for at-least-once workflow execution |
| **Compensating actions (saga)** | Undo previous steps when later steps fail | Saga orchestration entry |
| **Dead letter for poisoned tasks** | Tasks that always fail go to manual review | Required for poison detection |
| **Workflow-level retry** | Re-run the entire workflow | The blunt instrument |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Partial retry (only re-running failed tasks rather than the entire workflow) is the load-bearing operational feature** that makes workflow tools usable at scale — without it, a 10-hour pipeline that fails in the last task wastes 9.9 hours of work on every retry. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: every workflow tool used must support partial retry, and tasks must be designed to be idempotent (safe to retry). **The harness adjacency**: the loop runner already supports phase-level resume (a loop can be paused at any phase and resumed later), and the architectural commitment is that this is structurally partial retry at the phase granularity. **The connection to Saga orchestration**: the harness IS a saga framework (Saga §8), and the saga pattern's compensating-action discipline is the same as workflow tools' failure-handling discipline applied to long-running workflows. **The architectural recognition**: the harness has saga semantics, partial retry, and idempotent execution because of the event-sourced architecture, all without explicit workflow-orchestration infrastructure.

### 5. Workflow tools and their fit

| Tool | Primary model | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Apache Airflow** | DAG-as-Python, scheduled batch | Legacy data pipelines, broad ecosystem |
| **Dagster** | Software-defined assets, modern UX | The modern data-pipeline default |
| **Prefect** | Pythonic flows, dynamic DAGs | Lightweight alternative to Airflow |
| **Temporal** | Workflow-as-code, durable execution | Long-running workflows with state |
| **AWS Step Functions** | JSON state machines, AWS-integrated | AWS-aligned workflows |
| **Argo Workflows** | Kubernetes-native YAML workflows | Kubernetes shops |
| **Cadence** | Temporal predecessor | Legacy Uber-originated |
| **Inngest** | Event-driven serverless workflows | Modern serverless-first |
| **Trigger.dev** | Background-job-shaped workflows | Web app developer experience |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Temporal is the most architecturally aligned external tool for the orchestrator's loop pattern** because Temporal's primitives (workflows, activities, signals, durable state) map onto the harness's primitives (loops, skills, gates, event log) almost one-to-one — Temporal even uses an event log as its source of truth and replays events to reconstruct workflow state, which is exactly the harness's pattern. **Architectural recognition**: Temporal and the harness's loop runner are independently arrived at the same architectural pattern, which is strong evidence that the pattern is correct. **The harness equivalent of Temporal's "activities"**: skills. **The harness equivalent of Temporal's "workflows"**: loops. **The harness equivalent of Temporal's "signals"**: gates and questions. **The harness equivalent of Temporal's "durable state"**: the event log. **The architectural commitment**: future enhancements to the loop runner should look at Temporal's primitives as prior art rather than reinventing them. **Dagster is the right answer for any orchestrator-built app that needs traditional data-pipeline orchestration** (e.g., a daily training job for an ML feature, an hourly aggregation for analytics) because it's the modern Airflow successor with better developer experience.

### 6. The harness loop as workflow orchestration

| Workflow concept | Standard tool | Harness equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Workflow definition** | DAG in Python / YAML / JSON | `loop.json` + LOOP.md |
| **Task / activity** | Python function with retries | Skill |
| **Sensor / signal** | Wait-for-event primitive | Gate, question, navigator approval |
| **Workflow execution** | Workflow runner instance | Execution (`start_execution`) |
| **Durable state** | DB-backed workflow state | The event log |
| **Replay / resume** | From last checkpoint | From any event-log offset |
| **Scheduling** | Cron / sensor | Manual + autonomous loop trigger |
| **Observability** | Workflow run history | Execution log + dashboard |
| **Partial retry** | Re-run failed tasks only | Retry skill, advance phase |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The harness's loop runner is structurally a workflow orchestration tool**, and naming the equivalence is the most generative move in this entry. **Architectural recognition**: every primitive in the harness has a direct equivalent in the workflow-orchestration vocabulary, and the harness's approach (event log as durable state, replay-based resume, navigator-as-sensor) is the same pattern Temporal arrived at independently. **The architectural commitment**: future loop-runner enhancements borrow from the workflow-orchestration literature explicitly — partial retry granularity, dynamic fan-out, sub-loop composition, sensor primitives — rather than reinventing them. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: building a separate "task runner" or "job system" alongside the loop runner; the loop runner already IS that system, and any new orchestration need should extend it rather than parallel it.

### 7. Backfill, replay, and reprocessing

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Time-window backfill** | Re-run a workflow for a historical date range | Standard for fixing bugs |
| **Selective replay** | Re-run only specific tasks of a historical workflow | Required for partial fixes |
| **Idempotent reprocessing** | Re-running produces the same result | Required for safe backfill |
| **Snapshot-based reprocessing** | Replay against a frozen point-in-time | Required for reproducibility |
| **Side-by-side reprocessing** | Run new logic against historical data while old logic still runs | Risk-free validation |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Backfill is one of the operational features that distinguishes serious workflow tools from toy schedulers**, and the right backfill primitive lets you fix a bug retroactively by re-processing all the data the bug affected. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: any workflow tool must support backfill, and any task that's backfill-able must be idempotent. **The harness adjacency**: backfill is structurally event-log replay, and the harness gets it for free because every execution can be replayed from the event log at any offset. **The architectural recognition**: this is the same pattern as the WAL entry's commitment to replay-based reprocessing, the Stream processing entry's commitment to Kappa-style unification, and the data integration entry's commitment to projections-as-derived-state — all the same pattern, applied to different problems, all enabled by the event log.

### 8. Workflow observability

| Signal | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Workflow run history** | UI showing all past executions | Standard tool feature |
| **Task-level logs** | Per-task output stream | Required for debugging |
| **DAG visualization** | Graphical view of dependencies | Standard tool feature |
| **Run timing** | How long each task took | Performance signal |
| **Failure attribution** | Which task in which run failed why | Required for incident response |
| **Workflow SLOs** | Per-workflow latency and success-rate targets | Connects to SLI/SLO entry |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Workflow observability is the dimension where workflow tools have invested the most polish**, and the dashboards and run-history UIs of Dagster, Prefect, and Temporal are the gold standard. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: any workflow tool used must have first-class observability — run history, per-task logs, DAG visualization, failure attribution — and the operations team must actually use it. **The harness adjacency**: the orchestrator dashboard (deployed to Vercel) and the per-execution SSE stream are structurally a workflow-observability UI, and the architectural commitment is to keep evolving them toward the polish that production workflow tools have. **The connection to SLI/SLO §7**: the harness's navigator-experience SLOs include workflow-shaped concerns (loop completion rate, execution latency vs estimate), and the workflow-observability layer is the same data viewed differently. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: a workflow tool with no observability — operating one without seeing what it's doing is the source of every "the pipeline failed three days ago and nobody noticed" incident.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Eight things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **The lines between batch, streaming, and workflow have blurred; choose the tool by work shape; harness loop runner is closest to Temporal** (from §1).
2. **DAG as the core abstraction; harness loop's phase structure IS a DAG; loop.json IS structurally a DAG specification** (from §2). The most generative observation.
3. **Sensors and triggers as load-bearing primitive; gate IS structurally a workflow sensor with the navigator as external system; backfill is free via event-log replay** (from §3).
4. **Partial retry (only failed branches) as the load-bearing operational feature; harness already has phase-level resume; saga semantics from event sourcing** (from §4).
5. **Temporal is the most architecturally aligned external tool; one-to-one mapping with harness primitives; future loop-runner enhancements borrow from Temporal as prior art** (from §5).
6. **The harness loop runner IS a workflow orchestration tool; future orchestration needs extend the loop runner rather than parallel it** (from §6).
7. **Backfill is structurally event-log replay; same pattern as WAL replay, Kappa unification, and projections-as-derived-state** (from §7).
8. **Workflow observability as the polish dimension; harness dashboard + SSE stream IS the observability layer; navigator-experience SLOs include workflow-shaped concerns** (from §8).

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This is a resolution-confirmation entry that adds the most material yet to the "X IS structurally Y" pattern list** — the loop runner IS a workflow orchestration tool, the gate IS a workflow sensor, the loop's phase structure IS a DAG, loop.json IS a DAG specification, the dashboard IS a workflow observability UI, and Temporal's primitives map one-to-one onto harness primitives. The most generative move is **§5 + §6: the harness loop runner is structurally Temporal**, which is independent convergence on the same architectural pattern and validates the harness's approach.

**The most concrete near-term unlock at the harness layer** is **explicitly looking at Temporal's primitive set as prior art for future loop-runner enhancements** (workflow versioning, signal patterns, child workflows, continue-as-new for long-running loops), because Temporal has spent years resolving operational concerns the harness will eventually face. **The most architecturally interesting thread** is the recognition that **the harness has been building a Temporal-shaped system from the start without ever calling it that**, and the architectural value of naming the equivalence is that it makes years of workflow-orchestration literature legitimate prior art.

**Layer note**: applies to both layers. Harness loop runner IS a workflow orchestration system structurally equivalent to Temporal — loops as workflows, skills as activities, gates as signals, event log as durable state. App-layer workflow orchestration follows standard patterns: Dagster for data pipelines, Temporal for long-running workflows with human steps, Cron or its managed equivalent for simple time-triggered jobs, all with first-class observability.

---

## Geospatial systems

The discipline of storing, indexing, querying, and reasoning about data with locations on the Earth — spatial indexes, distance queries, polygon containment, routing, geocoding, mapping. Geospatial is interesting for the orchestrator because **servicegrid is the live case**: small contractors serving local customers means every job has a location, every contractor has a service area, and the operational core (matching contractors to jobs, routing jobs into a contractor's day, optimizing daily schedules) is fundamentally a geospatial problem.

The entry exists because geospatial is a category where the "just use a library" answer leads to subtle correctness bugs (coordinate systems, antimeridian crossing, polygon orientation) that only surface in production, and the right architecture commits early to a substrate that handles the math correctly.

This is a **choice-layer** entry **app-layer primary**.

### 1. Coordinate systems and the spherical-Earth question

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **WGS84 (lat/lon)** | The standard GPS coordinate system | The default; what every API expects |
| **Web Mercator (EPSG:3857)** | Projected for web maps | Map tiles, distance measurements distort with latitude |
| **UTM zones** | Projected per-zone for accurate distances | Surveying, local calculations |
| **Geographic vs projected** | Lat/lon on a sphere vs flat 2D coordinates | The fundamental distinction |
| **Antimeridian crossing** | The 180/-180 longitude line | The "polygon spans the dateline" bug source |
| **Pole handling** | Special cases at the poles | Rarely matters for consumer apps |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **WGS84 lat/lon is the right default for storage**, with projected coordinate systems used only at the moment of computation when distance accuracy matters. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: store all locations as WGS84 lat/lon, compute distances using a substrate that handles the spherical-Earth math correctly (PostGIS, S2, H3), and never roll your own great-circle distance calculation. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: storing coordinates as Web Mercator and then computing distances directly — this gives wrong answers everywhere except near the equator. **For servicegrid specifically**: contractors and jobs are stored as WGS84 lat/lon, and any distance/routing computation goes through a library that handles the math; the contractor's service area is a polygon in WGS84, and the "is this job in this contractor's area" check uses the library's polygon-containment primitive.

### 2. Spatial indexes

| Index | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **R-tree** | Hierarchical bounding boxes | The classic spatial index |
| **Geohash** | Encode lat/lon as a string with prefix-based spatial locality | Simple, language-agnostic |
| **S2 (Google)** | Hierarchical spherical cells | Production-ready, well-documented |
| **H3 (Uber)** | Hexagonal hierarchical cells | Used for delivery, ride-share, logistics |
| **Quadtree** | Recursive 4-way subdivision | Game maps, image tiling |
| **PostGIS GiST / SP-GiST** | Postgres-native R-tree variants | The default for SQL geospatial |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **PostGIS is the right default for SQL geospatial workloads** because it integrates with the rest of the SQL substrate, handles all the coordinate-system math correctly, and is battle-tested in every major mapping company. **Architectural commitment for servicegrid: PostGIS as the geospatial substrate** (extension on top of the existing Postgres), with WGS84 storage and PostGIS functions for distance, containment, and indexing. **H3 is the underrated alternative** for use cases where hexagonal cells are preferable (uniform neighbor distances, hierarchical coarse-to-fine queries, density binning) — relevant for any "show me jobs in this area" query. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: rolling a custom geohash-based index when PostGIS is already available; the operational simplicity of using one substrate (Postgres + PostGIS) outweighs the marginal performance differences for any workload short of Uber's scale.

### 3. Distance and proximity queries

| Query | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **K-nearest neighbors** | Find the N closest features to a point | "Closest contractors to this job" |
| **Within-radius** | Find all features within X meters of a point | "Jobs within 25km of contractor's home" |
| **Polygon containment** | Find features within a polygon | "Jobs in this contractor's service area" |
| **Polygon intersection** | Find polygons that overlap | "Service areas that overlap this neighborhood" |
| **Distance matrix** | All-pairs distances between two sets | Routing optimization input |
| **Geofencing** | Trigger when entity enters/leaves a region | Mobile app "you've arrived at the job" |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **K-nearest-neighbors is the load-bearing query for matching workloads**, and PostGIS supports it efficiently via the `<->` operator on a GiST index. **Architectural commitment for servicegrid**: the contractor-job matching is a KNN query bounded by service-area containment — find the top-K closest contractors to this job whose service area contains the job, ranked by distance. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: computing distances to every contractor in the database for every job; the index is what makes this scalable past the prototype stage. **Geofencing is the underrated query** for mobile workflows: "the contractor's phone has entered the customer's location, so the job auto-checks them in" is a structurally clean UX pattern that eliminates manual check-in steps.

### 4. Routing and optimization

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Shortest path (single OD pair)** | Find best route between two points | The basic routing primitive |
| **Distance / time matrix** | All-pairs travel times across N points | Required for vehicle routing |
| **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)** | Optimize multi-stop routes for one vehicle | Field service, delivery |
| **VRP with time windows (VRPTW)** | VRP plus per-stop arrival window constraints | The realistic case |
| **Multi-vehicle VRP** | Multiple drivers, shared pool of stops | Fleet routing |
| **Dynamic re-routing** | Re-optimize as new jobs arrive mid-day | Real-world necessity |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Vehicle Routing Problems are NP-hard in general**, but well-studied heuristics (OR-Tools, Vroom, OSRM) give near-optimal solutions in practice for the scale field service operates at. **Architectural commitment for servicegrid: use OR-Tools or Vroom for daily route optimization, with OSRM or a managed routing API (Mapbox Directions, HERE) for the underlying distance matrix.** **The connection to the geographic distribution entry §4**: the routing computation can run at the edge or at a regional origin; for servicegrid the right answer is regional origin because the computation is non-trivial and per-contractor-day. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: hand-rolling a TSP heuristic; the OR-Tools and Vroom solutions are tested against real-world constraints that hand-rolled code will get wrong (multiple time windows, vehicle capacity, driver breaks, return-to-depot semantics).

### 5. Map data and tile serving

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Raster tiles** | Pre-rendered PNG images per zoom level | Legacy, simple |
| **Vector tiles (MVT)** | Vector data per tile, styled client-side | The modern default |
| **OSM (OpenStreetMap)** | Open-source map data | The sovereign substrate |
| **Mapbox / MapTiler / Stadia** | Managed vector tile providers | Standard commercial choice |
| **Self-hosted tile server (TileServer GL, Tegola)** | Sovereign tile serving over OSM | When sovereignty matters |
| **Geocoding (address → coordinates)** | Convert text address to lat/lon | Required for any address-based UI |
| **Reverse geocoding (coordinates → address)** | Convert lat/lon to nearest address | Required for "you are here" |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **OSM is the load-bearing open substrate for sovereign map data**, and the architectural commitment for any app that wants to avoid Google/Mapbox lock-in is to base the map data on OSM and either self-host or use an OSM-based managed provider. **Architectural commitment for servicegrid: vector tiles from a managed OSM-based provider (MapTiler or Stadia Maps), with a self-hosted fallback (TileServer GL + OSM extracts) as the sovereign escape valve.** **Geocoding is the underrated dependency**: every address-based input requires geocoding to be useful, and the geocoding API is the dependency that's easiest to lock in to a single vendor — the architectural commitment is to keep geocoding pluggable so the vendor can be swapped (Nominatim for sovereign, Mapbox/HERE/Google for managed). **The forbidden anti-pattern**: using Google Maps as the default substrate without considering the lock-in cost — Google Maps pricing changes have repeatedly forced teams to migrate at large cost.

### 6. Geospatial at the harness layer (the dissolution case)

| Concern | App layer (servicegrid) | Harness (orchestrator) |
|---|---|---|
| **Coordinates** | Stored as WGS84 lat/lon | Not applicable |
| **Spatial indexes** | PostGIS GiST | Not applicable |
| **Distance queries** | PostGIS `<->` for KNN | Not applicable |
| **Routing** | OR-Tools / OSRM | Not applicable |
| **Map tiles** | Vector tiles from managed provider | Not applicable |
| **Geocoding** | Pluggable geocoding API | Not applicable |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Geospatial is one of the cleanest "doesn't apply at the harness layer" categories**, because the harness has no concept of physical location — the navigator's session, the agent invocations, the event log, all of it is location-free. **The architectural recognition**: this is yet another category where the local-first commitment dissolves a class of concerns, but in a less interesting way than consensus or geographic distribution because the dissolution is "the harness has no users with locations" rather than "the harness avoids a load-bearing complication". **The architectural commitment**: any future feature that wants to associate the navigator with a physical location should be challenged on whether it's actually a feature of the harness (probably not) or of an app the harness is running (probably).

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Six things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **WGS84 lat/lon as the storage default; PostGIS as the geospatial substrate; never roll custom great-circle math** (from §1).
2. **PostGIS GiST as the SQL geospatial index; H3 as the underrated alternative for hexagonal binning** (from §2).
3. **KNN bounded by polygon containment as the load-bearing query for contractor-job matching; geofencing for mobile auto-check-in** (from §3).
4. **OR-Tools or Vroom for daily VRP optimization; OSRM or managed routing API for distance matrix; never hand-roll TSP heuristics** (from §4).
5. **Vector tiles from managed OSM-based provider (MapTiler / Stadia); pluggable geocoding (Nominatim sovereign, Mapbox / HERE / Google managed); avoid Google Maps default lock-in** (from §5).
6. **Geospatial dissolves at the harness layer because the harness has no physical-location concept; one more dissolution but a less interesting one** (from §6).

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This is a choice-layer entry that resolves cleanly toward PostGIS + OSM + OR-Tools at the app layer and toward dissolution at the harness layer.** The most generative move is **§4: OR-Tools / Vroom for VRP optimization with OSRM for the distance matrix**, because it's the substrate that determines whether servicegrid's daily route optimization is a real feature or a half-broken toy — and rolling your own is the failure mode that catches most field-service startups.

**The most concrete near-term unlock for servicegrid** is **adopting PostGIS as the geospatial substrate from day one** (it's a Postgres extension, no separate process needed) so that the coordinate-system math, indexing, and KNN queries are correct from the start. **The most architecturally interesting thread** is the recognition that geospatial is one more category where the harness layer dissolves entirely — but in a less interesting way than consensus or geographic distribution, because it's an inherent dissolution rather than an architecturally-earned one.

**Layer note**: app-layer primary. servicegrid is the live geospatial case (contractor-job matching, daily route optimization, service-area containment, mobile geofencing). The substrate is PostGIS for storage and queries, OR-Tools or Vroom for routing, OSM-based managed providers for tiles and geocoding. Harness has no geospatial concerns because there's no concept of physical location.

---

## Service mesh

The discipline of pulling cross-cutting service-to-service concerns (mTLS, traffic management, retries, circuit breaking, observability, policy enforcement) out of application code and into a sidecar or kernel layer that runs alongside every service instance. Service mesh is interesting for the orchestrator because **it's the most extreme example of "infrastructure that becomes a load-bearing dependency" in modern distributed systems**, and the architectural question is honestly when (or whether) it's worth the operational cost.

The entry exists because service mesh is a real architectural choice for any team running many services in production, and getting the answer right (most teams don't need it; some teams desperately do) is the difference between a productive infrastructure investment and a years-long operational drag. It also exists because the harness's dissolution of every cross-service concern (because there are no services) is one more case study in what local-first eliminates.

This is a **doesn't-apply-but-transfers** entry **for both layers, almost entirely app-layer relevant**.

### 1. The cross-cutting service-to-service concerns

| Concern | What it is | Why it's hard |
|---|---|---|
| **mTLS** | Encrypted, mutually-authenticated traffic between services | Requires cert provisioning per service |
| **Traffic management** | Routing, weighted traffic shifting, retries, timeouts | Each service has to implement consistently |
| **Circuit breaking** | Fail fast when downstream is down | Requires per-call instrumentation |
| **Observability** | Per-call metrics, traces, logs | Each language has its own libraries |
| **Policy enforcement** | "Service A may call service B" | Requires per-service identity and policy engine |
| **Rate limiting** | Per-caller per-callee throttling | Coordination between services |
| **Failure injection / chaos** | Inject failures to test resilience | Standard SRE practice |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The argument for service mesh is that every service needs these capabilities and implementing them per-language per-service produces bugs, drift, and maintenance burden.** The argument against is that the operational cost (sidecar per pod, control-plane to operate, debugging complexity, latency overhead) often exceeds the benefit until the team has many services. **Architectural commitment**: service mesh is a tool with a real break-even point, and the right answer for any team smaller than ~20 services or smaller than ~50 engineers is "no service mesh, handle these concerns at the application layer or in a thin shared library" — and the right answer for teams above that threshold is "yes, the consistency and operational uniformity is worth the cost". **The orchestrator-built apps will not approach the threshold for the foreseeable future**, so the architectural commitment for any orchestrator-built app is "do not use a service mesh unless explicitly forced".

### 2. The sidecar pattern

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Sidecar proxy** | Per-pod L7 proxy that handles cross-cutting concerns | Envoy, Linkerd2-proxy |
| **Application-transparent** | App makes plain HTTP calls; sidecar adds mTLS, retries, observability | The whole point |
| **Out-of-process upgrades** | Sidecar can be updated independently of the app | Operational decoupling |
| **Per-pod overhead** | Sidecar consumes CPU and memory per pod | The cost dimension |
| **Sidecar startup ordering** | Sidecar must be ready before app starts making calls | A common bug source |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The sidecar pattern is the architectural insight that makes service mesh viable** — by running the proxy as a separate process colocated with the application, the application doesn't need to be modified, the proxy can be language-agnostic, and the proxy can be operated independently. **The cost**: every pod gets a second process (the sidecar), and at scale this is a meaningful resource overhead (typically 50–100 MB RAM per pod and a fraction of a CPU). **At the harness layer**: the sidecar pattern maps onto nothing because the harness is a single process; the architectural recognition is that the harness's "everything in one process" model is the opposite of the sidecar pattern's "concerns split across processes" model, and both are valid for different scales.

### 3. Control plane vs data plane

| Plane | Responsibility | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Control plane** | Configuration, policy, certificates, service discovery | Istio's istiod, Linkerd's control plane |
| **Data plane** | Actual traffic flowing through the proxies | Envoy sidecars, Linkerd2-proxy |
| **Push model** | Control plane pushes config to data plane | Standard |
| **Pull model** | Data plane pulls config from control plane | Standard |
| **Identity issuance** | Control plane issues per-service certificates | The mTLS automation primitive |
