| **Configuration drift** | Data plane config diverges from control plane intent | The bug source |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The control-plane / data-plane split is the load-bearing architectural pattern of service meshes**, and it's the same pattern as the IaC entry's separation of "what we want" from "what's running". **Architectural commitment**: any service mesh must keep the control plane and data plane as cleanly separated systems, and the configuration push/pull mechanism must be observable so drift is detectable. **The harness equivalent**: the loop runner is structurally the control plane (the loop definition is the desired state, the execution is the data plane), and the same separation discipline applies — never mix the two. **The connection to the configuration entry**: service mesh control-plane configuration is one more category of configuration that needs the seven-category taxonomy from Configuration §1, with explicit storage and audit discipline.

### 4. Service mesh implementations

| Mesh | Approach | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Istio** | Envoy-based, full-featured, complex to operate | Enterprise k8s shops |
| **Linkerd** | Rust-based, simpler, lighter | Mid-scale k8s, simpler operations |
| **Consul Connect** | HashiCorp-aligned, multi-platform | When already on Consul |
| **Cilium Service Mesh** | eBPF-based, sidecar-less | Modern, performance-focused |
| **AWS App Mesh** | AWS-managed, Envoy-based | AWS-aligned |
| **GCP Anthos Service Mesh** | GCP-managed Istio | GCP-aligned |
| **OSM (Open Service Mesh)** | CNCF, Microsoft-originated | Less actively developed |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Linkerd is the right service mesh for any team that has decided they need a mesh but doesn't want to take on Istio's operational complexity** — it's lighter, simpler, and covers 80% of Istio's features at 20% of the operational cost. **Cilium Service Mesh is the architecturally interesting modern alternative** because it eliminates the sidecar entirely by handling cross-cutting concerns in the kernel via eBPF, removing the per-pod resource overhead and the sidecar-startup-ordering bug source. **Architectural recognition: the sidecar-less mesh model (Cilium) is the direction the field is moving** because it addresses the dominant complaint about service meshes (resource overhead and operational complexity from the sidecar). **Architectural commitment**: if the orchestrator ever ships an app that genuinely needs a service mesh (which is unlikely at the foreseeable scale), the right answer is Linkerd today and Cilium when it matures.

### 5. The "do you need a service mesh" decision tree

| Question | If yes | If no |
|---|---|---|
| **Do you have more than ~20 production services?** | Maybe | No mesh |
| **Are the services in many different languages?** | Maybe | No mesh (use a shared library) |
| **Do you need mTLS between services?** | Maybe (mesh is one option) | No mesh |
| **Do you need fine-grained traffic shifting (canary, percentage rollouts)?** | Maybe | No mesh (use simpler tools) |
| **Do you need per-service identity for policy?** | Maybe (mesh is one option) | No mesh |
| **Do you have a dedicated platform team to operate the mesh?** | Maybe | No mesh — operating one without dedicated capacity is the failure mode |
| **Are most "yes" answers true simultaneously?** | Yes, adopt | No, defer |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Most teams that adopt a service mesh do so because "service meshes are best practice"**, not because they have a real architectural problem the mesh solves — and the operational cost of a poorly-justified mesh adoption typically exceeds the value by an order of magnitude. **Architectural commitment**: the decision to adopt a service mesh requires a pre-mortem documenting which specific problems the mesh will solve, what the operational cost will be, who will operate it, and what the rollback plan is if the cost exceeds the value. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: adopting Istio because the cloud-native blog posts say to, then spending six months debugging Envoy configuration without ever solving a real customer problem. **For any orchestrator-built app**: the answer is "no service mesh" until at least the third or fourth production service exists with cross-cutting concerns the application code can't reasonably handle.

### 6. The harness as the dissolution case (one more time)

| Service mesh concern | Standard answer | Harness equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **mTLS between services** | Sidecar-managed | Not applicable (no services) |
| **Traffic management** | Mesh routing rules | Not applicable (no traffic) |
| **Circuit breaking** | Sidecar-handled | In-process resilience patterns |
| **Cross-service observability** | Distributed tracing through sidecars | Single-process tracing (much simpler) |
| **Per-service identity** | SPIFFE/SPIRE | Not applicable (one identity) |
| **Policy enforcement** | OPA / Kyverno via mesh | Application-level (gates, auth) |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The harness layer dissolves service mesh entirely because there are no services to mesh**, and this is one more reinforcement of the convergent pattern from entry 18 (local-first eliminates a class of distributed-systems concerns). **Architectural recognition**: the entire service-mesh apparatus — sidecars, control plane, data plane, identity issuance, traffic management, mTLS automation, fine-grained policy enforcement — exists to solve problems the harness avoids by being a single process. **The architectural commitment**: any future feature proposal at the harness layer that would require service-mesh-shaped infrastructure should be challenged with "why isn't this a single process?" because the dissolution is one of the harness's load-bearing simplifications.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Six things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **Service mesh has a real break-even point at ~20 services or ~50 engineers; below that, "no service mesh, handle in app code or shared library" is the right answer** (from §1).
2. **The sidecar pattern is the load-bearing architectural insight; the cost is meaningful resource overhead per pod** (from §2).
3. **Control plane vs data plane separation is load-bearing; same pattern as IaC's "what we want vs what's running"; loop runner IS structurally the control plane for the data plane of execution** (from §3).
4. **Linkerd over Istio for any team that doesn't have dedicated platform capacity; Cilium Service Mesh is the architectural future (sidecar-less, eBPF-based)** (from §4).
5. **The decision to adopt a service mesh requires a pre-mortem; "best practice" is not a justification; orchestrator-built apps default to "no service mesh"** (from §5).
6. **The harness layer dissolves service mesh entirely; entire apparatus exists to solve problems the harness avoids by being a single process** (from §6).

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This is a doesn't-apply-but-transfers entry where the most generative move is the recognition that service mesh is over-adopted in the industry**, and the orchestrator-built apps' deliberate avoidance of service mesh is a positive architectural commitment, not a sign of immaturity. The second most generative is **§3: the loop runner IS structurally the control plane for the data plane of execution** — adding one more row to the "X IS Y" pattern list, even though service mesh is otherwise a non-applicable category at the harness layer.

**The most concrete near-term unlock at the app layer** is **none** — the architectural commitment is to avoid service mesh until forced. **The most architecturally interesting thread** is the recognition that **the harness's dissolution of service mesh, alongside the dissolutions of consensus, geographic distribution, distributed coordination, geospatial, and load balancing, forms a coherent architectural identity**: the harness avoids the entire distributed-systems apparatus by being a single local process, and every dissolution reinforces the others.

**Layer note**: applies almost entirely to the app layer. The orchestrator-built apps default to "no service mesh" and only adopt one if specific conditions are met (many services, many languages, dedicated platform team, real cross-cutting concerns the app layer can't handle). If a mesh is ever needed, Linkerd is the right starting point with Cilium as the future direction. Harness dissolves service mesh entirely because there are no services.

---

## Time and clocks

The discipline of dealing with time correctly in distributed systems — wall clocks vs monotonic clocks, clock skew, NTP, logical clocks, hybrid logical clocks, time zones, leap seconds, ISO 8601. Time is interesting for the orchestrator because **the standard distributed-systems horror stories about time (clock skew causing wrong ordering, time going backwards, NTP jumps, leap second crashes) almost entirely dissolve at the harness layer** because there's only one machine and only one clock — and the residual concerns (time zone display, ISO 8601 storage, monotonic clocks for measurement) apply uniformly.

The entry exists because time is the dimension where "obvious" choices most frequently produce subtle correctness bugs, and naming the disciplines explicitly prevents the most common ones (storing local time in the database, using wall clock for measurements, assuming clocks are monotonic, mishandling DST transitions). It also exists because the harness's monotonic event-log sequence numbers serve a role that distributed systems use logical clocks for, and naming the equivalence is generative.

This is a **doesn't-apply-but-transfers** entry **for both layers**.

### 1. Wall clocks vs monotonic clocks

| Clock | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Wall clock** | "What time is it" — synchronized to an external time source | Display, scheduling, timestamps |
| **Monotonic clock** | "How much time has passed" — guaranteed to never go backwards | Measuring durations, timeouts, intervals |
| **CPU cycle counter (TSC)** | Hardware counter, very low latency | Microbenchmarks |
| **NTP-disciplined clock** | Wall clock corrected by NTP, may jump | Standard server time |
| **Slewed clock** | NTP corrections applied gradually | Avoids time jumps |
| **Hardware clock** | Real-time clock chip, persists across reboots | Boot time, BIOS clock |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The single most common time-related bug is using a wall clock to measure a duration**, because the wall clock can jump (NTP correction, manual adjustment, DST in some libraries) and the resulting "negative duration" or "duration of 1 hour" crashes the timeout logic. **Architectural commitment**: every duration measurement uses a monotonic clock, every timestamp for display or storage uses a wall clock, and the two are never confused. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: `start = time.time(); ... ; elapsed = time.time() - start` — this is using wall clock for a duration and is wrong; the right pattern uses `time.monotonic()` (Python) or `process.hrtime()` (Node) or `Instant.now()` (Rust). **At the harness layer**: the architectural commitment applies the same way as everywhere else, and any retry/timeout/backoff logic must use monotonic time.

### 2. NTP and clock synchronization

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **NTP (Network Time Protocol)** | Periodic correction of wall clock from time servers | Standard for most servers |
| **Stratum levels** | Hierarchy of time servers (1 = atomic clock) | Standard NTP architecture |
| **Slewing vs stepping** | Gradual correction vs sudden jump | Slew is safer; step is faster to converge |
| **PTP (Precision Time Protocol)** | Sub-microsecond hardware-assisted sync | Trading floors, high-precision needs |
| **chrony / ntpd** | Standard NTP client implementations | Linux defaults |
| **NTP attack surface** | Man-in-the-middle can manipulate time | Real concern for high-stakes systems |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **NTP is the load-bearing infrastructure that everyone depends on and almost no one operates correctly** — the failure mode is "clocks drifted by minutes and we didn't notice until log timestamps stopped lining up across servers". **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: every server runs `chrony` (or the modern equivalent) with multiple time sources for redundancy, and clock skew across servers is alerted on. **At the harness layer**: NTP runs at the OS level on the navigator's machine and the harness doesn't operate it directly; the architectural recognition is that the harness inherits whatever clock discipline the navigator's machine has, and the architectural risk is small because there's only one clock.

### 3. Logical clocks and the "happens-before" relation

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Lamport timestamps** | Monotonic counter incremented on every event | Total order across distributed processes |
| **Vector clocks** | Per-process counter; vector merged on communication | Capture causal "happens-before" |
| **Hybrid Logical Clocks (HLC)** | Wall clock + logical counter | Combines best of both |
| **TrueTime (Spanner)** | Wall clock with bounded uncertainty | Strong consistency at global scale |
| **Causality tracking** | Events tagged with their causal predecessors | Required for true distributed correctness |
| **Total order broadcast** | All processes see events in the same order | Required for state machine replication |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Logical clocks exist because wall clocks are insufficient for ordering events in a distributed system** — the same wall-clock timestamp on two different machines doesn't mean the events happened simultaneously, and causally-related events can have wall timestamps in the wrong order. **Architectural recognition: the harness's monotonic event-log sequence numbers ARE Lamport timestamps for a single-process system**, and the single-process simplification eliminates the entire vector-clock and HLC apparatus because there's no possibility of concurrent events from different processes. **The architectural commitment**: the harness's sequence numbers are the canonical ordering primitive, and any distributed-systems concept that requires logical clocks (vector clocks, causality tracking, total order broadcast) is structurally not needed because total order is trivially provided by the single-writer event log. **At the app layer**: HLC is the right modern answer for any distributed system that needs both wall-clock-readable timestamps and correct causal ordering — used by CockroachDB, FoundationDB, and most modern distributed databases.

### 4. Time zones and the storage discipline

| Rule | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Store in UTC, display in local** | Database always UTC, conversion at the UI layer | The standard discipline |
| **ISO 8601 / RFC 3339** | Canonical text format with explicit offset | The standard storage format |
| **IANA time zone database** | Named zones (`America/Los_Angeles`) | The right time zone identifier |
| **Never store offset alone** | "+05:00" is ambiguous because of DST changes | Store the named zone instead |
| **Store user's preferred zone separately** | Lets users see times in their own context | Required for global users |
| **Future events need the named zone** | "8am next Tuesday in LA" can't be stored as UTC because of potential DST changes | Often missed |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The "store in UTC, display in local" rule is right for past timestamps and wrong for future scheduled events** — a future event scheduled for "8am Tuesday in Los Angeles" can't be safely stored as UTC because the UTC offset of LA might change before Tuesday (DST transitions, rare political changes), and storing the UTC value would silently shift the event by an hour. **Architectural commitment**: past timestamps are stored as UTC ISO 8601 strings, future scheduled events are stored as `(local_time, named_zone)` pairs and converted to UTC at the moment of execution, never earlier. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: storing local times with offset only — "8am +08:00" is ambiguous because it doesn't say which zone, and the offset can change. **At the harness layer**: every event in the event log carries a UTC timestamp (because the navigator's machine has a single timezone), and any future-scheduled work (notifications during waking hours, scheduled-with-cancel from notification systems §8) is stored as `(local_time, navigator's zone)` pairs.

### 5. The leap second and other calendar weirdness

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Leap second** | Occasional 1-second adjustment to UTC | The "23:59:60" timestamp question |
| **DST transitions** | Spring forward (lose an hour), fall back (repeat an hour) | Source of "the same clock time twice" bugs |
| **Year 2038 problem** | 32-bit Unix timestamps overflow | Real for legacy systems |
| **Calendar reform variations** | Julian vs Gregorian, regional differences | Historical-data complications |
| **Smearing leap seconds** | Spread the second across 24 hours | Google/AWS approach to avoid the discontinuity |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Leap seconds are the single most embarrassing operational time bug in distributed systems**, and the architectural answer in 2026 is "use a leap-second-smeared clock" (which is what Google, AWS, and Cloudflare provide via their internal NTP services) rather than handling the discontinuity at the application layer. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: any production server uses leap-second-smeared NTP. **DST transitions are the more common operational bug** because they happen twice a year and produce "the same wall-clock time twice in one day" or "this wall-clock time doesn't exist", and any code that assumes wall clock is monotonic crashes during the fall-back transition. **The architectural defense**: never use wall clock for measurement or ordering (§1), and never store future events as UTC (§4) because both shift across DST.

### 6. The harness as the simplification case

| Time concern | Standard distributed-systems answer | Harness equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Cross-machine clock skew** | NTP, HLC, TrueTime | Not applicable (one machine) |
| **Causal ordering of events** | Vector clocks, HLC | Sequence number on single event log |
| **Total order broadcast** | Paxos, Raft consensus | Free (single writer) |
| **Leap seconds** | Smeared NTP | Inherited from OS |
| **Time zone math for users** | Per-user zone storage, UI conversion | Per-navigator zone (single user) |
| **Future scheduled events** | `(local_time, named_zone)` storage | Same discipline applies |
| **Duration measurement** | Monotonic clock | Same discipline applies |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Time at the harness layer is one more category where the single-process model dissolves the distributed concerns** — no clock skew, no logical clock apparatus, no consensus needed for ordering, no cross-machine HLC bookkeeping. **Architectural recognition**: the harness's monotonic sequence numbers serve the role distributed systems need logical clocks for, and the dissolution is one more reinforcement of the convergent pattern from entry 18. **The residual disciplines that DO apply**: monotonic clock for duration measurement, UTC for past timestamps, named-zone storage for future scheduled events. These are the same disciplines that apply to non-distributed systems generally, and the harness adopts them as a matter of correctness rather than as a distributed-systems concern.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Six things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **Every duration measurement uses a monotonic clock; every timestamp for display/storage uses a wall clock; never confuse them** (from §1).
2. **NTP via chrony with redundant time sources at the app layer; harness inherits OS clock discipline** (from §2).
3. **The harness's monotonic event-log sequence numbers ARE Lamport timestamps for a single-process system; the single-process simplification eliminates vector clocks and HLC** (from §3).
4. **Past timestamps stored as UTC ISO 8601; future scheduled events stored as `(local_time, named_zone)` pairs; named zones, not offsets** (from §4).
5. **Leap-second-smeared NTP at the app layer; never assume wall clock is monotonic; DST transitions break code that does** (from §5).
6. **Time at the harness layer is one more dissolution; residual disciplines (monotonic for duration, UTC for past, named zone for future) apply uniformly** (from §6).

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This is a doesn't-apply-but-transfers entry where the dissolution recognition is the most generative observation**: §3 names that the harness's sequence numbers ARE Lamport timestamps adapted to single-process, and §6 enumerates the dissolved concerns. The pattern is consistent with consensus, geographic distribution, distributed coordination, geospatial, and service mesh — all categories where the harness's single-process model eliminates the apparatus other systems spend significant complexity on.

**The most concrete near-term unlock at the harness layer** is **none** — the architectural value is recognition. **The most architecturally interesting thread** is the recognition that the orchestrator's sequence-number primitive serves five roles simultaneously: WAL primitive, idempotency mechanism, ABA-defense, fencing token, and Lamport timestamp. One primitive, five roles, all derived from the single-writer event log — which is the kind of convergence the doc should call out explicitly in the meta-section.

**Layer note**: applies to both layers. Harness dissolves distributed-clock concerns entirely (no skew, no logical clocks needed, no cross-machine HLC); the residual disciplines (monotonic for duration, UTC for past timestamps, named zone for future events) apply as standard correctness practices. App-layer time follows standard distributed-systems patterns: NTP via chrony with redundancy, HLC for distributed causal ordering, leap-second-smeared NTP, UTC storage with explicit time zone display, named zones for future events.

---

## Recommender systems

The discipline of suggesting items (products, content, friends, jobs, songs) to users based on what they're likely to want — collaborative filtering, content-based filtering, matrix factorization, two-tower models, candidate generation + ranking, contextual bandits, exploration vs exploitation. Recommender systems are interesting for the orchestrator because **the harness has its own structurally analogous problem** — recommending which skill, which loop, which next move to the navigator at any moment — even though the population (N=1) is unusual and the domain is operational rather than consumer-content.

The entry exists because recsys is a category with deep theory and well-resolved patterns, and the harness has been answering an N-of-1 version of the recommendation problem ad-hoc through the leverage protocol (named in user-level CLAUDE.md) without ever calling it recommendation. Naming the equivalence makes the borrowing of patterns from recsys legitimate and gives the leverage protocol a richer theoretical grounding.

This is a **convergent-pattern resolution** entry **app-layer primary, with the harness as the N-of-1 case**.

### 1. The recommendation problem framing

| Variant | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Top-N recommendation** | Return the N best items for a user | Most recommender UIs |
| **Rating prediction** | Predict the rating a user would give an item | Movie ratings, classical Netflix Prize |
| **Next-item prediction** | What's the user likely to do next | Sequential recommender |
| **Slate recommendation** | Pick a coherent set of N items together | News feeds, ad slates |
| **Contextual recommendation** | Personalize based on time, location, device | Location-aware suggestions |
| **Conversational recommendation** | Iterative refinement through dialogue | Modern LLM-driven recsys |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Top-N is the dominant problem framing** because most consumer interfaces are list-shaped, but the framing matters because the right substrate depends on it. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: pick the framing first based on the UI surface (a single recommendation, a list, a feed, a slate), then pick the algorithm. **The harness adjacency**: the leverage protocol is a "next-move recommendation" — the harness suggests the next highest-leverage action to the navigator at the end of each loop, and the framing is structurally next-item prediction with the recommendation space being "loops the orchestrator can run" rather than "products to buy". **Architectural recognition**: the harness's leverage protocol IS a recommender system applied to operational decisions, with the navigator as the single user and the loop catalog as the item catalog.

### 2. Collaborative filtering vs content-based vs hybrid

| Approach | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **User-user collaborative filtering** | Find similar users, recommend what they liked | Classical, simple |
| **Item-item collaborative filtering** | Find items similar to ones the user liked | Amazon's classic approach |
| **Matrix factorization** | Decompose user-item matrix into latent factors | The Netflix Prize era |
| **Content-based** | Recommend items similar in content features | Cold-start friendly |
| **Hybrid** | Combine collaborative + content signals | The standard production answer |
| **Two-tower neural network** | Learn separate embeddings for users and items | Modern deep recsys |
| **Sequence models (Transformer-based)** | Capture temporal patterns in user behavior | Modern session-aware recsys |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Hybrid recommenders combining collaborative and content-based signals are the production standard** because pure collaborative filtering fails on cold-start (new users, new items) and pure content-based misses the social signal that "people like you also liked this". **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: hybrid recommenders by default, with the candidate generation + ranking architecture (§3) as the substrate. **The harness adjacency**: the leverage protocol is **content-based** (each loop and each move is judged on its own merits via the value equation from CLAUDE.md), not collaborative (there's only one navigator, no "people like you" signal possible). **The architectural recognition**: with N=1, collaborative filtering dissolves entirely, and the recommendation problem reduces to pure content-based + contextual + exploration-vs-exploitation — which is a much simpler and more tractable subset.

### 3. Candidate generation + ranking

| Stage | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Candidate generation** | Produce a small set (hundreds) from a huge catalog (millions) | Recall stage |
| **Ranking** | Order the candidates by predicted relevance | Precision stage |
| **Re-ranking** | Apply business rules, diversity, freshness | Final shaping |
| **ANN-based candidate generation** | Approximate nearest neighbor over embeddings | The modern default |
| **Multi-stage ranking** | Cheap model → expensive model → very expensive model | Cost optimization |
| **Item retrieval as search** | Treat candidate generation as a search problem | Connects to Search §6 |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The candidate-generation + ranking pattern is the load-bearing architecture for any recsys that operates over large catalogs**, and it's the same two-stage pattern as the search entry §6 (cheap recall + expensive rerank). **Architectural recognition**: candidate generation + ranking IS the same pattern as Search §6's two-stage retrieval, applied to a different problem — and the orchestrator's commitment to two-stage retrieval (cheap first stage + LLM rerank) transfers directly. **The harness adjacency**: the leverage protocol's candidate generation is "all available loops and all viable next moves", which is small enough that no real candidate-generation step is needed; the ranking stage is the value-equation calculation, and the rerank stage is the navigator's judgment after seeing the recommendation. **At the app layer**: dreamtime or any future content-recommendation feature uses two-stage retrieval with ANN-based candidate generation and an LLM-based reranker.

### 4. Cold start and exploration

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Cold-start user** | New user with no interaction history | Onboarding flows, content-based fallback |
| **Cold-start item** | New item with no users yet | Content-based + boosted exposure |
| **Exploration vs exploitation** | Show known-good vs try unknowns | The fundamental tradeoff |
| **Epsilon-greedy** | Show the best with probability 1-ε, random with ε | Simple baseline |
| **Thompson sampling** | Sample from posterior over item quality | Bayesian, principled |
| **Contextual bandits** | Bandits with per-user features | Modern production answer |
| **Cold-start as content-only** | Skip collaborative until enough data | Standard fallback |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Exploration vs exploitation is the load-bearing tradeoff for any system that learns from feedback**, and the architectural commitment is to allocate a fraction of recommendations to exploration so the system keeps learning rather than getting stuck on the first locally-good answer. **Contextual bandits are the modern production answer** because they're exploration-aware (Thompson sampling, UCB), feature-aware (contextual), and have well-established convergence guarantees. **The harness adjacency**: the leverage protocol's exploration question is "should the harness suggest the highest-value loop or sometimes suggest a different one to learn whether the value model is right?", and the answer should be Thompson sampling or epsilon-greedy applied at low rates — the harness should occasionally recommend a non-top loop to gather signal that calibrates the value equation. **Architectural recognition**: this is structurally the same as the experimentation entry's commitment to N-of-1 sequential calibration, applied to recommendation rather than skill version selection. **The connection to A/B testing §8**: the harness's calibration system should track per-loop value vs realized leverage across many runs, and the upgrade-proposal pipeline should treat the value equation as a hypothesis to be calibrated rather than a fixed truth.

### 5. Feedback loops and the unintended-consequences problem

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Filter bubble** | Users only see what they already agree with | News, social media |
| **Engagement maximization trap** | Optimizing for engagement amplifies outrage | The 2010s social media failure |
| **Position bias** | Items at the top get clicked regardless of quality | Required to correct in training |
| **Selection bias** | Training data is what was shown, not what would have been clicked | Off-policy correction |
| **Concept drift** | User preferences change over time | Decay-aware models |
| **Counterfactual evaluation** | "What would have happened if we'd shown X instead?" | Off-policy estimation |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Recommender feedback loops are the most thoroughly-studied failure mode in production ML systems**, and the lessons (don't optimize naively for engagement, correct for position bias, account for selection bias, watch for filter bubbles) apply uniformly across domains. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: any recommender that learns from user feedback explicitly accounts for position bias, selection bias, and feedback-loop amplification, and tracks diversity / fairness / coverage as guardrail metrics alongside the primary engagement metric. **The harness adjacency**: the harness's calibration loop has the same risk — if it always recommends the highest-calibrated-quality skill version, it will never explore lower-calibrated versions that might have improved with feedback, and the calibration data will become biased toward the version it kept recommending. **Architectural commitment for the harness**: the upgrade-proposal pipeline allocates a fraction of executions to non-top variants for exploration, the same way contextual bandits do at the app layer. This is the same insight from the experimentation entry's §4 (sequential testing and exploration), applied to the recommendation problem.

### 6. The harness as N-of-1 recommender

| Recsys concept | Standard answer | Harness equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **User base** | Many users | One navigator |
| **Item catalog** | Many products / videos / songs | Loop catalog + skill catalog + next moves |
| **Collaborative filtering** | "People like you" | Dissolves (N=1) |
| **Content-based features** | Item attributes | Loop properties (downstream unlock, dream-state alignment) |
| **Cold start** | New user / new item | Continuous (no warm catalog of recommendations) |
| **Exploration vs exploitation** | Contextual bandits | Same — apply at low rates to leverage protocol |
| **Feedback signal** | Clicks, purchases, ratings | Calibration scores, loop completion, navigator approval |
| **Re-ranking by business rules** | Diversity, freshness, fairness | Diversity (don't recommend the same loop twice), freshness (recent skill changes) |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The harness's leverage protocol IS structurally a content-based contextual recommender for the navigator**, and naming the equivalence makes the borrowing of patterns from recsys legitimate. **Architectural recognition**: the value equation in the leverage protocol is the ranking function, the loop catalog is the item set, the navigator's response (accepting, rejecting, modifying) is the feedback signal, and the calibration system is the off-policy correction layer. **The architectural commitment**: the leverage protocol should adopt the recsys discipline of (1) explicit guardrail metrics (don't always recommend the same loop type — diversity matters), (2) exploration via Thompson sampling at low rates, (3) feedback-loop correction so the value equation doesn't get stuck on a local optimum, (4) cold-start handling for newly-created loops via content-based estimation rather than waiting for feedback. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: treating the value equation as ground truth and never updating it from realized outcomes — this is the harness equivalent of "shipping a recsys with no online learning" and produces the same staleness failure mode.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Six things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **The harness's leverage protocol IS a recommender system applied to operational decisions, with the navigator as the single user and the loop catalog as the item catalog** (from §1).
2. **With N=1, collaborative filtering dissolves entirely; recommendation reduces to content-based + contextual + exploration-vs-exploitation, a tractable subset** (from §2).
3. **Candidate generation + ranking IS the same two-stage pattern as Search §6's cheap recall + LLM rerank; the harness's small loop catalog skips candidate generation** (from §3).
4. **Exploration via Thompson sampling or epsilon-greedy at low rates; the harness should occasionally recommend non-top loops to gather calibration signal** (from §4). Same as A/B testing's exploration discipline.
5. **Feedback loops are the most thoroughly-studied failure mode in production recsys; the harness's calibration loop has the same risk and needs the same defenses** (from §5).
6. **The harness's leverage protocol should adopt recsys discipline: guardrail metrics, exploration sampling, feedback-loop correction, cold-start handling** (from §6).

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This is a convergent-pattern resolution entry that recognizes the leverage protocol as a recommender system** — adding one more "X IS Y" recognition to the pattern list (the leverage protocol IS a content-based contextual recommender for the navigator). The most generative move is **§4 + §5 + §6: recognizing that the harness's recommendation system needs the same exploration and feedback-loop-correction disciplines as production recsys**, which gives the leverage protocol a path to becoming a real adaptive system rather than a fixed value-equation calculator.

**The most concrete near-term unlock at the harness layer** is **adding low-rate exploration to the leverage protocol** — instead of always recommending the top-value loop, occasionally recommend a non-top loop to gather signal about whether the value model is right. **The most architecturally interesting thread** is the recognition that the harness has been doing N-of-1 recommender systems all along under the name "leverage protocol", and that the recsys literature provides well-resolved patterns for the failure modes the leverage protocol will face as it accumulates more loops and more signal.

**Layer note**: applies primarily to the app layer for traditional content/product recommendation, with the harness as the N-of-1 case. App-layer recsys follows standard patterns: hybrid candidate generation + ranking, ANN-based retrieval, contextual bandits for exploration, off-policy correction for feedback-loop bias, diversity/freshness/fairness as guardrails. Harness leverage protocol IS a content-based contextual recommender for the navigator, with the calibration loop as the feedback layer and exploration discipline borrowed from recsys.

---

## Webhooks

The discipline of one system pushing events to another over HTTP — webhook delivery, signing, retries, idempotency, replay defense, subscription management, dead-letter handling, local-development tunneling. Webhooks are interesting for the orchestrator because **they're the standard 2026 mechanism for integrating with third-party services** (Stripe payment events, Slack interactive messages, GitHub PR events, calendar updates), and the harness is already on the receiving end of multiple webhook flows.

The entry exists because webhooks are the dimension where "we'll just receive HTTP POSTs" goes wrong most reliably — without signing verification, retries, idempotency, and dead-letter handling, every webhook integration is a security and reliability hole. The right architecture treats webhooks as an integration substrate with the same rigor as message queues, even though they look superficially simpler.

This is a **choice-layer** entry **for both layers**.

### 1. Webhook delivery guarantees

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **At-most-once** | Try once, drop on failure | Low-stakes events only |
| **At-least-once with retries** | Retry until 2xx response | The standard guarantee |
| **Exponential backoff schedule** | 1s → 5s → 30s → 5m → 1h → 6h → 24h | Standard schedule |
| **Maximum retry duration** | "We'll keep trying for 24 hours" | Standard ceiling |
| **Dead-letter for permanent failures** | Stop retrying, move to manual queue | Required hygiene |
| **Replay protocol** | Receiver can request redelivery of past events | Standard operational tool |
| **Receiver-side acknowledgment** | 2xx = received, 4xx = bad request, 5xx = retry | The standard contract |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **At-least-once delivery with exponential backoff is the universal standard for webhook delivery**, and the architectural commitment for any webhook receiver is to **be idempotent**, because retries will produce duplicates and the receiver must handle them safely. **Architectural commitment**: every webhook receiver tracks the highest-processed event ID per-source and ignores duplicates, the same idempotency-by-sequence-number pattern named throughout the doc. **For the harness specifically**: the harness's event log makes idempotency structurally easy — every webhook delivery can be persisted to the event log with the source's event ID as the dedup key, and the consumer reads from the log without worrying about duplicates. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: a webhook receiver that doesn't handle duplicates and processes the same event twice — Stripe will retry payment events on transient failures, and double-processing means double-charging the customer.

### 2. Signing and verification

| Mechanism | How it works | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **HMAC signature in header** | Sender signs payload with shared secret, receiver verifies | Stripe, GitHub, Slack — the standard |
| **Timestamp in signature** | Replay defense via signed timestamp | Required to prevent replay attacks |
| **Per-endpoint secret** | Different secret per webhook subscription | Standard isolation |
| **Public key signatures** | Sender signs with private key, receiver verifies with public | Used by some auth providers |
| **mTLS as authentication** | Mutual TLS between sender and receiver | Enterprise integration pattern |
| **IP allowlisting** | Only accept from known sender IPs | Weaker than signing; still used as defense in depth |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **HMAC signature verification is non-negotiable for any webhook receiver** — without it, anyone who knows the webhook URL can send fake events, and the receiver has no way to distinguish legitimate from forged. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: every webhook receiver verifies HMAC signatures before processing the payload, rejects requests with invalid signatures with a 401, and treats signature verification as the first line of defense. **The replay defense**: the signature must include a timestamp, and the receiver must reject requests with timestamps older than ~5 minutes — without this, an attacker who captures a legitimate webhook can replay it indefinitely. **For the harness specifically**: the same discipline applies — Slack interactive message signatures are verified before the harness processes the message, and the verification uses the official Slack SDK rather than a hand-rolled implementation.

### 3. Idempotency at the receiver

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Event ID dedup** | Receiver tracks processed event IDs | The standard pattern |
| **Idempotency key from sender** | Sender provides a unique key per event | Stripe's `Idempotency-Key` |
| **Per-source ID tracking** | Different ID space per webhook source | Required for correctness |
| **Time-bounded dedup window** | Track recent IDs for N hours, evict older | Memory management |
| **Persistent dedup store** | Survive restart with the dedup state | Required for correctness across restarts |
| **Idempotent business logic** | Processing twice produces the same final state | The strongest guarantee |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Idempotency at the receiver is the load-bearing correctness property for webhook integrations**, and the right pattern is "track event ID per source in a persistent store, ignore duplicates, treat retries as no-ops". **Architectural commitment**: the dedup store must be persistent (in the database, not in memory) so that a restart doesn't reset the state and cause duplicate processing of in-flight retries. **The harness adjacency**: the event log is the natural dedup store — every received webhook is persisted to the log with the source event ID as a unique key, and the database constraint enforces dedup at the storage layer. **The connection to message queues entry §3**: this is the same effectively-once-via-idempotency pattern named there, applied to webhook delivery instead of in-process message consumption.

### 4. Webhook subscription management

| Concept | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Endpoint registration** | Configure the URL the sender will POST to | Standard sender API |
| **Event type subscription** | Filter which events the receiver wants | Reduces volume |
| **Per-tenant endpoints** | Different webhook URLs per tenant | Standard for SaaS |
| **Endpoint health monitoring** | Sender disables endpoints that consistently fail | Required hygiene |
| **Endpoint rotation** | Migrate from old URL to new URL safely | Standard operational task |
| **Webhook secrets management** | Where the per-endpoint secret lives | Connects to Configuration §3 |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Webhook subscription management is the operational discipline that prevents the "we forgot we had a webhook pointing here" failure mode** — a webhook that points to an old endpoint will silently fail or accept stale events forever, and the only defense is an inventory of all subscriptions. **Architectural commitment for the app layer**: webhook subscriptions are tracked in a registry (typically the database table that owns the integration), with the URL, secret, event types, and last-success timestamp visible to operators. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: webhook URLs configured in vendor dashboards with no record on the receiving side; the only way to discover them is to crawl every vendor's settings page. **For the harness specifically**: every webhook integration the harness consumes (Slack, GitHub, calendar) has its registration tracked in a config file and its secret stored via the secrets discipline named in Configuration §3.

### 5. Local development and tunneling

| Tool | Mechanism | Standard fit |
|---|---|---|
| **ngrok** | Public tunnel to localhost | The classic tool |
| **Cloudflare Tunnel** | Tunnel via Cloudflare's edge | Sovereign-friendly alternative |
| **Tailscale Funnel** | Tunnel via Tailscale | When already on Tailscale |
| **localhost.run** | SSH-based public tunneling | Lightweight |
| **Webhook.site** | Inspect webhooks without writing code | Debugging tool |
| **Replay tools (Hookdeck, Svix)** | Capture and replay webhooks for development | Modern dev workflow |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **Local webhook development requires a public URL because senders can't reach localhost**, and the standard answer is a tunneling tool that exposes localhost via a public URL. **Architectural commitment for the harness**: the navigator's local development of webhook receivers uses Cloudflare Tunnel (sovereign-aligned, Cloudflare-integrated with the rest of the orchestrator's substrate choices) or Tailscale Funnel as the alternative. **Webhook capture-and-replay tools (Hookdeck, Svix) are the underrated dev workflow** because they let you debug a webhook integration without continuously triggering the sender — capture once, replay against your local code as many times as needed. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: skipping local webhook testing because "it's hard to set up" and discovering bugs in production; the tunneling tools make local testing trivial.

### 6. The harness as a webhook receiver

| Webhook concern | Standard answer | Harness equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Idempotency** | Per-source event ID dedup | Event log with unique key on source event ID |
| **Signature verification** | HMAC verify on every request | Same — first line of defense |
| **Retry handling** | Receiver returns 2xx on success, sender retries on 5xx | Same — return 2xx after persisting to event log |
| **Dead letter** | Sender's DLQ for permanently failed events | Sender-managed; harness has no DLQ for received webhooks |
| **Subscription registry** | Database of registered endpoints | Config file with secrets in `sops` |
| **Local development** | ngrok or equivalent | Cloudflare Tunnel |

**Architectural inspiration thread:** **The harness is structurally a webhook receiver for multiple integration sources** — Slack interactive messages, possibly GitHub events, possibly calendar updates, possibly Stripe payment events for any orchestrator-built billing integration. **The architectural recognition**: every webhook the harness receives becomes an event in the event log, with the source event ID as the dedup key, and downstream consumers (the loop runner, the projection layer, the navigator-experience SLO layer) read from the log without needing to know about webhooks. **The architectural commitment**: webhook receivers in the harness are thin shims that verify signatures, persist to the event log, and return 2xx — all business logic happens downstream as a consumer of the event log. **The forbidden anti-pattern**: doing business logic inside the webhook handler synchronously; this couples the receiver to the processor and means a slow processor causes the sender to retry, producing duplicates and amplifying failures.

### Inspiration threads that emerged

Six things from this walk that look like real architectural questions or commitments:

1. **At-least-once with exponential backoff is the universal webhook delivery standard; receivers must be idempotent because retries will produce duplicates** (from §1).
2. **HMAC signature verification non-negotiable; replay defense via signed timestamp; reject requests with timestamps older than ~5 minutes** (from §2).
3. **Idempotency via per-source event ID dedup in a persistent store; the harness's event log is the natural dedup store** (from §3). Same effectively-once pattern as message queues.
4. **Webhook subscriptions tracked in a registry with URL, secret, event types, last-success timestamp; never configure URLs in vendor dashboards without a record on the receiving side** (from §4).
5. **Cloudflare Tunnel as the sovereign-aligned local-development tunneling tool; Hookdeck/Svix for capture-and-replay during dev** (from §5).
6. **Webhook receivers in the harness are thin shims that verify, persist to event log, return 2xx; all business logic downstream as event-log consumers** (from §6).

### Meta-observation about this entry

**This is a choice-layer entry that resolves cleanly toward "webhook receivers are thin shims into the event log"** for the harness layer and toward standard webhook hygiene at the app layer. The most generative move is **§6: the harness's webhook receivers persist to the event log and let downstream consumers do the business logic** — this prevents the most common webhook bug (slow synchronous processing causing sender retries) and uses the existing event-log substrate as the integration point.

**The most concrete near-term unlock at the harness layer** is **adopting the "verify, persist to event log, return 2xx" pattern uniformly across all webhook receivers** so that no business logic happens inside a webhook handler. **The most architecturally interesting thread** is the recognition that the harness's event log absorbs another category of integration concern (webhook reception) the same way it absorbs CDC, message broker, and audit-log responsibilities — one substrate, many integration roles.

**Layer note**: applies to both layers. Harness webhook receivers verify signatures, persist to the event log with source event ID as dedup key, return 2xx, and let downstream consumers do business logic. App-layer webhook handling follows standard patterns: HMAC verification, idempotency, exponential backoff retries, dead-letter handling, subscription registry, Cloudflare Tunnel for local dev, Hookdeck or Svix for capture-and-replay workflows.

---

## Structure classes: the menu of structural decisions

Cross-cutting meta-analysis that enumerates the **classes of structural decision** a system designer faces, with representative example types in each class and pointers to the topical entries that walk each class in depth. This is the **structure slot in the meta-analysis trio** — paired with *Harness scaling axes* (what grows) and *Harness compression dimensions* (how growth is absorbed). The four-concept frame claims that "structure is upstream of compression because it sets the option space"; this entry makes the option space visible by listing the classes, not by claiming which ones the orchestrator has adopted.

**This is a menu, not an audit.** It does not claim which structural options the architecture picks — that's what the Quick Reference (adopted positions) and the Promotion Tracker (proposed/speculative positions) are for. This entry is pure inventory: *here are the classes of decisions you might face, and here are the options in each class.* Use it to orient, to identify which decisions a proposed feature depends on, and to navigate to the deep walks.

**This is not exhaustive.** It covers the classes of structural decisions that have surfaced as load-bearing across the doc's 60+ topical entries; new classes will surface as new entries are written. The current ~38 classes should be read as "the known menu," not "the complete menu."

### How to use this entry

When designing or evaluating a feature, run the menu:

1. **Which class(es) does this feature touch?** Most features touch 1–3 classes directly; a few touch many.
2. **Which option in each class does the architecture use today?** The Quick Reference or the topical entry tells you the adopted position.
3. **Does the feature require a different option?** If yes, it's a structural change; evaluate the full consequence bundle (which scaling axes it addresses, which compression moves it unlocks, which amplification it pays — see the four-concept frame entry for the four-question test).
4. **Are there classes where the option space is still open?** If the class is relevant and no option is adopted, the feature forces a new structural commitment, which is a bigger decision than composing with existing commitments.

This gives feature review a specific shape: surface the classes involved, identify which are fixed and which are open, evaluate the consequences of any new commitment the feature forces.

### The menu

| Class | Question it answers | Example options | Deep walk |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data model** | Shape of structured information | Relational / Document / KV / Graph / Time-series / Vector / Wide-column / Columnar | Database Selection §1 |
| **Index structure** | Layout for efficient access | B+tree / LSM / Hash / Inverted / Bitmap (roaring) / Bloom / Vector (HNSW, IVF, PQ) / Geospatial (R-tree, quadtree) / Trigram | Indexes §1 |
| **Storage primitive** | How bytes are persisted and addressed | Block / File / Object | Database Selection §4, File storage |
| **Large-object storage pattern** | How big binary payloads are handled | Direct upload / Chunked multipart / Content-addressable / Versioned / Streaming / Presigned URL | File storage and large object handling |
| **Isolation / consistency** | Concurrent-writer guarantees | Serializable / Snapshot / Repeatable read / Read committed / Read uncommitted | Transactions §3 |
| **Replication** | Data copying for durability and read scale | Single-leader / Multi-leader / Leaderless / Log-shipping / Git-style async sync | Replication |
| **Partitioning / sharding** | Data division for write scale | Hash / Range / Directory-based / Per-tenant or per-org | Sharding |
| **Event-log structure** | Organization of append-only streams | Single-partition / Partitioned with consumer groups / Per-entity / Log-compacted / Time-bucketed | Event streaming §1, §5 |
| **Schema evolution** | How data shapes change over time | Forward-compatible / Backward-compatible / Full / Event versioning / Schema registry | Event streaming §6, Database Selection §6 |
| **Stream processing** | Computation over event streams | Stateless transform / Stateful with windows / Exactly-once / At-least-once + idempotent / Watermark-based | Stream processing |
| **Tiered storage** | Cost/latency-tiered data lifetime | Single tier / Hot-cold / Hot-warm-cold / Iceberg/Delta/Hudi / Time-bucketed retention | Open table formats |
| **Backup / disaster recovery** | Surviving catastrophic loss | Snapshot-based / Continuous / WAL-archive / Incremental / Cross-region / Point-in-time recovery | Backup and disaster recovery |
| **Data integration / ETL / CDC** | External-system bridging | Batch ETL / ELT / CDC / Dual-write / Outbox pattern / Change stream | Data integration, ETL, and change data capture |
| **Caching** | Closer-to-use storage | LRU / LFU / ARC / TLRU / TTL-based / Write-through / Write-back / Write-around / Content-addressable / CDN/edge / In-process vs. distributed | Caching |
| **Retrieval / search** | Finding items in a corpus | Indexed lookup / Full-text (BM25) / Semantic (embeddings) / Two-stage / Graph traversal / Hybrid | Search and information retrieval |
| **Recommender** | Ranking and selection | Content-based / Collaborative filtering / Hybrid / Contextual bandit / Two-tower / Matrix factorization | Recommender systems |
| **Consistency model (CAP)** | Partition-time tradeoff | CP / AP / PA-EL / Per-resource / Spectrum: strong → sequential → causal → read-your-writes → eventual | CAP theorem and consistency tradeoffs |
| **Consensus / distributed coordination** | Distributed-actor agreement | Raft / Paxos / ZAB / Single-leader with lease / CRDTs / Vector clocks / Two-phase commit | Consensus and distributed coordination |
| **System model** | Timing and failure assumptions | Synchronous / Asynchronous / Partially synchronous / Crash-stop / Crash-recovery / Byzantine | System models in distributed systems |
| **Concurrency structure** | In-process parallelism | Threads + locks / Actor model / CSP channels / Async-await / STM / Multi-process isolation | Concurrency primitives |
| **Time / ordering** | Timestamp assignment and comparison | Wall clock / Monotonic clock / Lamport timestamp / Vector clock / HLC / Monotonic sequence | Time and clocks |
| **Failure recovery** | Handling dependency and internal failures | Retry + backoff / Circuit breaker / Bulkhead / Dead letter queue / Saga compensation / Forward/pivot/backward / Heartbeat timeout / Replay-based recovery | Resilience patterns |
| **Workflow / saga** | Multi-step process composition | Saga / State machine / DAG / Pipeline / Loop / Actor hierarchy / Workflow engine (Temporal, Cadence, Airflow, Step Functions) | Saga orchestration, Batch processing and workflow orchestration |
| **Batch processing / scheduling** | Non-real-time work orchestration | Cron / Event-driven / Priority queue / Fair scheduler / Window-based / Cascading DAGs | Batch processing and workflow orchestration |
| **Communication** | Component-to-component exchange | Request/response (REST, gRPC, JSON-RPC, MCP) / Message queue / Pub/sub / Event stream / Webhook / WebSocket, SSE / RPC | API design, Message queues and brokers, Webhooks |
| **Notification / push** | Proactive outbound messaging | Email / SMS / Push / In-app / Slack, Discord / Webhook / Scheduled-with-cancel / Priority tiers | Notification systems |
| **Auth (identity + authorization)** | Establishing identity and access | Session cookies / JWT / OAuth / OIDC / API keys / SSO, SAML / Capability tokens / mTLS / RBAC, ABAC, PBAC | Authentication and authorization |
| **Multi-tenancy** | Tenant isolation in shared infrastructure | Shared everything / Row-level security (RLS) / Shared DB, separate schema / Separate DB / Separate cluster | Multi-tenancy |
| **Data governance / privacy** | Regulated-data handling | Classification / Encryption at rest / Crypto-shredding / Right-to-delete / Audit trails / Retention policies / PII tokenization | Data governance, compliance, and privacy |
| **Observability** | Seeing inside the system | Structured logs / Metrics / Distributed traces / OpenTelemetry / Spans / Event log as observability substrate | Observability |
| **SLO / error budgets** | "Good enough" measurement | SLI / SLO / SLA / Error budget / Burn-rate alerting / Multi-window multi-burn-rate | SLIs, SLOs, SLAs, and error budgets |
| **Geographic distribution** | Physical placement and routing | Single region / Multi-region active-passive / Active-active / Edge / CDN / Anycast | Geographic distribution, edge, and CDN |
| **Configuration management** | Runtime parameterization | Build-time / Env vars / Secrets (sops+age, KMS, Vault) / Runtime flags / Feature flags / User prefs / Tenant config | Configuration management and feature flags |
| **Deployment / release** | Change rollout strategy | Blue-green / Canary / Rolling / Feature-flagged / All-at-once / Shadow traffic | CI/CD, infrastructure-as-code, and container orchestration |
| **A/B testing / experimentation** | Safe experimentation | Feature flags / A/B test / Multi-armed bandit / Sequential test / Guardrail metrics / CUPED variance reduction | A/B testing and experimentation infrastructure |
| **ML systems** | Training and serving infrastructure | Training pipeline / Feature store / Model registry / Online serving / Batch prediction / A/B model comparison | ML systems infrastructure |
| **Real-time / deadline** | Deadline semantics | Soft real-time / Hard real-time / Best-effort / Deadline scheduling / EDF | Real-time systems |
| **Mobile / offline-first sync** | Disconnected operation | Last-write-wins / CRDTs / Operational transforms / Manual merge / Event log sync | Mobile and offline-first sync |

**How to read this table**: one row per class. The *Class* column is the axis of structural decision; the *Question* is the "what am I even deciding here?"; the *Example options* column lists representative types within the class (not exhaustive — the deep walk has the full option space with tradeoffs); the *Deep walk* points at the topical entry that walks the class in depth. The ordering is rough and clusters related classes (storage/persistence → querying → distributed-systems theory → reliability → communication → app-layer → operations → specialized), but is not load-bearing.

**Notable absences**: classes that are *subsumed* by other entries and don't need their own row:

- **Load balancing, service discovery, service mesh** → subsumed by Communication
- **Networking protocols at depth** → subsumed by Communication
- **Geospatial** → subsumed by Data model and Index structure
- **Write-ahead log** → foundational substrate, used by Event-log structure, Replication, and Backup
- **Query engines, Time series databases** → specializations within Data model + Index structure
- **Disk I/O, Latency and throughput** → substrate-level concerns used across many classes
- **Payment and billing systems** → domain-specific, not a general structural class
- **Cost engineering and FinOps** → discipline that cuts across every class, not a class itself

### Meta-observation about the menu

**The menu is the pre-condition for every other architectural decision.** When the four-concept frame asks "which structural commitment does this depend on?", it's asking you to pick a class from this menu and identify which option is in play. When a feature requires a different option than the one currently adopted, it's proposing a structural change, which is a much bigger decision than a compression or scaling addition.

**Classes are taxonomically distinct but architecturally entangled.** Some choices in one class constrain choices in another:

- Adopting **event-sourced state** (Event-log structure) typically makes **CQRS** (a communication-structure choice) the natural fit
- Adopting **local-first single-machine** (a partitioning / replication choice) dissolves entire classes at the harness layer — CAP, Consensus, Multi-tenancy, most of Replication, most of Consensus
- Adopting **graph data model** (Data model) makes **graph traversal** (Retrieval structure) the natural primary access pattern
- Adopting **append-then-compact** (Event-log structure + Index structure) makes **bloom filters + roaring bitmaps** (Index structure) the natural read-amplification mitigation

The menu doesn't capture the entanglement explicitly — the topical entries and the cross-entry inspiration threads do. Use the menu to identify *which classes are in play*; use the topical entries to understand *how choices in those classes constrain each other*.

**The orchestrator's adopted positions within each class live elsewhere — not here.** For example, this menu lists "relational / document / key-value / graph / time-series / vector / wide-column / columnar" as data-model options. The Quick Reference at line 175 names *"graph-of-typed-nodes as the unifying data model"* as the adopted position. The menu tells you the option space; the Quick Reference tells you what's picked; the topical entry (Database Selection §1) walks the tradeoffs that justify the pick. Three different roles; keep them separate.

**Not all classes apply at all layers.** The harness dissolves several classes (CAP, Consensus, most of Replication, Multi-tenancy at the harness level) by structural choice. The app layer keeps them all. Use the menu as a starting inventory and prune based on which classes are structurally dissolved for the layer you're working at.

### Layer note

**The menu itself is domain-agnostic — every system makes choices within these classes.** The specific options and the adopted positions vary by layer (harness vs. app) and by project, but the classes are universal. This is the only entry in the "Harness-specific meta-analyses" section whose *content* isn't harness-specific; it's placed there because it anchors the other three meta-entries in the trio (the four-concept frame uses it, the scaling axes and compression dimensions presuppose it), but the menu itself applies to any system design decision at either layer.

**Harness-specific dissolutions to note when using the menu**: CAP, Consensus, Multi-tenancy, most of Replication, most of distributed-coordination concerns, service mesh, geographic distribution, and load balancing are structurally dissolved at the harness layer by the local-first single-machine commitment. These classes remain in the menu for reference and because they apply fully at the app layer, but for harness-layer feature review, you can usually skip them.

---

## Scaling axes

Master list of the axes along which the harness experiences **pressure** — what grows, strains, or presses on the system as it evolves. Paired with the **Tradeoffs** entry (which catalogs the shape/cost dimensions you pick positions on) and the **Harness scaling responses** entry (which walks the four response strategies: compress, expand, shed, defer).

Traditional distributed-systems scaling (cross-machine load balancing, multi-DC consensus, horizontal sharding, geographic distribution) is dissolved by the local-first commitment — those pressure axes don't apply at the harness layer. But "scaling" is a much bigger word than cross-machine coordination, and every other kind of scaling pressure is still in play. Scaling has been **transposed** from cross-machine coordination to intra-process hierarchy, concurrency, data volume, temporal persistence, resource budgets, attention economics, autonomy, and coordination.

This is a **doesn't-apply-but-transfers** entry with a cross-cutting structural observation — it inventories scaling pressures that other entries touch piecemeal but that no single entry names as a coherent taxonomy.

### 1. Hierarchical axes (nesting and multiplication)

### 1. Hierarchical axes (nesting and multiplication)

- **Orgs per harness instance** — one today, but servicegrid's multi-human case and the general "multiple cosmoses per navigator" direction makes this a live axis
- **Apps per org** — each org ships multiple products (servicegrid ships customer agents; pilgrimage ships games; dreamtime ships autonomous services); apps proliferate over time
- **Customer-facing agents per app** — servicegrid is the canonical example, where the app itself ships N agents (one per contractor); this is potentially the largest multiplicative factor in the whole hierarchy
- **Skills per org** — the skill library grows monotonically; thousands eventually
- **Loops per org** — loop library grows; also composed into sequences and lines
- **Memories per org** — accumulates with every decision, every calibration update, every handoff
- **Knowledge graph nodes and edges per org** — graph grows with every skill, loop, decision, pattern, memory, trace

### 2. Concurrency axes (parallel work within one process)

- **Shadow clones per human session (Mechanism 1: in-process Agent spawns)** — bounded by token budget, context-window accounting, and the process-level concurrency primitives
- **Shadow clones across sessions (Mechanism 2: separate terminal sessions)** — bounded differently; each has its own process and its own token budget
- **Concurrent loop executions** — different loops running at the same time on different parts of the cosmos
- **Concurrent external events** — webhooks, Slack messages, scheduled triggers, file-watch events; the channel layer is literally a concurrency-ingest layer
- **Concurrent projection builders** — as the CQRS commitment matures, each projection is an independent consumer of the event log that runs in parallel with others

### 3. Data volume axes

- **Event log volume per org per unit time** — the axis that drives the tiered-format pipeline decision; agents-as-learners make this spike
- **Trace/calibration volume** — every tool call, every skill completion, every gate decision produces an event
- **Projection state size** — every CQRS projection has its own materialized footprint that grows with the log
- **Search corpus size** — the six latent search surfaces (skills, memories, traces, decisions, loops, external docs) each grow independently
- **Dream state document size** — cosmos descriptions grow as the architecture deepens
- **Historical depth of available queries** — time travel gets more expensive as the log grows, even when the current state is small

### 4. Temporal axes

- **Session length** — long-running loops, autonomous mode, sleep-test scenarios where the loop must survive for hours or days unattended
- **Inter-interaction gap** — how long between human touches; the sleep test is specifically about this axis
- **Retention horizons** — hot/warm/cold tier lifetimes; each tier is an axis
- **Loop depth** — how many nested sub-loops can stack before context budget or coordination complexity breaks

### 5. Resource axes (the single-machine constraints that replace cross-machine ones)

- **Token budget per session, per org, per loop** — the dominant cost axis for LLM-driven systems; the orchestrator is structurally a high-token-cost system and the doc already commits to this
- **Context window budget per agent** — each agent has a finite context; as workload grows, compression pressure on context grows
- **Memory and disk on the single machine** — the literal resource ceiling; eventually some data has to move off-machine even in the local-first commitment (that's the cold-tier trigger)
- **LLM API call volume** — rate limits, latency budgets, connection reuse
- **Process and file-descriptor counts** — Mechanism 2 clones are separate processes; there's a kernel limit

### 6. Attention axes (unique to human-serving systems)

- **Autonomy level (fraction of work completed without human-in-the-loop oversight)** — autonomous mode, dark factory mode, gate auto-approval, delegated decision authority to shadow clones. As autonomy rises, the other attention axes dilate (the navigator's attention reaches further per unit of work), but the trust requirement, verification burden, recovery latency, and guardrail tightness rise in step. The sleep test is specifically the discipline that keeps autonomy safe — autonomous work must be checkpointable, replayable, and reversible, and failures must surface to the navigator within acceptable latency bounds. **This is the single most multiplicative scaling axis in the harness**: a 10x increase in autonomy is roughly a 10x increase in work output per unit navigator attention, bounded only by the trust and verification budget. Autonomy is upstream of every other attention axis — if the system is more autonomous, it reduces pressure on cognitive load, notification rate, and review queue depth simultaneously
- **Navigator attention budget** — the number of decisions, gates, proposals, notifications the human can review before attention breaks
- **Notification rate under quiet hours / sleep test** — the attention discipline the doc already commits to
- **Cognitive load per decision** — how much context a single review demands; the leverage protocol is partly a cognitive-load-reduction mechanism
- **Review queue depth** — upgrade proposals, gate approvals, memory confirmations, handoff summaries

### 7. Coordination axes (still present even without cross-machine)

- **Shadow clone coordination** — not distributed consensus, but still coordination; the event-log-as-coordination-substrate move from Event streaming §3 is the orchestrator's answer, but the coordination problem doesn't vanish
- **Cross-org operations** — if multiple orgs ever interact (lineage analysis, cross-org pattern detection), the coordination question returns in a different form
- **Saga coordination for multi-step operations** — compensation, retry, pivot recovery

### The structural point

The pattern across all of these is that **the harness has transposed scaling from cross-machine coordination to intra-process hierarchy, concurrency, data volume, temporal persistence, resource budgets, attention economics, autonomy, and coordination**. The scaling problem has the same general shape (what happens when N grows?) but the binding constraints are different. You don't have partition tolerance as a concern; you have token budget as a concern. You don't have network latency across DCs; you have context window pressure. You don't have distributed consensus; you have shadow clone coordination via event-log consumer groups. You don't have horizontal scaling across instances; you have autonomy level as a multiplicative factor on navigator attention reach.

For how the harness *responds* to these pressures (the four response strategies: compress, expand, shed, defer), see **Harness scaling responses**. For the shape-and-cost dimensions that options vary along, see **Tradeoffs**.

### Current doc coverage

Every axis above has its own bottleneck and its own scaling technique, and the doc currently names them piecemeal or not at all:

| Axis | Bottleneck when N grows | Named in the doc? |
|---|---|---|
| Orgs per instance | Cosmos boundary enforcement, per-org resource accounting | Partially (Sharding §1, §2) |
| Apps per org | Cross-app state, coordination across product lifecycles | No |
| Customer agents per app | App-layer scaling returns fully; two-layer commit applies | Yes (harness-vs-app framing) |
| Skills per org | Search latency, composition complexity | Partially (Search, Indexes) |
| Shadow clones in session | Token budget, context accounting, coordination overhead | Partially (Concurrency primitives) |
| Event log volume | Storage substrate, compaction cost, tier boundaries | Yes (tiered-format pipeline) |
| Trace/calibration volume | Curation bottleneck, training-data volume | Yes (trace flywheel, Open table formats thread #10) |
| Session length | Checkpoint frequency, sleep-test durability, token spend | Partially (sleep test, durability) |
| Token budget per session | Cost engineering, rate limits, backpressure | Yes (Cost engineering entry) |
| Context window | Summarization, projection, memory retrieval | No (scattered across entries) |
| Autonomy level | Trust, verification, guardrails, sleep-test durability | Partially (autonomous mode tools; sleep test; gate auto-approval) |
| Navigator attention | Notification discipline, proposal batching, quiet hours | Partially (Notification systems §4) |
| Shadow clone coordination | Event-log consumer groups, CRDTs for shared state | Yes (Event streaming §3) |
| Search corpus | FTS index size, posting list growth | Yes (Search, Indexes) |
| Projection state | Projection rebuild cost, storage per projection | No |

The "yes" cases are the ones the doc has named as first-class scaling concerns. The "no" and "partially" cases are the gaps — specifically **apps per org, context window, and projection state are entirely unnamed**, and several others (orgs per instance, skills per org, shadow clones in session, session length, autonomy level, navigator attention) are named only in passing inside other entries without being recognized as scaling concerns in their own right.

### Architectural inspiration thread

**The harness's architecture is dominated by scaling along these axes, not by scaling along traditional distributed-systems axes.** Every major commitment in the doc (event sourcing, CQRS, tiered-format pipeline, append-then-compact, per-org partitioning, sleep test, leverage protocol, calibration system, notification discipline) is a scaling technique for one or more of these transposed axes — but the doc currently describes them as individual architectural decisions rather than as points in a coherent scaling taxonomy. Naming the taxonomy makes the connections visible and gives future decisions a home. *"Which axis does this decision scale?"* becomes an answerable question for any proposed feature, and features that don't scale any axis can be flagged as premature or misaligned.

The specific near-term unlocks this framing surfaces:
- **Name context window budgeting as a first-class scaling concern** with its own entry or sharpening, since it's currently scattered and has no coherent architectural response
- **Name projection state growth** as an explicit scaling concern, since the CQRS commitment creates unbounded projection footprint that currently has no named management discipline
- **Name apps-per-org scaling** since multi-app orgs are already live (servicegrid + future) but cross-app coordination has no architectural framing
- **Name autonomy level as the most multiplicative scaling axis** — it's upstream of every attention axis and determines whether the attention budget matters at all — and commit to the sleep test + trust + verification + guardrails + recovery as the discipline that makes rising autonomy safe

### Layer note

**Applies almost entirely to the harness layer.** The app layer has its own scaling concerns but they look like traditional distributed-systems scaling (horizontal instances, load balancing, multi-DC, edge caching, etc.) because the app layer is a standard production web app stack. The transposed axes in this entry are specific to the harness's single-process single-machine commitment. Customer-facing agents per app is the one axis that straddles — it's a hierarchical axis from the harness's perspective but a horizontal-scaling axis from the app's perspective.

---

## Tradeoffs

Master list of the **shape and cost dimensions** along which design decisions pick a position. Paired with the **Scaling axes** entry (which catalogs pressure — what grows) and the **Harness scaling responses** entry (which walks the four response strategies). Where scaling axes describe pressures acting on the system, tradeoffs describe the dimensions each option varies along: every structural option sits at some specific point on each relevant tradeoff, and picking an option means picking a combination of positions.

This entry is a reference catalog consulted per structure category: each category has some subset of tradeoffs that apply to decisions in that category, and each option in the category sits somewhere on each of those tradeoffs. The tradeoff positions explain *why* an option is on the menu and *when* you'd pick it.

### How tradeoffs are used

For any decision, the tradeoff analysis has two levels:

- **Macro level** — which tradeoffs matter most for this workload? You can't maximize all dimensions simultaneously; axes trade against each other. Picking priorities is the macro move.
