- **Micro level** — where on each tradeoff does the option sit, and where does the workload need to sit? Picking positions is the micro move.

Both happen inside structure-category analysis. Per structure category, you identify which tradeoffs apply, then look at each option's position on those tradeoffs, then pick the option whose profile matches the workload's needs.

### The master list

Tradeoffs are grouped into two loose clusters: **amplification dimensions** (how much each decision pays on specific axes) and **shape dimensions / dilemmas** (how the decision takes form, two-axis tradeoffs). The distinction is partly stylistic — amplification dimensions are usually stated as "low ↔ high cost on axis X" while shape dimensions are stated as "this pole ↔ that pole" — but both are the same kind of thing: axes an option sits on. The shape dimensions ARE the canonical *named dilemmas* of system design (the doc uses "shape dimension" and "dilemma" interchangeably for them); see the *Named impossibility statements* section near the end for the related but distinct class of *trilemmas* (three-axis impossibilities like CAP, RUM, PACELC).

#### Amplification dimensions (cost)

| Tradeoff | Poles | Paid by (examples) |
|---|---|---|
| **Write amplification** | Low ↔ high | B+tree page rewrites; WAL doubling writes; secondary index maintenance; replication log-shipping |
| **Read amplification** | Low ↔ high | LSM multi-SSTable reads; unindexed queries; projection rebuilds; Parquet without bloom filters |
| **Compute amplification** | Low ↔ high | Log compaction passes; query planning; vector index maintenance; projection folding |
| **Token amplification** | Low ↔ high | Prompt repetition; context bloat; verbose tool outputs; inefficient re-read serialization |
| **Cognitive amplification** | Low ↔ high | Commitment count; review queue depth; choice overload; context-switching cost |
| **Complexity amplification** | Low ↔ high | Moving parts; hidden coupling; cross-cutting concerns; distributed state |
| **Attention amplification** | Low ↔ high | Unacknowledged notifications; gate review queue; interrupt-driven polling |

> **Formal grounding: the RUM conjecture.** Write, read, and space amplification (R, U, M in the original paper) are formally grounded as a tradeoff triangle by Athanassoulis et al. (SIGMOD 2016, *"Designing Access Methods: The RUM Conjecture"*): **you cannot simultaneously minimize all three — pick any two to optimize, and the third gets worse, by mathematical necessity.** Every access method (B+tree, LSM, hash, bloom filter, vector index, roaring bitmap) picks a point in the RUM triangle. The doc's broader amplification list (compute, latency, token, cognitive, complexity, sovereignty, attention, accuracy) extends RUM to a richer cost surface, but the original three remain the load-bearing core for any access-method decision. See **Indexes §1** for the full walk and the RUM-grounded reframing of B+tree-vs-LSM.

> **Formal grounding: time complexity (Big-O) for compute amplification.** Compute amplification ("CPU cycles per logical operation") is formally measured by **time complexity** — the asymptotic cost of an operation as a function of input size. The complexity ladder, in roughly increasing order of scalability:
>
> ```
>   O(2ⁿ)  →  O(n³)  →  O(n²)  →  O(n log n)  →  O(n)  →  O(log n)  →  O(1)
>    ↑                                                                    ↑
>  worst                                                                 best
> ```
>
> Each tier is roughly an order-of-magnitude difference in how the operation scales with input size. **A common architectural goal is "move up one tier on the time complexity ladder by paying an up-front cost"** — this is the formal version of compute compression. Examples:
>
> - **Indexing**: O(n) scan → O(log n) lookup (B+tree, hash) — pay write amp at index time
> - **Snapshotting**: O(n) replay → O(1) bootstrap — pay periodic snapshot cost
> - **Precomputation**: O(n) per query → O(1) per query (materialized views, rollups) — pay write amp at projection time
> - **Sharding**: O(n) total → O(n/k) per shard — pay coordination overhead
> - **Caching**: O(n) recompute → O(1) lookup — pay space amp for cached results
> - **Probabilistic structures**: O(n) exact → O(k) approximate (bloom, HLL) — pay accuracy amp for tier-jump
> - **Dynamic programming**: O(2ⁿ) recursive → O(n²) or O(n) memoized — pay space amp for memoization
>
> All of these are instances of the same meta-pattern: **identify the tier the operation is currently in, and pay an up-front cost to move it up the ladder by one or more tiers.** The up-front cost is usually compute amplification at write time, space amplification (more memory for the data structure that enables the tier jump), or accuracy amplification (lossy approximations that escape exact-tier requirements). See **Harness scaling responses** Compress strategy for *complexity-tier compression* as a named class of compression mechanism.
>
> **The predictive lens**: when evaluating any compression mechanism, ask *"what tier is the operation in now, and what tier does this move it to? what's the up-front cost?"* — this is a generative test for whether a compression move is worth doing. Most architectural compression decisions reduce to "we're in tier X, we want to be in tier Y, here's the up-front cost we're paying to get there."

#### Shape dimensions / dilemmas (form)

The shape dimensions in the table below ARE the canonical *named dilemmas* of system design — two-axis tradeoffs where you trade A for B and pick a position on the spectrum based on the workload's needs. The terms "shape dimension" and "dilemma" refer to the same thing in this doc; both labels are valid and used interchangeably. Dilemmas are distinct from **trilemmas** (three-axis impossibility statements like CAP / PACELC / RUM / project-management triangle), which get their own treatment in the *Named impossibility statements* section.

| Dilemma | Poles | Applies to |
|---|---|---|
| **Eager ↔ lazy** | Pre-compute at write time ↔ compute on demand at read time | Indexes, caches, projections, materialized views, memoization |
| **Push ↔ pull** | Producer drives flow ↔ consumer drives flow | Event streams, webhooks, message queues, notifications |
| **Sync ↔ async** | Caller blocks for completion ↔ caller returns and is notified later | Control flow, RPC styles, IO models, durability semantics |
| **Deterministic ↔ probabilistic** | Same input always → same output ↔ statistical / stochastic | ML systems, recommenders, retry jitter, Monte Carlo, gossip |
| **Optimistic ↔ pessimistic concurrency** | Assume contention rare, retry on conflict ↔ lock upfront | Transactions, concurrency, distributed coordination |
| **Early ↔ late binding** | Compile/design time ↔ runtime | Schema validation, type systems, dispatch, configuration |
| **Precision ↔ recall** | Few false positives ↔ catch everything | Retrieval, recommenders, alerting, PII classification |
| **Strong ↔ weak correctness** | Serializable / exactly-once ↔ eventual / read-uncommitted | Isolation, consistency, delivery semantics, consensus |
| **General ↔ specialized** | One primitive many uses ↔ specialized tool per use | Data models, index structures, workflow structures |
| **Foreclosed ↔ flexible** | Locked-in commitment ↔ preserved optionality | Any structural commitment; anticipation discipline |
| **Imperative ↔ declarative** | Caller specifies how ↔ caller specifies what; engine decides how | Query interfaces (SQL vs. ORM), config (Terraform vs. scripts), UI (React vs. imperative DOM), policy (Rego vs. middleware), build (Bazel vs. Make-as-script) |
| **Now ↔ later** | Pay at design time ↔ retrofit when needed | Schema versioning; deferred decisions; crossover-decision pattern |
| **Latency ↔ throughput** | Per-item speed ↔ aggregate rate (absorbs latency amplification) | Batching vs. per-request; stream vs. batch |
| **Space ↔ time** | Memory/disk for faster computation ↔ compute to save storage (absorbs space amplification) | Caches, indexes, denormalization vs. scan-on-demand |
| **Stateful ↔ stateless** | Service holds state across requests ↔ service is pure function of input | Service design, deployment, horizontal scaling capability |
| **Centralized ↔ decentralized** | Single coordinator ↔ peer-to-peer / federated | Architecture posture; distinct from sovereign ↔ scaled (which is about who runs it; centralized/decentralized is about whether there's a coordinator) |
| **Monolith ↔ microservices** | One deployable artifact ↔ many small services | Deployment architecture; team topology; release coupling |
| **CP ↔ AP** | Reject writes under partition ↔ accept but diverge | Distributed systems, replication, consensus |
| **Exact ↔ approximate** | Full accuracy ↔ bounded error (absorbs accuracy amplification) | Probabilistic data structures, sampling, approximate NN |
| **Sovereign ↔ scaled** | Local control, no external deps ↔ managed reach (absorbs sovereignty amplification) | Any tool / substrate choice; layer-dependent |
| **High ↔ low commitment density** | Many explicit structural commitments ↔ few commitments, decisions on runtime path | Architectural posture |

**Total: 7 amplification tradeoffs + 21 shape dilemmas = 28 tradeoffs** in the master list.

### Notes on overlap and role

Amplification and shape tradeoffs overlap in a few cases — the three noted in the shape table (latency ↔ throughput absorbs latency amplification; space ↔ time absorbs space amplification; exact ↔ approximate absorbs accuracy amplification; sovereign ↔ scaled absorbs sovereignty amplification). These aren't duplicated: the tradeoff appears once in the shape cluster and the old amplification framing is absorbed into it.

The distinction between amplification and shape tradeoffs is a **presentation convenience**, not a fundamental category split. Both are axes an option sits on. Amplification dimensions are the ones most naturally stated as "low cost ↔ high cost"; shape dimensions are the ones most naturally stated as "this pole ↔ that pole." You could merge them into one flat list; the two-cluster presentation just makes the amplification side easier to scan.

### How this fits structure categories

Each structure category has a subset of tradeoffs that apply to decisions in it. A category entry (see topical entries like Database Selection, Indexes, Replication, etc.) should list:

1. Which scaling axes press on decisions in this category (from Scaling axes entry)
2. Which tradeoffs to think through when picking among options (from this entry)
3. The options available, each with a "why it's on the menu" description
4. A short "how to pick" blurb guiding the reader through the tradeoffs toward a fit

The scaling axes tell you what pressures you're facing; the tradeoffs tell you what dimensions your options differ on; the options list gives you the actual choices; the how-to-pick blurb connects pressure and tradeoff positions to option selection.

### Layer note

**The tradeoffs list is domain-agnostic in shape — every system makes decisions that sit on these dimensions — but the weights differ by layer.** At the harness layer, sovereignty and cognitive/attention amplification are load-bearing; at the app layer, complexity amplification, latency-vs-throughput, and CP-vs-AP dominate. Use the tradeoff list as a starting inventory and weight per layer.

---

## Harness scaling responses

Cross-cutting meta-analysis of how the harness responds to scaling pressure. This is the **response half of scaling** — paired with *Harness scaling axes*, which covers the pressure half. When a scaling axis grows, the system absorbs that growth through one or more of four strategies: **compress, expand, shed, defer.**

At the harness layer, **compression dominates** because the other three strategies are mostly foreclosed by the harness's structural commitments. Local-first forecloses horizontal expansion and distribution; sovereignty forecloses offloading; the sleep test constrains shedding of high-stakes events; synchronous-durability commitments limit deferral of writes. What's left is compression, which is why the harness's response profile looks disproportionately like "compress everything." This entry enumerates all four response strategies, with the compression strategy walked in depth (since it's the dominant one) and expand/shed/defer walked more briefly.

### The four response strategies

| Strategy | What it does | Status at the harness layer |
|---|---|---|
| **Compress** | Use existing resources more efficiently — density, memoization, deduplication, projections, specialization, probabilistic approximation, abstraction | **Dominant.** Most of the harness's architectural machinery |
| **Expand** | Add resources to handle pressure — horizontal/vertical scaling, parallelization, distribution, offloading | **Mostly foreclosed.** Only shadow clones are in active use; horizontal scale-out, distribution, offloading all ruled out by structural commitments |
| **Shed** | Reduce pressure at the source — rejection, rate limiting, retention limits, filtering | **Used selectively.** Token budget caps, telemetry retention, gate auto-approval; constrained by sleep test on high-stakes events |
| **Defer** | Shift work in time — lazy evaluation, batching for later, prioritization, scheduled work | **Used sparingly.** Eager-vs-lazy projection tradeoffs, scheduled notifications; constrained by synchronous durability and navigator attention |

The rest of the entry walks each strategy.

## Strategy 1 — Compress (the dominant harness response)

The harness's primary response to scaling pressure. Its machinery is structurally a compression engine: the event log compresses state mutations into atoms; projections compress the log into query views; the knowledge graph compresses cross-reference relationships; skills compress workflow patterns; the leverage protocol compresses the action space; calibration data compresses past runs into priors. Every substantive architectural move in the doc is a compression move against one or more of the categories below. The ten categories cover the compression-shaped responses the harness uses.

> **Cross-cutting class: complexity-tier compression.** Many of the specific mechanisms below are instances of the same meta-pattern — **"pay an up-front cost to move an operation up one or more tiers on the time complexity ladder."** Indexing moves O(n) scan to O(log n) lookup. Snapshotting moves O(n) replay to O(1) bootstrap. Precomputation moves O(n) per query to O(1) per query. Caching moves O(n) recompute to O(1) lookup. Probabilistic structures (bloom, HLL, count-min) move O(n) exact to O(k) approximate. Sharding moves O(n) total to O(n/k) per shard. **Complexity-tier compression is recognized as a named class within Compress** because it cuts across the ten sub-categories below — every category that involves repeated operations on growing input has tier-jump opportunities. The up-front cost is usually paid in write amplification (index maintenance), space amplification (memoization, indexes, snapshots), or accuracy amplification (probabilistic approximation). See the *time complexity (Big-O)* note in the Tradeoffs entry's amplification section for the formal grounding.

### 1.1. Cognitive / attention compression (what the navigator sees vs. what's underneath)

- **Skills** — compress recurring conversation patterns into named, reusable primitives with structured inputs/outputs; each skill is a dense encoding of a workflow that would otherwise require the navigator to reconstruct the pattern from scratch each time
- **Loops** — compress workflow templates (engineering, bugfix, proposal, learning, etc.) into phase-structured executables with known gates and skills; the navigator picks a loop instead of assembling one
- **Loop sequences and lines** — compress multi-loop chains into recommended playbooks (engineering → bugfix → distribution is a line); the leverage protocol's multi-move planning is compression over the action space
- **Gates** — compress quality thresholds into binary pass/fail checkpoints with auto-approval rules; the navigator only sees the gate if it fails
- **Dream states** — compress goals into alignable targets at org/system/module tiers; the dream state *is* compression of "what does success look like?"
- **Handoffs** — compress N hours of session context into reload-ready summaries; the navigator can resume a cold session by reading one document instead of replaying history
- **Terminal views** — compress live system state into glanceable dashboards (coherence status, MECE taxonomy, game design status, skill tree status, roadmap progress, SRS status, sequencing, dreaming); each view is a purpose-built projection of underlying state
- **Calibrated estimates** — compress many past runs of the same skill into a multiplicative correction factor; the navigator sees "this will take ~45 minutes" instead of "here are 20 past runs"
- **Leverage protocol** — compresses the entire action space into a ranked recommendation with scoring rationale; N possible next moves → 1 top move + 2 alternatives
- **Daily status check** — compresses cross-cosmos state into a morning digest

### 1.2. Knowledge / semantic compression (relationships and meaning)

- **Knowledge graph** — compresses relationships between concepts (skills, loops, decisions, patterns, memories) into typed edges; the graph *is* compression of the cross-reference structure of the orchestrator's total knowledge
- **Memory entries** (orchestrator / loop / skill memory hierarchy) — compress past decisions, patterns, handoffs into retrievable context keyed by scope
- **Patterns** (via `record_pattern`) — compress recurring shapes ("this kind of thing keeps happening") into named, queryable archetypes that can be matched against future situations
- **Decisions** (via `record_decision`) — compress ADR-shaped reasoning into structured records with context, options, decision, consequences
- **MECE taxonomy** — compresses the total skill space into non-overlapping categories that collectively cover the whole; a compression of "what can the orchestrator do?" into a categorization
- **Skill coverage / gap analysis** — compresses "what's missing" into an addressable list
- **Topological analysis of the skill graph** — compresses dependency structure into ordering (what depends on what, what's isolated, what's highly connected)
- **Leverage scores** — compress value estimation into numeric rankings; the value equation `(DSA × 0.40 + Downstream Unlock × 0.25 + Likelihood × 0.15) ÷ (Time × 0.10 + Effort × 0.10)` is literally a compression function from many dimensions into one scalar

### 1.3. State / event compression (time into storable atoms)

- **Event log** — compresses state mutations into appendable immutable events; the canonical form of "what happened" into which all projections fold
- **Log compaction** — compresses event history into "latest event per key, earlier dropped"; the skill registry, knowledge graph, and calibration store are naturally log-compacted streams
- **Snapshots** — compress fold-over-events into bootstrap-ready checkpoints; turns O(n) replay into O(1) startup cost
- **Projections** — compress the event log into query-shaped views (knowledge graph view, calibration view, dashboard view, SRS view); each is a purpose-built compression of the log for a specific query pattern
- **Tiered storage (hot JSONL / warm Parquet / cold Iceberg)** — compresses temporal-age into denser formats as data cools; the Parquet row group is a dense columnar compression of many JSONL events
- **Bloom filters per immutable atom** — compress "does this atom contain X?" into a tiny probabilistic bit vector; the canonical mitigation for append-then-compact read amplification
- **Roaring bitmaps** — compress label-set membership across millions of rows into container-hybrid bitmaps; set operations become bitwise operations
- **Content-addressable keys** (`skill@version` and similar) — compress (identity, version) tuples into hashes that eliminate cache invalidation

### 1.4. Text / search compression (language corpora into queryable form)

- **FTS5 inverted index with BM25 ranking** — compresses text corpora into posting lists (term → list of document IDs); the corpus becomes O(log n) searchable instead of O(n) scannable
- **Trigram indexes** (GIN / GiST) — compress substring similarity into trigram sets for fast partial-match search
- **Vector embeddings** (optional per-project RAG) — compress semantic similarity into high-dimensional vectors that cluster by meaning
- **Summaries** (via `update_summary`, handoffs, conversation compaction) — compress long text into shorter text while preserving load-bearing content
- **Proposal-to-summary pipeline** — compresses upgrade proposals into navigator-facing digests
- **Six latent search surfaces** (skills, memories, traces, decisions, loops, external docs) — each surface is a compression of its corpus into search-ready posting lists; the unified harness search service compresses six interfaces into one

### 1.5. Token / LLM-specific compression

- **Context window management** — compresses prior conversation into retained-state through summarization, projection, and memory retrieval; a finite window forces aggressive compression of history
- **Prompt caching (provider-side)** — compresses repeated prompts into cached prefixes so subsequent calls only pay for the incremental tokens; load-bearing for high-volume LLM use
- **Skill composition** — compresses workflow into a single skill call instead of ad-hoc prompts; the skill *is* a compression of "everything the model needs to know to do X"
- **Shadow clone delegation** — compresses sub-task context by isolating clone context from main; the main agent doesn't carry the clone's working memory
- **Cheaper-model fallback** — compresses frontier-model output quality into cheaper-model capability through distillation, smaller models, or cheap-recall-then-frontier-verify
- **Rubric scoring** — compresses qualitative judgment (completeness, quality, friction, relevance) into numeric values that aggregate cleanly across runs
- **Calibration multipliers** — compress many-runs experience into per-skill priors that adjust future estimates without replaying all past runs

### 1.6. Structural / type compression (schemas and shapes)

- **Zod schemas at every persistent boundary** — compress runtime validation into compile-time contracts; "strict schemas at every persistent boundary, validated by code" is the doc's adopted position and is fundamentally a compression of the total "what shape is this?" question
- **Graph-of-typed-nodes as the unifying data model** — compresses the data model choice into one addressing layer (graph) with per-node shape selection; a compression of the database-selection decision space
- **Affordance vocabulary** — compresses feature comparisons ("cares about X, supports Y") into observer-scoped capabilities ("affords X to observer Y"); a compression of the format-selection discourse into one grammar
- **Append-then-compact structure** — compresses the time dimension into (immutable atoms + compactor); a compression of "how does state change over time?" into a single structural commitment with a known consequence bundle
- **Content-addressable naming** — compresses identity + version + content into a single hash-based key
- **Event envelope schemas** (versioned, typed) — compress "what's an event?" into a fixed shape with typed payload variants

### 1.7. Organizational / hierarchical compression (scope and coordination)

- **Cosmos boundaries** — compress the world into per-org scopes that share nothing; a compression of "everything" into "this org's everything"
- **Agent sets** — compress multi-agent coordination into named groups with shared lifecycle
- **Loop phase hierarchies** — compress workflow into nested trees (loop → phase → skill → step → tool call)
- **Modules and roadmap progress** — compress work into chunked progress units with explicit dependencies
- **MECE categorization** — compresses the total space of concerns into non-overlapping categories that collectively cover the whole (duplicated from §2 because MECE serves both as knowledge organization and as operational scope)
- **Phases and gates** — compress workflow into discrete ordered transitions with verification checkpoints
- **Per-org partitioning** — compresses "which data belongs to whom?" into a partition key

### 1.8. Experience / calibration compression (learning from runs)

- **Calibration data per (skill, context) pair** — compresses many past runs into a single prior (mean, variance, multiplicative correction factor)
- **Estimate correction multipliers** — compress repeated estimation errors into adjustment factors that improve future estimates
- **Review sessions** — compress multi-run analysis into approval/rejection decisions on upgrade proposals
- **Upgrade proposals** — compress "here's how the skill improved across the last N uses" into a single proposal document the navigator can approve or reject
- **Query log as learning signal** — compresses query patterns into skill creation opportunities (frequently-asked questions become candidate skills)
- **SRS cards (spaced repetition) generated from patterns and skills** — compress long-horizon learning into recall-scheduled cards
- **Score histories and score trends** — compress repeated scorecard runs into trend data

### 1.9. Communication compression (inter-system and inter-human)

- **Proactive messaging with priority tiers** (critical / attention / fyi) — compresses system events into notifications with built-in attention discipline
- **Slack thread per execution** — compresses execution state into a single threaded conversation the navigator can follow asynchronously
- **Scheduled-with-cancel notifications** — compress "tell me if X doesn't happen by T" into one primitive instead of requiring a polling loop
- **Voice responses** — compress text into audio (for hands-free use) or compress conversational intent back into text
- **Terminal render functions** — compress JSON state into human-readable text for glanceable display
- **Messaging channel abstractions** — compress multiple transports (Slack, Discord, Telegram, webhooks) into a unified channel interface

### 1.10. Process / workflow compression (running work shapes)

- **Loops as sagas** — compress multi-step workflows into compensation-aware units with forward / pivot / backward recovery
- **Phase transitions** — compress workflow state into discrete steps with explicit enter/exit conditions
- **Gate sync via hooks** — compresses "verify phase completion" into an automatic post-tool-use check that runs without navigator intervention
- **Auto-approval rules** — compress "known-safe operations" into silent passthrough, surfacing only the cases that need review
- **Completion protocols** — compress "how to wrap up a loop" into a shared skill
- **Handoff creation** — compresses session state into a reload-ready document (duplicated from §1 because handoffs are both cognitive compression and process compression)

## Strategy 2 — Expand (mostly foreclosed at the harness)

**Add resources to handle scaling pressure.** Includes horizontal scaling, vertical scaling, parallelization, distribution, offloading, and replication. At the harness layer, **most forms of expansion are structurally foreclosed** by the local-first + sovereignty commitments. The one active form is shadow clones, and even they're bounded by token budget and coordination overhead rather than by hardware.

### 2.1. Active forms (what the harness does use)

- **Shadow clones via in-process Agent spawns (Mechanism 1)** — bounded by token budget, context-window accounting, and process-level concurrency primitives. The main agent delegates sub-tasks to clones, which also *compresses* the main agent's context (see §1.5) — a hybrid expand-and-compress move
- **Shadow clones via separate terminal sessions (Mechanism 2)** — each clone runs in its own process with its own token budget; coordination happens via the event log's consumer-group pattern (Event streaming §3). More expensive than Mechanism 1 but provides stronger isolation
- **Parallel projection builders** — as the CQRS commitment matures, each projection is an independent consumer of the event log that runs in parallel with others; bounded by the projection state footprint and the event-log consumer-group mechanism
- **Parallel loop executions** — different loops can run simultaneously on different parts of the cosmos, bounded by the sequencing constraint that prevents conflicting work on the same resource

### 2.2. Foreclosed forms (what the harness structurally cannot use)

- **Horizontal scaling (more instances)** — ruled out by local-first single-process single-machine
- **Vertical scaling beyond the laptop** — ruled out by single-machine commitment; upgrading the machine is an option, but there's a ceiling
- **Distribution across nodes** — ruled out by local-first
- **Sharding / partitioning across machines** — ruled out by local-first (per-org partitioning within one machine is available, but that's logical partitioning, not physical)
- **Offloading to managed services** — ruled out by sovereignty commitment; the harness rejects external dependencies on principle
- **Replication across machines** — ruled out by local-first (replication as *backup* is fine; replication as *read-scale* is the foreclosed form)

At the app layer, all of these expansions are standard and common. The harness-vs-app distinction in the doc's Layered architecture section is largely about which response strategies are structurally available at each layer.

## Strategy 3 — Shed (used selectively)

**Reduce scaling pressure at the source by refusing, dropping, or filtering work.** The harness uses shedding selectively, constrained by the sleep test (which prohibits dropping high-stakes events) and by the commitment to durable audit trails for consequential decisions.

### 3.1. Active forms (what the harness does use)

- **Token budget caps** (Cost engineering, Resilience patterns §6) — refuse LLM API calls once the budget is exceeded; the primary defense against runaway spending under autonomous operation
- **Rate limiting on external events** — drop or queue excess webhook intake, channel events, scheduled triggers; backpressure signaling to producers where possible
- **Retention limits on low-value telemetry** (Event streaming §5) — time-based drops for high-volume low-stakes events (e.g., step-level progress logs); the counterpart to infinite retention on high-stakes events
- **Gate auto-approval rules** — compress the review queue by excluding known-safe operations from review. Shedding of *reviews*, not of *work*
- **Priority-tiered notifications with quiet-hours respect** (Notification systems §4) — drop low-priority notifications during quiet hours; defer medium-priority; escalate only critical
- **Sampled observability** — record every Nth event for volume-heavy telemetry categories where statistical shape matters more than individual completeness
- **Context window eviction** — when prior conversation exceeds the model's context window, drop the earliest-and-least-relevant portions (a selective form of shedding at the LLM boundary)

### 3.2. Constraints at the harness layer

- **No shedding of high-stakes events** — sleep test requires durable audit for consequential decisions
- **No dropping of synchronous writes** — sleep test requires confirmation before returning
- **No silent retention drops** — retention policy is explicit per event class, not opportunistic
- **No dropping of navigator-visible work without notification** — attention discipline requires the navigator be informed when work is shed

At the app layer, shedding is more aggressive — load shedding under traffic spikes, aggressive TTL caches, statistical sampling across the board. The harness is more conservative because the navigator's trust model depends on durability guarantees.

## Strategy 4 — Defer (used sparingly)

**Shift work in time — do it later, do it lazily, or do it in batches.** The harness uses deferral sparingly because several of its commitments (synchronous durability, navigator attention freshness, sleep-test survivability) push *against* deferring work.

### 4.1. Active forms (what the harness does use)

- **Lazy projection materialization** — some projections compute on-read rather than eagerly on every event; see the eager-vs-lazy tradeoff pattern in Tradeoff patterns §2. The canonical example: the knowledge graph is eagerly maintained; some skill-registry views are derived on demand
- **Batched event-log compaction** — compaction runs are deferred to low-activity windows, not triggered synchronously on every write; see Event streaming §5
- **Scheduled-with-cancel notifications** (Notification systems §8) — "tell me if X doesn't happen by T" as a primitive; the work is deferred up to T and canceled if the condition fulfills before then
- **Deferred skill reloading** — skills are reloaded on next execution, not on file change; avoids unnecessary work when nothing is running
- **Deferred index rebuilds** — FTS5 index rebuilds, trigram index updates can be deferred to low-load windows
- **Eager-vs-lazy tradeoff pattern throughout** — every caching, indexing, projection decision has an eager/lazy dimension and the harness picks per-use-case

### 4.2. Constraints at the harness layer

- **Synchronous durability on canonical state** — the event log's canonical writes cannot be deferred (sleep test)
- **Navigator-urgent work cannot be deferred** — priority-tiered notifications require timely delivery for the critical tier
- **Single-process limits deferral to within-process scheduling** — no external job queue, no cron-style scheduler across machines
- **Session lifetime bounds deferral** — work deferred past the session end has to be picked up by a later session, which creates its own coordination problem

At the app layer, deferral is much more common — background jobs, cron schedules, offline batch processing, queue-and-worker patterns, eventually-consistent reads. The harness's constraints make deferral a less-used strategy in favor of compression (which doesn't shift time) and selective parallelization.

## Why compression dominates at the harness

Running through the four strategies: compression is the large dominant response; expansion is mostly foreclosed by structural commitments; shedding is constrained by the sleep test; deferral is constrained by synchronous durability and navigator attention. **What's left after these constraints is compression, which is why the harness's response profile looks disproportionately like "compress everything."**

This isn't accidental — it's the direct consequence of the structural commitments. The harness picked local-first, sovereignty, sleep test, and synchronous durability specifically *because* the costs of the alternatives (distributed coordination, external dependencies, undetected failures, weak durability) were unacceptable for the navigator's-cosmos framing. Compression became the dominant response by elimination, not by preference.

Changing the structural commitments would change the response profile. If the harness dropped local-first, expansion would become available and some compression would be unnecessary. If it dropped the sleep test, shedding and deferral would expand. The response profile is downstream of the commitments; the commitments are the load-bearing decisions.

### The structural point

Every one of these compression moves does the same thing at a different level: **it reduces the total decision-ready surface area the navigator has to hold in working memory.** The harness is a compression engine because the navigator is a finite cognitive system and the problem space is not. Each compression is chosen because without it, some specific scaling axis would overflow the navigator's capacity (cognitive load, attention budget, context window, token cost, coordination overhead, supervisory reach).

The symmetry with the Harness scaling axes entry is precise: **scaling axes describe what grows; scaling responses describe what the harness does about the growth.** Every "named in the doc" scaling axis has a corresponding response in this entry, and the unnamed scaling axes (apps per org, context window, projection state) correspond to places where the response hasn't been fully designed yet.

The meta-observation: **the orchestrator is disproportionately a compression system.** A traditional web app is roughly 20% compression (indexes, caches, summaries) and 80% delivery (serving, coordinating, storing). The harness is roughly the inverse — most of its machinery exists to compress state, experience, workflow, and attention into navigator-operable primitives. The delivery function (actually running model calls, writing files, executing tools) is comparatively thin. Compression dominates in part because the other three response strategies (expand, shed, defer) are mostly foreclosed or constrained.

### Current doc coverage

| Dimension | What's compressed | Named in the doc? |
|---|---|---|
| Skills, loops, gates, dream states | Workflow patterns, quality thresholds, goals | Yes (foundational; implicit throughout) |
| Handoffs, terminal views, daily status | Session context, live state, cross-cosmos state | Partially (Observability for terminal views; not named as compression) |
| Leverage protocol | Action space | Yes (but framed as recommender, not as compression) |
| Calibrated estimates, calibration multipliers | Experience into priors | Yes (Calibration entry) |
| Knowledge graph | Cross-reference structure | Yes (foundational) |
| Patterns, decisions, memories | Recurring shapes, reasoning, context | Yes (Memory hierarchy) |
| MECE taxonomy, coverage analysis | Skill space structure | Yes (but not named as compression) |
| Leverage scores / value equation | Multi-dimensional value into scalar | Yes (leverage protocol; not named as compression) |
| Event log, log compaction, snapshots | State mutation history | Yes (Event streaming §5, §7) |
| Projections | Event log into query views | Yes (Event streaming §8) |
| Tiered storage (JSONL → Parquet → Iceberg) | Temporal-age into dense formats | Yes (Open table formats thread #10) |
| Bloom filters, roaring bitmaps | Set membership + label sets | Yes (Indexes §1, recently added) |
| FTS5 inverted indexes, trigrams | Text corpora | Yes (Search, Indexes) |
| Vector embeddings | Semantic similarity | Partially (Indexes, Search) |
| Six search surfaces | Per-corpus search | Yes (Search) |
| Summaries | Long text into short text | Partially (scattered) |
| Context window management | Prior conversation into retained state | No (scattered, not recognized as compression) |
| Prompt caching | Repeated prompts into cached prefixes | Partially (Cost engineering) |
| Skill composition | Workflow into one skill call | Yes (foundational; not named as compression) |
| Shadow clone delegation | Sub-task context isolation | Partially (Concurrency primitives) |
| Cheaper-model fallback | Frontier quality into cheap capability | Yes (Cost engineering §8, Resilience patterns §7) |
| Rubric scoring | Qualitative judgment into numeric | Yes (Calibration) |
| Zod schemas, graph-of-typed-nodes | Runtime shape validation, data model choice | Yes (Database selection, Quick reference) |
| Affordance vocabulary, append-then-compact | Capability discourse, state-change shape | Yes (recently added) |
| Content-addressable naming | Identity + version into hash | Yes (Caching §5) |
| Event envelope schemas | "What's an event?" into fixed shape | Yes (Event streaming §6) |
| Cosmos boundaries, per-org partitioning | World into scopes | Yes (Sharding) |
| Agent sets, phase hierarchies | Multi-agent and workflow structure | Partially (not recognized as compression) |
| Review sessions, upgrade proposals | Multi-run analysis into decisions | Yes (Learning) |
| Query log as learning signal | Query patterns into skill candidates | Yes (Search §7, promotion tracker) |
| SRS cards | Long-horizon learning into scheduled recall | Yes (foundational) |
| Proactive messaging with priority tiers | System events into attention discipline | Yes (Notification systems §4) |
| Slack thread per execution | Execution state into threaded conversation | Yes (project-specific) |
| Scheduled-with-cancel notifications | "Tell me if X doesn't happen" into one primitive | Yes (Notification systems §8) |
| Voice responses, terminal renders | Text ↔ audio, state → glanceable | Partially (not recognized as compression) |
| Loops as sagas, phase transitions, gate sync | Workflow shape, recovery, verification | Yes (Saga orchestration, foundational) |
| Auto-approval rules | Known-safe operations into silent passthrough | Partially |

**Gaps:** context window management, shadow clone delegation as compression, agent sets as compression, voice/terminal rendering as compression, and the general framing of the harness as a compression engine are all under-named relative to their actual architectural weight.

### Architectural inspiration thread

**Every compression move above is a response to a specific scaling axis from the Harness scaling axes entry.** The correspondence is:

- Skills, loops, leverage protocol → cognitive load, review queue depth, skills/loops per org
- Handoffs, summaries, terminal views, daily status → session length, inter-interaction gap, navigator attention
- Calibrated estimates, calibration multipliers → experience as a scaling axis (system gets better with use rather than worse)
- Knowledge graph, memories, patterns → knowledge graph nodes/edges, memories per org
- Event log, log compaction, snapshots, projections → event log volume, historical depth, projection state size
- Tiered storage, bloom filters, roaring bitmaps → event log volume, query latency at cold-tier scale
- Inverted indexes, search surfaces → search corpus size
- Context window management, prompt caching, clone delegation → token budget, context window, concurrent clones
- Cheaper-model fallback → token budget, LLM API call volume
- Zod schemas, affordance vocabulary, append-then-compact → structural commitment as scaling foundation
- Cosmos boundaries, per-org partitioning → orgs per instance, data volume
- Agent sets, phase hierarchies, saga patterns → shadow clone coordination, concurrent loops
- Review sessions, upgrade proposals, SRS → experience compression, long-horizon learning
- Proactive messaging, priority tiers, scheduled-with-cancel → navigator attention, autonomy level
- Auto-approval rules, gate sync, completion protocols → autonomy level (these are the mechanisms by which autonomy actually rises)

**The predictive lens this gives the doc**: any new scaling pressure should be met with a named compression response, and any new architectural feature should name which compression dimension it extends. Features that don't compress anything along some named dimension are probably misaligned; features that compress along an unnamed dimension are revealing a gap in the taxonomy.

The near-term unlocks this framing surfaces:
- **Name context window management as a first-class compression concern** with its own walk, since it's currently scattered and has no coherent architectural response despite being one of the hottest constraints in LLM-driven systems
- **Name agent sets and phase hierarchies as compression** of multi-agent coordination and workflow shape, rather than treating them as organizational primitives only
- **Name voice and terminal rendering as compression** of state into glanceable modality-appropriate form, not just as delivery
- **Recognize the harness as structurally a compression engine** and surface this as an adopted architectural position in the Quick Reference — the harness's primary job is to compress the navigator's cognitive load, and this should shape every design decision

### Layer note

**Applies almost entirely to the harness layer.** The app layer's compression concerns look more like traditional web app compression (caching, CDN, response compression, image optimization, minification) because the app layer is a standard production web app stack. The transposed compression dimensions in this entry — cognitive, experiential, calibration, coordination, autonomy-enabling — are specific to the harness's role as a navigator's-cosmos amplifier. Some dimensions straddle (knowledge graph, event log, search, token compression apply at both layers), but the cognitive/attention dimensions are uniquely harness-layer concerns.
